name: Closure
text: Combining any two elements of the group produces another element of the group.

name: Associativity
text: The order in which you perform the operations doesn't matter (as long as you don't change the sequence of elements).

name: Identity Element
text: There is a "do nothing" element that leaves everything unchanged.

name: Inverse Element
text: Every action can be undone.
depends_on: Set theory, binary operations.

name: Distributive laws
text: Multiplication distributes over addition — the link between the two operations.
depends_on: Group axioms (for the additive structure).

text: A field is a system where you can add, subtract, multiply, and divide (except by zero), and both addition and multiplication are commutative.
depends_on: Ring axioms, multiplicative inverses.

name: The Homomorphism Principle
text: The "right" way to relate two algebraic structures is through maps that respect the operations.
depends_on: The specific axiom system of the structure.

name: The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
text: The complex numbers are "complete" for polynomial equations — every polynomial equation has a solution in ℂ.
depends_on: Field axioms (ℂ as a field), completeness of ℝ.

name: Noether's Isomorphism Theorems
text: The structure of a quotient of an algebraic object is determined by the kernel of a homomorphism.
depends_on: Group/ring axioms, homomorphisms, quotient structures.

name: The Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory
text: The symmetries (automorphisms) of a field extension perfectly mirror the structure of its intermediate fields.
depends_on: Group theory, field extensions, polynomial theory.

name: Representation Theory
text: Groups can be studied by how they act as linear transformations on vector spaces.
depends_on: Group axioms, vector space axioms, linear algebra.

name: The Tensor Product
text: The tensor product is the "right" way to multiply vector spaces.
depends_on: Vector space axioms, universal properties.

name: Classification of Finite Abelian Groups
text: Every finite abelian group can be uniquely decomposed into a product of cyclic groups of prime-power order.
depends_on: Group axioms, Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.

name: Cayley's Theorem
text: Every abstract group can be realized as a group of permutations.
depends_on: Group axioms, symmetric group.

name: Sylow Theorems
text: Every finite group contains subgroups of the largest possible prime-power order, and these subgroups are all "essentially the same" (conjugate).
depends_on: - Group axioms

name: Burnside's Lemma (Cauchy-Frobenius Lemma)
text: To count the number of truly distinct objects (up to symmetry), average the number of objects fixed by each symmetry operation.
depends_on: - Group axioms, group actions

name: Jordan-Hölder Theorem
text: Every finite group can be broken down into simple groups (groups with no proper normal subgroups), and this decomposition is essentially unique — the "prime factorization" of groups.
depends_on: - Group axioms, normal subgroups, simple groups

name: Wedderburn's Little Theorem
text: In finite algebra, you get commutativity of multiplication "for free" — there are no noncommutative finite division rings.
depends_on: - Ring axioms, field axioms

name: Artin-Wedderburn Theorem
text: Semisimple rings — the "nicely decomposable" rings — are completely classified: they are products of matrix rings over division rings.
depends_on: - Ring axioms, module theory

name: Hilbert's Basis Theorem
text: If a ring has the property that every ideal can be described by finitely many generators, then so does its polynomial ring.
depends_on: - Ring axioms, ideal theory

name: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz
text: Over an algebraically closed field, a system of polynomial equations either has a solution or the polynomials generate the entire ring.
depends_on: - Ring axioms, Hilbert's Basis Theorem

name: Maschke's Theorem
text: For finite groups over "good" fields (characteristic zero or coprime to the group order), every representation breaks cleanly into irreducible building blocks.
depends_on: - Group axioms, representation theory

name: Schur's Lemma
text: If two irreducible representations are connected by a map that respects the group action, the map must be either zero or an isomorphism -- there is no middle ground.
depends_on: - Representation theory (Principle 5)

name: Nakayama's Lemma
text: Nakayama's lemma says that for finitely generated modules over local or semi-local rings, if an ideal in the Jacobson radical "absorbs" the module (IM = M), then the module must be zero.
depends_on: - Ring axioms, module theory

name: Homological Algebra (Ext and Tor)
text: Homological algebra studies the "obstructions" that arise when algebraic operations (like taking homomorphisms or tensor products) fail to preserve exact sequences.
depends_on: - Module theory (Axiom System 6)

name: The Classification of Finite Simple Groups
text: Every finite group can be built from simple groups (the Jordan-Holder theorem), and the classification identifies all possible simple building blocks.
depends_on: - Group axioms, Jordan-Holder theorem (Theorem 9)

name: The Snake Lemma
text: The snake lemma is the fundamental tool for building long exact sequences in algebra.
depends_on: - Module theory, exact sequences

name: The Quillen-Suslin Theorem (Serre's Conjecture)
text: Over a polynomial ring in several variables, every "locally free" module (one that looks free in a neighborhood of each point) is actually globally free -- it has a basis.
depends_on: - Module theory (Axiom System 6)

name: Morita Equivalence
text: Two rings are Morita equivalent if their module theories are indistinguishable -- every construction and theorem about modules over one ring translates perfectly to the other.
depends_on: - Module theory (Axiom System 6)

name: Tropical Geometry Foundations
text: Tropical geometry converts curved algebraic geometry into straight-line, piecewise-linear geometry.
depends_on: - Classical algebraic geometry (Nullstellensatz, Theorem 13)

name: Operads and Higher Algebraic Structures
text: Operads are a mathematical framework for describing algebraic operations with multiple inputs and one output, and the laws they satisfy.
depends_on: - Category theory, monoidal categories

name: The Langlands Correspondence for GL(n) over Function Fields
text: The Langlands correspondence is one of the deepest organizing principles in modern mathematics.
depends_on: - Class field theory (Number Theory: Principle 5)

name: Derived Algebraic Geometry
text: Derived algebraic geometry upgrades classical geometry by keeping track of all the "hidden" algebraic information that is normally lost.
depends_on: - Homological algebra (Principle 7)

name: Infinity-Operads and Higher Algebra (Lurie)
text: p-adic Hodge theory is the p-adic analogue of the deep connection between topology and calculus that Hodge theory provides over the complex numbers.
depends_on: - Galois representations, etale cohomology

name: Total Order Axioms
text: The real numbers are lined up in order, and this order is compatible with arithmetic.

name: Completeness (Least Upper Bound Property) — THE KEY AXIOM
text: There are no "gaps" in the real number line.
depends_on: Field axioms, order axioms.

name: The ε-δ Definition of Limit
text: f(x) approaches L as x approaches a if we can make f(x) as close to L as we want by making x sufficiently close to a.
depends_on: The real numbers (completeness, distance via absolute value).

name: Continuity
text: A function is continuous if small changes in input produce small changes in output.
depends_on: Limits (ε-δ definition).

name: The Derivative (Definition of Differentiation)
text: The derivative is the instantaneous rate of change — the slope of the tangent line at a point.
depends_on: Limits.

name: The Riemann Integral
text: The integral is the "area under the curve," computed as the limit of increasingly fine Riemann sums.
depends_on: Limits, completeness of ℝ.

name: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
text: Differentiation and integration are inverse operations.
depends_on: Continuity, derivative, integral, completeness.

name: Convergence of Sequences and Series
text: A sequence converges if its terms get arbitrarily close to a limit value and stay there.
depends_on: Completeness of ℝ (Cauchy completeness).

name: Compactness (Heine-Borel Theorem)
text: Compact sets are the "nice" sets where infinite processes behave well — every sequence has a convergent subsequence, and continuous functions achieve their bounds.
depends_on: Completeness, Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem.

name: Metric Space Axioms (Generalized Analysis)
text: A metric space is any set with a notion of "distance" satisfying three natural properties.
depends_on: Set theory, the concept of a function.

name: Measure and Integration (Lebesgue Theory)
text: A measure assigns a "size" (length, area, volume, probability) to sets in a way that is non-negative and additive.
depends_on: Set theory (σ-algebras), real number axioms.

name: Banach Fixed-Point Theorem (Contraction Mapping Principle)
text: If a map always brings points closer together in a complete space, it has exactly one fixed point, and iterating the map from anywhere converges to it.
depends_on: Completeness of the metric space.

name: The Implicit Function Theorem
text: If a system of equations is satisfied at a point and a certain non-degeneracy condition holds, then the equations locally define some variables as smooth functions of the others.
depends_on: Completeness of ℝ, Banach fixed-point theorem, differentiability.

name: Stokes' Theorem (General Form)
text: The integral of a derivative over a region equals the integral of the original form over the boundary.
depends_on: Differential forms, smooth manifolds, exterior derivative.

name: Banach and Hilbert Spaces (Functional Analysis)
text: Functional analysis extends linear algebra and analysis to infinite-dimensional spaces — spaces of functions, sequences, and operators.
depends_on: Completeness, vector space axioms, inner product.

name: Taylor's Theorem and Power Series
text: Any sufficiently smooth function can be approximated by a polynomial.
depends_on: Differentiability, completeness of ℝ.

name: Hahn-Banach Theorem
text: You can always extend a bounded linear measurement from a part of a space to the entire space without increasing its size.
depends_on: - Vector space axioms, sublinear functionals

name: Open Mapping Theorem (Banach-Schauder)
text: A surjective bounded linear map between complete spaces automatically maps open sets to open sets.
depends_on: - Banach spaces (completeness)

name: Closed Graph Theorem
text: For a linear operator between complete spaces, you can verify continuity by checking a seemingly weaker condition: that the graph is a closed set.
depends_on: - Banach spaces (completeness)

name: Riesz Representation Theorem
text: In a Hilbert space, every continuous linear measurement is secretly an inner product with some fixed element.
depends_on: - Hilbert space structure (inner product, completeness)

name: Stone-Weierstrass Theorem
text: On a compact space, any "sufficiently rich" collection of continuous functions can approximate any continuous function as closely as desired.
depends_on: - Compactness, continuity

name: Spectral Theorem for Self-Adjoint Operators
text: Every self-adjoint operator can be "diagonalized" using a spectral measure.
depends_on: - Hilbert spaces, self-adjoint operators

name: Fubini's Theorem
text: When integrating a function of two variables, you can compute the double integral by integrating one variable at a time, in either order.
depends_on: - Lebesgue measure and integration (Principle 8)

name: Dominated Convergence Theorem
text: If a sequence of functions converges pointwise and is uniformly bounded by an integrable function, then the limit of the integrals equals the integral of the limit.
depends_on: - Lebesgue measure and integration (Principle 8)

name: Arzela-Ascoli Theorem
text: A family of continuous functions on a compact space has a uniformly convergent subsequence if and only if the functions are uniformly bounded and "equally continuous" (equicontinuous).
depends_on: - Compactness (Principle 6), metric spaces (Principle 7)

name: Uniform Boundedness Principle (Banach-Steinhaus)
text: If a family of linear operators is bounded at every individual point, then there is a single bound that works for all points and all operators simultaneously.
depends_on: - Banach spaces (completeness)

name: The Sobolev Embedding Theorem
text: The Sobolev embedding theorem tells you that if a function has enough weak derivatives in L^p, it is automatically smoother than you might expect.
depends_on: - Lebesgue integration (Principle 8)

name: The Lax-Milgram Theorem
text: The Lax-Milgram theorem guarantees that a large class of variational problems have unique solutions.
depends_on: - Hilbert spaces (Principle 9)

name: Distribution Theory (Schwartz)
text: Distribution theory extends calculus to "functions" that are too singular to be ordinary functions, like the Dirac delta "function" (zero everywhere except at one point, yet integrating to 1).
depends_on: - Functional analysis (Principle 9)

name: Riesz-Thorin Interpolation Theorem
text: If a linear operator works well on two different L^p spaces, it automatically works on all the intermediate spaces in between, with a bound that interpolates smoothly between the two extremes.
depends_on: - L^p spaces, Lebesgue integration (Principle 8)

name: The Baire Category Theorem
text: In a complete space, "most" points (in the topological sense) avoid any countable collection of "thin" (nowhere-dense) sets.
depends_on: - Complete metric spaces (Principle 7)

name: Carleson's Theorem (Pointwise Convergence of Fourier Series)
text: If you decompose a square-integrable function into its Fourier series (sines and cosines), the partial sums of the series actually converge back to the original function at almost every point.
depends_on: - Fourier analysis, L^p spaces

name: Ultraproducts and Nonstandard Analysis
text: Nonstandard analysis provides a rigorous framework for infinitesimals -- infinitely small quantities that Leibniz and Euler used freely but that were banished by the epsilon-delta revolution.
depends_on: - Model theory, ultrafilters

name: Optimal Transport and the Wasserstein Distance
text: Optimal transport theory solves the problem of moving one distribution of mass to another at minimum cost.
depends_on: - Measure theory, Lebesgue integration (Principle 8)

name: The Regularity Structures Framework (Hairer)
text: Regularity structures provide a way to make mathematical sense of equations involving products of extremely rough (distributional) objects -- operations that are classically meaningless.
depends_on: - Distribution theory (Principle 10)

name: Random Matrix Universality (Wigner-Dyson-Mehta)
text: Schramm-Loewner evolution is a family of random curves that describes the interfaces in two-dimensional critical phenomena — the boundaries between phases in systems like percolation, the Ising model, and random walks at their critical points.
depends_on: - Complex analysis, conformal mappings, Loewner equation

name: The Differential Equation as the Language of Change
text: Most laws of nature and engineering are expressed as relationships between quantities and how they change.
depends_on: Calculus, analysis (existence and uniqueness theorems).

name: Existence and Uniqueness (Picard-Lindelöf Theorem)
text: If the rate of change depends smoothly on the current state, then the future is uniquely determined by the present.
depends_on: Analysis (completeness, Banach fixed-point theorem).

name: Linear Algebra as the Framework for Linear Systems
text: Linear algebra is the mathematics of systems of equations, transformations, and vector spaces.
depends_on: Field axioms, vector space axioms.

name: The Calculus of Variations and the Principle of Least Action
text: Nature "chooses" the path that extremizes (usually minimizes) a certain quantity — the action.
depends_on: Analysis (functional analysis, integration).

name: Fourier Analysis — Decomposition into Frequencies
text: Any signal can be broken down into a sum of pure sine and cosine waves at different frequencies.
depends_on: Analysis (L² spaces, measure theory, Hilbert space theory).

name: Optimization — Finding the Best Solution
text: Optimization finds the best solution among all feasible options.
depends_on: Calculus (gradients), linear algebra, convex analysis.

name: Numerical Approximation and Error Analysis
text: Since computers work with finite numbers, we must approximate continuous mathematics.
depends_on: Analysis (Taylor's theorem, error bounds), linear algebra.

name: Dimensional Analysis and Scaling
text: Physical laws must be dimensionally consistent.
depends_on: Algebra of dimensions, physical reasoning.

name: Dynamical Systems and Chaos Theory
text: Dynamical systems theory studies how systems evolve over time.
depends_on: Differential equations, topology (qualitative theory).

name: Perturbation Theory and Asymptotic Analysis
text: When a problem is "almost" a simpler problem, solve the simple version first and then correct for the small complication step by step.
depends_on: Differential equations, Taylor series, asymptotic analysis.

name: Integral Transforms (Laplace, Fourier, Z-Transform)
text: Integral transforms convert hard problems (differential equations) into easier ones (algebraic equations) by changing the domain of the function.
depends_on: Analysis (complex analysis, convergence), Fourier analysis.

name: Green's Functions and Fundamental Solutions
text: A Green's function is the system's response to a point impulse.
depends_on: Linear operators, distribution theory, PDEs.

name: Monte Carlo Methods and Stochastic Simulation
text: You can estimate complicated quantities (integrals, probabilities, expectations) by generating random samples and averaging.
depends_on: Probability theory (LLN, CLT), random number generation.

name: Finite Element Method
text: The finite element method breaks a complicated domain into small simple pieces, solves the problem approximately on each piece, and assembles the results.
depends_on: Variational principles, functional analysis (Sobolev spaces), linear algebra.

name: Noether's Theorem (Symmetry and Conservation Laws)
text: Symmetries in the laws of physics directly produce conservation laws.
depends_on: - Calculus of variations (Principle 3)

name: Optimal Control Theory (Pontryagin's Maximum Principle)
text: Optimal control theory finds the best way to steer a dynamical system over time.
depends_on: - Calculus of variations (Principle 3)

name: Wavelet Theory
text: Wavelets are "little waves" that can decompose signals into components that are localized in both time and frequency simultaneously.
depends_on: - Fourier analysis (Principle 4)

name: Information Theory (Shannon)
text: Information theory quantifies information, communication limits, and data compression.
depends_on: - Probability theory, logarithmic measures

name: Lyapunov Stability Theory
text: Lyapunov stability theory provides a method to prove stability of an equilibrium without solving the differential equation.
depends_on: - Differential equations (Principle 1)

name: Bifurcation Theory
text: Bifurcation theory studies how the qualitative behavior of a dynamical system changes as you tune a parameter.
depends_on: - Dynamical systems theory (Principle 8)

name: Homogenization Theory
text: Many physical materials have fine-scale structure (composites, porous media, polycrystals).
depends_on: - PDE theory, functional analysis (Sobolev spaces)

name: Spectral Methods for PDEs
text: Spectral methods solve differential equations by representing the solution as a sum of smooth basis functions (like Fourier modes or Chebyshev polynomials).
depends_on: - Fourier analysis (Principle 4), orthogonal polynomials

name: Compressed Sensing (Candes-Tao-Donoho)
text: Compressed sensing says that sparse signals -- those with few nonzero components relative to their dimension -- can be perfectly reconstructed from far fewer measurements than traditional sampling theory (Nyquist-Shannon) requires.
depends_on: - Linear algebra, sparsity

name: Tensor Network Methods
text: High-dimensional problems suffer from the "curse of dimensionality" -- the number of parameters grows exponentially with dimension.
depends_on: - Linear algebra, multilinear algebra

name: Randomized Numerical Linear Algebra
text: Classical matrix algorithms (SVD, eigendecomposition, least squares) are too slow for modern massive datasets.
depends_on: - Linear algebra, singular value decomposition

name: Model Order Reduction (Reduced Basis Methods)
text: Many engineering problems require solving the same type of equation millions of times for different parameter values (e.g., optimizing an airfoil shape, or real-time control of a thermal system).
depends_on: - Finite element method (Principle 13)

name: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)
text: Physics-informed neural networks embed known physical laws (differential equations) directly into the training process of a neural network.
depends_on: - Differential equations (Principle 1)

name: Data-Driven Discovery of Governing Equations (SINDy)
text: SINDy automatically discovers the mathematical equations governing a dynamical system from measured data.
depends_on: - Dynamical systems (Principle 8)

name: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and Scientific Machine Learning
text: Physics-informed neural networks encode the laws of physics directly into the training of a neural network.
depends_on: - Differential equations (Principle 1)

name: Wasserstein Gradient Flows and Optimal Transport in Applied Mathematics
text: Many evolution equations in physics can be understood as "rolling downhill" on an energy landscape, where the "distance" is the optimal transport distance.
depends_on: - Optimal transport theory

name: Topological Data Analysis in Applications
text: Persistent homology extracts the "shape" of data at all scales simultaneously.
depends_on: - Algebraic topology, homology (Geometry & Topology: Principle P5)

name: The Axiomatic Method
text: Mathematics works by choosing starting assumptions and rigorously deriving everything else from them.
depends_on: Logic (for the rules of inference).

name: Consistency as a Necessary Condition
text: A mathematical system must not contradict itself.
depends_on: Non-contradiction (from logic).

name: Logicism — Mathematics Reduces to Logic
text: Mathematics is just very elaborate logic.
depends_on: Logic, set theory (or a substitute like type theory).

name: Formalism — Mathematics as Symbol Manipulation
text: Mathematics is a game played with symbols according to rules.
depends_on: Formal languages, proof theory.

name: Intuitionism — Mathematics as Mental Construction
text: Mathematics is a human activity of building mental constructions.
depends_on: Intuitionistic logic (a weaker logic that rejects excluded middle).

name: The Principle of Mathematical Induction
text: If something is true for zero, and being true for any number means it's true for the next number, then it's true for all natural numbers.
depends_on: The concept of natural number, succession.

name: The Peano Axioms (Foundations of Arithmetic)
text: The natural numbers are built from zero by repeatedly taking successors, the successor function is one-to-one, zero is the starting point, and you can prove things about all numbers using induction.
depends_on: Logic, the concept of a function.

name: The Concept of Isomorphism (Structural Identity)
text: Mathematics doesn't care about *what* things are, only about *how they relate to each other*.
depends_on: The concept of structure-preserving maps (homomorphisms, bijections).

name: Definition by Recursion / Well-Founded Recursion
text: You can define mathematical objects by specifying a starting point and a rule for building each piece from previously built pieces.
depends_on: Well-ordering (or well-foundedness), induction.

name: Completeness and Categoricity
text: A complete theory settles every question.
depends_on: Model theory, logical consequence.

name: The Abstraction Principle and Foundational Crisis
text: The idea that "every definable collection is a set" leads to paradox.
depends_on: Set theory, self-reference.

name: Constructive Mathematics and the BHK Interpretation
text: In constructive mathematics, proving something exists means actually producing it.
depends_on: Intuitionistic logic.

name: The Well-Ordering Principle (General)
text: A set is well-ordered if no matter what subset you pick, there is always a smallest element.
depends_on: - Axiom of Choice (for the general theorem)

name: Gödel's Completeness Theorem (for Foundations)
text: First-order logic is perfectly calibrated: anything that must be true (in every possible interpretation) can be formally proved, and anything provable is indeed true.
depends_on: - First-order predicate logic

name: The Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem (for Foundations)
text: First-order logic cannot control the size of infinite structures.
depends_on: - Gödel's completeness theorem

name: The Curry-Howard Correspondence
text: Proofs and programs are secretly the same thing.
depends_on: - Intuitionistic logic

name: Structuralism in Mathematics
text: Chromatic homotopy theory reveals that the stable homotopy groups of spheres — one of the most fundamental and mysterious objects in all of mathematics — are organized into layers of increasing complexity, like a prism decomposing white light into its component colors.
depends_on: - Stable homotopy theory, spectra

name: Two Points Determine a Line
text: You can always draw a straight line between two points.

name: Line Extension
text: A straight line goes on forever in both directions.

name: Circle Construction
text: You can always draw a circle of any size around any point.

name: Right Angle Universality
text: A right angle is the same everywhere — it is a universal standard of perpendicularity.

name: The Parallel Postulate
text: Parallel lines never meet (in flat space).
depends_on: Primitive notions of point, line, plane.

name: Finite Intersections
text: A topology defines which subsets are "open" — this determines the notions of continuity, convergence, and connectedness.
depends_on: Set theory.

name: Homeomorphism (Topological Equivalence)
text: Two shapes are "the same" topologically if one can be continuously deformed into the other without tearing or gluing.
depends_on: Continuity, topological space axioms.

name: Euler Characteristic
text: The alternating sum of vertices, edges, and faces of a polyhedron is always 2.
depends_on: Homology theory (algebraic topology).

name: The Fundamental Group
text: The fundamental group measures the "holes" in a space by studying loops that cannot be shrunk to a point.
depends_on: Topological space axioms, group axioms, homotopy.

name: Riemannian Metric (Differential Geometry)
text: A Riemannian metric is a way of measuring distance on a curved surface (or higher-dimensional space) that can vary from point to point.
depends_on: Smooth manifolds, tangent spaces, tensor calculus.

name: Compactness in Topology
text: A compact space is one where you can always extract a finite amount of information to describe its covering.
depends_on: Topological space axioms, open cover definition.

name: Classification of Closed Surfaces
text: There is a complete list of all possible 2D surfaces — they are classified by their number of "handles" (genus) and whether they are orientable.
depends_on: Topological space axioms, fundamental group, Euler characteristic.

name: The Gauss-Bonnet Theorem
text: The total curvature of a closed surface is completely determined by its topology (the Euler characteristic).
depends_on: Riemannian metric, Euler characteristic, integration on manifolds.

name: Covering Spaces and the Fundamental Group
text: Covering spaces "unwrap" a topological space.
depends_on: Fundamental group, topological space axioms.

name: Homology and Cohomology
text: Homology counts the different types of holes in a space: H₀ counts connected components, H₁ counts loops, H₂ counts enclosed cavities, and so on.
depends_on: Topological space axioms, algebra (chain complexes, exact sequences).

name: Smooth Manifolds and Differential Forms
text: Smooth manifolds are spaces that locally look like ℝⁿ and support calculus.
depends_on: Topological space axioms, calculus, linear algebra.

name: Brouwer Fixed-Point Theorem
text: If you continuously stir a cup of coffee, at least one point ends up where it started.
depends_on: Homology (the proof uses the fact that the disk is not a retract of its boundary sphere).

name: Poincaré Duality
text: On a closed oriented manifold, k-dimensional holes are perfectly mirrored by (n-k)-dimensional holes.
depends_on: - Homology and cohomology (Principle 7)

name: The de Rham Theorem
text: There are two seemingly different ways to detect holes in a manifold: using differential forms (calculus) or using singular chains (algebra).
depends_on: - Smooth manifolds and differential forms (Principle 8)

name: The Atiyah-Singer Index Theorem
text: The number of solutions of a differential equation on a manifold is determined by the topology of the manifold.
depends_on: - Elliptic operators, characteristic classes

name: The Riemann-Roch Theorem
text: On an algebraic curve, the number of independent meromorphic functions with prescribed poles and zeros is determined by the degree of the divisor and the genus of the curve.
depends_on: - Riemann surfaces / algebraic curves

name: Fiber Bundles and Connections
text: A fiber bundle attaches a copy of a "fiber" space to each point of a "base" space in a smoothly varying way.
depends_on: - Smooth manifolds, Lie groups

name: Thurston's Geometrization Conjecture (Perelman's Theorem)
text: Every compact 3-dimensional space can be decomposed into pieces, each of which has one of exactly eight possible geometric structures.
depends_on: - Riemannian geometry, Ricci flow (Hamilton, 1982)

name: Nash Embedding Theorem
text: Any curved space, no matter how abstract, can be realized as a surface sitting inside a high-dimensional flat space without distorting any distances.
depends_on: - Riemannian metric (Principle 4)

name: Morse Theory
text: The topology of a manifold is encoded in the critical points of any "generic" smooth function on it.
depends_on: - Smooth manifolds, critical point theory

name: The h-Cobordism Theorem
text: In dimensions 6 and above, if two manifolds are connected by a "cylinder" that is topologically trivial (homotopically a product), then they are actually the same smooth manifold.
depends_on: - Morse theory (Principle 10)

name: Symplectic Geometry
text: Symplectic geometry studies spaces with a special structure that generalizes the phase space of classical mechanics.
depends_on: - Smooth manifolds and differential forms (Principle 8)

name: The Lefschetz Fixed-Point Theorem
text: The Lefschetz theorem provides an algebraic test for fixed points: compute an alternating sum of traces of the map's action on homology groups.
depends_on: - Homology and cohomology (Principle 7)

name: Characteristic Classes (Chern, Pontryagin, Stiefel-Whitney)
text: Characteristic classes are topological invariants that detect whether a vector bundle is "twisted." A trivial bundle (a product) has all characteristic classes zero, but a nontrivial bundle (like the Mobius band as a line bundle over the circle) has nonzero classes.
depends_on: - Fiber bundles and connections (Principle 9)

name: Seiberg-Witten Invariants and 4-Manifold Topology
text: Seiberg-Witten invariants are numbers computed from solutions of differential equations on 4-dimensional manifolds that detect subtle differences in smooth structure invisible to topology alone.
depends_on: - Smooth manifolds (Principle 8), fiber bundles and connections (Principle 9)

name: Persistent Homology and Topological Data Analysis
text: Persistent homology extracts the "shape" of data at all scales simultaneously.
depends_on: - Homology and cohomology (Principle 7)

name: The Uniformization Theorem
text: There are only three "shapes" for simply connected surfaces with a conformal structure: the sphere, the plane, and the hyperbolic plane.
depends_on: - Riemann surfaces, complex analysis

name: Cobordism Theory
text: Two manifolds are "cobordant" if they together form the boundary of a higher-dimensional manifold.
depends_on: - Smooth manifolds (Principle 8)

name: Tropical Geometry and Polyhedral Topology
text: Tropical geometry replaces smooth curves and surfaces with networks of straight-line segments and flat pieces.
depends_on: - Algebraic geometry, toric varieties

name: The Cobordism Hypothesis (Lurie)
text: The cobordism hypothesis says that a topological quantum field theory is entirely determined by what it assigns to a single point.
depends_on: - Cobordism theory, smooth manifolds

name: Motivic Cohomology and the Bloch-Kato Conjectures
text: Percolation theory studies when random networks become connected.
depends_on: - Probability theory, independence (Probability: Axiom System 1)

name: Law of Identity
text: A thing is what it is.
depends_on: Nothing — this is irreducible.

name: Law of Non-Contradiction
text: Nothing can both be and not be at the same time in the same respect.
depends_on: Negation (¬) and conjunction (∧) as primitive operations.

name: Law of Excluded Middle
text: For any statement, either it or its negation holds.
depends_on: Disjunction (∨) and negation (¬) as primitive operations.

name: Modus Ponens (Rule of Detachment)
text: If something is true, and that something implies a second thing, then the second thing is also true.
depends_on: Implication (→) as a primitive connective.

name: Universal Instantiation
text: If something is true for everything, it is true for any particular thing.
depends_on: The universal quantifier (∀) and the concept of a domain of discourse.

name: Existential Generalization
text: If something is true of a particular thing, then there exists something for which it is true.
depends_on: The existential quantifier (∃).

name: Axiom of Extensionality
text: Two sets are equal if and only if they have exactly the same members.
depends_on: The membership relation (∈) and equality (=) as primitives.

name: Axiom of Empty Set
text: The empty set exists.
depends_on: Extensionality (to guarantee uniqueness of ∅).

name: Axiom of Pairing
text: For any two objects, there exists a set containing exactly those two objects.
depends_on: Extensionality.

name: Axiom of Union
text: For any collection of sets, there exists a set that contains all elements that belong to at least one set in the collection.
depends_on: Extensionality.

name: Axiom of Power Set
text: For any set A, there exists a set containing all subsets of A.
depends_on: The subset relation (⊆), which is defined from ∈.

name: Axiom Schema of Separation (Aussonderung)
text: Given any set and any property, there exists a subset of that set containing exactly those elements satisfying the property.
depends_on: Extensionality. This is a schema (one axiom for each formula φ), not a single axiom.

name: Axiom Schema of Replacement
text: The image of a set under any definable function is also a set.
depends_on: Separation (as a generalization).

name: Axiom of Infinity
text: There exists at least one infinite set — specifically, a set that contains ∅ and, for each of its elements x, also contains x ∪ {x}.
depends_on: Empty set, union, pairing.

name: Axiom of Foundation (Regularity)
text: Every non-empty set has a member that shares no elements with the set.
depends_on: The other ZFC axioms.

name: Axiom of Choice (AC)
text: Given any collection of non-empty boxes, you can always choose one item from each box — even if the collection is infinite and there is no rule for making the choice.
depends_on: Independent of ZF — can be accepted or rejected without contradiction.

name: Gödel's First Incompleteness Theorem
text: No sufficiently powerful consistent system can prove all true statements about natural numbers.
depends_on: The system must be consistent, recursively enumerable, and strong enough to encode arithmetic.

name: Gödel's Second Incompleteness Theorem
text: A mathematical system cannot use its own axioms to prove that it is free of contradictions.
depends_on: First Incompleteness Theorem.

name: Gödel's Completeness Theorem (for First-Order Logic)
text: First-order logic is "complete" — anything that is true in all possible interpretations can be formally proved.
depends_on: First-order logic with standard deduction rules.

name: Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem
text: First-order logic cannot "pin down" the size of infinite structures.
depends_on: Completeness theorem, compactness theorem.

name: Martin's Axiom (MA)
text: Martin's Axiom says that certain "generic" objects can be found as long as you don't ask for too many conditions simultaneously (fewer than continuum-many).
depends_on: - ZFC axioms, forcing

name: The Compactness Theorem (for Logic & Set Theory)
text: You cannot create an inconsistency using infinitely many axioms unless some finite subset is already inconsistent.
depends_on: - First-order logic, completeness theorem

name: Ramsey Theory
text: Complete disorder is impossible.
depends_on: - Combinatorics, set theory

name: Large Cardinal Axioms (Overview)
text: Large cardinals are "very large infinities" whose existence cannot be proved from the standard axioms but can be assumed as extra axioms.
depends_on: - ZFC, elementary embeddings, ultrafilters

name: Borel Determinacy
text: Absoluteness means a statement's truth value does not change between different models of set theory.
depends_on: - ZFC, forcing, inner models

name: Cohen's Independence of the Continuum Hypothesis
text: The effective topos is a mathematical universe in which every function is computable, every set is constructively defined, and the classical law of excluded middle fails.
depends_on: - Topos theory, Grothendieck toposes

name: The Peano Axioms Define the Natural Numbers
text: The natural numbers are built from zero by counting: 0, 1, 2, 3, ...
depends_on: Mathematical Foundations.

name: The Well-Ordering Principle
text: Among any collection of natural numbers, there is always a smallest one.
depends_on: Peano axioms.

name: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
text: Every whole number greater than 1 is either prime or can be built by multiplying primes together, and there is only one way to do it (ignoring order).
depends_on: Definition of prime number, well-ordering principle.

name: Euclid's Theorem (Infinitude of Primes)
text: The list of primes never ends.
depends_on: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, well-ordering.

name: The Division Algorithm
text: Division with remainder always works and gives a unique answer.
depends_on: Well-ordering principle.

name: The Euclidean Algorithm
text: To find the greatest common divisor of two numbers, keep dividing and taking remainders until you hit zero.
depends_on: Division algorithm, well-ordering (guarantees termination).

name: Modular Arithmetic (Congruence)
text: Two numbers are "the same mod n" if they have the same remainder when divided by n.
depends_on: Division algorithm, equivalence relations.

name: Fermat's Little Theorem
text: Raise any number to the power (p-1), divide by the prime p, and the remainder is always 1.
depends_on: Modular arithmetic, group theory ((ℤ/pℤ)* is a group of order p-1).

name: Quadratic Reciprocity
text: Whether p is a square mod q is intimately related to whether q is a square mod p, in a symmetric and beautiful way.
depends_on: Modular arithmetic, Legendre symbol.

name: The Prime Number Theorem
text: The number of primes up to x is approximately x divided by the natural logarithm of x.
depends_on: Complex analysis, the Riemann zeta function ζ(s).

name: The Chinese Remainder Theorem
text: If you know the remainders when dividing by several coprime numbers, there is exactly one number (up to the product) that gives all those remainders.
depends_on: Modular arithmetic, Bézout's identity.

name: Dirichlet's Theorem on Primes in Arithmetic Progressions
text: There are infinitely many primes in any arithmetic progression whose first term and common difference share no common factor (e.g., infinitely many primes of the form 4k+1).
depends_on: Complex analysis, Dirichlet characters, L-functions.

name: Diophantine Equations and Fermat's Last Theorem
text: You cannot find whole numbers that satisfy the Pythagorean equation if the exponent is 3 or higher.
depends_on: Algebraic number theory, elliptic curves, modular forms.

name: p-adic Numbers
text: The p-adic numbers are an alternative way of measuring "closeness" — two numbers are close if their difference is divisible by a high power of p.
depends_on: Completeness, metric spaces, valuation theory.

name: The Riemann Hypothesis
text: The Riemann Hypothesis is the most important unsolved problem in mathematics.
depends_on: - Complex analysis, the Riemann zeta function

name: Hasse-Minkowski Theorem (Local-Global Principle)
text: To determine if a quadratic equation has a rational solution, it suffices to check that it has solutions in the real numbers and in all p-adic number systems.
depends_on: - p-adic numbers (Principle 3)

name: Class Field Theory
text: Class field theory completely classifies all abelian extensions (extensions with commutative Galois group) of a number field.
depends_on: - Galois theory, algebraic number theory

name: Mordell's Conjecture (Faltings' Theorem)
text: The polynomial method is the surprising discovery that polynomials — objects from algebra — can solve problems in combinatorics that seemed to require entirely different tools.
depends_on: - Finite fields, polynomial rings (Algebra: Ring Axioms)

name: Non-Negativity
text: Probabilities are never negative.

name: Normalization
text: Something must happen — the probability of the entire sample space is 1.

name: Countable Additivity (σ-Additivity)
text: The probability of one of several mutually exclusive events occurring is the sum of their individual probabilities.
depends_on: Measure theory (probability is a special case of a measure with total mass 1).

name: Conditional Probability and Bayes' Theorem
text: The probability of A given that B occurred is the probability of both happening divided by the probability of B.
depends_on: Kolmogorov axioms, definition of conditional probability.

name: Independence
text: Two events are independent if knowing that one occurred gives no information about whether the other occurred.
depends_on: Kolmogorov axioms.

name: Random Variable and Expectation
text: A random variable assigns a number to each outcome.
depends_on: Measure theory, Lebesgue integration, Kolmogorov axioms.

name: Law of Large Numbers (LLN)
text: The average of many independent observations converges to the expected value.
depends_on: Independence, expectation, Kolmogorov axioms.

name: Central Limit Theorem (CLT)
text: The sum (or average) of many independent random variables is approximately normally distributed, regardless of the original distribution.
depends_on: Independence, finite variance, Kolmogorov axioms.

name: Likelihood and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text: The likelihood measures how "plausible" each parameter value is given the observed data.
depends_on: Probability models, optimization.

name: The Bayesian Framework
text: Start with prior beliefs about parameters, observe data, update beliefs using Bayes' theorem to get posterior beliefs.
depends_on: Bayes' theorem, Kolmogorov axioms, prior specification.

name: Sufficiency and the Rao-Blackwell Theorem
text: A sufficient statistic captures all the information in the data about the parameter.
depends_on: Conditional expectation, probability models.

name: The Markov Property
text: A system has the Markov property if knowing the current state tells you everything you need to predict the future — the past history is irrelevant.
depends_on: Conditional probability, stochastic processes.

name: Martingale Theory
text: A martingale is a "fair game" — on average, the next value equals the current value.
depends_on: Conditional expectation, filtrations, measure theory.

name: The Cramér-Rao Lower Bound
text: There is a minimum possible variance for any unbiased estimator.
depends_on: Fisher information, unbiasedness.

name: Neyman-Pearson Lemma (Hypothesis Testing)
text: The best test for distinguishing two hypotheses is based on the ratio of how likely the data is under each hypothesis.
depends_on: Probability models, Type I/II error framework.

name: Large Deviations Principle (Cramér's Theorem)
text: The probability of a large fluctuation from the average decreases exponentially fast with the number of observations.
depends_on: Moment generating functions, convex analysis.

name: Concentration Inequalities
text: These inequalities bound the probability that a random variable or average deviates far from its expected value — essential for understanding when randomness is "well-behaved."
depends_on: Expectation, moment generating functions.

name: Fisher Information
text: Fisher information measures how much information a random observation carries about an unknown parameter.
depends_on: - Probability models, regularity conditions (differentiability of the likelihood)

name: Rao-Blackwell Theorem
text: If you have a sufficient statistic (a summary that captures all the information about the parameter), you can improve any estimator by conditioning it on the sufficient statistic.
depends_on: - Sufficiency (Principle 6), conditional expectation

name: Exponential Families
text: Exponential families are a unified class of probability distributions with especially clean mathematical properties.
depends_on: - Kolmogorov axioms, sufficiency

name: The Bootstrap Principle
text: Free probability theory is a version of probability that works for noncommutative objects like random matrices.
depends_on: - Probability theory, law of large numbers (Principle 4)

name: The Cosmological Principle
text: No place in the universe is "special." On large scales, it looks the same from every point and in every direction.
depends_on: Observational evidence (CMB, galaxy surveys).

name: The Friedmann Equations
text: These equations describe how the universe expands (or contracts).
depends_on: Einstein's field equations + cosmological principle (FLRW metric).

name: Hubble's Law (Hubble-Lemaître Law)
text: Distant galaxies are moving away from us, and the farther away they are, the faster they recede.
depends_on: Friedmann equations (H = ȧ/a).

name: Stellar Structure — Hydrostatic Equilibrium
text: A star is a balance between gravity (pulling inward) and pressure (pushing outward).
depends_on: Newtonian gravity, thermodynamics, nuclear physics.

name: Nuclear Fusion as the Energy Source of Stars
text: Stars shine by fusing light elements into heavier ones.
depends_on: Nuclear physics, E = mc², quantum tunneling (enables fusion at stellar temperatures far below the Coulomb barrier).

name: The Big Bang and Cosmic Evolution
text: The universe started as an incredibly hot, dense point and has been expanding ever since.
depends_on: Friedmann equations, thermodynamics, nuclear physics, particle physics.

name: Black Holes
text: When enough mass is compressed into a small enough volume, it collapses into a black hole — an object from which nothing can escape.
depends_on: General relativity, singularity theorems.

name: Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB)
text: The CMB is the "afterglow" of the Big Bang — ancient light that has been traveling through space for 13.8 billion years.
depends_on: Big Bang model, thermodynamics, atomic physics (recombination).

name: Olbers' Paradox and Its Resolution
text: Why is the night sky dark?
depends_on: Big Bang model, Hubble expansion, finite speed of light.

name: Dark Matter
text: Most of the matter in the universe is invisible — it doesn't emit or absorb light.
depends_on: Newtonian gravity / GR, observational astronomy, particle physics.

name: Dark Energy and the Cosmological Constant
text: The universe's expansion is speeding up, not slowing down.
depends_on: Friedmann equations, Type Ia supernova observations, CMB + BAO data.

name: Nucleosynthesis (Big Bang and Stellar)
text: The lightest elements (hydrogen, helium, lithium) were forged in the first minutes after the Big Bang.
depends_on: Nuclear physics, thermodynamics, Big Bang model, stellar structure.

name: Jeans Instability
text: A gas cloud in space will collapse under its own gravity if it is large and massive enough that gravity overwhelms the pressure trying to hold it up.
depends_on: - Newtonian gravity

name: Chandrasekhar Limit
text: There is a maximum mass that a white dwarf can have, about 1.4 times the mass of the Sun.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (Pauli exclusion principle, Fermi-Dirac statistics)

name: Eddington Luminosity
text: There is a maximum brightness a star can have — the Eddington luminosity.
depends_on: - Hydrostatic equilibrium (balance of gravity and radiation pressure)

name: Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff (TOV) Limit
text: Just as white dwarfs have a maximum mass (Chandrasekhar limit), neutron stars have their own maximum mass — the TOV limit.
depends_on: - General relativity (TOV equation = relativistic hydrostatic equilibrium)

name: Cosmic Inflation
text: In the first tiny fraction of a second after the Big Bang, the universe expanded exponentially fast — a region smaller than a proton was inflated to cosmic scales.
depends_on: - General relativity (Friedmann equations)

name: The Virial Theorem in Astrophysics
text: For a star or galaxy cluster in gravitational equilibrium, there is a precise relationship between its kinetic (thermal) energy and its gravitational energy.
depends_on: - Newtonian gravity

name: The Saha Ionization Equation
text: The Saha equation tells you what fraction of atoms are ionized at a given temperature and density.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics (Boltzmann and Saha distributions)

name: Stellar Structure Equations (Complete Set)
text: Four equations completely determine the structure of a star: how mass is distributed, how pressure balances gravity, how energy flows outward (by radiation or convection), and how nuclear reactions generate energy.
depends_on: - Newtonian gravity (or TOV equation for compact objects)

name: Bondi Accretion (Spherical Accretion)
text: When a massive object (like a black hole or star) sits in a sea of gas, it gravitationally attracts the surrounding gas and accretes it.
depends_on: - Fluid dynamics (Euler equations, steady-state flow)

name: Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) — Frozen-In Flux
text: In a hot, electrically conducting gas (plasma), the magnetic field is trapped in the fluid and moves with it — the field lines are "frozen in." This coupling between the magnetic field and the fluid motion creates a rich set of phenomena: magnetic pressure, magnetic tension, and waves that travel along field lines.
depends_on: - Fluid dynamics (Navier-Stokes / Euler equations)

name: The Epoch of Reionization
text: After the Big Bang cooled enough for atoms to form, the universe went dark for hundreds of millions of years.
depends_on: - Cosmology (structure formation, first objects)

name: The Tully-Fisher Relation and Distance Indicators
text: Brighter spiral galaxies rotate faster.
depends_on: - Virial theorem (Principle 13)

name: Baryon Acoustic Oscillations (BAO)
text: Sound waves traveled through the early universe (when it was a hot plasma of photons and baryons) just like sound waves through air.
depends_on: - Friedmann equations (Law 1), cosmological principle (Postulate 1)

name: Gravitational Wave Standard Sirens
text: Gravitational waves from merging compact objects (neutron stars, black holes) encode their distance directly in the signal strength -- the waveform tells you both the intrinsic "brightness" and the observed amplitude, giving the distance without needing any distance ladder.
depends_on: - Gravitational waves (Relativity: Principle 5)

name: Kilonova Physics and r-Process Nucleosynthesis
text: When two neutron stars collide, they fling out extremely neutron-rich material at a significant fraction of the speed of light.
depends_on: - Nuclear physics (r-process, nuclear binding energies, beta-decay rates)

name: 21-cm Cosmology
text: Neutral hydrogen atoms emit or absorb radio waves at a specific wavelength (21 cm) when the electron's spin flips.
depends_on: - Atomic physics (hydrogen hyperfine transition)

name: Multi-Messenger Astronomy
text: Just as human senses work better together (seeing and hearing an event gives more information than either alone), astronomy becomes far more powerful when combining different types of signals from the same cosmic event.
depends_on: - Gravitational wave detection (Principles 5, 13, 21)

name: The Hubble Tension and Precision Cosmology
text: Two fundamentally different ways of measuring the expansion rate of the universe give incompatible answers.
depends_on: - Friedmann equations (FLRW cosmology)

name: Fast Radio Bursts and Cosmological Probes
text: Multi-messenger astronomy is the practice of observing the universe using fundamentally different "messengers" — gravitational waves, light, neutrinos, and cosmic rays — simultaneously.
depends_on: - General relativity, gravitational wave theory (Principle C5)

name: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia)
text: Objects don't change their motion unless something pushes or pulls them.
depends_on: The concept of an inertial reference frame.

name: Newton's Second Law (F = ma)
text: Force equals mass times acceleration.
depends_on: Concepts of force, mass, and acceleration; calculus.

name: Newton's Third Law (Action-Reaction)
text: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction.
depends_on: Newton's Second Law.

name: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
text: Every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
depends_on: Newton's Second Law, the concept of action at a distance.

name: Conservation of Energy
text: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed from one form to another.
depends_on: Newton's laws, the concept of potential energy.

name: Conservation of Momentum
text: In an isolated system, the total momentum before an event equals the total momentum after.
depends_on: Newton's Second and Third Laws.

name: Conservation of Angular Momentum
text: Spinning objects keep spinning at the same rate unless twisted by an external torque.
depends_on: Newton's laws, definition of torque (τ = r × F).

name: The Principle of Least Action (Lagrangian Mechanics)
text: Nature chooses the path that makes the action stationary.
depends_on: Calculus of variations, the concept of generalized coordinates.

name: Hamiltonian Mechanics and Phase Space
text: Rewrite mechanics in terms of position and momentum (not velocity).
depends_on: Lagrangian mechanics, Legendre transform.

name: Noether's Theorem
text: Symmetry implies conservation.
depends_on: Lagrangian mechanics, continuous symmetry groups.

name: Routhian Mechanics
text: Routhian mechanics is a hybrid between Lagrangian and Hamiltonian approaches.
depends_on: - Lagrangian mechanics (Principle 4)

name: Symplectic Integrators
text: When simulating Hamiltonian systems numerically, standard methods (like Runge-Kutta) violate the geometric structure of the phase space, causing energy to drift over long simulations.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics (Principle 5)

name: D'Alembert's Principle
text: D'Alembert's principle transforms a dynamics problem into a statics problem by treating inertial forces as negative applied forces.
depends_on: Newton's second law, principle of virtual work.

name: Liouville's Theorem
text: If you follow a swarm of phase space points evolving under Hamilton's equations, the volume they occupy never changes — the "fluid" of phase space is incompressible.
depends_on: Hamilton's equations, symplectic structure of phase space.

name: Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion
text: Planetary orbits are ellipses, planets move faster when closer to the Sun, and more distant planets take longer to orbit.
depends_on: Newton's law of gravitation, Newton's second law.

name: The Virial Theorem
text: For bound gravitational systems, the average kinetic energy is half the magnitude of the average potential energy.
depends_on: Newton's laws, time averaging.

name: The KAM Theorem (Kolmogorov-Arnold-Moser)
text: For nearly integrable systems, most of the orderly behavior survives small perturbations.
depends_on: Hamiltonian mechanics, perturbation theory, number theory (Diophantine conditions).

name: Maupertuis' Principle (Principle of Least Action — Original Form)
text: Maupertuis' principle is the earliest variational principle in physics: nature chooses the path that minimizes (or makes stationary) the "effort" of motion at fixed energy.
depends_on: - Conservation of energy

name: Bertrand's Theorem
text: If you want a particle orbiting under a central force to always return to its starting point (a closed orbit), there are exactly two force laws that guarantee this: the inverse-square law (gravity, electrostatics) and the harmonic oscillator (springs).
depends_on: - Newton's laws of motion

name: Hamilton-Jacobi Theory
text: Hamilton-Jacobi theory reformulates mechanics as a problem of finding wavefronts in configuration space.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics

name: Poincaré Recurrence Theorem
text: In a bounded system governed by Hamilton's equations, the system will eventually return to a state arbitrarily close to where it started.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics

name: Adiabatic Invariants
text: When you slowly change the conditions of a periodic system, a special combination of energy and frequency stays constant.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics (action-angle variables)

name: Arnold Diffusion
text: The KAM theorem guarantees that most regular orbits survive small perturbations, but in systems with three or more degrees of freedom, the remaining chaotic regions are connected.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics (N ≥ 3 degrees of freedom)

name: Canonical Perturbation Theory
text: Canonical perturbation theory provides a systematic way to approximate the behavior of a nearly-integrable Hamiltonian system.
depends_on: - Hamilton-Jacobi theory

name: Noether's Second Theorem
text: While Noether's first theorem connects global symmetries to conservation laws, her second theorem addresses local (gauge) symmetries — those parameterized by arbitrary functions.
depends_on: - Lagrangian mechanics (calculus of variations)

name: Nambu Mechanics (Generalized Hamiltonian Systems)
text: Standard Hamiltonian mechanics uses one energy function and the Poisson bracket (a two-input operation) to generate time evolution.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics (Principle 5)

name: Contact Geometry in Mechanics (Dissipative Systems)
text: Symplectic geometry describes energy-conserving systems perfectly, but real mechanical systems lose energy to friction, damping, and dissipation.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics, symplectic geometry

name: Port-Hamiltonian Systems
text: Port-Hamiltonian systems provide a unified framework for modeling physical systems as energy-storing, energy-dissipating, and energy-exchanging components connected through "ports." Any complex system -- mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, thermal -- can be modeled by connecting port-Hamiltonian subsystems.
depends_on: - Hamiltonian mechanics (Principle 5)

name: The Kolmogorov-Arnold Representation Theorem
text: The Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem says that any continuous function of several variables, no matter how complicated, can be written as sums and compositions of continuous functions of just one variable.
depends_on: - Continuous function theory, real analysis

name: Geometric Mechanics and Reduction Theory
text: Geometric mechanics reveals that the symmetries of a mechanical system determine its mathematical structure.
depends_on: - Lagrangian and Hamiltonian mechanics (Principles 4, 5)

name: Non-Smooth Mechanics and Contact Dynamics
text: The Casimir effect demonstrates that empty space is not truly empty — quantum mechanics requires that even a perfect vacuum contains fluctuating electromagnetic fields.
depends_on: - Quantum electrodynamics, vacuum energy

name: The Band Theory of Solids
text: Electrons in a crystal don't have arbitrary energies — they live in "bands" of allowed energies.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (Schrödinger equation in periodic potential), Pauli exclusion principle.

name: Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking and Order Parameters
text: When a system cools below a critical temperature, it can spontaneously "choose" a preferred direction or configuration, breaking the symmetry of the underlying laws.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics (free energy minimization), group theory (symmetry classification).

name: Phase Transitions and Universality
text: Near a phase transition, all systems with the same basic symmetry behave the same way, regardless of their microscopic details.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, renormalization group theory.

name: Superconductivity (BCS Theory)
text: At very low temperatures, electrons pair up and move through a material without any resistance.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (many-body state, Cooper pairing), phonon physics, spontaneous symmetry breaking.

name: Topological Phases of Matter
text: Some properties of materials are determined by the "shape" of their quantum states in an abstract mathematical sense.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, topology, band theory.

name: The Fermi Liquid Theory
text: Even though electrons in metals interact strongly with each other, they often behave as if they are nearly free particles with modified properties.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, Pauli exclusion principle, statistical mechanics.

name: Bose-Einstein Condensation
text: At extremely low temperatures, a collection of bosonic particles all "collapses" into the same quantum state, forming a single giant quantum object visible to the naked eye.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (Bose-Einstein statistics), statistical mechanics.

name: Ginzburg-Landau Theory of Phase Transitions
text: Near a phase transition, you can describe the system using a single "order parameter" (like magnetization in a magnet) and write down a simple energy expression.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics (free energy minimization), symmetry considerations, calculus of variations.

name: Anderson Localization
text: Disorder can trap electrons — not by barriers, but by quantum interference.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (wave interference), random potentials.

name: The Renormalization Group (RG) in Condensed Matter
text: To understand large-scale behavior, you can systematically "zoom out" — averaging over small-scale details.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, scale invariance, field theory.

name: The Hubbard Model and Mott Insulators
text: When electrons repel each other strongly enough, they refuse to move even though band theory says they should be metallic.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, second quantization, band theory.

name: The Kondo Effect
text: A single magnetic atom in a metal creates a cloud of electrons around it that screens its magnetism at low temperatures, producing unusual resistance behavior.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, many-body theory, renormalization group.

name: Mermin-Wagner Theorem
text: In one and two dimensions, thermal fluctuations are strong enough to destroy any ordered state that breaks a continuous symmetry.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics

name: The Kosterlitz-Thouless Transition
text: The KT transition is a phase transition with no conventional order parameter.
depends_on: - 2D XY model (or equivalent: superfluids, superconducting films)

name: Berry Phase (Geometric Phase)
text: When you slowly change the parameters of a quantum system and bring them back to where they started, the quantum state remembers the path — it picks up a phase that depends only on the geometry of the path, not on how fast you went.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (adiabatic theorem)

name: Laughlin's Argument (Fractional Quantum Hall Effect)
text: In a very strong magnetic field at very low temperatures, electrons in two dimensions form an exotic quantum fluid with remarkable properties: the Hall conductance is quantized at rational fractions of e²/h, and the excitations carry fractional electric charge and exhibit statistics that are neither bosonic nor fermionic.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (2D electron gas in magnetic field)

name: Spin-Orbit Coupling
text: An electron's spin and its motion are coupled: the spin "feels" its motion through the electric field it moves in.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (spin)

name: Density Functional Theory (DFT) and the Kohn-Sham Equations
text: DFT reduces the impossibly complex many-electron problem (solving a wavefunction in 3N dimensions) to solving for the electron density (a function of just 3 variables).
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (many-electron Schrodinger equation)

name: Topological Insulators and the Z₂ Invariant
text: A topological insulator is insulating in its interior but conducts electricity on its surface through special states that are immune to impurities and defects.
depends_on: - Band theory (Bloch's theorem)

name: Green's Function Methods in Many-Body Physics
text: The Green's function describes how a particle added to a many-body system propagates through it — absorbing the effects of all the interactions along the way.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (second quantization, time ordering)

name: The Quantum Spin Hall Effect
text: The quantum spin Hall effect creates a pair of conducting channels at the edges of a 2D material: electrons with spin-up flow one way, electrons with spin-down flow the other way.
depends_on: - Band theory with spin-orbit coupling

name: Bosonization and the Tomonaga-Luttinger Liquid
text: In one dimension, the familiar picture of electrons behaving as independent quasiparticles (Fermi liquid) completely fails.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (second quantization, 1D systems)

name: Weyl Semimetals and Topological Semimetal Phases
text: Weyl semimetals are materials where electrons behave as if they are massless Weyl fermions -- particles first predicted in 1929 but never observed as fundamental particles.
depends_on: - Band theory / Bloch theorem (Principle 1)

name: Non-Hermitian Physics and Exceptional Points
text: Standard quantum mechanics requires Hamiltonians to be Hermitian (self-adjoint), guaranteeing real energies and unitary time evolution.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (postulates, Hilbert space)

name: Time Crystals (Discrete and Floquet)
text: Crystals break spatial translation symmetry: atoms arrange in a repeating pattern in space.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics, many-body physics

name: Moire Physics and Flat Bands in Twisted Bilayer Graphene
text: When two sheets of graphene are stacked with a tiny twist (~1.1 degrees), something remarkable happens: the electrons slow down dramatically, forming "flat bands" where kinetic energy nearly vanishes.
depends_on: - Band theory (Principle 1)

name: Topological Superconductivity and Majorana Fermions
text: Topological superconductors host Majorana fermions at their edges -- exotic quasiparticles that are their own antiparticles.
depends_on: - Superconductivity (Principle 4)

name: Moire Physics and Flat Band Systems
text: When two sheets of graphene are twisted by about 1.1 degrees relative to each other, the electrons slow down dramatically -- their effective mass diverges and their energy bands become nearly flat.
depends_on: - Bloch's theorem and band theory (Principle 1)

name: Non-Hermitian Topology in Condensed Matter
text: Non-Hermitian topology extends the classification of topological phases to systems that are not energy-conserving -- where particles can be created, destroyed, or have finite lifetimes.
depends_on: - Topological band theory (Berry phase, Chern numbers)

name: Quantum Spin Liquids
text: Non-Hermitian physics describes systems that exchange energy with their environment — systems with amplification, absorption, or one-way coupling.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics, eigenvalue problems

name: Coulomb's Law
text: Electric currents and changing electric fields create magnetic fields.
depends_on: Concepts of electric and magnetic fields, charge, current.

name: The Lorentz Force Law
text: The force on a charged particle is due to both the electric field (pushes along E) and the magnetic field (pushes perpendicular to velocity and B).
depends_on: Maxwell's equations (define E and B), Newton's second law.

name: Conservation of Charge
text: Electric charge is neither created nor destroyed.
depends_on: Maxwell's equations (follows mathematically from taking the divergence of the Ampère-Maxwell law and using Gauss's law).

name: Electromagnetic Waves
text: Oscillating electric and magnetic fields propagate through space as waves at the speed of light.
depends_on: Maxwell's equations.

name: Gauge Invariance
text: The potentials that generate the electromagnetic fields are not unique — different potentials can describe the same physics.
depends_on: The potential formulation of Maxwell's equations.

name: Poynting's Theorem (Energy Conservation in EM Fields)
text: Electromagnetic energy is conserved: the rate of change of energy in a region plus the energy flowing out equals the work done on charges.
depends_on: Maxwell's equations.

name: Electromagnetic Boundary Conditions
text: When electromagnetic fields cross a boundary between different materials, certain components must be continuous and others can jump.
depends_on: Maxwell's equations in integral form.

name: Lenz's Law
text: Nature resists changes in magnetic flux — induced currents always flow in the direction that tries to maintain the original flux.
depends_on: Faraday's law, conservation of energy.

name: Biot-Savart Law
text: A small piece of current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field that circulates around it, inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
depends_on: The concept of current, magnetic field.

name: Larmor Formula (Electromagnetic Radiation from Accelerating Charges)
text: Accelerating electric charges radiate electromagnetic waves.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations

name: The Skin Effect
text: Alternating current does not penetrate deeply into a conductor.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (specifically Faraday's law and Ampere-Maxwell law in conducting media)

name: Maxwell Stress Tensor (Electromagnetic Momentum and Forces)
text: Electromagnetic fields carry momentum and can exert forces on objects.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations

name: Electromagnetic Induction — Faraday's Law (Integral Form)
text: A changing magnetic flux through a circuit generates a voltage (EMF) that drives current.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (Faraday's law in differential form)

name: Jefimenko's Equations (Time-Dependent Generalizations of Coulomb and Biot-Savart)
text: Jefimenko's equations give the exact electric and magnetic fields produced by any moving charge and current distribution, properly accounting for the finite speed of light.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations

name: Gauge Fixing — Lorenz and Coulomb Gauges
text: Because many different potential functions can produce the same physical electric and magnetic fields, we need to pick a specific convention (gauge) to make the potentials unique.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (potential formulation)

name: Electromagnetic Duality
text: In vacuum, Maxwell's equations have a hidden symmetry: you can rotate electric and magnetic fields into each other, and the physics does not change.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (source-free form)

name: Debye Shielding
text: In a plasma or salt solution, a charged particle attracts opposite charges and repels like charges, forming a cloud that screens its electric field.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (Gauss's law / Poisson equation)

name: Waveguide Theory (Guided Electromagnetic Waves)
text: Waveguides channel electromagnetic waves along a defined path, just as pipes carry water.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (boundary value problems)

name: Antenna Theory (Radiation from Current Distributions)
text: Antenna theory describes how electric currents radiate electromagnetic waves into space and how antennas receive them.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations, retarded potentials

name: London Equations (Superconducting Electrodynamics)
text: Superconductors have zero electrical resistance and expel magnetic fields from their interior (the Meissner effect).
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (Law System 1)

name: The Evanescent Wave and Near-Field Electrodynamics
text: When light bounces off an internal surface at a steep angle (total internal reflection), it does not simply stop at the boundary.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations, boundary conditions (Principle 6)

name: Plasma Oscillations and Electromagnetic Wave Dispersion in Plasmas
text: Plasmas are collections of free electrons and ions that respond collectively to electromagnetic disturbances.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (Law System 1)

name: Electromagnetic Radiation Pressure and Momentum Transfer
text: Light pushes on things.
depends_on: - Maxwell stress tensor (Principle 9)

name: Transformation Optics and Electromagnetic Cloaking
text: Transformation optics uses the mathematical equivalence between curved space and material properties to design devices that control light in extraordinary ways.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (Law System 1)

name: Casimir Effect (Vacuum Electromagnetic Fluctuations)
text: Even in a perfect vacuum, quantum mechanics predicts that electromagnetic fields fluctuate randomly.
depends_on: - Quantum electrodynamics, vacuum fluctuations

name: Topological Electromagnetism and Photonic Topological Insulators
text: Topological photonics applies ideas from topological insulators to light.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (fundamental EM laws)

name: Non-Hermitian Physics in Electromagnetism
text: Altermagnetism is a newly discovered type of magnetism that breaks the century-old classification of magnets into just ferromagnets and antiferromagnets.
depends_on: - Electromagnetism, magnetic ordering

name: The Continuum Hypothesis
text: We treat fluids as smooth, continuous substances rather than collections of individual molecules.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics (justification that molecular averages yield smooth macroscopic fields).

name: Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)
text: Mass is neither created nor destroyed.
depends_on: Continuum hypothesis, conservation of mass.

name: The Navier-Stokes Equations (Conservation of Momentum)
text: Newton's second law for fluids: the acceleration of a fluid element equals the sum of pressure forces, viscous forces, and body forces (like gravity).
depends_on: Newton's second law, continuum hypothesis, constitutive relation (Newtonian fluid: stress ∝ strain rate).

name: The Reynolds Number
text: The Reynolds number measures the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces.
depends_on: Navier-Stokes equations, dimensional analysis.

name: Bernoulli's Principle
text: Where fluid flows faster, the pressure is lower.
depends_on: Conservation of energy, Euler's equations (inviscid limit of Navier-Stokes).

name: Turbulence
text: Turbulence is the chaotic, swirling motion seen in fast-flowing rivers, smoke plumes, and atmospheric storms.
depends_on: Navier-Stokes equations, high Reynolds number.

name: Vorticity and Circulation
text: Vorticity measures how much the fluid spins at each point.
depends_on: Euler's equations, vector calculus.

name: Euler Equations for Ideal Fluids
text: For fluids with negligible viscosity, the only forces are pressure and gravity.
depends_on: Newton's second law, continuum hypothesis, inviscid approximation.

name: Stokes' Law (Low Reynolds Number Drag)
text: A small, slow-moving sphere in a thick fluid feels a drag force proportional to its speed, its size, and the fluid's viscosity.
depends_on: Navier-Stokes equations in the Stokes (creeping flow) limit (Re << 1).

name: Boundary Layer Theory (Prandtl)
text: When a fast-moving fluid hits a surface, the "stickiness" (viscosity) only matters in a very thin layer near the surface.
depends_on: Navier-Stokes equations, Reynolds number.

name: Potential Flow and Irrotational Motion
text: When a fluid has no rotation (no swirling), its flow can be described by a single potential function satisfying a well-studied equation — making many problems solvable in closed form.
depends_on: Euler equations, vector calculus, Kelvin's circulation theorem.

name: Dimensional Analysis and Similarity in Fluids
text: Two fluid flows that "look the same" in dimensionless terms behave identically — this is why wind tunnel models work.
depends_on: Buckingham π theorem, Navier-Stokes equations.

name: Helmholtz Vortex Theorems
text: In an ideal fluid, vortices are remarkably persistent structures.
depends_on: - Euler equations (inviscid flow)

name: Rayleigh-Taylor Instability
text: When heavy fluid sits on top of light fluid, the configuration is unstable — like trying to balance a book on top of a ball.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes/Euler equations

name: Compressible Flow and Shock Waves
text: When an object moves faster than sound, it creates a shock wave — an abrupt, discontinuous change in pressure, density, and temperature.
depends_on: - Euler equations (compressible form)

name: The No-Slip Condition
text: Fluid directly touching a solid surface does not slip along it — it sticks.
depends_on: - Molecular interactions between fluid and solid

name: The Kutta-Joukowski Theorem (Aerodynamic Lift)
text: The lift on a wing is directly proportional to the circulation (net swirling flow) around it.
depends_on: - Potential flow theory

name: The Vorticity Equation
text: The vorticity equation describes how swirling motion (vorticity) is created, stretched, tilted, and diffused in a fluid.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (curl operation)

name: Stokes Flow (Creeping Motion)
text: At very low Reynolds numbers — the world of bacteria, sperm cells, and settling dust particles — viscosity completely dominates over inertia.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (Re << 1 limit)

name: Taylor-Couette Instability
text: When the inner cylinder spins fast enough, the smooth circular flow between two cylinders breaks up into a beautiful stack of donut-shaped vortex rolls.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (cylindrical geometry)

name: The Dean Number and Secondary Flows in Curved Channels
text: When fluid flows through a curved pipe (like a garden hose coiled in a loop), the faster-moving fluid near the center is flung outward by centrifugal force, while slower fluid near the walls is pushed inward.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (curved geometry)

name: Shallow Water Equations
text: When a layer of water is much wider than it is deep (oceans, rivers, dam breaks), vertical motion can be neglected and the flow is described by the shallow water equations.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (depth-integrated, hydrostatic limit)

name: Microfluidics and Low-Reynolds-Number Transport
