text: At the microscale, fluids behave very differently from everyday experience.
depends_on: - Stokes flow / creeping motion (Principle 13)

name: Magnetohydrodynamic Dynamo Theory
text: The dynamo problem explains how planets, stars, and galaxies generate their magnetic fields.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (Law 2), Maxwell's equations

name: Viscoelastic Fluid Dynamics (Oldroyd-B and Beyond)
text: Ordinary (Newtonian) fluids like water resist deformation through viscosity alone.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (Law 2)

name: Lattice Boltzmann Method
text: Instead of solving the Navier-Stokes equations directly, the lattice Boltzmann method simulates fluid flow by tracking imaginary particles hopping on a grid.
depends_on: - Boltzmann equation (kinetic theory)

name: Active Fluids and Biological Fluid Mechanics
text: Active fluids are liquids full of swimming organisms or self-propelled particles.
depends_on: - Stokes flow (Principle 13)

name: Active Matter Hydrodynamics
text: Active matter physics describes collections of self-propelled entities -- swimming bacteria, crawling cells, flocking birds, vibrated grains -- that consume energy internally rather than being driven by external forces.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (Principles 1-3)

name: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Fluid Mechanics
text: Elastic turbulence is a remarkable phenomenon where adding tiny amounts of polymer to a fluid causes chaotic, turbulent-like flow even when the fluid is moving extremely slowly — conditions under which ordinary fluids flow perfectly smoothly.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations, low-Reynolds-number flow (Laws L1-L2, Principle P5)

name: Fermat's Principle (Principle of Least Time)
text: Light always takes the path that minimizes travel time.
depends_on: The concept of refractive index, calculus of variations.

name: Snell's Law of Refraction
text: When light passes from one medium to another, it bends.
depends_on: Fermat's principle (or Maxwell's equations + boundary conditions).

name: The Wave Nature of Light (Huygens' Principle)
text: Light propagates as a wave.
depends_on: The wave equation (from Maxwell's equations).

name: Superposition and Interference
text: Waves add together.
depends_on: Linearity of Maxwell's equations.

name: Photons and Quantum Optics
text: Light comes in individual particles (photons) whose energy is proportional to their frequency.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics.

name: Planck's Radiation Law
text: A hot object radiates light at all frequencies, with a characteristic spectrum that depends only on temperature.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (energy quantization), statistical mechanics.

name: Diffraction (Fraunhofer and Fresnel)
text: Light bends around obstacles and spreads through narrow openings.
depends_on: Huygens-Fresnel principle, wave equation, Fourier analysis.

name: Polarization of Light
text: Light waves vibrate in specific directions perpendicular to their travel.
depends_on: Maxwell's equations (transverse nature of EM waves).

name: Brewster's Angle
text: At a special angle of incidence, the reflected light becomes perfectly polarized — only the component vibrating parallel to the surface bounces back.
depends_on: Maxwell's equations (Fresnel equations), Snell's law.

name: Coherence
text: For interference to be visible, the light waves must be "in step" with each other.
depends_on: Wave theory of light, Fourier analysis, statistical optics.

name: Abbe Diffraction Limit
text: There is a fundamental limit to how small an object a microscope (or any optical system) can resolve, set by the wavelength of light.
depends_on: - Wave nature of light (diffraction)

name: Kramers-Kronig Relations
text: If you know how a material absorbs light at all frequencies, you can calculate how it bends light at any frequency, and vice versa.
depends_on: - Causality (response cannot precede cause)

name: The Optical Theorem
text: The total amount of scattering (in all directions) from an object is completely determined by how much the forward-scattered wave interferes with the incident wave.
depends_on: - Wave scattering theory

name: Fresnel Equations
text: When light hits a surface between two materials, some reflects and some transmits.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (electromagnetic boundary conditions)

name: Nonlinear Optics
text: At very high light intensities, materials respond nonlinearly to light.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (wave propagation in nonlinear media)

name: Gaussian Beam Optics and the ABCD Matrix Formalism
text: Laser beams are not perfectly parallel — they spread due to diffraction.
depends_on: - Wave equation (paraxial approximation)

name: Photonic Crystals and Photonic Bandgaps
text: Just as the periodic arrangement of atoms in a crystal creates energy bands and bandgaps for electrons, a periodic arrangement of different dielectric materials creates frequency bands and bandgaps for light.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations in periodic media

name: Squeezed States and Quantum Noise Limits
text: Even a perfect laser beam has quantum noise — irreducible fluctuations imposed by the uncertainty principle.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (Heisenberg uncertainty principle for field quadratures)

name: Metamaterials and Negative Refraction
text: Metamaterials are engineered structures that bend light in ways no natural material can.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (effective medium theory)

name: Fourier Optics
text: A lens naturally performs Fourier transforms on light.
depends_on: - Wave equation (paraxial approximation)

name: Adaptive Optics
text: Earth's atmosphere makes stars twinkle and blurs astronomical images.
depends_on: - Wave optics (wavefront propagation through turbulence)

name: Orbital Angular Momentum of Light
text: Light can "twist" as it travels through space, carrying a corkscrew-like orbital angular momentum separate from its spin (polarization).
depends_on: - Wave optics, Huygens' principle (Principle 2)

name: Ghost Imaging and Quantum Imaging
text: Ghost imaging creates a picture of an object using light that never directly interacts with the imaging detector in the usual way.
depends_on: - Quantum optics (Principle 4), entangled photon pairs

name: Attosecond Optics and High-Harmonic Generation
text: Attosecond optics creates the shortest controlled bursts of light ever produced -- lasting billionths of a billionth of a second.
depends_on: - Nonlinear optics (Principle 10)

name: Structured Light (Spatial Light Modulation and Vector Beams)
text: Instead of using light as a simple, uniform beam, structured light sculpts every property of a light beam -- its shape, its twist, its polarization pattern -- point by point across the beam.
depends_on: - Fourier optics (Principle 15)

name: Optical Frequency Combs
text: An optical frequency comb is like a ruler for light, with millions of perfectly spaced "teeth" spanning the visible and infrared spectrum.
depends_on: - Laser physics, mode-locked lasers

name: Quantum Key Distribution (BB84 Protocol)
text: Quantum key distribution uses the quantum properties of photons to create an unbreakable encryption key.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (no-cloning theorem, measurement disturbance)

name: Topological Photonics
text: Topological photonics engineers light-guiding structures where the flow of light is protected by topology -- the global mathematical structure of the photonic band structure.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations and wave optics

name: Orbital Angular Momentum of Light and Structured Light
text: Photonic topological insulators are engineered materials that force light to travel in one direction along their edges without any possibility of being scattered backward, even around sharp corners or past defects.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations, photonic band theory (Electromagnetism: Principle P14)

name: Quantum Field Theory (QFT) Framework
text: The universe is made of fields, not particles.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (all postulates), special relativity, Lagrangian mechanics.

name: Gauge Symmetry — The Organizing Principle
text: The forces of nature arise from symmetry requirements.
depends_on: Lie group theory, Noether's theorem, Lagrangian field theory.

name: The Higgs Mechanism (Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking)
text: A special field (the Higgs field) fills all of space.
depends_on: Gauge symmetry (SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y), spontaneous symmetry breaking, Goldstone's theorem.

name: The Standard Model Particle Content
text: There are 17 fundamental particles: 12 matter particles (quarks and leptons), 4 force carriers (plus 8 gluon color states), and the Higgs.
depends_on: QFT, gauge symmetry, Higgs mechanism.

name: Renormalization and Running Coupling Constants
text: The strength of forces changes with energy.
depends_on: QFT, gauge symmetry.

name: The Quark Model and Color Charge
text: Protons and neutrons are not fundamental — they are made of quarks held together by gluons.
depends_on: SU(3)_C gauge symmetry, QFT.

name: CP Violation
text: The laws of physics are not quite the same for matter and antimatter.
depends_on: Weak interaction, CKM matrix, quark mixing.

name: Lepton Universality
text: The electron, muon, and tau interact with the weak and electromagnetic forces in exactly the same way — the only thing different about them is their mass.
depends_on: Gauge symmetry (SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y), Standard Model structure.

name: The CPT Theorem
text: If you replace every particle with its antiparticle (C), mirror-reflect everything (P), and reverse time (T), the physics is exactly the same.
depends_on: Lorentz invariance, locality, unitarity.

name: Asymptotic Freedom
text: Quarks interact more weakly at high energies (short distances) — they become "free" inside the proton at high energies.
depends_on: Non-abelian gauge theory, renormalization group.

name: Confinement
text: You can never see a free quark.
depends_on: QCD (SU(3)_C gauge theory), non-perturbative effects.

name: Neutrino Oscillations and Masses
text: Neutrinos change flavor as they travel.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (superposition), mass-squared differences.

name: Goldstone's Theorem
text: When a system's ground state spontaneously breaks a continuous symmetry, massless particles automatically appear — one for each broken symmetry direction.
depends_on: - Spontaneous symmetry breaking

name: Anomaly Cancellation
text: Quantum effects can destroy gauge symmetries that exist at the classical level.
depends_on: - Gauge symmetry

name: The CKM Matrix
text: When quarks change flavor via the weak force, they do not couple to pure mass eigenstates.
depends_on: - Electroweak theory (W boson couplings to quarks)

name: The Seesaw Mechanism
text: Neutrinos are incredibly light — billions of times lighter than the electron.
depends_on: - Neutrino mass and oscillation data

name: Effective Field Theory
text: Effective field theory is the principle that physics at low energies does not depend on the details of high-energy physics — you can describe nature at any scale using only the degrees of freedom relevant at that scale, with the effects of heavier particles encoded in a systematic expansion.
depends_on: - Quantum field theory (renormalization, symmetry)

name: Lattice QCD
text: Lattice QCD solves the strong force from first principles by putting quarks and gluons on a discrete grid and computing the path integral numerically using powerful supercomputers.
depends_on: - QCD Lagrangian (SU(3) gauge theory with quarks)

name: Baryon Asymmetry and the Sakharov Conditions
text: The universe is made almost entirely of matter, with essentially no antimatter — but the fundamental laws of physics treat matter and antimatter nearly symmetrically.
depends_on: - Particle physics (CP violation, baryon number)

name: Grand Unification
text: At very high energies, the three forces of the Standard Model (electromagnetic, weak, strong) may merge into a single unified force described by a larger symmetry group.
depends_on: - Standard Model gauge structure

name: The Anomalous Magnetic Moment
text: The way an electron or muon behaves in a magnetic field is affected by virtual particles constantly popping in and out of existence around it.
depends_on: - QFT / QED (Principle 1)

name: Deep Inelastic Scattering and Parton Distribution Functions
text: When you shoot a high-energy electron at a proton, the electron bounces off one of the quarks or gluons inside.
depends_on: - QCD / asymptotic freedom (Principle 9)

name: The Naturalness Problem (Hierarchy Problem)
text: The Higgs boson has a mass of 125 GeV, but quantum effects from high-energy virtual particles should push this mass up to the Planck scale (10^19 GeV) unless there is an incredible cancellation to 32 decimal places.
depends_on: - Higgs mechanism (Principle 3)

name: QCD Sum Rules and Form Factors
text: QCD sum rules provide a bridge between the fundamental theory of quarks and gluons (QCD) and the observed properties of hadrons (protons, mesons, etc.).
depends_on: - QCD / asymptotic freedom (Principle 9)

name: Dark Matter Candidates and Detection Principles
text: Dark matter is the invisible substance that holds galaxies together, but we do not know what it is made of.
depends_on: - Standard Model (Principles 1-3)

name: On-Shell Scattering Amplitudes and the Amplituhedron
text: Feynman diagrams, while powerful, become impossibly complicated for processes involving many particles.
depends_on: - Quantum field theory (Principle 1)

name: Non-Perturbative Dualities and the Swampland Program
text: Not every mathematically consistent quantum field theory can be coupled to gravity.
depends_on: - Quantum field theory, gauge symmetry

name: Amplitudes and the Positive Geometry Program
text: Effective field theory is the insight that you do not need to know everything about nature at the highest energies to make precise predictions at lower energies.
depends_on: - Quantum field theory, renormalization group (Principle P6)

name: State Space (Hilbert Space)
text: A quantum system's state is a vector in an abstract mathematical space.
depends_on: Linear algebra (vector spaces), functional analysis (Hilbert spaces).

name: Observables as Hermitian Operators
text: Physical measurements correspond to mathematical operators.
depends_on: Postulate 1, linear operator theory, spectral theorem.

name: The Born Rule (Measurement Probabilities)
text: Quantum mechanics is inherently probabilistic.
depends_on: Postulates 1 and 2, inner product structure of Hilbert space.

name: State Collapse (Measurement Postulate)
text: Measurement disturbs the system.
depends_on: Postulates 1-3.

name: Time Evolution (The Schrödinger Equation)
text: Between measurements, the quantum state evolves smoothly and deterministically according to the Schrödinger equation.
depends_on: Postulate 1, the Hamiltonian operator.

name: Composite Systems (Tensor Product)
text: To describe multiple quantum systems together, combine their state spaces using the tensor product.
depends_on: Postulate 1, tensor product of Hilbert spaces.

name: The Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
text: Certain pairs of physical properties (like position and momentum) cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
depends_on: Postulates 1-3, the commutator [x̂, p̂] = iℏ.

name: The Correspondence Principle
text: Quantum mechanics must agree with classical physics for large objects.
depends_on: All postulates, classical mechanics.

name: The Pauli Exclusion Principle
text: Two electrons in an atom cannot have the exact same set of quantum numbers.
depends_on: Spin-statistics theorem (from quantum field theory), Postulate 6.

name: Bell's Theorem and Nonlocality
text: Quantum mechanics produces correlations between distant particles that cannot be explained by any theory where particles carry pre-determined values and communicate only at the speed of light.
depends_on: Postulates 3 and 6, entanglement.

name: The No-Cloning Theorem
text: You cannot make a perfect copy of an unknown quantum state.
depends_on: Postulate 1 (linearity of Hilbert space), Postulate 5 (unitary evolution).

name: The Spin-Statistics Theorem
text: The way a particle behaves in groups (bunching together vs.
depends_on: Special relativity (Lorentz invariance), quantum field theory, causality (microcausality).

name: Decoherence
text: Quantum superpositions are fragile — interaction with the environment causes them to rapidly "leak out" into the environment, making the system appear classical.
depends_on: Postulates 5 and 6, density matrix formalism.

name: The Path Integral Formulation
text: A quantum particle takes all possible paths simultaneously.
depends_on: Postulate 5, classical action, Lagrangian mechanics.

name: Quantum Entanglement and the EPR Argument
text: Two entangled particles are correlated in a way that has no classical explanation — measuring one instantly affects the possible measurement outcomes of the other, even if they are light-years apart.
depends_on: Postulate 6 (tensor product), Born rule.

name: The Quantum Zeno Effect
text: A watched pot never boils — quantum mechanically.
depends_on: - Postulate 4 (state collapse upon measurement)

name: The Aharonov-Bohm Effect
text: A magnetic field locked inside a solenoid — completely inaccessible to electrons traveling outside — still affects those electrons through the vector potential.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (phase of wavefunction)

name: The Density Matrix Formalism
text: Not all quantum states can be described by a simple wavefunction.
depends_on: - Postulate 1 (Hilbert space)

name: The Ehrenfest Theorem
text: On average, quantum particles follow Newton's laws.
depends_on: - Postulates 2 and 5 (observables as operators, Schrödinger equation)

name: The WKB Approximation
text: When quantum effects are small (large objects, high energies), the wavefunction can be approximated using classical trajectories.
depends_on: - Schrödinger equation (Postulate 5)

name: The Wigner Function and Phase-Space Quantum Mechanics
text: The Wigner function is the closest quantum analogue of a classical phase-space distribution.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (wavefunctions, density matrix)

name: Quantum Error Correction
text: Quantum information is extraordinarily fragile — any interaction with the environment can corrupt it.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (superposition, entanglement, no-cloning theorem)

name: Berry Phase in Quantum Mechanics
text: When you slowly cycle the parameters of a quantum system back to their starting values, the quantum state picks up a phase that depends only on the geometry of the path — not on how fast you went or how long it took.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (adiabatic theorem, time evolution)

name: Quantum Teleportation
text: Quantum teleportation transfers a quantum state from one location to another without physically moving the particle carrying it, using pre-shared entanglement and classical communication.
depends_on: - Quantum entanglement (shared Bell pair)

name: The Lindblad Master Equation (Open Quantum Systems)
text: Real quantum systems are never perfectly isolated -- they interact with their environment, losing energy, gaining noise, and decohering.
depends_on: - Density matrix formalism (Principle 9)

name: Many-Body Perturbation Theory (Diagrammatic Methods)
text: When many quantum particles interact, exact solutions are impossible.
depends_on: - Second quantization, Wick's theorem

name: Quantum Channels and Completely Positive Maps
text: In the real world, quantum systems interact with their environment, causing noise, decoherence, and information loss.
depends_on: - Quantum state postulates (Postulates 1-4)

name: The Kochen-Specker Theorem (Quantum Contextuality)
text: The Kochen-Specker theorem proves that quantum measurement outcomes cannot be pre-determined values that are simply revealed by measurement.
depends_on: - Quantum state space (Postulate 1), observables (Postulate 2)

name: Quantum Resource Theories
text: Quantum resource theories provide a unified framework for understanding what makes quantum systems useful for information processing.
depends_on: - Quantum state space (Postulate 1)

name: Quantum Thermodynamics
text: Quantum thermodynamics asks: how do the laws of thermodynamics change when systems are genuinely quantum mechanical -- when they can be in superpositions, entangled, or measured?
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (Laws 1-4)

name: Non-Hermitian Quantum Mechanics
text: Standard quantum mechanics assumes energy is conserved (Hermitian Hamiltonian).
depends_on: - Schrodinger equation and Hilbert space formalism (Postulates 1-5)

name: Quantum Error Correction and the Threshold Theorem
text: Magnetic skyrmions are tiny whirlpools of magnetization — nanoscale structures where the magnetic moments twist into a knot that is topologically protected, meaning it cannot be smoothly unwound into a uniform state.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics, exchange interaction, spin-orbit coupling

name: The Principle of Relativity (Special Relativity)
text: No experiment performed inside a lab can determine whether the lab is at rest or moving at constant velocity.
depends_on: The concept of inertial frames.

name: The Constancy of the Speed of Light
text: No matter how fast you move, you always measure light traveling at the same speed c.
depends_on: Electromagnetism (c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀)).

name: Lorentz Transformations
text: Space and time coordinates mix when transforming between reference frames.
depends_on: Postulates 1 and 2.

name: Mass-Energy Equivalence
text: Mass is a form of energy.
depends_on: Lorentz transformations, conservation of energy and momentum.

name: Minkowski Spacetime
text: Space and time are not separate — they are woven into a single four-dimensional fabric called spacetime.
depends_on: Lorentz transformations.

name: The Equivalence Principle (General Relativity)
text: In a small room, you cannot tell whether you are in a gravitational field or in an accelerating elevator.
depends_on: Newton's observation that gravitational and inertial mass are equal (tested to extraordinary precision).

name: Einstein's Field Equations
text: "Matter tells spacetime how to curve, and spacetime tells matter how to move" (John Wheeler).
depends_on: Equivalence principle, Riemannian geometry, energy-momentum conservation.

name: Geodesic Motion
text: Objects in free fall follow the straightest possible paths through curved spacetime.
depends_on: Einstein's field equations, Riemannian geometry (geodesics).

name: The Schwarzschild Solution
text: The Schwarzschild metric describes the spacetime geometry around a non-rotating, uncharged mass.
depends_on: Einstein's field equations, spherical symmetry, vacuum (T_μν = 0).

name: Gravitational Waves
text: Accelerating masses create ripples in spacetime that propagate at the speed of light.
depends_on: Einstein's field equations (linearized approximation).

name: Penrose Singularity Theorem
text: Under very general conditions, gravitational collapse inevitably produces singularities (points where curvature diverges and GR breaks down).
depends_on: Einstein's field equations, energy conditions, global causal structure.

name: General Covariance (Diffeomorphism Invariance)
text: Coordinates are human inventions, not features of nature.
depends_on: Differential geometry (manifolds, tensors).

name: Hawking Radiation (Black Hole Thermodynamics)
text: Black holes are not completely black — they emit faint thermal radiation due to quantum effects near the event horizon.
depends_on: - General relativity (black hole solutions)

name: Frame Dragging (Lense-Thirring Effect)
text: A spinning massive object twists the fabric of spacetime around it, dragging everything — even light and space itself — in the direction of rotation.
depends_on: - Einstein's field equations

name: Birkhoff's Theorem
text: The gravitational field outside any spherically symmetric mass distribution is always the Schwarzschild solution — even if the mass is pulsating or collapsing.
depends_on: - Einstein's field equations

name: Cosmic Censorship Conjecture
text: Nature hides its singularities behind event horizons — you can never see a singularity from the outside.
depends_on: - Einstein's field equations

name: Cosmological Redshift
text: As the universe expands, the light traveling through it is stretched to longer (redder) wavelengths.
depends_on: - General relativity (FLRW metric, expanding universe)

name: The ADM Formalism (3+1 Decomposition)
text: The ADM formalism splits Einstein's curved 4-dimensional spacetime into a sequence of 3-dimensional spatial "snapshots" evolving in time.
depends_on: - Einstein's field equations

name: The Kerr Metric (Rotating Black Holes)
text: The Kerr metric describes the spacetime geometry around a spinning black hole.
depends_on: - Einstein's field equations

name: Gravitational Lensing
text: Massive objects act as gravitational lenses, bending and focusing light from objects behind them.
depends_on: - General relativity (geodesic equation for null rays in curved spacetime)

name: The Gravitational Wave Quadrupole Formula
text: Gravitational waves are generated by accelerating masses, but unlike electromagnetic waves (which start with dipole radiation), the lowest-order gravitational radiation comes from the mass quadrupole — the time-varying asymmetry in the mass distribution.
depends_on: - Linearized general relativity (weak-field, slow-motion approximation)

name: The Holographic Principle
text: The holographic principle says that all the information about what happens inside a volume of space can be encoded on its boundary surface -- like a hologram that stores a 3D image on a 2D film.
depends_on: - Bekenstein-Hawking entropy (Principle 7)

name: Loop Quantum Gravity (Quantized Geometry)
text: Loop quantum gravity proposes that space itself is made of discrete, granular units at the Planck scale.
depends_on: - ADM formalism (Principle 10)

name: The Positive Mass Theorem (Schoen-Yau / Witten)
text: The positive mass theorem proves that the total mass of any isolated gravitational system (as measured from far away) is always non-negative, provided energy density is non-negative locally.
depends_on: - Einstein's field equations (Law 1)

name: Gravitational Memory Effect
text: Gravitational waves do not just oscillate and pass -- they leave a permanent imprint.
depends_on: - Gravitational waves (Principle 5)

name: Numerical Relativity and Binary Black Hole Mergers
text: Numerical relativity uses supercomputers to solve Einstein's equations for the most violent events in the universe: colliding black holes and neutron stars.
depends_on: - Einstein field equations (Law 1)

name: The BMS Group and Asymptotic Symmetries
text: When you study gravity in regions far from all matter and radiation, the symmetry group of spacetime is not the expected Poincare group (translations, rotations, boosts) but a much larger, infinite-dimensional group called BMS.
depends_on: - General relativity, asymptotic flatness

name: Gravitational Wave Memory and Asymptotic Symmetries
text: After a gravitational wave passes, it leaves a permanent imprint: test masses do not return to their original positions but are permanently displaced.
depends_on: - Einstein field equations (General Relativity)

name: The Holographic Principle and AdS/CFT
text: Baryon acoustic oscillations are the imprint of ancient sound waves that traveled through the universe during its first 380,000 years, when it was a hot plasma of photons and matter.
depends_on: - General relativity, Friedmann equations (Astrophysics: Principle C1)

name: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
text: Thermal equilibrium is transitive.
depends_on: The concept of thermal equilibrium.

name: First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation of Energy)
text: Energy is conserved.
depends_on: Conservation of energy (from mechanics), the concept of heat.

name: Second Law of Thermodynamics
text: Nature has a preferred direction.
depends_on: The first law, the concept of temperature, and the concept of reversibility.

name: Third Law of Thermodynamics (Nernst's Theorem)
text: You can never cool something to absolute zero.
depends_on: Second law, quantum mechanics (the ground state has no degeneracy in a perfect crystal).

name: Boltzmann's Entropy Formula (Statistical Foundation)
text: Entropy measures how many microscopic arrangements are compatible with what we observe macroscopically.
depends_on: Probability theory, the equal a priori probability postulate.

name: The Boltzmann Distribution
text: At thermal equilibrium, high-energy states are exponentially less probable than low-energy states.
depends_on: Boltzmann's entropy, the postulate of equal a priori probabilities.

name: The Equipartition Theorem
text: In classical physics, each way a system can store energy (each "spring" or "kinetic mode") holds the same average energy at a given temperature.
depends_on: Boltzmann distribution, classical Hamiltonian.

name: The Gibbs Entropy Formula (Information-Theoretic Foundation)
text: Entropy measures uncertainty about the microscopic state.
depends_on: Probability theory, information theory.

name: Free Energy and the Variational Principle
text: Systems at constant temperature evolve toward states that balance low energy against high entropy.
depends_on: First and second laws, definition of entropy and temperature.

name: Carnot's Theorem
text: There is a maximum possible efficiency for converting heat to work, and it depends only on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs.
depends_on: Second law of thermodynamics.

name: Maxwell Relations
text: Thermodynamic variables are interconnected through exact differential relations.
depends_on: First and second laws, thermodynamic potentials.

name: Gibbs Phase Rule
text: The number of independent variables you can freely change (temperature, pressure, composition) while maintaining phase equilibrium is determined by the number of components and phases.
depends_on: Chemical equilibrium, free energy minimization.

name: Fluctuation-Dissipation Theorem
text: How a system responds to being pushed (dissipation) is directly related to how much it fluctuates on its own (noise).
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, linear response theory.

name: Ergodic Hypothesis and Ensemble Equivalence
text: If you watch one system for a very long time, it will sample all accessible states, giving the same statistics as looking at many copies of the system at one instant.
depends_on: Hamiltonian dynamics, measure theory.

name: Onsager Reciprocal Relations
text: When multiple transport processes occur simultaneously (heat conduction, diffusion, electrical conduction), the cross-coupling coefficients are symmetric.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics

name: Jarzynski Equality
text: Even if you drive a system violently out of equilibrium, you can recover the equilibrium free energy difference by averaging an exponential function of the work over many repetitions.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics (canonical ensemble)

name: Detailed Balance
text: At equilibrium, every microscopic process is exactly balanced by its reverse.
depends_on: - Microscopic reversibility (time-reversal symmetry)

name: Landauer's Principle
text: Erasing information has a minimum thermodynamic cost.
depends_on: - Second law of thermodynamics

name: The Fluctuation Theorem (Crooks and Evans-Searles)
text: The second law says entropy tends to increase, but for small systems over short times, entropy can spontaneously decrease.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics

name: Gibbs-Duhem Relation
text: The intensive thermodynamic variables (temperature, pressure, chemical potentials) are not all independent — they are constrained by the Gibbs-Duhem relation.
depends_on: - First and second laws of thermodynamics

name: Maxwell Construction (Equal-Area Rule)
text: The van der Waals equation of state predicts an S-shaped curve in the P-V diagram below the critical temperature, including an unphysical region where pressure increases with volume.
depends_on: - First and second laws of thermodynamics

name: Critical Phenomena and Universality
text: Near a critical point, every system in the same universality class behaves identically, regardless of whether it is a magnet, a liquid-gas system, or a superfluid.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics

name: Nernst Heat Theorem (Unattainability Formulation)
text: You can never actually reach absolute zero — each successive step of cooling gets progressively harder and produces diminishing returns.
depends_on: - Second law of thermodynamics

name: Wang-Landau Algorithm (Flat-Histogram Methods)
text: Instead of simulating a system at one temperature at a time (like standard Monte Carlo), the Wang-Landau algorithm estimates how many states exist at each energy level.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics (partition function, density of states)

name: Tsallis Statistics (Non-Extensive Statistical Mechanics)
text: Standard statistical mechanics assumes that entropy is additive -- the entropy of two independent systems is the sum of their individual entropies.
depends_on: - Boltzmann-Gibbs statistical mechanics (as the q → 1 limit)

name: Stochastic Thermodynamics
text: Classical thermodynamics describes average behavior of large systems.
depends_on: - Second law of thermodynamics (Law 3)

name: Active Matter Thermodynamics
text: Active matter systems -- flocks of birds, schools of fish, bacterial colonies, synthetic self-propelled colloids -- are fundamentally different from equilibrium systems because each constituent continuously burns fuel to move.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics (Boltzmann distribution as the equilibrium limit)

name: Thermodynamic Uncertainty Relations
text: The thermodynamic uncertainty relation puts a fundamental lower bound on the fluctuations of any current (flow of particles, energy, etc.) in a non-equilibrium system.
depends_on: - Stochastic thermodynamics (Principle 19)

name: Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH)
text: The ETH explains why isolated quantum systems appear to reach thermal equilibrium even though their time evolution is unitary (reversible).
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (Postulates 1-6)

name: Time Crystals and Discrete Time-Translation Symmetry Breaking
text: Just as a regular crystal breaks spatial symmetry (atoms arrange in a repeating pattern rather than uniformly), a time crystal breaks time-translation symmetry: the system's state repeats at a period different from the driving frequency, and this behavior is stable and robust.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics, equilibrium and non-equilibrium phases

name: Stochastic Thermodynamics and Fluctuation Theorems
text: The Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis explains the deepest puzzle of quantum statistical mechanics: why does an isolated quantum system, evolving unitarily and thus never losing information, behave as if it thermalizes?
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics, eigenvalue problem

name: The Beer-Lambert Law (Spectrophotometric Quantitation)
text: The darker a solution appears (the more light it absorbs), the more of the absorbing chemical is present.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (discrete electronic transitions and their probabilities); electromagnetic theory (photon absorption); statistical mechanics (population of energy levels).

name: The Calibration Principle (External and Internal Standards)
text: To measure how much of something is in a sample, you first measure a set of known samples (standards) to build a calibration curve --- a graph of signal vs.
depends_on: Law 1 (Beer-Lambert, as one example of a signal-concentration relationship); statistics (linear regression, uncertainty propagation).

name: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S/N) and Information Content
text: Every measurement has two components: the actual signal from your analyte and random noise from various sources (electronics, light fluctuations, environmental disturbances).
depends_on: Probability and statistics (normal distribution, central limit theorem); quantum mechanics (shot noise, photon statistics); electronics (thermal noise, bandwidth).

name: Limit of Detection (LOD) and Limit of Quantification (LOQ)
text: The detection limit is the smallest amount of a substance you can reliably tell apart from "nothing there." If the signal from your sample is at least three times larger than the typical fluctuations in a blank measurement, you can be confident something is present.
depends_on: Principle 3 (Signal-to-Noise); statistics (hypothesis testing, Type I and Type II errors, normal distribution); Principle 2 (Calibration, for sensitivity $m$).

name: Mass Balance (Conservation of Mass in Analytical Systems)
text: When you dissolve a substance in water, the total amount of that substance (in all its various dissolved forms) must add up to what you originally put in.
depends_on: Conservation of mass (general chemistry); equilibrium constant expressions (physical chemistry); stoichiometry.

name: Charge Balance (Electroneutrality)
text: Every solution must be electrically neutral --- the total positive charges must exactly balance the total negative charges.
depends_on: Conservation of charge (physics); Principle 5 (Mass Balance, for complete system of equations); equilibrium constants.

name: Selectivity and Specificity (Resolution of Chemical Information)
text: A good analytical method must not only detect your target substance but also distinguish it from everything else in the sample.
depends_on: Physical chemistry (thermodynamics of partitioning, kinetics of mass transfer); Principles 1--4 (measurement, calibration, S/N, detection limits); molecular recognition chemistry.

name: The Nernst Equation (Electroanalytical Quantitation)
text: The voltage of an electrochemical cell depends on the concentrations of the species involved.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (Gibbs free energy, $\Delta G = -nFE$); equilibrium chemistry; electrochemistry (half-cell reactions, standard potentials).

name: The Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation (Buffer and pH Calculations)
text: When you mix a weak acid with its conjugate base, the pH of the resulting buffer solution depends on the ratio of the two forms and the acid's intrinsic strength ($\text{p}K_a$).
depends_on: Principle 5 (Mass Balance); equilibrium chemistry (acid dissociation constants); Principle 6 (Charge Balance, for complete buffer calculations).

name: Chromatographic Plate Theory and Resolution
text: A chromatographic column separates compounds by repeatedly partitioning them between a stationary phase and a mobile phase.
depends_on: Principle 7 (Selectivity, for $\alpha$ and $k'$); physical chemistry (partition equilibria, diffusion); statistics (Gaussian peak shape, variance additivity).

name: Mass Spectrometric Separation and Detection (m/z Analysis)
text: A mass spectrometer weighs individual molecules by first giving them an electrical charge, then separating them by how heavy they are relative to that charge.
depends_on: Physics (electromagnetism, Lorentz force, ion optics); vacuum technology; Principle 2 (Calibration, for quantitative MS); Principle 3 (Signal-to-Noise, for MS detection limits).

name: Titration and the Equivalence Point
text: A titration is the controlled addition of a known reagent to an unknown solution until the reaction is exactly complete.
depends_on: Principles 5--6 (Mass Balance and Charge Balance, for calculating titration curves); stoichiometry; equilibrium chemistry (for predicting the equivalence point pH and shape of titration curves).

name: Spectroscopic Selection Rules
text: Not all transitions between energy levels produce observable spectral lines.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (transition dipole moment, perturbation theory); group theory (symmetry of wave functions and operators); Law 1 (Beer-Lambert, for the intensity of allowed transitions).

name: Resolution and the Separation Factor in Chromatography
text: Resolution measures how well two peaks are separated.
depends_on: Principle 10 (Chromatographic Plate Theory, for $N$ and $H$); Principle 7 (Selectivity, for $\alpha$); physical chemistry (partition equilibria, thermodynamics of retention).

name: The Boltzmann Distribution in Spectroscopy
text: Native mass spectrometry is the ability to weigh intact molecular machines — protein complexes consisting of multiple protein subunits held together by weak non-covalent forces — with extraordinary precision.
depends_on: - Mass spectrometry, electrospray ionization (Principles P1-P3)

name: The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology
text: DNA is the master blueprint of life.
depends_on: Organic chemistry (nucleic acid and protein structure); hydrogen bonding and base-pairing (Watson-Crick); thermodynamics of biopolymer interactions.

name: Enzyme Kinetics --- The Michaelis-Menten Equation
text: Enzymes speed up reactions by binding substrates and lowering the activation energy.
depends_on: Chemical kinetics (rate laws, steady-state approximation); physical chemistry (thermodynamics of binding); organic chemistry (enzyme-substrate interactions).

name: Molecular Recognition --- Lock-and-Key and Induced Fit Models
text: Enzymes and other biological molecules recognize their partners through shape complementarity and a precise arrangement of chemical interactions --- like a hand fitting into a glove.
depends_on: Organic chemistry (non-covalent interactions, stereochemistry); physical chemistry (thermodynamics of binding, $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$); structural biology (protein 3D structure).

name: Thermodynamic Coupling and the Role of ATP
text: Many essential biological reactions are energetically "uphill" and would not occur on their own.
depends_on: Physical chemistry (Gibbs free energy, $\Delta G$ additivity); general chemistry (conservation of energy); enzyme catalysis (Law 2, for the coupling mechanism).

name: Protein Folding and the Thermodynamic Hypothesis
text: A protein's amino acid sequence contains all the information needed to fold it into its correct three-dimensional shape.
depends_on: Physical chemistry (thermodynamics, entropy, enthalpy); organic chemistry (non-covalent interactions, hydrophobic effect); Principle 3 (Molecular Recognition, for intramolecular interactions).

name: Allosteric Regulation (Monod-Wyman-Changeux Model)
text: Many proteins act as molecular switches that can be toggled between "on" and "off" states by the binding of regulatory molecules at sites distant from the active site (allosteric sites).
depends_on: Physical chemistry (thermodynamics of conformational equilibria); Principle 3 (Molecular Recognition); Principle 5 (Protein Folding, for conformational states); statistical mechanics (multi-site binding).

name: Membrane Transport and the Chemiosmotic Principle
text: Cell membranes control what enters and leaves.
depends_on: Physical chemistry (thermodynamics, electrochemistry, Nernst equation); Principle 4 (ATP as energy currency); general chemistry (equilibrium, ion activities).

name: Metabolic Pathways and Bioenergetic Logic
text: Cells break down food molecules (glucose, fatty acids, amino acids) through a series of linked chemical pathways to produce ATP.
depends_on: Principle 4 (Thermodynamic Coupling / ATP); Principle 7 (Chemiosmotic Principle); Law 2 (Enzyme Kinetics); Principle 6 (Allosteric Regulation, for flux control); physical chemistry (redox potentials, $\Delta G$ of coupled reactions).

name: Lipid Bilayer Structure and Membrane Transport
text: Cell membranes are made of a double layer of fat-like molecules (phospholipids) that spontaneously form a barrier between the cell's interior and exterior.
depends_on: Physical chemistry (hydrophobic effect, thermodynamics of self-assembly); Principle 4 (ATP as energy currency for active transport); electrochemistry (Nernst equation for ion gradients); Principle 7 (Chemiosmotic Principle, for proton-coupled transport).

name: Signal Transduction Cascades
text: Cells communicate through a relay system: a signal molecule binds a receptor on the cell surface, which triggers a chain of intracellular events that ultimately change the cell's behavior (gene expression, metabolism, movement, division, or death).
depends_on: Principle 3 (Molecular Recognition, for receptor-ligand binding); Principle 6 (Allosteric Regulation, for conformational activation); Law 2 (Enzyme Kinetics, for kinase/phosphatase activity); Principle 9 (Membrane Structure, for receptor localization).

name: Le Chatelier's Principle Applied to Metabolism
text: Le Chatelier's principle --- the idea that disturbing an equilibrium causes the system to shift to counteract the disturbance --- is a powerful organizing principle for understanding metabolism.
depends_on: Physical chemistry (Le Chatelier's principle, Gibbs free energy, equilibrium constants); Law 2 (Michaelis-Menten kinetics, for enzyme rates); Principle 4 (Thermodynamic Coupling / ATP); Principle 8 (Metabolic Pathways).

name: Enzyme Inhibition Types and Lineweaver-Burk Analysis
text: Enzymes can be slowed down by inhibitor molecules in several different ways.
depends_on: Law 2 (Michaelis-Menten Kinetics); Principle 3 (Molecular Recognition, for inhibitor binding); physical chemistry (equilibrium constants $K_i$, $K_i'$).

name: Lipid Bilayer Self-Assembly
text: Biological membranes form spontaneously because the lipid molecules that compose them have a split personality: one end likes water (hydrophilic head) and the other end avoids water (hydrophobic tail).
depends_on: Physical chemistry (hydrophobic effect, entropy of water, Gibbs free energy of assembly); organic chemistry (amphiphilic molecular structure); Principle 9 (Lipid Bilayer / Membrane Transport, as the broader context).

name: Chaperone-Assisted Protein Folding
text: Synthetic genomics is the ultimate test of biological understanding: building a living cell from scratch using chemically made DNA.
depends_on: - Molecular biology, gene expression (Principle P1)

name: Conservation of Mass (Lavoisier's Law)
text: Matter is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
depends_on: Newtonian mechanics (mass as an invariant property); implicitly assumes non-relativistic conditions where mass-energy equivalence ($E = mc^2$) produces negligible mass changes.

name: Dalton's Atomic Theory
text: Everything is made of tiny, indestructible particles called atoms.
depends_on: Law 1 (Conservation of Mass); empirical observations of definite and multiple proportions.

name: Law of Definite Proportions (Proust's Law)
text: Water is always 11.2% hydrogen and 88.8% oxygen by mass, no matter where you find it or how you make it.
depends_on: Postulate 2 (Dalton's Atomic Theory) --- fixed whole-number ratios of atoms produce fixed mass ratios.

name: Avogadro's Law and the Mole Concept
text: A balloon of hydrogen and an identical balloon of oxygen at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of molecules, even though they have different masses.
depends_on: Postulate 2 (Dalton's Atomic Theory); ideal gas behavior; kinetic molecular theory.

name: The Periodic Law
text: When elements are listed in order of their atomic number, their properties repeat in a predictable, periodic pattern.
depends_on: Postulate 2 (Dalton's Atomic Theory); quantum mechanical model of the atom (electron configuration, Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle).

name: Chemical Bonding --- Ionic and Covalent Models
text: Atoms bond because the result is more stable (lower energy) than the separated atoms.
depends_on: Law 5 (Periodic Law, for electronegativity trends); quantum mechanics (orbital theory, Pauli exclusion); Coulomb's law (electrostatics for ionic bonding).

name: Stoichiometric Principles and the Balanced Equation
text: A chemical equation must have the same number of each type of atom on both sides.
depends_on: Law 1 (Conservation of Mass); Postulate 2 (Dalton's Atomic Theory); Law 4 (Avogadro's Law / Mole Concept); Law 3 (Definite Proportions).

name: Electron Configuration --- Aufbau Principle, Hund's Rule, and the Pauli Exclusion Principle
text: Electrons fill the lowest-energy orbitals first, with at most two electrons per orbital (spinning in opposite directions).
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (quantum numbers $n, l, m_l, m_s$); Postulate 2 (Dalton's Atomic Theory for the concept of distinct elements); electrostatic shielding (Slater's rules for $Z_{\text{eff}}$).

name: VSEPR Theory (Molecular Geometry)
text: The shape of a molecule is determined by the electron pairs around the central atom spreading out as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion.
depends_on: Principle 6 (Chemical Bonding, for the concept of bonding and lone electron pairs); Pauli exclusion principle (electron pair formation); Coulomb repulsion between electron clouds.

name: Electronegativity and Molecular Polarity
text: Electronegativity measures how strongly an atom pulls on shared electrons.
depends_on: Principle 6 (Chemical Bonding); quantum mechanics (electron density distribution); Law 5 (Periodic Law, for trends in $\chi$).

name: The Ideal Gas Law
text: The ideal gas law connects the pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of a gas in a single equation.
depends_on: Law 4 (Avogadro's Law); kinetic molecular theory; statistical mechanics (Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution).

name: Colligative Properties
text: While traditional chemistry focuses on making and breaking covalent bonds, supramolecular chemistry studies how molecules spontaneously organize themselves through weaker forces -- hydrogen bonds, electrostatics, and shape complementarity.
depends_on: - Chemical bonding (Principle 6)

name: Green Chemistry Principles
text: Green chemistry redesigns chemical processes from the ground up to prevent pollution rather than cleaning it up afterward.
depends_on: - Conservation of mass (Law of Conservation of Mass)

name: Supramolecular Chemistry and Self-Assembly Design Principles
text: Deep eutectic solvents are a new class of environmentally friendly liquid media made by simply mixing two cheap, nontoxic solid chemicals that together form a liquid at room temperature.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics, phase diagrams (Principles P1-P3)

name: Periodicity and Periodic Trends
text: As you move across the periodic table from left to right, atoms get smaller, hold their electrons more tightly, and become more electronegative --- all because the nucleus pulls harder while shielding stays roughly constant.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (electron configuration, orbital energies); general chemistry (periodic law).

name: Crystal Field Theory (CFT)
text: When ligands surround a metal ion, they push on the metal's $d$ electrons unevenly, raising the energy of some $d$ orbitals and lowering others.
depends_on: Principle 1 (Periodicity, for $d$-electron count and charge); electrostatics; quantum mechanics (orbital angular momentum, degeneracy).

name: Ligand Field Theory (LFT) and Molecular Orbital Treatment
text: Crystal field theory treats ligands as point charges, but real ligands form actual bonds with the metal.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Crystal Field Theory); molecular orbital theory; group theory (symmetry-adapted linear combinations); $\pi$-bonding concepts.

name: Hard-Soft Acid-Base (HSAB) Principle
text: Some metal ions are "hard" (small and highly charged, like Al$^{3+}$) and prefer to bind "hard" ligands (small and electronegative, like F$^-$).
depends_on: Principle 1 (Periodicity, for size and charge trends); Lewis acid-base theory; polarizability; electrostatics vs. covalent bonding preferences.

name: Coordination Chemistry and the 18-Electron Rule
text: Transition metal complexes are often most stable when the metal has 18 electrons in its valence shell, which fills all of its bonding orbitals.
depends_on: Molecular orbital theory (nine valence orbitals on transition metals); Principles 2--3 (Crystal/Ligand Field Theory for $d$-orbital splitting).

name: Wade's Rules (Polyhedral Skeletal Electron Pair Theory)
text: The 3D shape of a boron cluster or metal cluster depends on how many electron pairs are available for holding the framework together.
depends_on: Molecular orbital theory (bonding in electron-deficient species); Principle 5 (Electron Counting); Stone's tensor surface harmonic theory for deeper justification.

name: Symmetry and Group Theory in Inorganic Chemistry
text: The symmetry of a molecule dictates which orbitals can combine, which spectroscopic transitions are allowed, and how many distinct vibrational frequencies exist.
depends_on: Abstract algebra (group theory, representation theory); quantum mechanics (symmetry of Hamiltonians); linear algebra.

name: The Jahn-Teller Effect
text: When a molecule has electrons that could occupy two or more orbitals of equal energy, the molecule distorts its shape to break this tie and lower its energy.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Crystal Field Theory, for $d$-orbital degeneracy); Principle 7 (Symmetry and Group Theory); vibronic coupling theory.

name: The Nephelauxetic Effect
text: When a metal ion forms a complex, the electrons on the metal spread out (become more "cloud-like") because they partially share space with the ligand orbitals.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Crystal Field Theory, for the concept of Racah parameters and $d$--$d$ transitions); Principle 3 (Ligand Field Theory, for covalent mixing); atomic spectroscopy (free-ion term symbols and Racah parameters).

name: Tolman's Cone Angle (Steric Effects in Ligands)
text: In organometallic chemistry, the size of the ligands around a metal matters enormously.
depends_on: Principle 5 (18-Electron Rule, for understanding coordination number preferences); Principle 3 (Ligand Field Theory, for electronic parameter); van der Waals radii; organometallic reaction mechanisms.

name: The Isolobal Analogy
text: The isolobal analogy says that certain organic fragments and metal-containing fragments are electronically equivalent --- they have matching orbitals with similar shapes and electron counts.
depends_on: Molecular orbital theory (frontier orbital symmetry, energy, and electron count); Principle 5 (18-Electron Rule, for determining frontier orbital character of metal fragments); Principle 6 (Wade's Rules, which the analogy complements for cluster compounds).

name: The Irving-Williams Series (Stability of Transition Metal Complexes)
text: When the same ligand binds to different divalent first-row transition metals, copper(II) complexes are almost always the most stable, with stability increasing from manganese to copper and then dropping at zinc.
depends_on: Principle 1 (Periodicity, for ionic radius trends); Principle 2 (Crystal Field Theory, for CFSE); Principle 8 (Jahn-Teller Effect, for Cu$^{2+}$ maximum); thermodynamics ($\Delta G = -RT\ln K$).

name: Coordination Chemistry Isomerism
text: Just as organic molecules can have different structural arrangements with the same formula, coordination compounds can arrange their ligands around a metal center in multiple ways.
depends_on: Principle 7 (Symmetry and Group Theory, for classifying isomers and determining chirality); Principle 2 (Crystal Field Theory, for understanding stability differences between isomers); coordination chemistry fundamentals.

name: Pauling's Rules for Ionic Crystal Structures
text: Electrocatalysts speed up the chemical reactions that occur in fuel cells, electrolyzers, and batteries.
depends_on: - Crystal field theory (Principle 2)

name: Metal-Organic Framework Design Principles
text: Metal-organic frameworks are molecular tinker-toys: metal clusters (nodes) connected by organic molecules (struts) into crystalline networks with enormous internal surface area.
depends_on: - Coordination chemistry, crystal field theory (Principle 1)

name: Single-Atom Catalysis
text: The RNA World hypothesis proposes that before DNA and proteins, life was based on RNA — a molecule versatile enough to both carry genetic information and speed up chemical reactions.
depends_on: - Organic chemistry, nucleotide chemistry

name: The Radioactive Decay Law
text: Radioactive atoms decay randomly, but the overall rate is predictable: in any time interval, a fixed fraction of the remaining atoms will decay.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (tunneling for alpha decay, weak interaction for beta decay); probability theory (Poisson statistics of random decay events).

name: Nuclear Binding Energy and the Semi-Empirical Mass Formula
text: The mass of a nucleus is less than the sum of the masses of its individual protons and neutrons.
depends_on: Special relativity ($E = mc^2$); nuclear physics (strong force properties, liquid drop model); electrostatics (Coulomb repulsion); quantum mechanics (pairing energy, shell effects).

name: Nuclear Stability and the Valley of Stability
text: Not every combination of protons and neutrons makes a stable nucleus.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Binding Energy); strong nuclear force (saturation, short range); Coulomb force; quantum mechanics (shell model, spin-orbit coupling).

name: Alpha Decay and Quantum Tunneling
text: Heavy radioactive nuclei can emit alpha particles even though, classically, the alpha particle does not have enough energy to escape the nuclear Coulomb barrier.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (tunneling through a potential barrier); Principle 2 (Binding Energy, for $Q$-value calculation); Coulomb's law (barrier height).

name: Beta Decay and the Weak Nuclear Force
text: In beta decay, a neutron transforms into a proton (or a proton into a neutron) inside the nucleus, emitting an electron (or positron) and a neutrino.
depends_on: Weak nuclear force (W and Z bosons); quantum mechanics (Fermi's golden rule); special relativity (kinematics of three-body decay); Principle 3 (Nuclear Stability, for determining which nuclei undergo beta decay).

name: Nuclear Fission
text: When a heavy nucleus like uranium-235 absorbs a neutron, it can split into two smaller nuclei, releasing a large amount of energy and additional neutrons.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Binding Energy curve, liquid drop model); Principle 3 (Nuclear Stability, neutron-to-proton ratio adjustment); quantum mechanics (tunneling through fission barrier); neutron physics (cross sections, moderation).

name: Nuclear Fusion and Stellar Nucleosynthesis
text: Fusion is the process that powers the Sun and all stars: light nuclei (hydrogen, helium) are squeezed together at extreme temperatures and pressures to form heavier nuclei, releasing enormous energy.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Binding Energy, for energy release); Principle 4 (Quantum Tunneling, for sub-barrier fusion); statistical mechanics (Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution at stellar temperatures); plasma physics (confinement).

name: Q-Value of Nuclear Reactions
text: The $Q$-value tells you how much energy a nuclear reaction releases or requires.
depends_on: Special relativity ($E = mc^2$); Principle 2 (Binding Energy, since $Q$ reflects the difference in binding energies); conservation of energy and momentum.

name: Nuclear Cross-Section and Reaction Rate
text: A nuclear cross-section is an effective "target area" presented by a nucleus to an incoming particle.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (scattering theory, partial wave analysis); Principle 2 (Binding Energy, for compound nucleus formation); Principle 4 (Quantum Tunneling, for Coulomb barrier penetration); Principle 3 (Nuclear Stability, for resonance states).

name: Nuclear Isomerism
text: Just as atoms can exist in excited states, so can nuclei.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (angular momentum selection rules, multipole radiation); Principle 3 (Nuclear Shell Model, for spin assignments and level structure); electromagnetic theory (multipole radiation).

name: Radioactive Equilibrium (Secular and Transient)
text: In a chain of radioactive decays, parent and daughter nuclides eventually reach a balance.
depends_on: Law 1 (Radioactive Decay Law); differential equations (Bateman equations); Principle 3 (Nuclear Stability, for identifying decay chains and modes).

name: Gamma Decay and Electromagnetic Transitions
text: The r-process is the cosmic mechanism that creates the heaviest elements in nature — gold, platinum, uranium, and thorium.
depends_on: - Nuclear physics, neutron capture cross sections (Principles P1-P4)

name: Carbon Tetravalence and Hybridization
text: Carbon can form exactly four bonds, and it can arrange them in three different geometries --- tetrahedral, trigonal planar, or linear --- by mixing its atomic orbitals.
depends_on: Quantum mechanical orbital theory; Pauli exclusion principle; energy considerations of hybrid orbital formation.

name: Functional Group Reactivity
text: You do not need to memorize every organic reaction individually.
depends_on: Principle 1 (Carbon Tetravalence); electronegativity (Pauling); molecular orbital theory; thermodynamics and kinetics.

name: Reaction Mechanisms --- Nucleophilic and Electrophilic Pathways
text: In every organic reaction, electrons flow from electron-rich species to electron-poor species.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Functional Group Reactivity); kinetics (rate laws, transition state theory); thermodynamics (stability of intermediates).

name: Stereochemistry and Chirality
text: Many organic molecules can exist as mirror-image forms (like left and right hands) that are chemically identical to non-chiral reagents but behave differently in biological systems and with other chiral molecules.
depends_on: Principle 1 (Tetrahedral Carbon / Hybridization); group theory (symmetry operations); Principle 3 (Reaction Mechanisms for stereochemical outcomes).

name: Aromaticity and Huckel's Rule
text: Benzene and similar ring-shaped molecules with the right number of $\pi$ electrons are extraordinarily stable --- much more stable than you would predict from simple bond energies.
depends_on: Molecular orbital theory (cyclic conjugation, degenerate orbital sets); Principle 1 (Carbon hybridization, $sp^2$); Huckel molecular orbital theory (secular determinant for cyclic systems).

name: Thermodynamic vs. Kinetic Control of Reactions
text: Organic reactions often can give more than one product.
depends_on: Physical chemistry (Gibbs free energy, Arrhenius/Eyring kinetics); Principle 3 (Reaction Mechanisms); Boltzmann distribution.

name: Retrosynthetic Analysis (Corey's Logic of Chemical Synthesis)
text: To figure out how to make a complex molecule, work backward.
depends_on: Principles 1--6 (all preceding organic chemistry principles); comprehensive knowledge of named reactions and transforms; computational chemistry (for modern computer-aided synthesis planning).

name: Curly Arrow Mechanism (Electron Pair Movement)
text: Curly arrows are the language of organic chemistry mechanisms.
depends_on: Principle 2 (Functional Group Reactivity, for identifying electron sources and sinks); Principle 3 (Nucleophilic/Electrophilic Mechanisms); Lewis electron-pair model.

name: Markovnikov's Rule and Anti-Markovnikov Addition
text: When you add HBr to an alkene like propene, the hydrogen goes to the end with more hydrogens and the bromine goes to the middle carbon --- because this pathway goes through a more stable carbocation.
depends_on: Principle 3 (Reaction Mechanisms, electrophilic addition, radical mechanisms); Principle 1 (Carbon Hybridization, for carbocation stability); physical chemistry (thermodynamic stability of intermediates).

name: Woodward-Hoffmann Rules (Conservation of Orbital Symmetry)
text: Some organic reactions happen in a single concerted step where bonds break and form simultaneously through a cyclic rearrangement of electrons.
depends_on: Molecular orbital theory (orbital symmetry, HOMO-LUMO interactions); Principle 5 (Aromaticity, for the Huckel/Mobius analysis of transition states); quantum mechanics.

name: Baldwin's Rules for Ring Closure
text: When a molecule tries to close on itself to form a ring, the ease of ring closure depends on the ring size, whether the bond being broken is inside or outside the ring, and the geometry of the carbon being attacked.
depends_on: Principle 1 (Carbon Hybridization, for molecular geometry); Principle 3 (Reaction Mechanisms, for trajectory of nucleophilic attack); molecular geometry and strain (Baeyer strain theory).

name: Cram's Rule (Diastereoselectivity in Nucleophilic Addition)
text: When you add a nucleophile to a carbonyl group that sits next to a chiral center, one diastereomer of the product usually predominates.
depends_on: Principle 4 (Stereochemistry and Chirality); Principle 3 (Reaction Mechanisms, nucleophilic addition); conformational analysis; steric and electronic effects.

name: E1 and E2 Elimination Mechanisms
text: In traditional synthesis, you build a molecule step by step from simple starting materials.
depends_on: - C-H activation (Principle P26)

name: Photoredox Catalysis in Organic Synthesis
text: Photoredox catalysis uses visible light to create reactive radical species from ordinary organic molecules under remarkably mild conditions.
depends_on: - Molecular orbital theory, radical stability (Principle 2)

name: Skeletal Editing and Molecular Metamorphosis
text: Diversity-oriented synthesis is a strategy for making chemical libraries that explore the broadest possible range of molecular shapes, rather than variations on a single theme.
depends_on: - Organic synthesis, retrosynthetic analysis (Principle P10)

name: The Gibbs Free Energy Criterion for Spontaneity
text: A reaction will proceed on its own (spontaneously) only if it lowers the system's Gibbs free energy, which balances the trade-off between releasing heat (enthalpy) and increasing disorder (entropy).
depends_on: First Law of Thermodynamics (energy conservation); Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy increase); definition of enthalpy $H = U + PV$.

name: The Equilibrium Constant and the Law of Mass Action
text: Every reversible reaction reaches a balance point (equilibrium) where the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient, equals a fixed number at a given temperature.
depends_on: Law 1 (Gibbs Free Energy); definition of chemical potential and activity.

name: Chemical Kinetics --- Rate Laws and the Arrhenius Equation
text: The speed of a reaction depends on the concentrations of the reactants and on temperature.
depends_on: Conservation of mass (stoichiometry of rate); Boltzmann distribution (fraction of molecules with energy $\geq E_a$); statistical mechanics.

name: The Nernst Equation (Electrochemical Equilibrium)
text: The voltage of a battery or electrochemical cell depends on the concentrations of the chemicals involved.
depends_on: Law 1 (Gibbs Free Energy); Law 2 (Equilibrium Constant); Faraday's laws of electrolysis; definition of electrode potential.

name: Le Chatelier's Principle (Response to Perturbation)
text: If you disturb a system at equilibrium --- by adding more reactant, changing the temperature, or changing the pressure --- the system will adjust to partially undo what you did.
depends_on: Law 1 (Gibbs Free Energy); Law 2 (Equilibrium Constant); thermodynamic stability criteria.

name: Hess's Law (Additivity of Reaction Enthalpies)
text: The heat released or absorbed by a reaction is the same whether it happens in one step or a series of steps.
depends_on: First Law of Thermodynamics (enthalpy as a state function); Law 1 (Conservation of Mass for stoichiometry).

name: The Boltzmann Distribution and Partition Function
text: At a given temperature, molecules distribute themselves among available energy levels in a specific way: lower-energy states are more populated than higher-energy states, with the ratio determined by temperature.
depends_on: Classical and quantum mechanics (energy levels); probability theory; second law of thermodynamics (maximum entropy at equilibrium).

name: The Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Phase Boundaries)
text: The Clausius-Clapeyron equation tells you how the boiling (or melting) point of a substance changes with pressure.
depends_on: Law 1 (Gibbs Free Energy, for phase equilibrium condition $\Delta G = 0$); First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics; ideal gas law (for the simplified vapor-liquid form).

name: Debye-Huckel Theory (Electrolyte Solutions)
text: When salts dissolve in water, the dissolved ions do not behave ideally because they attract and repel each other through electrostatic forces.
depends_on: Law 2 (Equilibrium Constant, for the concept of activity); Coulomb's law (electrostatics); statistical mechanics (Boltzmann distribution of ions around a central ion); Poisson-Boltzmann equation.

name: Transition State Theory (Eyring Equation)
text: Transition state theory says that for a reaction to occur, the reactants must climb an energy hill to reach an unstable, fleeting arrangement called the transition state.
depends_on: Law 3 (Chemical Kinetics / Arrhenius); Law 1 (Gibbs Free Energy); Principle 7 (Boltzmann Distribution); statistical mechanics (partition functions of reactants and transition state).

name: Onsager Reciprocal Relations
text: When multiple transport processes occur simultaneously in a system (such as heat flow and diffusion), they can influence each other.
depends_on: Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy production); statistical mechanics (microscopic reversibility, fluctuation-dissipation theorem); linear response theory.

name: The Van der Waals Equation (Real Gases)
text: Most chemical reactions proceed monotonically toward equilibrium, but some systems far from equilibrium can oscillate rhythmically between states, producing dramatic color changes, spatial patterns, and traveling waves.
depends_on: - Chemical kinetics (Law 3)

name: Mechanochemistry and Non-Equilibrium Activation
text: Mechanochemistry uses mechanical force -- grinding, pulling, shearing -- to drive chemical reactions instead of heating or adding solvents.
depends_on: - Chemical kinetics, transition state theory

name: Photoredox Catalysis
text: Microwave-assisted synthesis uses microwave radiation to heat chemical reactions from the inside out, rather than conducting heat from an external source.
depends_on: - Chemical kinetics, Arrhenius equation (Principle P5)

name: The Born-Oppenheimer Approximation
text: Because nuclei are thousands of times heavier than electrons, the electrons move so much faster that we can solve for their behavior while treating the nuclei as stationary.
depends_on: Schrodinger equation; the large nuclear/electronic mass ratio; adiabatic theorem.

name: The Variational Principle
text: If you guess a wave function for a molecule, calculating its energy will always give you a value that is equal to or higher than the true energy.
depends_on: Postulate 1 (Born-Oppenheimer, for electronic Hamiltonian); completeness of Hilbert space; Hermitian nature of quantum mechanical operators.

name: The Antisymmetry Principle and Slater Determinants
text: Electrons are indistinguishable fermions, and swapping any two of them must flip the sign of the wave function.
depends_on: Pauli exclusion principle; spin-statistics theorem (from relativistic quantum field theory); quantum mechanical postulates about identical particles.

name: Molecular Orbital Theory and the LCAO Approximation
text: When atoms come together to form a molecule, their atomic orbitals combine to form molecular orbitals that spread over the whole molecule.
depends_on: Postulate 1 (Born-Oppenheimer); Principle 2 (Variational Principle); Principle 3 (Antisymmetry / Slater determinants); linear algebra.

name: The Hartree-Fock Self-Consistent Field Method
text: Instead of trying to solve the impossible problem of all electrons interacting simultaneously, we approximate by having each electron "see" the average effect of all the others.
depends_on: Principles 2, 3, 4 (variational principle, antisymmetry, LCAO-MO); mean-field approximation; iterative numerical methods.

name: Electron Correlation and Post-Hartree-Fock Methods
text: The Hartree-Fock method misses the detailed, instantaneous electron-electron interactions (electron correlation).
depends_on: Axiom 5 (Hartree-Fock as reference); Principle 2 (Variational Principle, for CI); perturbation theory (for MP methods); exponential ansatz (for coupled cluster).

name: Density Functional Theory (Hohenberg-Kohn-Sham Framework)
text: Instead of dealing with a complicated many-electron wave function, DFT says the electron density --- a simple function of just three spatial coordinates --- contains all the information needed to determine the ground-state energy and properties.
depends_on: Postulate 1 (Born-Oppenheimer); Hohenberg-Kohn existence theorem; variational principle applied to density functionals.

name: Basis Set Completeness and Convergence
text: Quantum chemical calculations use a finite set of mathematical functions (the basis set) to describe the electron orbitals.
depends_on: Theorem 4 (LCAO-MO Theory); Principle 2 (Variational Principle, which guarantees that larger basis sets give equal or lower energies); linear algebra (expansion in finite basis).

name: Coupled-Cluster Theory and the CCSD(T) Gold Standard
text: Coupled-cluster theory is the most accurate practical method for calculating the energies and properties of molecules.
depends_on: Axiom 5 (Hartree-Fock as reference); Principle 6 (Electron Correlation); Principle 8 (Basis Set Completeness for converged results); many-body perturbation theory (for the (T) correction).

name: Time-Dependent Density Functional Theory (TD-DFT)
text: While standard DFT calculates the ground-state properties of molecules, TD-DFT extends this to excited states and how molecules respond to light.
depends_on: Principle 7 (DFT, Hohenberg-Kohn-Sham framework); time-dependent quantum mechanics; linear response theory; Principle 8 (Basis Set, for practical calculations).

name: Potential Energy Surfaces and Conical Intersections
text: A potential energy surface is like a topographic map of a molecule's energy as its atoms move.
depends_on: Postulate 1 (Born-Oppenheimer Approximation, and its breakdown); Principle 2 (Variational Principle, for locating stationary points); multivariable calculus (gradient, Hessian); non-adiabatic dynamics.

name: Moller-Plesset Perturbation Theory
text: Ab initio molecular dynamics combines quantum-mechanical electronic structure calculations with classical molecular dynamics to simulate the motion of atoms without relying on pre-fitted force fields.
depends_on: - Born-Oppenheimer Approximation (Postulate 1)

name: Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials
text: Machine-learned potentials use artificial intelligence to learn the quantum mechanical energy of molecules and materials from a training set of expensive quantum calculations.
depends_on: - Born-Oppenheimer approximation (Principle 1)

name: Quantum Computing for Chemistry
text: Machine learning force fields are neural networks trained on quantum chemistry calculations that can predict molecular energies and forces thousands to millions of times faster than the original quantum calculations, with nearly the same accuracy.
depends_on: - Born-Oppenheimer approximation (Axiom 1)

name: Cell Theory
text: Every living thing is made of cells, cells are the smallest unit of life, and new cells can only come from existing cells.
depends_on: Microscopy (observational technology), disproof of spontaneous generation.

name: Membrane Theory and Selective Permeability
text: Every cell is wrapped in a thin, flexible membrane made of a double layer of fat molecules studded with proteins.
depends_on: Lipid chemistry (amphipathic molecules, hydrophobic effect), thermodynamics of diffusion, protein structure.

name: Cellular Compartmentalization
text: Eukaryotic cells are divided into specialized rooms (organelles), each with its own environment tailored to particular tasks.
depends_on: Membrane theory (Principle 2), evolutionary biology (endosymbiosis), biochemistry of organelle-specific pathways.

name: Cell Cycle Regulation
text: Cells do not divide haphazardly.
depends_on: DNA replication (Molecular Biology, Principle 3), protein regulation (phosphorylation, ubiquitin-proteasome degradation), gene expression regulation.

name: Signal Transduction
text: Cells constantly receive messages from their surroundings -- hormones, growth factors, signals from neighboring cells.
depends_on: Membrane theory (Principle 2), protein structure-function (Molecular Biology), enzyme kinetics.

name: Cellular Energy Transduction (Chemiosmosis)
text: Cells generate their energy currency (ATP) by using a molecular dam.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (free energy, electrochemistry), membrane theory (Principle 2), redox chemistry.

name: Programmed Cell Death (Apoptosis)
text: Cell death is not always accidental -- cells can be programmed to self-destruct in a clean, orderly fashion.
depends_on: Signal transduction (Principle 5), gene expression regulation, protein-protein interactions, mitochondrial function.

name: Endosymbiotic Theory
text: Mitochondria (the cell's power plants) and chloroplasts (the photosynthesis factories in plants) were once free-living bacteria.
depends_on: Cell theory (Postulate 1), membrane biology (Principle 2), prokaryotic cell biology, molecular phylogenetics.

name: Cytoskeletal Dynamics
text: The cell's internal skeleton is not a rigid scaffold -- it is constantly being built and torn down.
depends_on: Protein polymerization chemistry, GTP/ATP hydrolysis, membrane theory (Principle 2), motor protein function.

name: Vesicular Transport and the SNARE Hypothesis
text: Cells have an internal postal system.
depends_on: Membrane theory (Principle 2), compartmentalization (Principle 3), protein structure-function, GTPase regulatory cycles.

name: Cell-Cell Adhesion and Junctions
text: The cells of your body are not just loosely packed together -- they are connected by an array of specialized junctions that serve different purposes.
depends_on: Membrane theory (Principle 2), cytoskeletal dynamics (Principle 9), protein-protein interactions, calcium-dependent biochemistry.

name: Autophagy
text: Cells in a tissue constantly compare themselves to their neighbors.
depends_on: - Principle 7 (Programmed Cell Death, for loser elimination)

name: Liquid-Liquid Phase Separation in Cells
text: Cells organize much of their interior without membranes, using a physical principle familiar from oil and water: phase separation.
depends_on: - Polymer physics (Flory-Huggins theory)

name: Extracellular Vesicles and Intercellular Communication
text: Cells communicate not only through individual signaling molecules but also by releasing tiny membrane-enclosed packages (vesicles) containing proteins, RNA, and other cargo.
depends_on: - Membrane theory (Principle 2)

name: Organelle Contact Sites and Inter-Organelle Communication
text: Organelles were once thought to be isolated compartments floating in the cytoplasm.
depends_on: - Membrane theory (Principle 2)

name: Cellular Mechanotransduction
text: Cells are not just chemical machines -- they are also mechanical sensors.
depends_on: - Cytoskeleton dynamics (Principle 3)

name: Ferroptosis and Regulated Cell Death Pathways
text: Ferroptosis is a recently discovered way that cells die, triggered by a chain reaction of iron-catalyzed fat damage in cell membranes.
depends_on: - Membrane biology (Principle 2)

name: Spatial Proteomics and Subcellular Mapping
text: Spatial proteomics reveals where proteins are located inside cells and which other proteins they are near.
depends_on: - Organelle biology (Principle 6)

name: Liquid-Liquid Phase Separation in Cell Signaling
