text: Cells organize their internal signaling not only with membranes but also by forming droplet-like condensates of proteins.
depends_on: - Phase separation (Principle 24)

name: 3D Genome Architecture and Topologically Associating Domains
text: Dosage compensation solves a fundamental problem in biology: how do males and females, which differ in the number of sex chromosomes they carry, ensure that genes on the sex chromosome are expressed at the same level?
depends_on: - Gene expression regulation, chromatin modification

name: Morphogen Gradients and Positional Information
text: During development, cells need to know "where they are" in the embryo so they can become the right type of tissue.
depends_on: Diffusion physics, receptor-ligand binding, transcriptional regulation (gene expression thresholds).

name: Cell Fate Determination and Commitment
text: A fertilized egg can become any cell type in the body.
depends_on: Gene expression regulation, morphogen signaling (Principle 1), epigenetic mechanisms (chromatin modification).

name: Embryonic Induction
text: During development, one group of cells can instruct a neighboring group to become a particular tissue type.
depends_on: Morphogen signaling (Principle 1), cell fate determination (Principle 2), receptor biology, competence as a developmental state.

name: Turing Patterns and Self-Organization
text: Some of the patterns in nature -- the stripes on a zebra, the spots on a leopard, the branching of blood vessels, the spacing of hair follicles -- can arise spontaneously from simple chemical interactions.
depends_on: Diffusion physics, chemical kinetics, mathematical analysis of partial differential equations.

name: Stem Cell Differentiation and Self-Renewal
text: Stem cells are the body's reserve supply of unspecialized cells that can both copy themselves (self-renew) and transform into specialized cell types (differentiate).
depends_on: Cell fate determination (Principle 2), signal transduction, epigenetic regulation, cell cycle control.

name: Hox Genes and the Genetic Toolkit of Body Plan Specification
text: A remarkably conserved set of master control genes, called Hox genes, tells each segment of the body what to become.
depends_on: Gene expression regulation, transcription factor biology, evolutionary conservation, chromosomal organization.

name: Morphogenesis and Mechanical Forces
text: Building an organism is not just about chemical signals -- it also requires physical forces.
depends_on: Cell adhesion molecules, cytoskeletal dynamics, mechanical physics (surface tension, elasticity), signal transduction.

name: Gastrulation and Germ Layer Specification
text: Early in development, the embryo undergoes a dramatic rearrangement called gastrulation, in which a simple ball of cells transforms into a layered structure with three distinct tissue layers.
depends_on: Morphogen signaling (Principle 1), embryonic induction (Principle 3), morphogenesis and mechanical forces (Principle 7), cell fate determination (Principle 2).

name: Lateral Inhibition and Notch-Delta Signaling
text: When a group of identical cells needs to produce a mixture of different cell types -- say, neurons scattered among non-neuronal cells -- lateral inhibition provides the mechanism.
depends_on: Cell-cell signaling, morphogen gradients (Principle 1), gene expression regulation, feedback loop dynamics.

name: Programmed Cell Death (Apoptosis) in Development
text: Death is a normal part of development.
depends_on: Gene expression regulation, cell survival signaling (growth factor/neurotrophin signaling), mitochondrial biology, caspase biochemistry.

name: Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition
text: During development, cells sometimes need to break free from their neighbors, change shape, and migrate to distant parts of the embryo.
depends_on: Cell adhesion (Principle 7), signal transduction (TGF-beta, Wnt, Notch), transcription factor regulation, extracellular matrix biology.

name: Asymmetric Cell Division and Cell Polarity
text: When a stem cell divides, it often does so asymmetrically -- one daughter stays a stem cell while the other begins to differentiate.
depends_on: Cell polarity establishment, cytoskeletal organization, cell fate determination (Principle 2), stem cell biology (Principle 5).

name: Developmental Timing and Heterochrony
text: Development is not just about where things happen, but when.
depends_on: Gene regulatory networks, oscillatory gene expression, hormonal signaling, evolutionary biology (heterochrony and evo-devo).

name: Regeneration and Transdifferentiation
text: Some animals, like salamanders, can regrow entire limbs after amputation.
depends_on: Stem cell biology (Principle 5), cell fate determination (Principle 2), morphogen signaling (Principle 1), wound healing, dedifferentiation and reprogramming.

name: Placental Development and Maternal-Fetal Interaction
text: Mammalian development depends on the placenta, a remarkable temporary organ that connects mother and fetus.
depends_on: Cell fate determination (Principle 2), epigenetics (genomic imprinting), morphogenesis (Principle 7), evolutionary biology (parental conflict theory).

name: Waddington's Epigenetic Landscape
text: Most transcription factors cannot access genes wrapped tightly around histone spools.
depends_on: - Principle P19 (Chromatin Remodeling, for the remodeling machinery recruited by pioneers)

name: Synthetic Embryology and Embryo Models
text: Scientists can now grow structures that look and behave like early embryos, starting not from sperm and egg but from stem cells in a dish.
depends_on: - Morphogen gradients (Principle 1)

name: Chronobiology and Developmental Timing Mechanisms
text: Embryos have internal clocks that control the timing of development.
depends_on: - Morphogen gradients (Principle 1)

name: Mechanical Morphogenesis
text: Embryos are sculpted not only by chemical signals (morphogens) but also by physical forces.
depends_on: - Morphogen gradients (Principle 1)

name: Single-Cell Lineage Tracing
text: Single-cell lineage tracing uses molecular "barcodes" -- unique DNA sequences that accumulate mutations as cells divide -- to reconstruct the family tree of every cell in a developing organism.
depends_on: - Cell fate determination (Principle 2)

name: Interspecies Chimeras and Blastocyst Complementation
text: Interspecies chimeras are animals built from cells of two different species.
depends_on: - Stem cell pluripotency (Principle 5)

name: MicroRNA Networks in Developmental Timing
text: Organoids are miniature organs grown from stem cells in a dish.
depends_on: - Stem cell biology, self-renewal and differentiation (Principle P3)

name: Energy Flow Through Ecosystems (Trophic Dynamics)
text: All the energy in an ecosystem comes from sunlight (captured by plants) or, in rare cases, from chemical energy (captured by microbes).
depends_on: Thermodynamics (first and second laws), photosynthesis, metabolic physiology.

name: Competitive Exclusion Principle (Gause's Principle)
text: If two species need the exact same resource and there is not enough to go around, one will always outcompete the other and drive it to extinction.
depends_on: Population dynamics, resource limitation, interspecific competition.

name: Niche Theory
text: Every species has its own "role" or "address" in the ecosystem -- the conditions it needs, the food it eats, the habitat it uses, and the time of day it is active.
depends_on: Competitive exclusion (Principle 2), species physiology and tolerances, environmental variability.

name: Population Growth and Carrying Capacity
text: Left unchecked, a population would grow exponentially -- doubling and doubling again.
depends_on: Resource availability, reproduction rates, mortality rates, density-dependent regulation.

name: Island Biogeography
text: Islands that are larger and closer to the mainland have more species than islands that are smaller and more remote.
depends_on: Population dynamics (Principle 4), dispersal biology, extinction probability, area effects on population viability.

name: Biodiversity and Ecosystem Function
text: Ecosystems with more species generally function better -- they are more productive, more stable over time, and more resistant to invasion and disturbance.
depends_on: Niche theory (Principle 3), population dynamics (Principle 4), species interactions, community assembly.

name: Biogeochemical Cycling
text: The atoms that make up living things are constantly being recycled.
depends_on: Microbial metabolism, photosynthesis, decomposition, geochemistry, thermodynamics.

name: Lotka-Volterra Predator-Prey Dynamics
text: Predators and prey are locked in a cycle: when prey are abundant, predators thrive and multiply.
depends_on: Population growth (Principle 4), differential equations, consumer-resource interactions.

name: Ecological Succession
text: After a disturbance -- a volcanic eruption, a forest fire, an abandoned farm field -- ecosystems rebuild themselves in a predictable sequence.
depends_on: Population dynamics (Principle 4), competitive exclusion (Principle 2), niche theory (Principle 3), species dispersal, disturbance ecology.

name: Keystone Species
text: In every ecosystem, some species have an outsized influence -- remove them, and the whole community changes dramatically.
depends_on: Species interactions (competition, predation, mutualism), community structure, trophic dynamics (Law 1).

name: Metapopulation Dynamics
text: Many species do not exist as a single large population but as a collection of smaller populations scattered across patches of suitable habitat, linked by occasional migration.
depends_on: Population dynamics (Principle 4), dispersal biology, landscape ecology, habitat fragmentation.

name: Ecological Stoichiometry
text: Organisms need specific proportions of chemical elements (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus) to build their bodies.
depends_on: Biogeochemical cycling (Principle 7), trophic dynamics (Law 1), nutrient limitation (Liebig's law), organismal physiology.

name: Disturbance Ecology and the Intermediate Disturbance Hypothesis
text: Ecosystems are not static -- they are regularly disturbed by fires, storms, floods, and other events.
depends_on: Succession (Principle 9), competitive exclusion (Principle 2), population dynamics (Principle 4), landscape ecology.

name: Trophic Cascades and Top-Down Control
text: When wolves were reintroduced to Yellowstone, elk changed their grazing behavior, willows and aspens recovered along riverbanks, beaver populations returned, and even river channels changed.
depends_on: Energy flow (Law 1), predator-prey dynamics (Principle 8), keystone species (Principle 10), food web structure.

name: Species-Area Relationship and Habitat Fragmentation
text: Larger areas support more species -- this is one of the most universal rules in ecology.
depends_on: Island biogeography (Principle 5), population dynamics (Principle 4), habitat diversity, dispersal biology.

name: r/K Selection Theory
text: Some species are built for rapid reproduction in unpredictable environments -- think bacteria, dandelions, and mice, which produce many offspring quickly but invest little in each one.
depends_on: Population growth and carrying capacity (Principle 4), natural selection, life history theory, trade-off economics.

name: Ecosystem Resilience and Tipping Points
text: Every organism leaves traces of its DNA in the environment — in water, soil, or air.
depends_on: - Principle 6 (Biodiversity and Ecosystem Function, for the ecological questions eDNA addresses)

name: Rewilding and Trophic Restoration Ecology
text: Rewilding means bringing back missing animals -- especially large predators and herbivores -- to restore natural ecosystem processes.
depends_on: - Trophic cascades (Principle 14)

name: Planetary Boundaries and Earth System Ecology
text: Earth's environment has limits -- thresholds beyond which conditions become dangerously unstable for civilization.
depends_on: - Biogeochemical cycling (Principle 7)

name: Urban Ecology
text: Cities are not ecological wastelands -- they are novel ecosystems where evolution and ecology operate in fast-forward.
depends_on: - Energy flow through ecosystems (Law 1)

name: Environmental DNA (eDNA) Biodiversity Monitoring
text: Every organism leaves traces of its DNA in the environment -- in water, soil, and even air.
depends_on: - Population ecology (Principle 3)

name: Microplastic Biogeochemistry and Ecological Impacts
text: Microplastics -- tiny fragments of plastic smaller than a grain of rice -- are everywhere: in the ocean, in soil, in drinking water, in the air, and inside our bodies.
depends_on: - Biogeochemical cycling (Principle 7)

name: Fire Ecology and Pyrodiversity
text: The biological pump is the ocean's mechanism for pulling carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere and storing it in the deep ocean.
depends_on: - Photosynthesis, primary production (Principle P1)

name: Natural Selection
text: Organisms that are better suited to their environment tend to survive longer and have more offspring.
depends_on: Heritable variation (Genetics, Principle 6), differential fitness, population genetics (Hardy-Weinberg as null model).

name: Genetic Drift
text: In any real (finite) population, allele frequencies wobble randomly from one generation to the next, simply due to chance -- which individuals happen to survive and reproduce.
depends_on: Finite population size, probability theory (stochastic sampling), Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (as the no-drift baseline).

name: Mutation as the Raw Material of Evolution
text: Evolution needs raw material -- new genetic variants -- and the only way to get them is through random mutations.
depends_on: DNA replication fidelity, DNA repair mechanisms, environmental mutagens, molecular biology of DNA.

name: Common Descent
text: All life on Earth is related.
depends_on: Molecular biology (universal genetic code, conserved biochemistry), paleontology, comparative anatomy, phylogenetic methods.

name: Fitness Landscapes and Adaptive Evolution
text: Imagine a hilly terrain where the height represents how well-adapted an organism is.
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), genetic drift (Principle 2), epistasis, genotype-phenotype mapping.

name: Speciation
text: New species form when populations of the same species become separated -- by geography, behavior, or other barriers -- and evolve independently until they can no longer interbreed.
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), genetic drift (Principle 2), geographic and ecological factors, reproductive biology.

name: Phylogenetic Inference and the Tree of Life
text: We can reconstruct the family tree of life by comparing DNA sequences, protein structures, or physical features of organisms.
depends_on: Common descent (Principle 4), mutation (Principle 3), molecular biology (DNA sequences), statistical inference.

name: Kin Selection and Hamilton's Rule
text: Why would an animal sacrifice its own survival to help a relative?
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), population genetics, relatedness coefficients, Mendelian inheritance.

name: Punctuated Equilibrium
text: The fossil record shows that most species remain essentially unchanged for millions of years (stasis), and then new species appear relatively suddenly in geological terms.
depends_on: Speciation (Principle 6), fossil record, population genetics, developmental constraints.

name: Red Queen Hypothesis
text: In Lewis Carroll's *Through the Looking-Glass*, the Red Queen tells Alice, "It takes all the running you can do, to keep in the same place." Evolution works the same way: species must keep evolving just to survive, because their enemies (parasites, predators, competitors) are evolving too.
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), coevolution, frequency-dependent selection, population genetics.

name: Neutral Theory of Molecular Evolution
text: At the molecular level, most evolutionary changes are not driven by survival advantages -- they are random.
depends_on: Genetic drift (Principle 2), mutation (Principle 3), population genetics, effective population size.

name: Sexual Selection
text: Natural selection is not just about survival -- it is also about mating.
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), population genetics, quantitative genetics, mate choice behavior.

name: Cope's Rule, Bergmann's Rule, and Allen's Rule
text: Several patterns recur across evolution: animal lineages tend to evolve toward larger body size over time (Cope's rule); warm-blooded animals in cold climates tend to be bigger than their relatives in warm climates (Bergmann's rule); and animals in cold climates tend to have shorter ears, legs, and tails (Allen's rule).
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), thermodynamics (heat exchange), allometry (scaling of surface area to volume), phylogenetic comparative methods.

name: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium as Evolutionary Null Model
text: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes what happens to gene frequencies in a population when nothing interesting is going on -- no selection, no drift, no migration, no mutation, and random mating.
depends_on: Mendelian genetics (segregation and independent assortment), probability theory, idealized population assumptions.

name: Fisher's Fundamental Theorem of Natural Selection
text: Fisher's theorem says that the faster evolution by natural selection proceeds, the more genetic variation in survival and reproduction exists in the population.
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), population genetics (Hardy-Weinberg as baseline), quantitative genetics (additive genetic variance).

name: Phylogenetic Systematics (Cladistics)
text: To reconstruct the tree of life, we must distinguish between similarities inherited from a recent common ancestor (which are informative) and similarities inherited from a distant ancestor or evolved independently (which are misleading).
depends_on: Common descent (Principle 4), phylogenetic inference (Principle 7), character evolution, comparative anatomy/molecular biology.

name: Coevolution and the Geographic Mosaic Theory
text: When two species interact closely -- a predator and its prey, a parasite and its host, a plant and its pollinator -- they drive each other's evolution.
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), Red Queen hypothesis (Principle 10), population genetics, geographic variation, species interactions.

name: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo)
text: Remarkably, the same genes that build a fly are also used to build a mouse -- they are just used differently.
depends_on: Common descent (Principle 4), natural selection (Principle 1), developmental biology (Hox genes, morphogen signaling), molecular biology (gene regulation).

name: Inclusive Fitness and Multilevel Selection
text: Does natural selection act primarily on genes, individuals, or groups?
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), kin selection (Principle 8), population genetics, Price equation, game theory.

name: Evolutionary Game Theory
text: In many situations, an organism's best strategy depends on what everyone else is doing.
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 1), frequency-dependent selection, population genetics, game theory (mathematics).

name: Macroevolution and the Tempo and Mode of Evolution
text: Adaptive radiation is when one species rapidly splits into many different species, each evolving to use a different food source, habitat, or lifestyle.
depends_on: - Principle 1 (Natural Selection, as the driving force)

name: Cultural Evolution and Gene-Culture Coevolution
text: Humans evolve in two ways simultaneously: through genes (like all organisms) and through culture (unique to our species in its complexity).
depends_on: - Natural selection (Principle 1)

name: Phylogenomics and the Tree of Life
text: Building the tree of life -- the family tree of all living organisms -- used to rely on comparing a few genes at a time.
depends_on: - Phylogenetic systematics (Principle 16)

name: Eco-Evolutionary Dynamics
text: Eco-evolutionary dynamics recognizes that evolution is not always slow.
depends_on: - Natural selection (Principle 1)

name: Paleogenomics and Ancient DNA
text: Paleogenomics reads the DNA of organisms that died thousands to hundreds of thousands of years ago, revealing the genetic history of species and populations.
depends_on: - Common descent (Principle 4)

name: Holobiont Theory and Hologenomic Evolution
text: The holobiont theory argues that organisms and their microbiomes are not separate entities but function as a single biological unit.
depends_on: - Common descent (Principle 4)

name: De Novo Gene Birth as an Evolutionary Mechanism
text: Molecular convergence reveals that evolution is more predictable than we might expect.
depends_on: - Natural selection and adaptation (Principle P1)

name: Mendel's Law of Segregation
text: Every organism carries two copies of each gene (one from each parent).
depends_on: Meiosis (physical mechanism of allele segregation), diploidy, random fertilization.

name: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment
text: The inheritance of one trait is generally independent of the inheritance of another trait (provided the genes are on different chromosomes).
depends_on: Law of Segregation (Law 1), random orientation of homologous chromosome pairs at metaphase I of meiosis.

name: Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance
text: Mendel's abstract "factors" are real physical things -- segments of DNA on chromosomes.
depends_on: Mendel's Laws (Laws 1-2), cell biology of meiosis, cytological observation of chromosomes.

name: Linkage and Recombination
text: Genes that sit close together on the same chromosome tend to be inherited as a package.
depends_on: Chromosomal Theory (Principle 3), meiotic cell division, DNA double-strand break repair mechanisms.

name: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
text: If nothing is disturbing a population -- no natural selection, no mutations, no migration, no random fluctuations, and everyone mates randomly -- then the genetic makeup of the population will stay the same generation after generation.
depends_on: Mendel's Laws (Laws 1-2), probability theory, assumptions of ideal population (no selection, mutation, drift, migration, or assortative mating).

name: Mutation as the Source of All Genetic Variation
text: Every genetic difference between organisms -- every new trait, every disease allele, every evolutionary innovation -- began as a random change in DNA.
depends_on: DNA chemistry, replication fidelity and error rates, DNA repair mechanisms.

name: Epigenetic Inheritance and Gene Regulation Beyond Sequence
text: It is not just the DNA sequence that matters -- how the DNA is packaged and chemically tagged also controls which genes are active.
depends_on: Molecular biology of chromatin, DNA methyltransferases, histone-modifying enzymes, gene expression regulation.

name: Quantitative Trait Loci and Polygenic Inheritance
text: Most traits that matter -- height, intelligence, disease risk, crop yield -- are not controlled by a single gene with simple Mendelian inheritance.
depends_on: Mendel's Laws (Laws 1-2), linkage and recombination (Principle 4), Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (Axiom 5), statistics (ANOVA, regression).

name: Genetic Drift and Bottleneck/Founder Effects
text: In small populations, chance plays a huge role in determining which alleles survive.
depends_on: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (Axiom 5), probability theory, effective population size concept.

name: Molecular Clock Hypothesis
text: If you compare the DNA or protein sequences of two species, the number of differences between them is roughly proportional to how long ago they diverged from a common ancestor.
depends_on: Mutation (Principle 6), neutral theory, phylogenetic inference, paleontological calibration.

name: Genome Duplication and Gene Families
text: One of evolution's most powerful tricks is gene duplication -- making an extra copy of a gene.
depends_on: DNA replication and recombination (Molecular Biology), mutation (Principle 6), natural selection, chromosomal theory (Principle 3).

name: CRISPR and Genome Editing Principles
text: CRISPR is a revolutionary tool that allows scientists to edit the DNA of any organism with unprecedented precision.
depends_on: Watson-Crick base pairing (Molecular Biology), DNA repair mechanisms, microbial adaptive immunity, guide RNA design.

name: The Genetic Code
text: The genetic code is the dictionary that cells use to translate DNA into protein.
depends_on: Central Dogma (Molecular Biology), Watson-Crick base pairing, ribosome structure and function, tRNA biochemistry.

name: Genomic Imprinting
text: Most genes are expressed from both the copy you inherited from your mother and the copy from your father.
depends_on: Epigenetic inheritance (Principle 7), DNA methylation, chromatin regulation, Mendelian genetics (Laws 1-2), evolutionary theory (parental conflict).

name: Horizontal Gene Transfer in Genomics
text: Genomes are not just inherited vertically from parents -- genes can also jump sideways between unrelated organisms, especially in bacteria.
depends_on: Molecular biology of DNA transfer, comparative genomics, phylogenetics (Principle 10 and Molecular Clock), bacterial genetics.

name: Non-Coding RNA and Genome Regulation
text: For decades, scientists focused on the ~1.5% of the genome that encodes proteins and dismissed the rest as "junk DNA." We now know that much of the non-protein-coding genome is actually transcribed into RNA molecules that play critical regulatory roles -- controlling when and how much protein-coding genes are expressed, silencing transposable elements, and organizing the three-dimensional structure of chromosomes.
depends_on: Molecular biology (transcription, RNA processing), epigenetic regulation (Principle 7), Watson-Crick base pairing, chromatin biology.

name: Population Genetics and Allele Frequency Dynamics
text: Population genetics provides the mathematical machinery for understanding evolution at its most fundamental level: how the frequencies of gene variants change in populations over time.
depends_on: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (Axiom 5), Mendelian genetics (Laws 1-2), mutation (Principle 6), probability theory, effective population size.

name: Structural Variation and Copy Number Variation
text: Our genomes differ from each other not just in single-letter changes (SNPs) but in larger rearrangements: whole chunks of DNA can be deleted, duplicated, flipped, or moved.
depends_on: Chromosomal theory (Principle 3), DNA recombination and repair mechanisms, genome duplication (Principle 11), genomic technology.

name: Pharmacogenomics and Genotype-Phenotype Translation
text: People respond differently to the same drug because of their genetic makeup.
depends_on: Mendelian genetics (Laws 1-2), mutation (Principle 6), quantitative genetics (Principle 8), biochemistry (enzyme kinetics), molecular biology.

name: Genome-Wide Association Studies and Polygenic Risk Scores
text: Genetic studies find many variants associated with complex traits like height, but they only explain a fraction of the known heritability.
depends_on: - Principle 20 (GWAS and Polygenic Risk Scores)

name: Pangenome and Structural Variation Genomics
text: For decades, genetics relied on a single "reference genome" -- one composite DNA sequence representing the human species.
depends_on: - Chromosomal theory (Principle 3)

name: RNA Modifications and Epitranscriptomics
text: Just as DNA can be chemically modified (epigenetics), RNA is also decorated with chemical marks that change how it behaves without altering its sequence.
depends_on: - Central Dogma (gene regulation, Principle 5)

name: Base Editing and Prime Editing
text: Base editing and prime editing are precision genome editing tools that change individual DNA letters without cutting both strands of the DNA double helix.
depends_on: - CRISPR-Cas9 system (Principle 12)

name: Spatial Transcriptomics
text: Spatial transcriptomics measures which genes are active in each location within a tissue, combining the power of genomic profiling with the context of tissue architecture.
depends_on: - Gene expression (Principle 7)

name: De Novo Gene Birth
text: New genes do not always come from copying existing genes -- they can also be born from scratch, from stretches of DNA that previously did not encode any protein.
depends_on: - Mutation and variation (Principle 6)

name: Telomere Biology and Replicative Aging
text: The Sc2.0 project is building the entire genome of baker's yeast from scratch using chemically synthesized DNA, redesigned according to rational principles.
depends_on: - DNA synthesis, genome assembly

name: Koch's Postulates (Microbial Causation of Disease)
text: To prove that a particular germ causes a particular disease, you must: find it in every sick individual, grow it in the lab, use the lab-grown germ to make a healthy animal sick with the same disease, and then recover the same germ from the newly sick animal.
depends_on: Pure culture technique (Robert Koch, 1881), microscopy, animal models.

name: Microbial Metabolic Diversity
text: Microbes can eat almost anything and live almost anywhere.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (free energy, redox potentials), biochemistry (electron transport chains, enzyme diversity), organic and inorganic chemistry.

name: Horizontal Gene Transfer
text: Bacteria do not just inherit genes from their parents -- they can also pick up genes from completely unrelated organisms.
depends_on: DNA chemistry, bacteriophage biology, plasmid biology, molecular biology of recombination.

name: Quorum Sensing (Cell-Cell Communication)
text: Bacteria can "talk" to each other using chemical signals.
depends_on: Signal molecule chemistry, diffusion physics, gene expression regulation, population density.

name: Biofilm Formation and Microbial Community Organization
text: In nature, bacteria rarely swim around as lone cells.
depends_on: Quorum sensing (Principle 4), surface chemistry and adhesion, EPS production, gene regulation.

name: Microbial Ecology and the Microbiome
text: Microbes do not live in isolation -- they form complex communities everywhere, including in and on our own bodies.
depends_on: Microbial metabolic diversity (Principle 2), ecological theory (competition, mutualism), molecular tools (16S sequencing, metagenomics).

name: Microbial Resistance and Persistence
text: Bacteria can become resistant to antibiotics in two ways: by acquiring genetic changes that neutralize the drug (resistance), or by entering a dormant, hibernation-like state that allows them to survive treatment without being genetically resistant (persistence).
depends_on: Natural selection (Evolutionary Biology), horizontal gene transfer (Principle 3), mutation, microbial genetics.

name: Winogradsky Column Principle (Microbial Ecology Stratification)
text: A Winogradsky column is a simple glass cylinder filled with mud, water, and nutrients, left in the light for weeks.
depends_on: Microbial metabolic diversity (Principle 2), redox chemistry, diffusion physics, light penetration.

name: Endospore Resistance
text: Some bacteria have an ultimate survival strategy: when food runs out, they transform themselves into endospores -- essentially indestructible hibernation capsules.
depends_on: Bacterial cell biology, gene regulation (sigma factor cascades), DNA protection chemistry, thermodynamics of water activity.

name: Phage Biology and Lysogeny
text: Bacteriophages -- viruses that attack bacteria -- have two strategies.
depends_on: Horizontal gene transfer (Principle 3), microbial genetics, gene regulation, molecular biology of DNA recombination.

name: CRISPR-Cas Adaptive Immunity
text: Bacteria have their own immune system, and it works remarkably like a molecular "most wanted" list.
depends_on: Phage biology (Principle 10), horizontal gene transfer (Principle 3), molecular biology of DNA-RNA interactions, nuclease biochemistry.

name: Microbial Syntrophy
text: Some microbes can only eat certain foods if they have a partner that immediately consumes their waste products.
depends_on: Microbial metabolic diversity (Principle 2), thermodynamics (free energy, equilibrium), interspecies metabolite transfer, anaerobic microbiology.

name: Germ Theory of Disease
text: Diseases like tuberculosis, cholera, and influenza are caused by tiny living organisms -- germs -- that enter the body, multiply, and cause harm.
depends_on: Koch's postulates (Postulate 1), microscopy, pure culture technique, epidemiology.

name: Viral Replication Strategies
text: Viruses cannot reproduce on their own -- they must hijack a living cell's machinery.
depends_on: Molecular biology (Central Dogma, DNA/RNA replication), cell biology (membrane biology, endocytosis), phage biology (Principle 10).

name: Microbial Pathogenesis and Virulence Factors
text: Pathogenic microbes cause disease using a specific toolkit of virulence factors -- molecular weapons that help them stick to tissues, invade cells, evade the immune system, and produce toxins.
depends_on: Koch's postulates (Postulate 1), germ theory (Principle 13), microbial genetics, host immune system biology, molecular biology.

name: Microbial Symbiosis and Endosymbiotic Theory
text: Microbes do not just cause disease -- most of them are beneficial or neutral partners.
depends_on: Microbial metabolic diversity (Principle 2), microbial ecology (Principle 6), evolutionary biology (coevolution), immunology.

name: Extremophiles and the Limits of Life
text: Life can survive in places we once thought impossible: boiling hot springs, frozen Antarctic lakes, highly acidic mine drainage, and intensely radioactive environments.
depends_on: Microbial metabolic diversity (Principle 2), protein biochemistry (thermostability, acid resistance), membrane biology, evolutionary biology.

name: Microbial Genetics and Regulation
text: Bacteria are masters of genetic regulation -- they can turn genes on and off rapidly in response to their environment.
depends_on: Molecular biology (transcription, translation), biochemistry (enzyme regulation, signal transduction), DNA-protein interactions.

name: Microbial Cell Structure and the Prokaryote-Eukaryote Divide
text: The microbial world contains two fundamentally different cell architectures.
depends_on: Cell biology (membrane theory, organelle function), biochemistry (peptidoglycan, lipid chemistry), evolutionary biology (three domains of life).

name: The Global Virosphere
text: A group of archaea called ASGARD archaea carry genes for proteins that were thought to be exclusively eukaryotic — like actin and ubiquitin.
depends_on: - Principle 16 (Microbial Symbiosis and Endosymbiotic Theory)

name: Synthetic Cells and Minimal Genomes
text: What is the minimum set of instructions needed to build a living cell?
depends_on: - Microbial genetics and regulation (Principle 18)

name: Phage Therapy and Bacteriophage Biology
text: Phage therapy uses viruses that specifically infect and kill bacteria to treat infections -- the natural enemies of bacteria deployed as living medicines.
depends_on: - Viral replication strategies (Principle 14)

name: Microbial Dark Matter and the Uncultivated Majority
text: Most microbes on Earth have never been grown in a laboratory -- they are the "dark matter" of the microbial world.
depends_on: - Microbial genetics and genomics (Principle 18)

name: Engineered Microbial Consortia and Synthetic Ecology
text: Instead of engineering a single microbe to do everything, synthetic ecology builds communities of specialized microbes that work together, each handling a different task.
depends_on: - Quorum sensing (Principle 15)

name: Holobiont Theory and Host-Microbe Coevolution
text: Every multicellular organism lives in intimate partnership with microbes -- the holobiont concept says these are not separate organisms but a functional unit.
depends_on: - Microbial ecology and the microbiome (Principle 6)

name: CRISPR-Based Adaptive Immunity and Phage-Bacteria Coevolution
text: The minimal cell is the simplest possible living organism that can grow and divide on its own.
depends_on: - Molecular biology, gene expression (Principle P1)

name: The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology
text: DNA serves as the master blueprint.
depends_on: Nucleotide chemistry, hydrogen bonding specificity, thermodynamics of polymerization.

name: Watson-Crick Base Pairing
text: The two strands of DNA are held together by specific pairing rules: A always pairs with T, and G always pairs with C.
depends_on: Hydrogen bond chemistry, Chargaff's parity rules, stereochemistry of purines and pyrimidines.

name: Semi-Conservative Replication
text: When a cell copies its DNA, it unzips the double helix and builds a new matching strand on each half.
depends_on: Watson-Crick base pairing (Principle 2), DNA polymerase enzymology, topoisomerase activity.

name: Universality of the Genetic Code
text: Almost every living organism on Earth uses the same "dictionary" to translate DNA sequences into proteins.
depends_on: Central Dogma (Principle 1), tRNA aminoacylation specificity, ribosome structure.

name: Regulation of Gene Expression
text: Cells do not express all their genes all the time.
depends_on: Central Dogma (Principle 1), protein-DNA interactions, thermodynamics of binding equilibria.

name: Macromolecular Structure Determines Function
text: The shape of a biological molecule dictates what it can do.
depends_on: Amino acid chemistry, hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic effect, thermodynamics of folding.

name: Enzymatic Catalysis and Molecular Specificity
text: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions by providing a favorable environment for the reaction to occur.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (free energy, activation energy), protein structure (Principle 6), organic chemistry of substrates.

name: RNA World Hypothesis
text: Before DNA and proteins existed, life may have been run entirely by RNA.
depends_on: RNA chemistry (base pairing, secondary/tertiary structure), ribozyme catalysis, prebiotic chemistry.

name: Wobble Base Pairing
text: The genetic code has 61 codons for 20 amino acids, meaning many amino acids are specified by more than one codon.
depends_on: Watson-Crick base pairing (Principle 2), genetic code (Principle 4), tRNA structure, modified nucleoside chemistry.

name: Operon Model (Jacob-Monod)
text: Bacteria organize related genes into clusters called operons, which are transcribed together as a single unit.
depends_on: Central Dogma (Principle 1), gene expression regulation (Principle 5), protein-DNA interactions, allosteric regulation.

name: Post-Translational Modifications
text: The life of a protein does not end when it is translated.
depends_on: Protein structure (Principle 6), enzyme specificity (Principle 7), signal transduction, metabolic regulation.

name: RNA Interference and Gene Silencing
text: Cells have a set of quality-control inspectors for protein synthesis.
depends_on: - Principle P16 (Nonsense-Mediated Decay, as one component of the surveillance network)

name: Long Non-Coding RNA (lncRNA) Biology
text: The human genome produces tens of thousands of long RNA molecules that do not make proteins.
depends_on: - Gene regulation (Principle 5)

name: mRNA Therapeutics and Synthetic RNA Biology
text: mRNA therapeutics use messenger RNA as a drug: instead of delivering a protein or a small molecule, you deliver the instructions for making the protein, and the patient's own cells do the manufacturing.
depends_on: - Central Dogma (Principle 1)

name: RNA Modifications and Epitranscriptomic Regulation
text: Just as DNA has an epigenetic layer of chemical marks that regulate gene activity, RNA has its own set of chemical modifications that control its fate.
depends_on: - Central Dogma (Principle 1)

name: Condensate Biology and Phase Separation in Gene Regulation
text: Transposable elements — once dismissed as "junk DNA" — are now recognized as one of the most powerful forces driving genome evolution.
depends_on: - DNA replication, reverse transcription

name: The Neuron Doctrine
text: The brain is not a single continuous mesh of fibers, as was once thought, but is built from billions of individual nerve cells (neurons) that communicate with each other at specific connection points (synapses).
depends_on: Cell theory (Cell Biology), microscopy, histological staining techniques.

name: The All-or-None Principle
text: Neurons fire on a binary, all-or-nothing basis -- like pulling a trigger.
depends_on: Membrane potential (Physiology), voltage-gated ion channel properties, threshold dynamics.

name: The Hodgkin-Huxley Model of the Action Potential
text: The nerve impulse works like a controlled explosion of electrical current across the cell membrane.
depends_on: Membrane potential (Physiology, Nernst/Goldman equations), voltage-gated ion channel biophysics, electrical circuit theory (capacitance, conductance).

name: Synaptic Transmission
text: Neurons communicate at junctions called synapses.
depends_on: Action potential propagation (Principle 3), calcium signaling, vesicle exocytosis, receptor-ligand binding.

name: Hebbian Learning (Synaptic Plasticity)
text: "Neurons that fire together, wire together." When two neurons are repeatedly active at the same time, the connection between them gets stronger.
depends_on: Synaptic transmission (Principle 4), NMDA receptor biophysics, calcium signaling, protein synthesis.

name: Neural Coding
text: The brain must represent everything you see, hear, feel, think, and decide using only electrical impulses in neurons.
depends_on: All-or-none principle (Law 2), action potential (Principle 3), synaptic transmission (Principle 4), information theory.

name: Modularity and Hierarchical Organization of the Nervous System
text: The brain is not a homogeneous mass -- it is organized into specialized regions that handle different tasks (vision, hearing, movement, language, emotion, memory), connected by specific wiring.
depends_on: Neuron doctrine (Postulate 1), neuroanatomy, developmental wiring mechanisms, synaptic plasticity (Principle 5).

name: Cajal's Laws of Neural Polarity
text: Cajal recognized that neurons are not just randomly connected -- they are organized with a clear directionality and purpose.
depends_on: Neuron doctrine (Postulate 1), neuroanatomy, synaptic transmission (Principle 4).

name: Long-Term Potentiation and Long-Term Depression (LTP/LTD)
text: When synapses are strongly activated (as happens during learning), they can be durably strengthened -- this is LTP.
depends_on: Synaptic transmission (Principle 4), Hebbian learning (Principle 5), NMDA receptor biophysics, calcium signaling, protein synthesis.

name: Receptive Fields and Feature Detection
text: Each sensory neuron is tuned to respond to a particular kind of stimulus in a particular location.
depends_on: Neural coding (Principle 6), modularity and hierarchy (Principle 7), synaptic transmission (Principle 4), sensory transduction.

name: Predictive Coding and the Free Energy Principle
text: Your brain does not passively receive and process sensory information -- it actively predicts what it expects to see, hear, and feel, and then compares these predictions with actual sensory input.
depends_on: Neural coding (Principle 6), modularity and hierarchy (Principle 7), Bayesian inference, information theory, synaptic plasticity (Principle 5).

name: Mirror Neuron System
text: When a monkey reaches for a peanut, certain neurons in its brain fire.
depends_on: Neural coding (Principle 6), modularity (Principle 7), motor cortex organization, synaptic plasticity (Principle 5).

name: Dale's Principle
text: For a long time, neuroscientists assumed that each neuron uses only one chemical messenger.
depends_on: Synaptic transmission (Principle 4), neurotransmitter biochemistry, vesicular packaging and release.

name: The Blood-Brain Barrier
text: The brain has its own security system -- a tightly sealed barrier between the blood and brain tissue that keeps out most substances, including toxins, pathogens, and unfortunately most drugs.
depends_on: Endothelial cell biology, tight junction proteins, astrocyte biology, membrane transport, vascular physiology.

name: Neuroplasticity and Structural Remodeling
text: The brain is not hardwired -- it continuously reshapes itself based on experience.
depends_on: Synaptic transmission (Principle 4), Hebbian learning (Principle 5), LTP/LTD (Principle 9), neural stem cell biology, gene expression regulation.

name: Excitation-Inhibition Balance
text: The brain maintains a delicate balance between excitation (signals that activate neurons) and inhibition (signals that silence them).
depends_on: Synaptic transmission (Principle 4), Hodgkin-Huxley model (Principle 3), neural coding (Principle 6), interneuron diversity.

name: Neurotransmitter Systems and Neuromodulation
text: The brain has several broadcasting systems that release chemical modulators (dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, acetylcholine) across wide brain areas to set the overall mood, arousal, attention, and motivation.
depends_on: Synaptic transmission (Principle 4), Dale's principle (Principle 13), receptor pharmacology, G-protein signaling.

name: Myelination and Saltatory Conduction
text: Many nerve fibers are wrapped in a fatty insulation called myelin, with small gaps (nodes of Ranvier) at regular intervals.
depends_on: Hodgkin-Huxley model (Principle 3), cable theory (passive spread of voltage along axons), glial cell biology, ion channel distribution.

name: Neurovascular Coupling and Functional Neuroimaging
text: Scientists can grow miniature, simplified brain-like structures from human stem cells in a dish.
depends_on: - Principle 7 (Modularity and Hierarchical Organization, for the brain architecture being modeled)

name: Connectomics and Neural Circuit Reconstruction
text: Connectomics is the ambitious effort to map every neuron and every connection in a brain -- the nervous system's complete wiring diagram.
depends_on: - Neuron doctrine (Principle 1)

name: Neuromodulation and Brain-Computer Interfaces
text: Brain-computer interfaces read electrical signals from the brain and translate them into commands for computers, robotic arms, or communication devices.
depends_on: - Neural coding (Principle 6)

name: Predictive Coding and the Free Energy Principle
text: The brain is fundamentally a prediction machine.
depends_on: - Neural coding (Principle 6)

name: Glial Biology and the Tripartite Synapse
text: The brain is not just neurons -- glial cells, once thought to be passive support cells, are active participants in brain computation.
depends_on: - Neuron doctrine (Principle 1)

name: Neural Oscillations and Communication Through Coherence
text: Brain waves are not just a sign of brain activity -- they are a communication system.
depends_on: - Neural coding (Principle 6)

name: Neuroimmune Interactions and Microglial Synapse Sculpting
text: The connectome is the complete wiring diagram of a brain — a map of every neuron and every connection between them.
depends_on: - Neuron doctrine, synaptic transmission (Principles P1-P2)

name: Homeostasis
text: Your body constantly works to keep its internal conditions -- temperature, blood sugar, hydration, pH -- within a narrow, healthy range.
depends_on: Cell biology (membrane transport, cellular signaling), thermodynamics, organ system integration.

name: Negative Feedback Loops
text: When something in your body goes too high, a negative feedback system detects it and brings it back down.
depends_on: Homeostasis (Principle 1), sensor/receptor physiology, neural and hormonal signaling.

name: Positive Feedback and Feedforward Control
text: Unlike negative feedback (which corrects deviations), positive feedback amplifies them -- pushing a process to completion rapidly.
depends_on: Homeostasis (Principle 1), signal amplification mechanisms, neural and hormonal signaling.

name: Osmotic Balance and Fluid Compartments
text: Your body is mostly water, divided into compartments inside and outside cells.
depends_on: Membrane theory (Cell Biology), thermodynamics of solutions, renal physiology.

name: Membrane Potential and Electrochemical Signaling
text: Every cell in your body has a tiny electrical charge across its membrane -- the inside is slightly negative relative to the outside.
depends_on: Ion channel and pump physiology, thermodynamics of ion diffusion, membrane selective permeability (Cell Biology).

name: Metabolic Regulation and Energy Balance
text: Your body carefully manages its energy budget -- balancing the calories you consume against the calories you burn.
depends_on: Biochemistry (metabolic pathways, enzyme kinetics), endocrinology (hormonal signaling), thermodynamics (energy conservation).

name: Fick's Principle and Mass Transport
text: Substances move through the body in two ways: by diffusion (molecules spreading from areas of high concentration to low) and by convection (being carried by flowing blood).
depends_on: Physics of diffusion, fluid dynamics, cardiovascular physiology, respiratory physiology.

name: Starling's Law of the Heart
text: The more blood that fills the heart before it contracts, the harder it squeezes.
depends_on: Cardiac muscle physiology, sarcomere mechanics, calcium signaling, hemodynamics.

name: Boyle's Law Applied to Respiration
text: Breathing works by simple physics.
depends_on: Physics of gases (Boyle's law), respiratory muscle physiology, lung mechanics (compliance, resistance), anatomy of the thoracic cavity.

name: Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation and the Hill Equation
text: Hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells, behaves in a remarkably clever way.
depends_on: Protein structure-function (hemoglobin allostery), gas physics (partial pressures), acid-base chemistry, thermodynamics of binding.

name: Renal Countercurrent Multiplication
text: Your kidneys can produce urine that is much more concentrated than your blood, allowing you to conserve water.
depends_on: Osmotic balance (Principle 4), membrane transport (active transport, aquaporins), endocrine regulation (ADH), renal anatomy (loop of Henle architecture).

name: Kleiber's Law (Metabolic Scaling)
text: There is a remarkably universal relationship between an animal's body size and how much energy it burns: metabolic rate scales with the 3/4 power of body mass.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (energy balance), cardiovascular/transport network geometry, surface-area-to-volume scaling, allometry.

name: Starling Forces and Capillary Fluid Exchange
text: Fluid constantly leaks out of your blood capillaries into the surrounding tissues and is pulled back in.
depends_on: Osmotic balance (Principle 4), cardiovascular hemodynamics, protein chemistry (albumin), membrane permeability, lymphatic physiology.

name: Action Potential Propagation and Saltatory Conduction
text: Once a nerve impulse starts at one end of a nerve fiber, it travels like a domino chain -- each section of the fiber triggers the next.
depends_on: Membrane potential (Principle 5), Hodgkin-Huxley ion channel kinetics, cable theory (passive electrical properties), myelination biology.

name: Hormonal Feedback Loops and the Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis
text: Your body's hormone systems are organized like a chain of command with built-in braking mechanisms.
depends_on: Homeostasis (Principle 1), negative feedback (Principle 2), membrane potential and signaling (Principle 5), receptor biology, circulatory transport.

name: Acid-Base Balance and Buffer Systems
text: Your blood must stay at a very specific pH (slightly alkaline) for enzymes and proteins to function.
depends_on: Chemical equilibrium (Henderson-Hasselbalch equation), respiratory physiology (Principle 9), renal physiology (Principle 11), buffer chemistry.

name: The Cardiac Cycle and Hemodynamics
text: The heart works as a pump that squeezes and relaxes in a regular cycle.
depends_on: Muscle physiology (cardiac muscle contraction), Starling's law (Principle 8), fluid dynamics (Poiseuille's law), electrical conduction system of the heart.

name: Muscle Contraction (Sliding Filament Theory)
text: Muscles contract when millions of tiny molecular motors (myosin) ratchet along protein cables (actin), pulling them past each other like two interlocking combs being slid together.
depends_on: Membrane potential and excitation (Principle 5), calcium signaling, protein structure (actin, myosin), ATP biochemistry, excitation-contraction coupling.

name: Immune System Principles (Innate and Adaptive Immunity)
text: Lungs produce a soapy coating for the inside of air sacs.
depends_on: - Principle 9 (Boyle's Law Applied to Respiration, for pressure-volume relationships)

name: Chronobiology and Peripheral Clocks
text: Nearly every cell in your body has its own internal clock, ticking on a roughly 24-hour cycle.
depends_on: - Homeostasis (Principle 1)

name: The Gut Microbiome and Host Physiology
text: The trillions of bacteria living in your gut are not just passive passengers -- they form a metabolic organ that profoundly influences your health.
depends_on: - Homeostasis (Principle 1)

name: Optogenetics Foundations in Physiology
text: Optogenetics gives researchers a "light switch" for specific cell types.
depends_on: - Membrane potential and ion channels (Principle 5)

name: Organ-on-Chip Microphysiological Systems
text: Organ-on-chip devices are thumbnail-sized chips that mimic human organs.
depends_on: - Homeostasis (Principle 1)

name: Circadian Transcriptomics and Chronopharmacology
text: Nearly half of all genes in the body turn on and off on a daily schedule, and different organs run different circadian programs.
depends_on: - Circadian rhythms and molecular clock (Principle 28)

name: Interorgan Crosstalk via Circulating Factors
text: Memory engrams are the physical traces of memories in the brain — specific groups of neurons that are activated during a learning experience and physically changed so that reactivating these same neurons later brings the memory back.
depends_on: - Synaptic plasticity, LTP/LTD (Neuroscience: Principle P2)

name: Conservation of Mass (Mass Balance Principle)
text: Matter is neither created nor destroyed -- it only moves from place to place or changes form.
depends_on: Conservation of mass (chemistry/physics); system boundary definition; measurement of fluxes.

name: Biogeochemical Cycles
text: The elements essential for life -- carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water, and others -- continuously cycle between the atmosphere, oceans, rocks, soils, and living organisms.
depends_on: Conservation of Mass (Principle 1); thermodynamics; biology (metabolic processes); geology (weathering, volcanism, sedimentation); chemistry (redox reactions, dissolution, precipitation).

name: Thermodynamic Laws Applied to Ecosystems
text: Energy flows through ecosystems but is not recycled -- it enters as sunlight, is captured by plants, passes through herbivores and predators, and is ultimately lost as heat at every step.
depends_on: First and second laws of thermodynamics; photosynthesis and respiration biochemistry.

name: Carrying Capacity and Logistic Growth
text: No population can grow forever.
depends_on: Conservation of mass and energy; resource limitation; population ecology; thermodynamic constraints on productivity (Principle 3).

name: Ecosystem Services and Natural Capital
text: Nature provides humanity with a vast array of essential services for free: clean air, clean water, pollination of crops, flood protection, climate regulation, soil formation, and much more.
depends_on: Ecology (ecosystem functioning, biodiversity-function relationships); Biogeochemical Cycles (Principle 2); Thermodynamic Laws (Principle 3); economics (valuation methods).

name: Tragedy of the Commons
text: When a resource is shared and no one owns it -- like a public fishery, the atmosphere, or groundwater -- each individual has an incentive to take as much as possible, because if they don't, someone else will.
depends_on: Rational choice theory (economics); carrying capacity (Principle 4); mass balance (Principle 1); game theory (prisoner's dilemma structure).

name: Planetary Boundaries
text: There are limits to how much humanity can alter Earth's systems before we risk pushing the planet into a fundamentally different and potentially hostile state.
depends_on: Biogeochemical Cycles (Principle 2); Mass Balance (Principle 1); Climate Science (radiative forcing); Ecology (biodiversity-stability relationships); systems dynamics (nonlinear feedbacks, tipping points).

name: Bioaccumulation and Biomagnification
text: Certain pollutants (especially those that dissolve in fat and resist breakdown) become more concentrated as they move up the food chain.
depends_on: Mass Balance (Principle 1); Thermodynamic Laws applied to ecosystems (Principle 3); chemistry (partitioning behavior, lipophilicity, chemical persistence); trophic ecology.

name: The Precautionary Principle
text: When there is a reasonable scientific basis for concern that something could cause serious or irreversible environmental harm -- but the science is not yet conclusive -- we should err on the side of caution rather than waiting for complete proof.
depends_on: Risk assessment (probability and severity of harm); scientific uncertainty; ethics (intergenerational equity, duty of care); environmental law and governance.

name: Environmental Kuznets Curve
text: As countries industrialize and grow richer, pollution initially gets worse -- factories belch smoke, rivers become contaminated, forests are cleared.
depends_on: Economics (growth theory, structural transformation); environmental regulation; technology development; Carrying Capacity (Principle 4); Tragedy of the Commons (Principle 6, for pollutants that are commons problems).

name: Ecological Footprint Concept
text: The ecological footprint asks a simple question: how much of the Earth's productive surface does it take to support a given person, city, or country?
depends_on: Carrying Capacity (Principle 4); Biogeochemical Cycles (Principle 2); Thermodynamic Laws in Ecosystems (Principle 3); land-use ecology; carbon cycle science.

name: Island Biogeography Applied to Conservation
text: Islands close to the mainland have more species than distant islands, and bigger islands have more species than small ones.
depends_on: Population ecology (carrying capacity, minimum viable populations); Ecosystem Services (Principle 5); probability of colonization and extinction; species-area relationships.

name: Resilience Theory (Adaptive Cycles and Panarchy)
text: Nature-based solutions use ecosystems to solve human problems.
depends_on: - Ecosystem Services (Principle 5)

name: Carbon Dioxide Removal (CDR) and Negative Emissions
text: To achieve net-zero emissions and limit warming to 1.5-2 C, we need not only to reduce emissions but also to actively remove CO2 already in the atmosphere.
depends_on: - Biogeochemical carbon cycle

name: Circular Economy Principles
text: The circular economy replaces the traditional "take-make-waste" linear economy with a system where waste is designed out, products are kept in use as long as possible, and materials are recycled back into the economy at the end of their useful life.
depends_on: - Mass balance (conservation of matter)

name: Microplastic and Nanoplastic Biogeochemistry
text: Microplastics -- tiny fragments of plastic from packaging, clothing, tires, and cosmetics -- have spread to every corner of the planet: deep ocean sediments, Arctic ice, mountain air, human blood, and placentas.
depends_on: - Bioaccumulation and biomagnification (Principle 8)

name: Environmental Tipping Points and Early Warning Signals
text: The circular economy reimagines our economic system to eliminate the concept of waste.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics, material and energy conservation

name: Uniformitarianism (Actualism)
text: "The present is the key to the past." The same physical and chemical laws that govern erosion, sedimentation, volcanism, and tectonics today also governed them millions of years ago.
depends_on: Constancy of physical and chemical laws (physics, chemistry foundations).

name: Principle of Superposition
text: In a stack of rock layers that hasn't been overturned, the bottom layer was deposited first and is the oldest; each layer above it is progressively younger.
depends_on: Gravity (sediments settle downward); Uniformitarianism (deposition processes are consistent).

name: Principle of Original Horizontality
text: Sediments naturally settle in flat, horizontal layers under gravity.
depends_on: Gravity; fluid dynamics of sediment transport and deposition.

name: Principle of Lateral Continuity
text: A sedimentary layer originally forms as a continuous sheet across its basin.
depends_on: Superposition; Original Horizontality; understanding of erosion processes.

name: Principle of Cross-Cutting Relationships
text: A fault or an igneous intrusion (like a dike) must be younger than the rocks it cuts through, because those rocks had to exist before they could be cut or intruded.
depends_on: Superposition; causality (an event must postdate its substrate).

name: The Rock Cycle
text: Earth's outer shell is cracked into large pieces (plates) that slowly drift, collide, and slide past each other.
depends_on: Newtonian mechanics; thermodynamics (mantle convection); Euler's rotation theorem; seismological observations.

name: Principle of Inclusions and Components
text: If one rock contains pieces of another rock, the pieces must be older -- they had to exist before they could be incorporated.
depends_on: Superposition; Cross-Cutting Relationships; causality.

name: Walther's Law of Facies Succession
text: The rock layers you see stacked vertically in a cliff were originally side-by-side environments on the Earth's surface.
depends_on: Uniformitarianism (Principle 1); Superposition (Principle 2); Lateral Continuity (Principle 4); understanding of depositional environments.

name: Principle of Faunal Succession (Geological Dating)
text: Ocean basins are born, grow, and die over hundreds of millions of years.
depends_on: Plate Tectonics (Principle 7); mantle convection; Uniformitarianism (Principle 1); the Rock Cycle (Principle 6).

name: Bowen's Reaction Series (Mineral Crystallization)
text: Minerals do not all crystallize from magma at once -- they form in a specific order as the magma cools.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (Gibbs free energy minimization, phase equilibria); chemistry (silicate mineral chemistry, solid-solution behavior); the Rock Cycle (Principle 6).

name: Catastrophism vs. Uniformitarianism Debate
text: Ar-Ar dating improves on potassium-argon dating by allowing geologists to test whether a rock's age has been disturbed by later heating events.
depends_on: - Radiometric Dating (Principle 14)

name: Critical Zone Science
text: The Critical Zone is the thin skin of the Earth where life meets rock -- from treetops to deep groundwater.
depends_on: - Uniformitarianism (Principle 1)

name: Planetary Geology and Comparative Geomorphology
text: Planetary geology studies the geology of other worlds -- Mars, Venus, the Moon, icy moons -- to understand how geological processes work under different conditions.
depends_on: - Uniformitarianism (Principle 1)

name: Snowball Earth Hypothesis
text: Twice during the Neoproterozoic, Earth appears to have frozen over almost completely -- ice sheets reaching the equator and oceans covered in kilometers-thick ice.
depends_on: - Uniformitarianism (Principle 1)

name: Mantle Plume Dynamics and Hotspot Volcanism
text: Subduction zones are where oceanic plates dive back into the Earth's interior, carrying with them enormous quantities of water locked into the crystal structures of minerals.
depends_on: - Plate tectonics, subduction (Principles P4-P5)

name: Seismic Wave Propagation (Elastodynamic Wave Equation)
text: When an earthquake occurs, energy radiates outward as waves through the solid Earth.
depends_on: Newton's second law; continuum mechanics; Hooke's law of elasticity.

name: Snell's Law of Refraction (Seismic Ray Theory)
text: As seismic waves pass through layers of rock with different properties, they bend -- just as light bends when passing from air to water.
depends_on: Seismic wave equation (Principle 1); Fermat's principle of least time.

name: Newton's Law of Gravitation Applied to the Earth (Gravity and Isostasy)
text: Earth acts like a giant magnet, but this magnetism isn't caused by a permanent magnet -- it's generated by swirling motions of molten iron in the outer core.
depends_on: Maxwell's equations; Navier-Stokes equations for fluid flow; thermodynamics (convection in the outer core driven by heat loss and compositional buoyancy).

name: Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction in the Earth
text: Heat flows from the hot interior of the Earth toward the cooler surface by conduction through rock, and by convection in the mantle.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (first and second laws); Fourier's law (physics); knowledge of radiogenic heat sources (nuclear physics).

name: Isostasy (Airy and Pratt Models)
text: Earth's surface features are in a floating balance with the mantle below.
depends_on: Newton's law of gravitation (Principle 3); Archimedes' principle; fluid statics; viscous flow of the asthenosphere.

name: Mantle Convection
text: The Earth's mantle, though solid, flows very slowly over millions of years (like very thick honey).
depends_on: Thermodynamics; Navier-Stokes equations (Stokes limit); Fourier's law of heat conduction; Rayleigh-Benard convection theory.

name: Rayleigh Number and Convection Onset
text: Whether a fluid heated from below simply conducts heat upward or begins to overturn and convect depends on a single dimensionless number -- the Rayleigh number.
depends_on: Navier-Stokes equations; Fourier's law of heat conduction (Principle 5); buoyancy (Archimedes' principle); viscous flow theory.

name: Bullen's Earth Model (Seismic Discontinuities)
text: Earth is not uniform inside -- it has distinct layers separated by sharp boundaries where seismic waves suddenly speed up, slow down, or disappear.
depends_on: Seismic Wave Propagation (Principle 1); Snell's Law (Principle 2); mineral physics (phase transitions under pressure and temperature).

name: Curie Temperature and Magnetic Domains
text: Certain iron-bearing minerals in rocks act like tiny magnets.
depends_on: Geodynamo theory (Principle 4); thermodynamics; quantum mechanics (exchange interaction in ferromagnetic materials); Fourier's law of heat conduction (Principle 5).

name: Love and Rayleigh Wave Propagation
text: Surface waves travel along the Earth's surface like ripples on a pond, and they cause the most shaking and damage during earthquakes.
depends_on: Seismic Wave Propagation (Principle 1); elasticity theory (boundary conditions at a free surface); layered media theory.

name: Geoid and Reference Ellipsoid
text: Human activities can cause earthquakes.
depends_on: - Elastic Rebound Theory (Principle 13)

name: Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS)
text: DAS turns ordinary fiber-optic cables -- the same cables that carry internet traffic -- into thousands of seismic sensors.
depends_on: - Seismic wave propagation

name: Induced Seismicity Principles
text: Human activities -- injecting wastewater underground, filling reservoirs, hydraulic fracturing, and geothermal operations -- can trigger earthquakes by changing pressure on underground faults.
depends_on: - Elastic rebound theory

name: Mantle Plume Seismic Imaging and Deep Earth Structure
text: Advanced seismic imaging has now photographed the Earth's deep interior in three dimensions, revealing the structure of mantle plumes -- hot columns of rock rising from near the core to feed surface volcanism.
depends_on: - Seismic tomography (Principle 21)

name: Glacial Isostatic Adjustment and Mantle Viscosity
text: The post-perovskite phase transition is a fundamental change in the crystal structure of the most abundant mineral in Earth's lower mantle that occurs just above the core-mantle boundary, nearly 3,000 km beneath our feet.
depends_on: - Seismic wave propagation (Principle P1)

name: The Ideal Gas Law for the Atmosphere (Equation of State)
text: Air behaves like an ideal gas: its pressure, density, and temperature are related by a simple equation.
depends_on: Kinetic theory of gases; molecular physics.

name: The Hydrostatic Equation
text: Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude because there is less air above you as you go higher.
depends_on: Newton's second law (force balance); Ideal Gas Law (Principle 1); gravity.

name: The Coriolis Effect
text: Because Earth rotates, anything moving freely over its surface appears to curve -- to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere.
depends_on: Newton's laws in rotating reference frames; Earth's rotation.

name: Radiative Energy Balance (Stefan-Boltzmann and Planetary Energy Budget)
text: Earth absorbs sunlight and radiates heat back to space as infrared radiation.
depends_on: Stefan-Boltzmann law; Planck's law of blackbody radiation; Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation; Beer-Lambert law of absorption.

name: Adiabatic Lapse Rate
text: When air rises, it expands (because pressure decreases with height) and cools -- about 10 degrees C per kilometer for dry air.
depends_on: First law of thermodynamics; Ideal Gas Law (Principle 1); Hydrostatic Equation (Principle 2).

name: Clausius-Clapeyron Relation (Moisture and Saturation)
text: Warmer air can hold exponentially more water vapor before it becomes saturated and forms clouds or rain.
depends_on: Second law of thermodynamics; phase equilibrium thermodynamics; Ideal Gas Law (Principle 1).

name: Geostrophic Balance
text: At large scales, winds don't blow directly from high to low pressure.
depends_on: Coriolis Effect (Principle 3); Hydrostatic Equation (Principle 2); Newton's second law.

name: Conservation of Energy in the Climate System (First Law of Thermodynamics)
text: Energy in the climate system is conserved.
depends_on: First law of thermodynamics; Radiative Energy Balance (Principle 4); Stefan-Boltzmann law.

name: Milankovitch Cycles (Orbital Forcing)
text: Earth's orbit around the Sun is not fixed -- it wobbles and stretches over tens of thousands of years.
depends_on: Celestial mechanics (Kepler's laws, gravitational perturbations by Jupiter and Saturn); Radiative Energy Balance (Principle 4); ice-albedo feedback.

name: Bjerknes Feedback (ENSO Dynamics)
text: In the tropical Pacific, the ocean and atmosphere form a coupled feedback loop.
depends_on: Ekman transport (ocean dynamics); Walker circulation (atmospheric dynamics); Radiative Energy Balance (Principle 4); Coriolis Effect (Principle 3); ocean-atmosphere coupling.

name: Rossby Wave Dynamics
text: The jet stream does not blow straight around the Earth -- it meanders in giant waves called Rossby waves, which are thousands of kilometers long.
depends_on: Conservation of Potential Vorticity; Coriolis Effect (Principle 3); Geostrophic Balance (Principle 7); beta-plane approximation (variation of *f* with latitude).

name: Stefan-Boltzmann Law Applied to Planetary Temperature
text: Any object radiates energy in proportion to the fourth power of its temperature -- so doubling the temperature increases radiation 16-fold.
depends_on: Blackbody radiation theory (Planck's law); thermodynamics (second law); Radiative Energy Balance (Principle 4).

name: Hadley, Ferrel, and Polar Cell Circulation
text: The atmosphere cleans itself primarily through one molecule: the hydroxyl radical, OH.
depends_on: - Ozone Photochemistry (Principle 20)

name: AI-Based Weather Prediction
text: Artificial intelligence weather models, trained on decades of historical weather data, can now predict weather as accurately as the world's best physics-based models -- and they do it in seconds rather than hours.
depends_on: - Primitive equations and NWP (atmospheric dynamics)

name: Climate Tipping Elements and Cascade Risks
text: Earth's climate contains several "tipping elements" -- large components like ice sheets, ocean circulation, and the Amazon rainforest -- that can undergo rapid, irreversible change once a temperature threshold is crossed.
depends_on: - Radiative balance (Principle 2)

name: Atmospheric Rivers and Extreme Precipitation
text: Atmospheric rivers are invisible rivers of water vapor in the sky -- narrow bands stretching thousands of kilometers that carry as much water as the Amazon River.
depends_on: - Clausius-Clapeyron relation (Principle 6)

name: Snowball Earth Climate Dynamics
text: Climate tipping cascades are the nightmare scenario of climate change: once one part of the Earth system crosses a critical threshold, it could trigger a domino effect of irreversible changes in other parts.
depends_on: - Greenhouse effect, radiative forcing (Principles P6-P7)

name: Thermohaline Circulation and Ocean Stratification
text: Ocean water density depends mainly on temperature and salt content.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (heat exchange, phase changes); equation of state for seawater; gravity (buoyancy-driven flow); Coriolis effect (planetary rotation shapes the circulation pathways).

name: Ekman Transport
text: When wind blows steadily across the ocean surface, it doesn't push water directly in the wind's direction.
depends_on: Coriolis effect; Newton's second law applied to ocean flow; boundary-layer friction theory.

name: Geostrophic Flow in the Ocean
text: Large ocean currents don't flow from high pressure to low pressure in a straight line.
depends_on: Coriolis effect; hydrostatic balance; equation of state for seawater (Principle 1).

name: Ocean Surface Wave Mechanics (Airy Wave Theory)
text: Ocean waves are generated by wind and travel as undulations of the sea surface.
depends_on: Newton's laws; potential flow theory (irrotational, inviscid fluid); conservation of mass and momentum; gravity as the restoring force.

name: Sverdrup Balance (Wind-Driven Circulation)
text: The large-scale pattern of ocean circulation in each basin (the great subtropical and subpolar gyres) is determined by the pattern of winds blowing over the ocean.
depends_on: Coriolis effect (and its latitudinal variation, *beta*); Geostrophic Balance (Principle 3); Ekman Transport (Principle 2).

name: Conservation of Potential Vorticity
text: Spinning water columns in the ocean tend to conserve their total spin.
depends_on: Conservation of angular momentum; Coriolis effect; conservation of mass.

name: Salinity-Density Relationship and Haline Processes
text: The saltiness of seawater strongly affects its density: saltier water is denser.
depends_on: Equation of state for seawater (Principle 1); conservation of mass (freshwater budget); thermodynamics.

name: Stommel's Model (Western Boundary Currents)
text: The great ocean currents along western coastlines -- the Gulf Stream, the Kuroshio -- are narrow, fast, and powerful, while the return flows on the eastern sides of ocean basins are broad and sluggish.
depends_on: Sverdrup Balance (Principle 5); beta effect (variation of Coriolis parameter with latitude); vorticity dynamics; friction.

name: Redfield Ratio (C:N:P = 106:16:1)
text: Marine phytoplankton consistently incorporate carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus in the ratio 106:16:1 when they grow, and release these elements in the same ratio when they decompose.
depends_on: Biogeochemistry (nutrient cycling); photosynthesis and respiration biochemistry; conservation of mass; Thermohaline Circulation (Principle 1, for nutrient distribution via deep water transport).

name: Deep Water Formation
text: The deep ocean gets its water from the surface at only a few special places on Earth -- mainly the North Atlantic near Greenland and the seas around Antarctica.
depends_on: Thermohaline Circulation (Principle 1, equation of state); surface heat loss and freshwater balance; sea-ice formation (brine rejection); Coriolis effect (constraining sinking pathways and deep boundary currents).

name: Tidal Forcing and Resonance
text: Ocean tides are caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon (and to a lesser extent the Sun), which stretches the ocean into two bulges on opposite sides of the Earth.
depends_on: Newton's law of gravitation; fluid dynamics (shallow-water wave equations); resonance theory (forced oscillations); ocean basin geometry and bathymetry.

name: Ocean Acidification (Carbonate Chemistry)
text: The ocean acts as a giant sponge for CO_2, absorbing about a quarter of what humans emit.
depends_on: Carbonate chemistry equilibria; Henry's law (CO_2 gas-liquid partitioning); thermodynamics (temperature and pressure dependence of equilibrium constants); Thermohaline Circulation (Principle 1, distributing DIC and alkalinity).

name: Ocean Stratification and the Thermocline
text: The ocean is layered like a parfait.
depends_on: Equation of state for seawater (Principle 1); solar radiation (surface heating); wind mixing; Thermohaline Circulation (Principle 1, for deep water properties).

name: The Ocean Carbon Cycle (Biological and Solubility Pumps)
text: The ocean is Earth's largest carbon reservoir.
depends_on: Redfield Ratio (Principle 9); Thermohaline Circulation (Principle 1); Deep Water Formation (Principle 10); Ocean Stratification (Principle 13); Ocean Acidification (Principle 12, for carbonate system interactions).

name: El Nino-Southern Oscillation (ENSO)
text: Deep on the ocean floor where tectonic plates spread apart, superheated water laden with dissolved minerals gushes from chimney-like vents.
depends_on: - Thermohaline Circulation (Principle 1, for ambient water properties)

name: Ocean Deoxygenation
text: The ocean is losing oxygen as it warms.
depends_on: - Thermohaline circulation and ocean mixing

name: Ocean Acidification
text: The ocean absorbs enormous quantities of CO2 from the atmosphere, which makes seawater more acidic.
depends_on: - Carbonate chemistry equilibrium

name: Ocean Mesoscale Eddies and Submesoscale Dynamics
text: The ocean is filled with spinning whirlpools called mesoscale eddies -- some as large as a small country, lasting for months, and containing the vast majority of the ocean's kinetic energy.
depends_on: - Geostrophic flow (Principle 3)

name: Ocean Deoxygenation and Expanding Oxygen Minimum Zones
text: Marine heatwaves are prolonged periods of unusually hot ocean temperatures — the oceanic equivalent of atmospheric heat waves.
depends_on: - Ocean heat content, thermodynamics (Principles P1-P2)

name: Faunal Succession (Law of Fossil Succession)
text: Fossils appear in the rock record in a consistent, predictable order that reflects the history of life.
depends_on: Principle of Superposition (Geology); evolutionary change (biology -- species evolve, go extinct, and are replaced by different species); irreversibility of extinction.

name: Principle of Correlation
text: If the same distinctive fossil species is found in rock layers on different continents, those rock layers are approximately the same age -- even if the rocks themselves look completely different.
depends_on: Faunal Succession (Principle 1); Lateral Continuity (Geology); assumption that species have finite, synchronous temporal ranges across their geographic extent.

name: Taphonomy (Laws of Fossil Preservation)
text: Fossils are not a random sample of past life -- they are heavily biased.
