depends_on: Chemistry (diagenesis, mineral replacement); physics (fluid dynamics of transport, compaction); biology (decomposition, scavenging); sedimentology (burial rate, grain size, depositional environment).

name: Principle of Biotic Succession and Evolutionary Change
text: Life on Earth has continuously evolved over billions of years -- new species emerge, existing species go extinct, and the overall composition of the biosphere changes over time.
depends_on: Darwinian evolution (natural selection, variation, heredity); Faunal Succession (Principle 1); geological time (deep time concept).

name: Mass Extinction and Recovery
text: Five times in Earth's history, catastrophic events caused most species on the planet to go extinct over geologically short periods.
depends_on: Faunal Succession (Principle 1); Taphonomy (Principle 3); Biotic Succession (Principle 4); geological and astronomical forcing mechanisms.

name: Biostratigraphy (Index Fossil Principle)
text: Some fossils are especially useful for dating rocks because the organisms existed for only a short time (geologically speaking), were widespread across the globe, and are common and easy to identify.
depends_on: Faunal Succession (Principle 1); Correlation (Principle 2); Biotic Succession (Principle 4); Superposition (Geology).

name: Principle of Fossil Assemblage and Paleoecology
text: Fossil communities can tell us about ancient environments.
depends_on: Uniformitarianism (Geology); Taphonomy (Principle 3); evolutionary biology (taxonomic uniformitarianism weakens with greater phylogenetic distance).

name: Cope's Rule (Body Size Increase)
text: Over evolutionary time, many groups of animals tend to get bigger.
depends_on: Natural selection (directional and stabilizing); Biotic Succession (Principle 4); ecological competition and predation; population genetics.

name: Signor-Lipps Effect (Apparent vs. Actual Extinction)
text: Even if every species in a group went extinct at exactly the same moment (say, from an asteroid impact), their fossils would not all disappear at the same point in the rock record.
depends_on: Taphonomy (Principle 3); statistical sampling theory; the incompleteness of the fossil record; Biostratigraphy (Principle 6).

name: Lazarus Taxa and Zombie Lineages
text: Sometimes a species seems to vanish from the fossil record for millions of years and then reappear -- these are called Lazarus taxa, named after the biblical figure raised from the dead.
depends_on: Taphonomy (Principle 3); Biostratigraphy (Principle 6); phylogenetic systematics; sedimentary reworking processes.

name: Molecular Clock (Paleontological Calibration)
text: DNA accumulates mutations at a roughly steady rate over millions of years, like a molecular stopwatch.
depends_on: Molecular evolution (mutation, neutral theory); Faunal Succession (Principle 1, for calibration points); Biostratigraphy (Principle 6, dating calibration fossils); phylogenetic systematics; Bayesian statistics.

name: Dollo's Law of Irreversibility
text: Evolution does not exactly retrace its steps.
depends_on: Molecular genetics (mutation accumulation in pseudogenes, loss of regulatory elements); developmental biology (canalization, pathway dependencies); Biotic Succession (Principle 4); probability theory (the vanishing probability of reversing multiple independent changes simultaneously).

name: Law of Superposition (Steno)
text: In a stack of undisturbed sedimentary rocks, the oldest rocks are at the bottom and the youngest are at the top -- like a stack of papers, where the first page laid down is at the bottom.
depends_on: Gravity (sediments settle downward); original horizontality (sediments are deposited in approximately horizontal layers); the assumption that the sequence has not been overturned by tectonic forces.

name: The Fossil Record and the Geologic Time Scale
text: The geologic time scale is the calendar of Earth's history, built primarily from the fossil record.
depends_on: Faunal Succession (Principle 1); Law of Superposition (Principle 13); Biostratigraphy (Principle 6); radiometric dating (physics of radioactive decay).

name: Punctuated Equilibrium vs. Gradualism
text: For the first half of Earth's history, the atmosphere contained essentially no free oxygen.
depends_on: - Biotic Succession (Principle 4)

name: Molecular Paleontology and Ancient Biomolecules
text: Molecular paleontology reads the chemical messages preserved in fossils -- ancient DNA, proteins, and lipid molecules that survive long after an organism dies.
depends_on: - Taphonomy and fossil preservation (Principle 3)

name: Paleoclimate Proxies and Deep-Time Climate
text: Since we cannot directly measure the temperature, rainfall, or CO2 levels of the distant past, paleoclimatologists use "proxies" -- chemical and physical measurements in rocks, sediments, ice cores, and fossils that record past climate conditions.
depends_on: - Stratigraphy and chronostratigraphy (Principles 1, 9)

name: Snowball Earth and the Neoproterozoic Revolution
text: The Snowball Earth glaciations were not just climate events -- they may have been the trigger for the most important biological revolution in Earth's history.
depends_on: - Mass extinction and recovery (Principle 5)

name: Molecular Clock Calibration and Total Evidence Dating
text: Biostratigraphy is the primary tool for dating and correlating rocks across the globe using fossils.
depends_on: - Faunal succession (Principle P1)

name: Asymptotic Complexity (Big-O, Big-Omega, Big-Theta)
text: Asymptotic notation describes how the running time or space usage of an algorithm grows as the input size grows, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms.
depends_on: Mathematical analysis (limits, inequalities)

name: Divide-and-Conquer Paradigm
text: Break a big problem into smaller pieces of the same kind, solve each piece, and combine the results.
depends_on: Asymptotic complexity (Principle 1), recurrence relations

name: The Master Theorem
text: For any divide-and-conquer algorithm, you can determine its running time by comparing the cost of dividing/combining (n^d) with the rate at which subproblems multiply (a relative to b).
depends_on: Divide-and-conquer paradigm (Principle 2), asymptotic complexity (Principle 1)

name: Amortized Analysis
text: Some operations are occasionally expensive but usually cheap (like resizing a dynamic array).
depends_on: Asymptotic complexity (Principle 1)

name: Comparison-Based Sorting Lower Bound
text: No sorting algorithm that works by comparing pairs of elements can do better than roughly n log n comparisons.
depends_on: Decision tree model, Stirling's approximation (log(n!) = Theta(n log n)), asymptotic complexity (Principle 1)

name: Dynamic Programming
text: If a problem can be broken into overlapping smaller problems and the best solution is built from the best solutions to those smaller problems, then solve each small problem once, remember the answer, and reuse it.
depends_on: Optimal substructure, overlapping subproblems (problem properties), asymptotic complexity (Principle 1)

name: Greedy Algorithms and the Matroid Framework
text: Sometimes the best strategy is to always pick the best option available right now.
depends_on: Optimal substructure, matroid theory, asymptotic complexity (Principle 1)

name: Graph Algorithmic Foundations (BFS/DFS and Shortest Paths)
text: BFS and DFS are the two fundamental ways to explore a graph systematically.
depends_on: Graph theory, asymptotic complexity (Principle 1), greedy principle (Principle 7, for Dijkstra)

name: Randomized Algorithms
text: Sometimes flipping a coin makes algorithms simpler and faster.
depends_on: Probability theory, asymptotic complexity (Principle 1)

name: Approximation Algorithms (PTAS)
text: When a problem is NP-hard, you cannot find the optimal solution efficiently.
depends_on: NP-completeness (Principles 5-6 of Theory of Computation), asymptotic complexity (Principle 1), linear programming

name: Network Flow (Max-Flow Min-Cut)
text: How much can you ship from a source to a destination through a network of pipes with limited capacities?
depends_on: Graph theory, asymptotic complexity (Principle 1), linear programming duality

name: Hashing (Universal and Perfect)
text: Hashing maps data to fixed-size indices for fast lookup.
depends_on: Probability theory, asymptotic complexity (Principle 1)

name: NP-Hard Optimization Heuristics
text: Modern computers have multiple levels of memory, each faster but smaller.
depends_on: - Asymptotic Complexity (Principle 1)

name: Rationality and Utility Maximization
text: A rational agent is one that makes the best possible decisions according to its goals and beliefs.
depends_on: Probability theory, decision theory, expected value

name: PAC Learning (Probably Approximately Correct)
text: PAC learning asks: how many examples do you need to learn a concept well enough, with high confidence?
depends_on: Probability theory, computational complexity, VC dimension (Vapnik and Chervonenkis, 1971)

name: No Free Lunch Theorem
text: There is no single learning algorithm that works best for every problem.
depends_on: Combinatorics, averaging over all possible functions

name: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
text: A model's error comes from three sources: bias (systematic error from wrong assumptions -- underfitting), variance (sensitivity to the specific training data -- overfitting), and irreducible noise.
depends_on: Probability theory, statistical estimation theory

name: Universal Approximation Theorem
text: Neural networks can approximate any continuous function to any desired accuracy, given enough neurons.
depends_on: Real analysis (density of function classes), neural network architecture

name: Bellman Equation and Dynamic Programming in Reinforcement Learning
text: The best action to take right now depends on the immediate reward plus the best you can do from the resulting state onward.
depends_on: Markov decision processes, probability theory, contraction mapping theorem

name: Bayes Optimality and the Bayesian Framework
text: If you have prior beliefs about the world and you observe data, Bayes' theorem tells you how to update your beliefs optimally.
depends_on: Probability theory (Bayes' theorem), decision theory

name: Reward Hypothesis (Reinforcement Learning)
text: Every goal an intelligent agent might have -- from winning a chess game to driving safely to acting ethically -- can be expressed as maximizing a single number over time: cumulative reward.
depends_on: Rationality (Principle 1), Markov decision processes, utility theory

name: Markov Decision Processes
text: An MDP is the formal framework for sequential decision-making under uncertainty.
depends_on: Probability theory, dynamic programming, Bellman equation (Principle 6)

name: Attention Mechanism (Transformer Architecture)
text: The attention mechanism lets a model look at all parts of its input simultaneously and decide which parts are most relevant to each other.
depends_on: Linear algebra (matrix operations), neural networks, universal approximation (Principle 5)

name: Representer Theorem
text: When using kernel methods (like SVMs), you might worry about searching over an infinite-dimensional function space.
depends_on: Functional analysis (RKHS), optimization, regularization

name: Sample Complexity
text: How much data do you need to learn well?
depends_on: PAC learning (Principle 2), VC dimension, probability theory

name: The Turing Test (Behavioral Criterion for Intelligence)
text: Can a machine fool a human into thinking it is also human during a text conversation?
depends_on: Theory of computation (Turing machines), philosophy of mind (behaviorism vs. functionalism)

name: Backpropagation Algorithm (Gradient Descent for Neural Networks)
text: Backpropagation is the algorithm that makes training deep neural networks practical.
depends_on: Calculus (chain rule), linear algebra, Universal Approximation Theorem (Principle 5)

name: Scaling Laws and Emergent Capabilities
text: Classical statistics says overly complex models overfit.
depends_on: - Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Principle 3)

name: Existence of One-Way Functions
text: A one-way function is easy to compute but practically impossible to reverse.
depends_on: Computational complexity (P, NP), notion of negligible functions

name: Computational Hardness Assumptions
text: Specific cryptographic systems assume specific math problems are hard.
depends_on: One-way functions (Principle 1), computational complexity theory

name: Kerckhoffs's Principle
text: The enemy knows the system.
depends_on: Adversarial thinking, threat modeling

name: Shannon's Perfect Secrecy and the One-Time Pad
text: If you encrypt a message with a truly random key that is as long as the message itself and never reused, the encrypted message is mathematically unbreakable -- not just computationally hard, but information-theoretically impossible to crack, even with unlimited computing power.
depends_on: Information theory (Shannon entropy), probability theory

name: Public-Key Cryptography and Trapdoor Functions
text: Public-key cryptography lets anyone encrypt a message using a public key, but only the holder of the corresponding private key can decrypt it.
depends_on: One-way functions (Principle 1), trapdoor functions, computational hardness (Principle 2)

name: Semantic Security and Indistinguishability
text: An encryption scheme is truly secure if the ciphertext leaks absolutely no useful information about the message to any efficient adversary.
depends_on: Computational hardness (Principle 2), one-way functions (Principle 1), probability theory

name: Cryptographic Hash Functions and the Random Oracle Model
text: A cryptographic hash function produces a fixed-size "fingerprint" of any input.
depends_on: One-way functions (Principle 1), computational hardness (Principle 2)

name: Zero-Knowledge Proofs
text: A zero-knowledge proof lets you prove you know a secret without revealing the secret itself.
depends_on: One-way functions (Principle 1), interactive proofs, computational hardness (Principle 2)

name: Homomorphic Encryption Foundations
text: Homomorphic encryption lets you compute on encrypted data without ever decrypting it.
depends_on: Computational hardness (Principle 2), lattice-based cryptography (Principle 10), semantic security (Principle 6)

name: Lattice-Based Cryptography
text: Lattice-based cryptography uses the geometry of high-dimensional grids (lattices) as its foundation.
depends_on: Computational hardness (Principle 2), linear algebra, geometry of numbers

name: Digital Signatures (EUF-CMA Security)
text: A digital signature proves that a message came from the claimed sender and has not been tampered with.
depends_on: One-way functions (Principle 1), public-key cryptography (Principle 5), hash functions (Principle 7)

name: Random Oracle Model
text: Imagine verifying a million-transaction computation in milliseconds with a tiny proof.
depends_on: - Zero-Knowledge Proofs (Principle 8)

name: Shannon Entropy
text: Entropy measures the average amount of "surprise" or uncertainty in a random variable.
depends_on: Probability theory (probability distributions, expectation)

name: Source Coding Theorem (Shannon's First Theorem)
text: You can compress data down to its entropy rate but no further.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), typicality (asymptotic equipartition property)

name: Channel Capacity and the Noisy Channel Coding Theorem (Shannon's Second Theorem)
text: Every communication channel has a maximum rate (capacity) at which information can be transmitted reliably.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), mutual information (Principle 4)

name: Mutual Information
text: Mutual information measures how much knowing one variable tells you about another.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), conditional entropy

name: Data Processing Inequality
text: Processing data can only destroy information, never create it.
depends_on: Mutual information (Principle 4), Markov chains

name: Kraft-McMillan Inequality and Prefix-Free Codes
text: There is a precise constraint on how short codewords can be: you cannot have too many short codewords.
depends_on: Combinatorics, binary trees

name: Rate-Distortion Theory
text: If you are willing to tolerate some loss (distortion) in your representation, you can compress data below the entropy.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), mutual information (Principle 4), convex optimization

name: Kolmogorov Complexity
text: Kolmogorov complexity measures the intrinsic information content of a specific string -- the length of the shortest computer program that generates it.
depends_on: Theory of computation (Turing machines, uncomputability), probability theory

name: Channel Coding Converse
text: Shannon showed you can communicate reliably below capacity.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), mutual information (Principle 4), Fano's inequality

name: Entropy Rate of Stochastic Processes
text: Real data has memory: English text, DNA, music -- each symbol depends on what came before.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), Markov chains, ergodic theory

name: Fisher Information Link
text: Fisher information measures how much a measurement tells you about an unknown parameter -- how "informative" data is for estimation.
depends_on: Probability theory, statistical estimation, Shannon entropy (Principle 1)

name: Differential Entropy
text: Differential entropy extends Shannon's information measure from discrete to continuous random variables.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), probability theory, calculus

name: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
text: KL divergence measures how different one probability distribution is from another.
depends_on: Shannon entropy (Principle 1), probability theory, logarithmic inequalities

name: Error-Correcting Codes (Hamming Distance and Capacity-Achieving Codes)
text: When two parties want to simulate a correlated conversation without direct communication, how much shared randomness do they need?
depends_on: - Mutual Information (Principle 4)

name: Lambda Calculus
text: Lambda calculus is the simplest possible programming language: it has only variables, function definitions (lambda), and function application.
depends_on: Variable binding, substitution (mathematical logic)

name: Church-Rosser Theorem (Confluence)
text: No matter what order you simplify a lambda calculus expression, if you reach a final answer, it will be the same answer.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), beta-reduction

name: Curry-Howard Correspondence
text: Programs are proofs.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), type theory (Principle 4), propositional and predicate logic

name: Type Theory and Type Systems
text: Types classify programs by what kind of value they produce.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), mathematical logic

name: Denotational Semantics
text: Denotational semantics gives programs a mathematical meaning by mapping them to mathematical objects (functions, sets, etc.).
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), domain theory (order theory, topology), category theory

name: Operational Semantics and Abstract Machines
text: Operational semantics describes what a program does step by step, like an instruction manual.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), type theory (Principle 4)

name: Strong Normalization of Typed Lambda Calculi
text: In sufficiently restricted typed systems, every computation terminates.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), type theory (Principle 4), Curry-Howard correspondence (Principle 3)

name: Hindley-Milner Type Inference
text: In languages like ML, OCaml, and Haskell, you almost never have to write type annotations -- the compiler figures out the types for you.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), type theory (Principle 4), unification

name: Monads and Effects
text: Pure functional programs have no side effects -- they are just mathematical functions.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), category theory, denotational semantics (Principle 5)

name: Garbage Collection Theory
text: Garbage collection frees programmers from manually managing memory, preventing entire classes of bugs (use-after-free, double-free, memory leaks).
depends_on: Graph theory (reachability), lambda calculus (Principle 1), operational semantics (Principle 6)

name: Continuation-Passing Style (CPS)
text: Normally, when a function finishes, it returns a value to whoever called it.
depends_on: Lambda calculus (Principle 1), operational semantics (Principle 6)

name: Rice's Theorem Applied to Compilers
text: Can a type system capture exactly which programs terminate?
depends_on: - Type Theory (Principle 4)

name: Amdahl's Law
text: The speedup from parallelism is limited by the part of the work that cannot be parallelized.
depends_on: Basic algebra, the decomposition of work into serial and parallel portions

name: CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem)
text: In a distributed system, when the network fails (and it will), you must choose between giving every user the correct answer (consistency) and giving every user some answer (availability).
depends_on: Distributed system model, asynchronous communication

name: FLP Impossibility (Consensus in Asynchronous Systems)
text: In a network where messages can be delayed arbitrarily and computers can crash, there is no foolproof way for all working computers to agree on something.
depends_on: Asynchronous system model, crash failure model

name: End-to-End Principle
text: Do not build complex features into the middle of the network; put intelligence at the edges.
depends_on: Layered abstraction (Principle 5), modularity in system design

name: Layered Abstraction and Modularity
text: Build complex systems as layers, like a stack of pancakes.
depends_on: Abstraction and encapsulation principles from software engineering

name: Little's Law and Queuing Theory
text: In any stable queue (network routers, CPU task schedulers, web servers), the average number of things waiting equals the arrival rate times the average wait.
depends_on: Probability theory, stochastic processes

name: State Machine Replication and Consensus
text: To make a service reliable, run copies of it on multiple machines and make sure they all do the same thing in the same order.
depends_on: FLP impossibility (Principle 3), partial synchrony model, distributed system model

name: Lamport Clocks and Logical Time
text: In a distributed system, you cannot trust wall clocks -- they drift and disagree.
depends_on: Distributed system model, partial order theory

name: Two Generals Problem
text: If two parties communicate over an unreliable channel, they can never be absolutely certain that they agree.
depends_on: Communication theory, common knowledge (epistemic logic)

name: Consistent Hashing
text: When servers are added to or removed from a distributed system, you do not want to reshuffle all data.
depends_on: Hashing, distributed system model

name: MapReduce Model
text: MapReduce lets you process massive data sets by breaking the work into two simple steps: Map (process each piece of data independently) and Reduce (combine results by key).
depends_on: Distributed system model, functional programming (map and fold), fault tolerance

name: Queuing Theory (M/M/1 Model)
text: The M/M/1 queue is the simplest model of a server handling requests.
depends_on: Probability theory (Poisson processes, exponential distributions), Little's Law (Principle 6)

name: ACID Properties (Transaction Processing)
text: ACID properties are the guarantees that make databases reliable.
depends_on: Concurrency control theory, write-ahead logging, CAP Theorem (Principle 2, for understanding relaxations in distributed settings)

name: Cache Hierarchy and the Locality Principle
text: CXL lets computers access remote memory almost as easily as local memory, with full hardware cache coherence.
depends_on: - Cache Hierarchy and Locality (Principle 14)

name: Turing Machine as the Standard Model of Computation
text: A Turing machine is an idealized computer with an infinite tape, a read/write head, and a finite set of rules.
depends_on: Finite automata theory, mathematical logic

name: Undecidability of the Halting Problem
text: There is no general algorithm that can determine, for every possible program and input, whether that program will eventually stop or run forever.
depends_on: Turing machine model (Principle 2), diagonalization argument

name: Rice's Theorem
text: You cannot write a general algorithm to check any non-trivial property about what a program computes.
depends_on: Undecidability of the halting problem (Principle 3), Turing machine model (Principle 2)

name: Nondeterminism and Complexity Classes P and NP
text: P contains problems solvable quickly.
depends_on: Turing machine model (Principle 2), Church-Turing thesis (Principle 1)

name: Cook-Levin Theorem (NP-Completeness of SAT)
text: The problem of determining whether a Boolean formula can be made true by some assignment of its variables is the "hardest" problem in NP -- every other NP problem can be efficiently transformed into an instance of SAT.
depends_on: Definition of NP (Principle 5), polynomial-time reductions, Turing machine model (Principle 2)

name: Time and Space Hierarchy Theorems
text: Given strictly more time or space, a Turing machine can solve strictly more problems.
depends_on: Turing machine model (Principle 2), diagonalization arguments

name: Recursion Theorem (Kleene's Fixed-Point Theorem)
text: There always exists a program that can refer to its own description and still function correctly.
depends_on: Turing machine model (Principle 2), universal computation

name: Reduction (Many-One and Turing)
text: A reduction shows that one problem is at most as hard as another by transforming instances of the first into instances of the second.
depends_on: Turing machine model (Principle 2), halting problem (Principle 3)

name: Space Complexity (PSPACE, L)
text: Space complexity measures how much memory an algorithm needs.
depends_on: Turing machine model (Principle 2), complexity classes (Principle 5)

name: Randomized Computation (BPP)
text: Randomized algorithms flip coins while computing.
depends_on: Turing machine model (Principle 2), probability theory, complexity classes (Principle 5)

name: Interactive Proofs (IP = PSPACE)
text: Computing the determinant of a matrix is easy (Gaussian elimination), but computing the permanent -- which looks almost identical but uses addition instead of subtraction -- appears to be exponentially harder.
depends_on: - P and NP Complexity Classes (Principle 5)

name: Pseudorandomness and Derandomization
text: Can we always replace randomness with determinism without losing efficiency?
depends_on: - Randomized computation / BPP (Principle 11)

name: Quantum Computational Complexity (QMA, Quantum PCP, NLTS)
text: When you compute a polynomial (like the determinant or permanent of a matrix), how many arithmetic operations do you need?
depends_on: - Algebraic Complexity VP vs VNP (Principle 26)

name: Identity Laws
text: A category is a collection of objects and arrows (morphisms) between them, where arrows can be composed and there is a "do nothing" arrow for each object.
depends_on: A foundational framework (set theory or an alternative like type theory).

name: Functors (Structure-Preserving Maps Between Categories)
text: A functor is a "translation" between categories that preserves the structure of morphisms and composition.
depends_on: Definition of category.

name: Natural Transformations
text: A natural transformation is a "systematic way" of transforming one functor into another that respects the structure of all morphisms.
depends_on: Categories, functors.

name: The Yoneda Lemma
text: An object is completely determined by how other objects map into it.
depends_on: Categories, functors, natural transformations.

name: Universal Properties
text: A universal property defines an object by what it does (its relationship to everything else) rather than what it is (its internal construction).
depends_on: Categories, morphisms.

name: Adjoint Functors
text: Two functors are adjoint if they are "mirror images" of each other in a precise sense — each translates problems in one category to equivalent problems in the other.
depends_on: Categories, functors, natural transformations.

name: Limits and Colimits
text: Limits are generalized products/intersections; colimits are generalized sums/unions.
depends_on: Categories, universal properties.

name: Monad Theory
text: A monad captures the idea of a "computational context" or "algebraic theory" within category theory.
depends_on: Categories, functors, natural transformations, adjoint functors.

name: Cartesian Closed Categories
text: A Cartesian closed category is one where you can form products (pairs) and function spaces internally.
depends_on: Categories, products, adjoint functors.

name: Topos Theory
text: A topos is a category that behaves like a "generalized universe of sets." It has enough structure to do set-theoretic constructions (products, function spaces, power sets) internally.
depends_on: Categories, limits, Cartesian closed categories, subobject classifiers.

name: Kan Extensions
text: A Kan extension is the universal way to "extend" or "push forward" a functor along another functor.
depends_on: Categories, functors, natural transformations, limits and colimits.

name: Adjoint Functor Theorem (Freyd)
text: The Adjoint Functor Theorem tells you exactly when a "forgetful" functor has a corresponding "free" construction.
depends_on: - Adjoint functors (Principle 4)

name: Beck's Monadicity Theorem
text: Beck's theorem tells you when a category of "structured objects" is really just the category of algebras for a monad.
depends_on: - Monad theory (Principle 6)

name: Abelian Categories and Homological Algebra
text: Abelian categories are the abstract setting for homological algebra — the study of exact sequences, chain complexes, and derived functors.
depends_on: - Category axioms, additive structure

name: Enriched and Higher Category Theory
text: Ordinary categories only have objects and morphisms.
depends_on: - Category axioms, monoidal categories

name: Derived Categories and Homological Methods
text: Derived categories provide a framework where the "true" content of homological algebra lives.
depends_on: - Abelian categories (Principle 10)

name: Sheaf Theory
text: A sheaf tracks data that is defined locally and can be consistently glued together.
depends_on: - Topological space axioms (or Grothendieck topologies)

name: Infinity Categories (Quasicategories)
text: Infinity categories are categories where composition of morphisms is defined only "up to homotopy," and these homotopies are themselves related by higher homotopies, and so on.
depends_on: - Category theory (all previous principles)

name: Operads and Algebraic Structures
text: Operads are a way to organize and study algebraic structures (associative, commutative, Lie, etc.) by focusing on the abstract patterns of operations and their compositions, separated from any particular algebraic object.
depends_on: - Category axioms, monoidal categories

name: Homotopy Limits and Colimits
text: Ordinary limits and colimits are fragile: they can change drastically if you replace an object with a weakly equivalent one.
depends_on: - Limits and colimits (Principle 5)

name: Algebraic K-Theory
text: Algebraic K-theory is a vast generalization of the idea of dimension (K_0) and determinant (K_1) to a whole hierarchy of invariants.
depends_on: - Category theory (functors, exact categories)

name: Motivic Homotopy Theory (A^1-Homotopy)
text: In topology, we study spaces by continuously deforming them (homotopy theory).
depends_on: - Category theory (model categories, ∞-categories)

name: Applied Category Theory (Compositional Modeling)
text: Applied category theory takes the abstract language of categories and uses it to model real-world systems: electrical circuits, chemical reaction networks, databases, programming languages, and even natural language.
depends_on: - Category axioms, monoidal categories

name: Topos Theory and Elementary Topoi
text: A topos is a mathematical universe that behaves enough like the category of sets to do mathematics inside it, but where the logic can differ from classical logic.
depends_on: - Category axioms, limits and colimits (Principles 1-5)

name: Enriched Category Theory and Infinity-Categories
text: An operad is a mathematical structure that encodes the rules of composition — how operations with multiple inputs can be plugged together.
depends_on: - Monoidal Categories (Principle 7)

name: Completeness
text: A rational agent can always compare any two options — no pair is "incomparable."

name: Transitivity
text: Preferences are consistent — no cycles.

name: Continuity (Archimedean Property)
text: There are no "infinitely good" or "infinitely bad" outcomes — you can always find a mixture that matches any intermediate preference.

name: Independence
text: Mixing two options with a common third option does not change the preference ordering between the first two.
depends_on: Probability theory, preference relations.

name: The Principle of Dominance
text: If one option is better in some scenarios and no worse in any, choose it.
depends_on: Preference ordering over outcomes.

name: Bayes' Rule as a Decision Principle
text: When you observe new evidence, update your probabilities using Bayes' theorem, then make decisions based on your updated beliefs.
depends_on: Savage's axioms, Kolmogorov axioms, Bayes' theorem.

name: Minimax and Maximin (Decision Under Ignorance)
text: Prepare for the worst case.
depends_on: Preference ordering, but NOT probability (this is for decision under complete uncertainty).

name: Prospect Theory (Kahneman-Tversky)
text: People do not evaluate outcomes in absolute terms but relative to a reference point (usually the status quo).
depends_on: VNM utility axioms (as the baseline being modified), reference dependence, probability weighting.

name: Minimax Regret (Savage)
text: Instead of minimizing the worst outcome, minimize the worst "I wish I had done something else" feeling.
depends_on: Preference ordering, state space, the concept of opportunity cost.

name: Ellsberg Paradox and Ambiguity Aversion
text: People dislike not knowing the odds, above and beyond disliking risk itself.
depends_on: Savage's axioms (as the violated baseline), the distinction between risk and uncertainty.

name: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem (Social Choice)
text: There is no perfect way to aggregate individual preferences into a group decision.
depends_on: Complete and transitive individual preferences, three or more alternatives.

name: Bayesian Decision Theory
text: A Bayesian decision-maker starts with beliefs about the world, updates those beliefs as evidence arrives using Bayes' theorem, and then chooses the action that maximizes expected utility under the updated beliefs.
depends_on: Savage's axioms, Bayes' theorem, prior distributions.

name: Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)
text: Real decisions often involve trade-offs between multiple goals that cannot be reduced to a single number.
depends_on: Utility theory, preference ordering over multiple attributes.

name: Regret Theory
text: Regret theory says people care not just about what they get, but about what they could have gotten if they had chosen differently.
depends_on: - Utility theory (as baseline)

name: Value of Information
text: How much is it worth to learn something before you decide?
depends_on: - Bayesian decision theory (Principle 8)

name: Causal Decision Theory vs Evidential Decision Theory
text: Should you make decisions based on what your action would cause to happen (causal decision theory), or based on what your action is evidence for (evidential decision theory)?
depends_on: - Expected utility theory (Axiom System 1)

name: Proper Scoring Rules and Calibration
text: A proper scoring rule rewards honest probability assessments: if you truly believe rain is 70% likely, a proper scoring rule makes it optimal for you to report 70%, not some other number.
depends_on: - Probability theory, Bayesian framework

name: Jeffrey's Rule of Probability Kinematics
text: Standard Bayesian updating assumes you learn something for certain: "event E occurred." But real evidence is often uncertain -- you might become more confident in E without being certain.
depends_on: - Kolmogorov axioms, Bayes' theorem

name: Imprecise Probabilities and Robust Bayesianism
text: Standard probability theory assumes you have a single precise probability for every event.
depends_on: - Probability theory (Kolmogorov axioms)

name: Mean Field Games
text: When millions of agents make strategic decisions simultaneously (commuters choosing routes, traders choosing portfolios, firms setting prices), tracking every individual interaction is impossible.
depends_on: - Game theory (Nash equilibrium)

name: Revenue Equivalence Theorem
text: The revenue equivalence theorem is one of the most surprising results in economics: under standard assumptions (risk-neutral bidders, independent private values, same distribution), all "reasonable" auction formats generate exactly the same expected revenue for the seller.
depends_on: - Expected utility theory (Axiom System 1)

name: The Secretary Problem (Optimal Stopping)
text: You are interviewing candidates one by one, and after each interview you must decide: hire this person or move on.
depends_on: - Probability theory, order statistics

name: Info-Gap Decision Theory
text: When you face deep uncertainty -- you do not even know the probabilities, and your estimates could be wildly wrong -- standard expected utility theory has nothing to stand on.
depends_on: - Decision theory fundamentals (utility, preferences)

name: Algorithmic Decision Theory
text: Classical decision theory assumes the decision-maker has unlimited computational power to evaluate all options and compute optimal strategies.
depends_on: - Expected utility theory (Axiom System 1)

name: Logical Decision Theory and Functional Decision Theory
text: Standard decision theory comes in two flavors: causal (act based on what your actions cause) and evidential (act based on what your actions tell you about the world).
depends_on: - Expected utility theory (Axiom System 1)

name: Rational Inattention (Sims)
text: Standard Bayesian reasoning requires a precise probability for every event.
depends_on: - Bayesian Decision Theory (Principle 8)

name: Rational Players
text: Players choose strategies to get the best outcome for themselves, taking into account what others might do.
depends_on: Decision theory (VNM utility axioms).

name: Common Knowledge of the Game
text: Everyone knows the rules, everyone knows that everyone knows the rules, etc.
depends_on: Logic, epistemic frameworks.

name: The Minimax Theorem (Von Neumann)
text: In a zero-sum game (one player's gain is the other's loss), there is a well-defined "fair outcome" — both players can guarantee at least this value using their optimal strategies.
depends_on: Zero-sum game structure, mixed strategies (randomization), compactness/convexity.

name: Nash Equilibrium (Existence)
text: In any finite game, there is at least one stable outcome where no one wants to change their behavior, given what everyone else is doing.
depends_on: Utility theory, Brouwer fixed-point theorem, mixed strategy spaces.

name: Dominant Strategy Equilibrium
text: If one option is always best no matter what others do, rational players will choose it.
depends_on: Rationality assumption.

name: The Folk Theorem (Repeated Games)
text: If a game is played repeatedly and players are patient enough, cooperation can emerge — players can punish defectors in future rounds.
depends_on: Nash equilibrium, repeated game structure, patience (discount factor).

name: Mechanism Design (Reverse Game Theory)
text: Instead of analyzing a given game, design the game so that rational players end up doing what you want.
depends_on: Nash equilibrium, dominant strategies, Bayesian games.

name: Prisoner's Dilemma
text: Two players would both be better off cooperating, but each has a selfish incentive to defect.
depends_on: Dominant strategy equilibrium, Nash equilibrium.

name: Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS)
text: An evolutionarily stable strategy is one that, once adopted by a population, cannot be invaded by any alternative strategy.
depends_on: Nash equilibrium, population dynamics, natural selection.

name: Shapley Value
text: When a group of players cooperate and create value together, how should they divide the payoff fairly?
depends_on: Cooperative game theory, coalition structure.

name: Auction Theory
text: How should you sell something to get the best price?
depends_on: Bayesian games, mechanism design, Nash equilibrium.

name: Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text: In a game played in stages, a subgame perfect equilibrium requires that players' strategies make sense not just overall, but at every possible point in the game.
depends_on: Nash equilibrium, extensive-form games, backward induction.

name: Correlated Equilibrium (Aumann)
text: In a correlated equilibrium, players follow recommendations from a neutral mediator, and no one wants to deviate from the recommendation given what they know.
depends_on: - Nash equilibrium, probability distributions

name: The Revelation Principle
text: When designing rules for strategic agents with private information, you never need to consider complicated indirect mechanisms.
depends_on: - Bayesian games, mechanism design (Principle 3)

name: Bayesian Games (Games of Incomplete Information)
text: In a Bayesian game, players have private information (their "type") that others don't know.
depends_on: - Nash equilibrium, Bayesian updating

name: The Nash Bargaining Solution
text: When two parties negotiate, the Nash bargaining solution predicts the outcome: the agreement that maximizes the product of their gains relative to their fallback positions.
depends_on: - Utility theory, Pareto efficiency

name: Replicator Dynamics (Evolutionary Game Theory)
text: The replicator equation models natural selection in a game-theoretic setting: strategies that perform above average grow in frequency, while strategies below average decline.
depends_on: - Evolutionarily stable strategy (Principle 5)

name: Price of Anarchy
text: The price of anarchy measures how much society loses when selfish individuals reach equilibrium instead of cooperating optimally.
depends_on: - Nash equilibrium, social welfare functions

name: Algorithmic Game Theory and Computational Complexity of Equilibria
text: Finding a Nash equilibrium, even though one always exists, is computationally hard -- not in the standard NP-complete sense, but in its own complexity class (PPAD).
depends_on: - Nash equilibrium existence (Theorem 2)

name: The Sprague-Grundy Theorem
text: The Sprague-Grundy theorem provides a complete theory for "who wins" in a vast class of combinatorial games (Nim, Chomp, many position games).
depends_on: - Combinatorial game theory

name: Network Formation Games
text: Network formation games ask: when self-interested agents choose who to connect with, what network structures emerge?
depends_on: - Nash equilibrium, game theory

name: Epistemic Game Theory (Interactive Epistemology)
text: Standard game theory assumes players play Nash equilibria, but why?
depends_on: - Nash equilibrium (Theorem 2)

name: Potential Games and Congestion Games
text: Potential games are games where all players' incentives can be captured by a single function (the potential).
depends_on: - Nash equilibrium (Theorem 2)

name: Algorithmic Mechanism Design
text: Traditional mechanism design assumes everyone can compute optimal strategies instantly.
depends_on: - Mechanism design (Principle 3)

name: Cooperative Game Theory and the Shapley Value
text: In an infinite game where two players take turns choosing numbers forever, does one of them always have a winning strategy?
depends_on: - Nash Equilibrium (Theorem 2)

name: Soundness of First-Order Logic
text: Anything you can prove is actually true (in every possible interpretation).
depends_on: The definition of proof (syntactic) and truth in a model (semantic).

name: Completeness of First-Order Logic (Gödel, 1929)
text: Anything that is true in all possible interpretations can be formally proved.
depends_on: FOL axioms and rules, model theory.

name: Compactness Theorem
text: If every finite piece of a theory is consistent, then the whole (possibly infinite) theory is consistent.
depends_on: Completeness theorem (syntactic proof) or ultraproducts (semantic proof).

name: The Deduction Theorem
text: Proving ψ from the assumption φ is the same as proving "if φ then ψ" unconditionally.
depends_on: Hilbert-style axioms for propositional/FOL.

name: Tarski's Undefinability of Truth
text: No language can contain its own truth predicate without falling into paradox (the Liar paradox formalized).
depends_on: Self-reference, diagonal argument.

name: Gentzen's Cut-Elimination Theorem
text: You never need to introduce intermediate results (lemmas) to prove something — you can always find a direct proof.
depends_on: Sequent calculus (Gentzen's proof system).

name: Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem
text: First-order logic cannot pin down the size of infinite structures.
depends_on: Completeness theorem, compactness.

name: Model-Theoretic Semantics (Tarski)
text: Formal sentences are meaningless strings until you provide an interpretation.
depends_on: First-order logic, set theory.

name: Craig's Interpolation Theorem
text: If a conclusion follows from a premise, there is an intermediate statement using only the shared vocabulary that connects them.
depends_on: Cut-elimination (the proof goes through Gentzen's sequent calculus).

name: The Completeness of Propositional Logic (Post)
text: Propositional logic has a perfect proof system AND you can always check in finite time whether a formula is valid.
depends_on: Truth tables, propositional axiom system.

name: Herbrand's Theorem
text: To prove a first-order statement, you only need to find finitely many concrete witnesses.
depends_on: - First-order logic, Skolemization

name: Beth's Definability Theorem
text: If a concept is uniquely determined by a theory, then it can be explicitly written down as a formula within that theory.
depends_on: - Craig's interpolation theorem (Principle 5)

name: Strong Normalization and the Curry-Howard Correspondence
text: In well-typed programs, every computation terminates.
depends_on: - Typed lambda calculus

name: Lindström's Theorem
text: First-order logic occupies a unique sweet spot: it is the most expressive logic that still has well-behaved model-theoretic properties (compactness and Löwenheim-Skolem).
depends_on: - Compactness theorem (Theorem 3)

name: Church-Turing Undecidability of First-Order Logic
text: While propositional logic is decidable (truth tables work), first-order logic is not.
depends_on: - First-order logic, recursion theory

name: Ordinal Analysis and Proof-Theoretic Strength
text: Proof-theoretic ordinal analysis measures the "logical strength" of mathematical theories using ordinal numbers.
depends_on: - Godel's incompleteness theorems

name: Reverse Mathematics
text: Reverse mathematics asks: given a mathematical theorem, what are the weakest axioms needed to prove it?
depends_on: - Second-order arithmetic, computability theory

name: Descriptive Complexity
text: Descriptive complexity shows that computational complexity is really about logical expressiveness.
depends_on: - First-order and second-order logic

name: Ehrenfeucht-Fraisse Games
text: EF games turn the abstract question "can these two structures be distinguished by a first-order sentence?" into a concrete game.
depends_on: - First-order logic (Axiom System 2)

name: Proof Mining
text: Proof mining takes existing mathematical proofs -- even those using non-constructive methods like the axiom of choice -- and systematically extracts hidden quantitative information.
depends_on: - Proof theory / ordinal analysis (Principle 7)

name: Realizability and the Dialectica Interpretation
text: Realizability assigns a computer program to every constructive proof: a proof of "A implies B" becomes a program that transforms evidence for A into evidence for B, and a proof of "there exists x with property P" becomes a program that computes such an x.
depends_on: - Recursion theory, partial recursive functions

name: Proof Complexity
text: Proof complexity asks: how long must a proof be?
depends_on: - Propositional logic (Axiom System 1)

name: Homotopy Type Theory (Logical Foundations)
text: Standard logic treats equality as simple: either two things are equal or they are not.
depends_on: - Martin-Lof type theory

name: Proof Complexity and the NP vs coNP Problem
text: How long does a mathematical proof need to be?
depends_on: - Godel's Incompleteness (Theorem 4)

name: The Cumulative Hierarchy
text: Sets are built layer by layer — starting from nothing (∅), each layer contains all possible subsets of the previous layer.
depends_on: ZFC axioms (especially Power Set, Union, Foundation, Infinity).

name: Cantor's Theorem and the Hierarchy of Infinities
text: The set of all subsets of any set is strictly larger than the original set.
depends_on: Power Set axiom, the concept of cardinality.

name: The Continuum Hypothesis (CH) — Independent of ZFC
text: The Continuum Hypothesis says there is no "size of infinity" between the integers and the real numbers.
depends_on: ZFC axioms, forcing (Cohen), inner models (Gödel).

name: Ordinal Numbers and Transfinite Induction
text: Ordinals extend counting beyond the finite: 0, 1, 2, ..., then ω (the first infinite ordinal), then ω+1, and so on.
depends_on: ZFC (Infinity, Replacement, Foundation).

name: Cardinal Arithmetic
text: Cardinal numbers measure "how many." Infinite arithmetic is strange: ℵ₀ + ℵ₀ = ℵ₀ (countable + countable = countable), but the power set always jumps up.
depends_on: ZFC axioms, Axiom of Choice (for well-ordering and cardinal comparison).

name: Forcing (Cohen's Method)
text: Forcing is a method for building new mathematical universes.
depends_on: ZFC, model theory, partial orders.

name: The Axiom of Choice and Its Equivalents
text: Given any collection of non-empty bins, you can always pick one item from each bin simultaneously, even if there are infinitely many bins and no rule for choosing.
depends_on: ZF axioms (AC is independent of them).

name: The Constructible Universe (Gödel's L)
text: Gödel built a "minimal" universe of sets — the constructible universe — where only sets that can be explicitly defined from earlier sets are allowed.
depends_on: ZF axioms, ordinals, transfinite recursion.

name: Large Cardinal Axioms
text: "Large cardinals" are extremely large infinities whose existence cannot be proved from the standard axioms but can be assumed as additional axioms.
depends_on: ZFC, inner models, elementary embeddings.

name: Descriptive Set Theory
text: Descriptive set theory studies "well-behaved" subsets of the real numbers and related spaces — sets that can be described by mathematical formulas.
depends_on: ZFC, large cardinal axioms, topology of Polish spaces.

name: Infinite Combinatorics
text: Infinite combinatorics asks: in any large enough collection, must there be large structured subsets?
depends_on: ZFC, cardinal arithmetic, Ramsey theory.

name: Axiom of Determinacy and Its Consequences
text: If you assume that every game on the natural numbers is determined (one player must have a winning strategy), then the theory of the real numbers becomes beautifully regular: every set of reals is measurable, there are no "pathological" sets.
depends_on: ZF (contradicts AC in full), large cardinal axioms (for PD).

name: Martin's Axiom and Forcing Axioms
text: Forcing axioms are strong principles that say "generic objects exist" without actually doing forcing constructions.
depends_on: - ZFC, forcing (Principle 6)

name: Inner Model Theory
text: Inner model theory builds precise, canonical universes of sets that accommodate large cardinals in the most controlled way possible.
depends_on: - ZFC, constructible universe (Principle 8)

name: Singular Cardinal Combinatorics (PCF Theory)
text: While cardinal arithmetic at regular cardinals is largely undetermined by ZFC (the continuum can be almost anything), at singular cardinals there are deep structural constraints.
depends_on: - ZFC, cardinal arithmetic (Principle 5)

name: Generic Absoluteness and Woodin's Program
text: Generic absoluteness means that certain mathematical truths about the real numbers cannot be changed by any forcing construction -- they are settled once and for all by large cardinal axioms.
depends_on: - Large cardinal axioms (Principle 9), forcing (Principle 6)

name: Solovay's Model
text: Solovay showed that it is consistent with a reasonable form of set theory for every set of real numbers to be measurable -- no pathological non-measurable sets exist.
depends_on: - ZFC, forcing (Principle 6)

name: Square Principles and Combinatorial Set Theory
text: The square principle provides a globally coherent way to "climb" through the ordinals below kappa^+.
depends_on: - ZFC, constructible universe (Principle 8)

name: Easton's Theorem
text: At regular cardinals, the axioms of set theory place almost no constraints on the size of power sets: 2^{ℵ₀} could be ℵ₁, ℵ₂, ℵ_{ω₁}, or almost any cardinal you like, and similarly for all regular cardinals simultaneously.
depends_on: - Forcing (Principle 6)

name: Cardinal Characteristics of the Continuum
text: Even if we cannot determine the exact value of 2^{ℵ₀}, we can define many natural "sizes" related to combinatorial properties of the real numbers.
depends_on: - ZFC, cardinal arithmetic (Principle 5)

name: Woodin's HOD Conjecture and V = Ultimate L
text: Woodin's program aims to resolve the fundamental underdetermination of set theory (the fact that ZFC cannot settle questions like the Continuum Hypothesis).
depends_on: - Constructible universe (Principle 8), inner model theory (Principle 14)

name: The Axiom of Projective Determinacy (PD)
text: Projective sets are those definable from open sets by taking complements, projections, and countable unions/intersections -- a natural hierarchy of "definable" sets of real numbers.
depends_on: - Descriptive set theory (Principle 10)

name: Homotopy Type Theory and Univalent Foundations
text: Homotopy Type Theory provides an alternative foundation for mathematics where the basic objects are not sets but "shapes" (homotopy types).
depends_on: - Martin-Lof type theory

name: Generic Absoluteness and Forcing Axioms
text: Forcing axioms say: "generic objects that could exist (in some forcing extension) already exist in the actual universe." These are the strongest axioms consistent with ZFC (short of large cardinals) and they settle many questions that ZFC alone cannot.
depends_on: - Forcing and independence (Principle 6)

name: Inner Model Theory and Core Models
text: Many mathematical statements are independent of the standard axioms — they can be made true or false by choosing different models.
depends_on: - Forcing (Principle 6)

name: A System Is More Than the Sum of Its Parts (Emergence)
text: When parts interact, the whole exhibits properties that none of the parts possess individually.
depends_on: The concept of a system with interacting components.

name: Feedback
text: Negative feedback = thermostat (corrects deviations).
depends_on: Causal loops, time delays, signal theory.

name: Homeostasis and Equilibrium
text: Stable systems resist change and return to their normal state after being disturbed.
depends_on: Feedback (especially negative feedback), dynamical systems theory.

name: The Principle of Requisite Variety (Ashby's Law)
text: Only variety can absorb variety.
depends_on: Information theory (variety as a measure of complexity), feedback.

name: Self-Organization
text: Order can arise from disorder without a central controller — pattern emerges from the interactions of components following simple local rules.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (open systems, far from equilibrium), nonlinear dynamics, feedback.

name: Hierarchy and Modularity
text: Complex systems are built in layers, like nested boxes.
depends_on: Emergence, modularity.

name: Entropy, Dissipative Structures, and Open Systems
text: Left alone, things fall apart (entropy increases).
depends_on: Thermodynamics (second law, entropy), open system dynamics, nonlinear dynamics.

name: Network Theory
text: Many real-world systems — social networks, the internet, neural networks, ecosystems — are organized as networks with specific structural properties.
depends_on: Graph theory, probability theory, statistical mechanics.

name: Autopoiesis
text: A living cell creates its own membrane and internal machinery, which in turn keep the cell alive to create more membrane and machinery.
depends_on: Emergence (Principle 1), operational closure, self-organization (Principle 5).

name: Attractors and Stability in Dynamical Systems
text: Complex systems tend to settle into characteristic patterns of behavior — called attractors.
depends_on: Differential equations, feedback, nonlinear dynamics.

name: Cybernetics and Control Theory
text: Cybernetics studies how systems regulate themselves through feedback and information.
depends_on: Feedback (Principle 2), information theory, linear algebra and differential equations.

name: Resilience and Adaptive Cycles
text: Resilience is not about resisting change but about being able to absorb shocks and still function.
depends_on: Feedback, attractors and stability (Principle 10), hierarchy and modularity (Principle 6).

name: Viable System Model (Beer)
text: Any organization that wants to survive must have five essential functions: doing the work, coordinating the parts, managing resources, watching the environment, and maintaining identity.
depends_on: - Cybernetics and control theory (Principle 11)

name: Soft Systems Methodology (Checkland)
text: Many real-world problems are not well-defined engineering problems but messy human situations where people disagree about what the problem even is.
depends_on: - General systems theory (Principle 1 — emergence)

name: System Dynamics (Forrester)
text: System dynamics builds simulation models of complex systems using stocks (things that accumulate, like water in a bathtub), flows (rates of filling and draining), and feedback loops.
depends_on: - Feedback (Principle 2)

name: Complex Adaptive Systems
text: Complex adaptive systems are collections of many interacting agents that learn and adapt.
depends_on: - Emergence (Principle 1), self-organization (Principle 5)

name: Conant-Ashby Theorem (Good Regulator Theorem)
text: To control something well, you must have an internal model of it.
depends_on: - Cybernetics and control theory (Principle 11)

name: Catastrophe Theory
text: Catastrophe theory studies how smooth, gradual changes in conditions can cause sudden, dramatic shifts in behavior.
depends_on: - Dynamical systems, bifurcation theory

name: Anticipatory Systems
text: Anticipatory systems act based on predictions of their own future, not just reactions to the present.
depends_on: - Cybernetics and control theory (Principle 11)

name: Multilevel / Multi-Scale Systems
text: Real systems operate simultaneously at multiple scales: molecules form cells, cells form organs, organs form organisms, organisms form ecosystems.
depends_on: - Hierarchy and modularity (Principle 6)

name: Network Science and Multilayer Networks
text: Real-world networks are not isolated: the power grid depends on the communication network, which depends on the power grid.
depends_on: - Network theory (Principle 8)

name: Digital Twin Concept and Theory
text: A digital twin is a living virtual copy of a real physical system -- an engine, a bridge, a patient, or even a city -- that updates itself continuously with real-world data.
depends_on: - Systems theory, feedback (Principle 2)

name: System of Systems Engineering (SoSE)
text: Many of the most important and challenging systems in the modern world are not single systems but "systems of systems": the internet is a system of systems (independently operated networks cooperating), as are military command structures, smart grids, healthcare systems, and autonomous vehicle fleets.
depends_on: - General systems theory (Principle 1)

name: Agent-Based Modeling Foundations
text: Instead of writing equations for the whole system, agent-based modeling builds the system from the bottom up: define simple rules for how individuals behave, let them interact, and watch what emerges.
depends_on: - Complex adaptive systems (Principle 16)

name: Viable System Model (Beer)
text: If you randomly remove pieces from a network, at what point does it fall apart?
depends_on: - Complex Adaptive Systems (Principle 16)

name: Cultural Relativism
text: To understand another culture, you must step outside your own assumptions and see the world through their eyes.
depends_on: Epistemological humility, awareness of ethnocentrism

name: Participant Observation (Ethnographic Method)
text: To understand a culture, you must live in it.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), language competence, ethical research practices

name: Kinship Systems
text: Every society organizes itself around relationships of kinship -- who is related to whom and what that means.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), structural analysis (Principle 5)

name: The Gift and Exchange Theory (Mauss)
text: In most human societies, exchanging gifts is not just about the stuff -- it is about the relationships.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), participant observation (Principle 2)

name: Structural Analysis (Levi-Strauss)
text: Beneath the surface diversity of human cultures, there are deep patterns.
depends_on: Saussurean linguistics (Principle 1 of Linguistics), cultural relativism (Principle 1)

name: Holism and the Four-Field Approach
text: Anthropology tries to understand everything about being human -- our biology, our cultures, our languages, and our past.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), the recognition that biological and cultural evolution interact

name: Liminality (Turner)
text: In every culture, there are rituals that mark major life transitions: becoming an adult, getting married, being initiated into a group.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), participant observation (Principle 2)

name: Thick Description (Geertz)
text: Anthropology is not just about describing what people do -- it is about understanding what it means to them.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), participant observation (Principle 2), hermeneutics

name: Mode of Production (Marx)
text: How a society produces its livelihood determines its social structure, politics, and culture.
depends_on: Historical materialism, political economy, social stratification

name: Ethnocentrism
text: Ethnocentrism is judging other cultures by your own standards -- assuming that the way your culture does things is the normal, natural, or best way.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), cross-cultural comparison

name: Biological vs. Cultural Evolution
text: Humans evolve in two ways: biologically (through genes, over thousands of years) and culturally (through learning and teaching, which can happen in a single generation).
depends_on: Evolutionary biology, holism (Principle 6), cultural relativism (Principle 1)

name: Structural Functionalism (Radcliffe-Brown)
text: Structural functionalism asks: what does this custom or institution do for society?
depends_on: Participant observation (Principle 2), comparative method, Durkheim's sociology.

name: Practice Theory (Bourdieu's Habitus)
text: We act the way we do not because we follow explicit rules or make conscious calculations, but because our upbringing and social position have given us a "feel for the game" — a set of deeply ingrained habits, tastes, and dispositions (habitus).
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), structural analysis (Principle 5), social stratification.

name: Cultural Materialism (Harris)
text: To understand why people believe what they believe and organize their societies the way they do, start with how they make a living and how they reproduce.
depends_on: Mode of production (Principle 9), holism (Principle 6), ecological anthropology

name: Multi-Sited Ethnography (Marcus)
text: Traditional anthropology studied one village in depth.
depends_on: Participant observation (Principle 2), cultural relativism (Principle 1), world-systems theory

name: Post-Colonial Anthropology
text: Anthropology has a colonial past.
depends_on: Cultural relativism (Principle 1), ethnocentrism (Principle 10), practice theory (Principle 13)

name: Medical Anthropology
text: Design anthropology brings anthropologists' skills -- deep observation, understanding context, questioning assumptions -- into the world of design.
depends_on: - Participant observation (Principle 2)

name: Forensic Anthropology
text: Forensic anthropology uses knowledge of the human skeleton to help solve crimes, identify disaster victims, and document human rights violations.
depends_on: - Biological/physical anthropology (Principle 6/11)

name: Digital Anthropology
text: When mass graves are discovered in Guatemala, Argentina, or Bosnia, forensic anthropologists are called to identify the dead — giving names back to victims of political violence and providing evidence for courts.
depends_on: - Cultural Relativism (Principle 1)

name: Scarcity
text: There is not enough of everything for everyone.
depends_on: Physical reality of finite resources

name: Supply and Demand
text: When the price of something goes up, people want less of it (demand falls) and producers want to make more of it (supply rises).
depends_on: Scarcity (Principle 1), rational choice (Principle 3)

name: Rational Choice Theory
text: People have consistent preferences and make choices to get the best outcome they can afford.
depends_on: Logic (completeness, transitivity), mathematical optimization

name: Diminishing Marginal Returns (and Diminishing Marginal Utility)
text: The more of something you have, the less additional benefit you get from one more unit.
depends_on: Scarcity (Principle 1), calculus (concavity of production and utility functions)

name: Comparative Advantage (Ricardo)
text: Even if you are better at everything than your trading partner, you both benefit from trading if you each focus on what you are relatively best at.
depends_on: Scarcity (Principle 1), opportunity cost

name: General Equilibrium and the Welfare Theorems
text: Under idealized conditions (perfect competition, no externalities, complete information), markets automatically reach an efficient outcome -- one where you cannot make anyone better off without making someone else worse off.
depends_on: Rational choice (Principle 3), supply and demand (Principle 2), fixed-point theorems (Kakutani)

name: Market Failures and Externalities
text: Markets sometimes fail to produce efficient outcomes.
depends_on: General equilibrium and welfare theorems (Principle 6), rational choice (Principle 3)

name: Coase Theorem
text: If people can bargain freely and cheaply, they will negotiate their way to the efficient outcome no matter who starts with the legal right.
depends_on: Rational choice (Principle 3), property rights, welfare theorems (Principle 6)

name: Moral Hazard and Adverse Selection
text: Adverse selection: when buyers cannot tell good from bad, the bad drives out the good.
depends_on: Rational choice (Principle 3), market failures (Principle 7), information asymmetry

name: Phillips Curve
text: In the short run, lower unemployment tends to come with higher inflation (more spending chases limited goods).
depends_on: Supply and demand (Principle 2), rational expectations

name: Ricardian Equivalence
text: It does not matter whether the government taxes you now or borrows now and taxes you later -- if you are rational and farsighted, you save the money from a tax cut because you know future taxes must rise to repay the debt.
depends_on: Rational choice (Principle 3), intertemporal optimization, government budget constraint

name: Efficient Market Hypothesis
text: You cannot consistently beat the market because prices already incorporate all available information.
depends_on: Rational choice (Principle 3), competition, information

name: Nash Equilibrium
text: A Nash equilibrium is a situation where every player is doing the best they can given what everyone else is doing, so no one has a reason to change.
depends_on: Rational choice (Principle 3), mathematical fixed-point theorems

name: Modigliani-Miller Theorem
text: How a firm pays for its operations -- debt versus equity -- does not affect its total value under ideal conditions.
depends_on: Efficient markets (Principle 12), rational choice (Principle 3), no-arbitrage condition

name: Heckscher-Ohlin Model
text: Countries export what they are naturally equipped to produce.
depends_on: Comparative advantage (Principle 5), supply and demand (Principle 2), factor endowments

name: Keynesian Multiplier and Aggregate Demand
text: When the government spends an extra dollar, it does not just add one dollar to the economy -- it adds more, because the person who receives that dollar spends part of it, and the person who receives that spending spends part of it, and so on.
depends_on: Supply and demand (Principle 2), marginal propensity to consume, scarcity (Principle 1)

name: Solow Growth Model
text: Why do economies grow?
depends_on: Diminishing marginal returns (Principle 4), scarcity (Principle 1), calculus (differential equations)

name: Taylor Rule
text: Standard economics treats the environment as a subset of the economy — a source of resources and a sink for waste.
depends_on: - Solow Growth Model (Principle 17)

name: Arbitrariness of the Sign (Saussure)
text: There is no reason why a dog is called "dog" in English and "chien" in French.
depends_on: Semiotic theory (the study of signs)

name: Compositionality (Frege's Principle)
text: You can understand sentences you have never heard before because you know the meanings of the individual words and the grammatical rules for combining them.
depends_on: Syntax (structural rules for combination), semantics (meaning of parts)

name: Universal Grammar (Chomsky)
text: Humans are born with a built-in blueprint for language.
depends_on: Cognitive science (computational theory of mind), biology (genetic basis of language), evidence from language acquisition

name: Chomsky Hierarchy of Formal Languages
text: Languages (in the mathematical sense of sets of strings) come in layers of complexity.
depends_on: Automata theory, formal language theory, theory of computation

name: Pragmatic Principles (Grice's Maxims)
text: Communication works because speakers and listeners cooperate.
depends_on: Compositionality (Principle 2) for literal meaning, social cooperation

name: Structural Levels of Linguistic Analysis
text: Language is organized in layers, like an onion.
depends_on: Arbitrariness of the sign (Principle 1), compositionality (Principle 2)

name: Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis (Linguistic Relativity)
text: Does the language you speak shape how you think?
depends_on: Arbitrariness of the sign (Principle 1), cognitive science, cross-linguistic comparison

name: Merge Operation (Minimalism)
text: All the complex structure of human language -- sentences within sentences, questions, passives, relative clauses -- may arise from a single, recursive operation: take two things and combine them.
depends_on: Universal Grammar (Principle 3), recursion, compositionality (Principle 2)

name: Optimality Theory
text: Language follows rules, but rules can conflict.
depends_on: Universal Grammar (Principle 3), constraint-based approaches, structural levels (Principle 6)

name: Language Acquisition Device (LAD)
text: Children learn language with remarkable speed and accuracy despite receiving imperfect input, without formal lessons, and at an age when they cannot tie their shoes.
depends_on: Universal Grammar (Principle 3), cognitive science, developmental psychology

name: Phonological Universals
text: Despite speaking 7,000+ different languages, humans use a remarkably similar set of sound building blocks.
depends_on: Structural levels (Principle 6), articulatory phonetics, perceptual constraints, Universal Grammar (Principle 3)

name: Historical Linguistics and the Comparative Method
text: Languages change over time in regular, predictable ways.
depends_on: Structural levels (Principle 6), phonological analysis, the regularity of sound change.

name: Speech Act Theory (Austin, Searle)
text: Saying "I promise to be there" is not just describing a state of affairs — it is the act of promising.
depends_on: Pragmatic principles (Principle 5), compositionality (Principle 2), social context.

name: Morphological Typology
text: Languages differ dramatically in how they build words.
depends_on: Structural levels (Principle 6), cross-linguistic comparison, phonological universals (Principle 11)

name: Language Contact and Creolization
text: When people who speak different languages must communicate, new languages can emerge.
depends_on: Universal Grammar (Principle 3), historical linguistics (Principle 12), arbitrariness of the sign (Principle 1)

name: Construction Grammar
text: Instead of a rule-generating grammar plus a dictionary of words, construction grammar says language is a big collection of patterns -- from individual words to abstract sentence templates -- each pairing a form with a meaning.
depends_on: Compositionality (Principle 2 -- extends and challenges it), structural levels (Principle 6), cognitive science

name: Discourse Analysis
text: How can computers process, understand, and generate human language?
depends_on: - Chomsky hierarchy (Principle 4)

name: Sign Language Linguistics
text: Sign languages are not simply gesture systems or manual codes for spoken languages.
depends_on: - Universal Grammar / language faculty (Principle 3)

name: Documentary Linguistics
text: Language follows mathematical laws with surprising precision.
depends_on: - Structural Levels of Linguistic Analysis (Principle 6)

name: Sovereignty
text: Sovereignty means "who is ultimately in charge." A sovereign state is the highest authority in its territory -- no one can overrule it domestically, and no foreign power has the right to interfere.
depends_on: Social contract theory (Principle 2), the concept of legitimate authority

name: Social Contract Theory
text: Why should anyone obey the government?
depends_on: State of nature thought experiments, rational choice

name: Separation of Powers
text: Do not give all power to one person or group.
depends_on: Sovereignty (Principle 1), social contract theory (Principle 2), recognition of the corrupting tendency of unchecked power

name: Collective Action Problem
text: If everyone benefits from clean air, but cleaning up is costly and no one can be excluded from breathing clean air, then everyone has an incentive to let others do the work.
depends_on: Rational choice theory, game theory (Prisoner's Dilemma)

name: Median Voter Theorem
text: In a majority-rule election on a single issue, the winning position is the one preferred by the voter in the middle.
depends_on: Rational choice (voters and candidates), single-peaked preferences, one-dimensional policy space

name: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
text: There is no perfect voting system.
depends_on: Rational choice (complete, transitive preferences), formal logic

name: Institutional Design and Path Dependence
text: The rules matter.
depends_on: Social contract theory (Principle 2), collective action (Principle 4)

name: Selectorate Theory
text: Political leaders stay in power by keeping their essential supporters happy.
depends_on: Rational choice, collective action (Principle 4), institutional design (Principle 7)

name: Democratic Peace Theory
text: Democracies almost never fight wars against other democracies.
depends_on: Sovereignty (Principle 1), institutional design (Principle 7)

name: Prisoner's Dilemma in International Relations
text: Nations face situations where each country is tempted to cheat (arm while others disarm, protect its industry while demanding free trade), but if everyone cheats, everyone is worse off.
depends_on: Rational choice, game theory, collective action (Principle 4)

name: Veto Player Theory
text: The more people (or institutions) who can block a policy change, and the more they disagree, the harder it is to change anything.
depends_on: Separation of powers (Principle 3), spatial voting models, institutional design (Principle 7)

name: Duverger's Law
text: In countries where each district elects one representative and the most votes win (like the US and UK), you end up with two major parties.
depends_on: Rational choice, institutional design (Principle 7), median voter theorem (Principle 5)

name: Condorcet Paradox (Voting Cycles)
text: Even if every individual voter has perfectly rational preferences, the group can end up going in circles.
depends_on: Rational choice (individual transitivity), majority rule, preference diversity

name: Power Transition Theory
text: The most dangerous moment in international politics is when a rising power is about to overtake the dominant one.
depends_on: Sovereignty (Principle 1), institutional design (Principle 7), realist theory of international relations

name: Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
text: No fair voting system is immune to strategic voting.
depends_on: Arrow's impossibility theorem (Principle 6), rational choice, game theory

name: Hegemonic Stability Theory
text: Voting alone does not make a democracy legitimate -- what matters is the quality of the public conversation that precedes the vote.
depends_on: - Social contract (Principle 2)

name: Computational Social Science
text: The digital age generates vast amounts of data about human behavior: every tweet, search, purchase, and phone call leaves a trace.
depends_on: - Rational choice and institutional theory (Principles 2, 7)

name: AI Governance and the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence
text: As AI systems become more powerful and pervasive -- making decisions about hiring, criminal justice, medical diagnosis, and even military targeting -- the question of how to govern them becomes urgent.
depends_on: - Sovereignty and institutional design (Principles 1, 7)

name: Quadratic Voting and Mechanism Design for Democracy
text: In standard voting, everyone gets one vote regardless of how much they care about an issue.
depends_on: - Arrow's impossibility theorem (Principle 6)

name: Polarization Dynamics and Affective Polarization
text: Why do authoritarian regimes persist?
depends_on: - Democratic Theory (Principle 7)

name: Classical Conditioning (Pavlovian Conditioning)
text: If a bell always rings before food appears, eventually the bell alone makes you salivate.
depends_on: Neuroscience (synaptic plasticity, long-term potentiation)

name: Operant Conditioning (Instrumental Conditioning)
text: We do more of what gets rewarded and less of what gets punished.
depends_on: Classical conditioning (Principle 1) for foundational learning mechanisms, neuroscience (dopamine reward circuits)

name: Weber-Fechner Law (Psychophysics)
