text: You notice changes relative to what you already have.
depends_on: Sensory neuroscience, mathematics (logarithmic and power functions)

name: Information Processing Model of Cognition
text: The mind works somewhat like a computer: information comes in through the senses, gets filtered by attention, held briefly in working memory (which has strict capacity limits), and either forgotten or stored in long-term memory.
depends_on: Information theory (Shannon's entropy), computer science (computational metaphor), neuroscience

name: Cognitive Dissonance Theory
text: When your actions contradict your beliefs, you feel uncomfortable.
depends_on: Motivation theory, self-concept

name: Dual-Process Theory of Cognition
text: You have two ways of thinking: a fast, intuitive mode that operates automatically (gut feelings, snap judgments) and a slow, deliberate mode that requires effort (careful analysis, logical reasoning).
depends_on: Information processing model (Principle 4), cognitive dissonance (Principle 5, for motivated reasoning)

name: Developmental Stages and Critical Periods
text: Children's minds develop in stages, not just by accumulating more knowledge but by changing how they think.
depends_on: Neuroscience (synaptic pruning, myelination), evolutionary biology

name: Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs
text: You worry about food before friendship, and friendship before finding your purpose.
depends_on: Motivation theory, humanistic psychology

name: Piaget's Stages of Cognitive Development
text: Children do not just know less than adults -- they think differently.
depends_on: Constructivism, neuroscience (brain maturation)

name: Attachment Theory (Bowlby)
text: The bond between an infant and caregiver is not just emotional comfort -- it shapes how the child relates to others for the rest of their life.
depends_on: Evolutionary biology, developmental neuroscience

name: Prospect Theory (Kahneman-Tversky)
text: Losing $100 hurts about twice as much as gaining $100 feels good.
depends_on: Rational choice (critique), psychophysics (Weber-Fechner), cognitive biases

name: Working Memory Model (Baddeley)
text: Working memory is your mental workspace -- the limited amount of information you can hold in mind and manipulate at any moment.
depends_on: Information processing model (Principle 4), neuroscience (prefrontal cortex function)

name: Fundamental Attribution Error
text: When someone cuts you off in traffic, you think "what a jerk" (personality).
depends_on: Social cognition, dual-process theory (Principle 6)

name: Social Identity Theory (Tajfel)
text: A large part of who you think you are comes from the groups you belong to -- your nation, team, profession, religion.
depends_on: Social cognition, self-concept, categorization processes

name: Learned Helplessness (Seligman)
text: If you repeatedly experience situations where nothing you do makes a difference, you stop trying -- even when the situation changes and you could succeed.
depends_on: Classical and operant conditioning (Principles 1-2), cognitive attribution theory

name: Yerkes-Dodson Law
text: A little stress helps you perform; too much stress makes you choke.
depends_on: Neuroscience (stress hormones, prefrontal function), attention theory

name: Obedience to Authority (Milgram)
text: Ordinary people will do terrible things when told to by someone in authority.
depends_on: Social influence, fundamental attribution error (Principle 13), group dynamics

name: Bandura's Social Learning Theory
text: ACT starts from a radical premise: trying to control or eliminate painful thoughts and feelings is not just ineffective — it is often the cause of psychological problems.
depends_on: - Relational Frame Theory (Principle 37)

name: Social Facts (Durkheim)
text: Society is more than the sum of its individuals.
depends_on: Distinction between individual and collective levels of analysis

name: Structuration Theory (Giddens)
text: Society shapes people, and people shape society -- simultaneously.
depends_on: Social facts (Principle 1), action theory (Weber)

name: Symbolic Interactionism
text: Society is built from the bottom up through face-to-face interactions.
depends_on: Pragmatist philosophy (Dewey, James), semiotics (the study of signs)

name: Social Stratification and Inequality
text: Every society has a hierarchy.
depends_on: Social facts (Principle 1), structuration (Principle 2), economic principles

name: Collective Behavior and Social Movements
text: Sometimes people come together to change society -- through protests, revolutions, or movements.
depends_on: Social stratification (Principle 4), symbolic interactionism (Principle 3)

name: Socialization and the Social Construction of Reality
text: You were not born knowing how to behave in your society; you were taught, gradually and thoroughly, from birth.
depends_on: Symbolic interactionism (Principle 3), social facts (Principle 1)

name: Anomie (Durkheim)
text: When the rules of society break down -- during rapid change, economic crisis, or social upheaval -- people lose their moral compass.
depends_on: Social facts (Principle 1), social regulation, moral integration

name: Iron Cage (Weber)
text: Modern society traps us in bureaucratic systems designed for efficiency and control.
depends_on: Social facts (Principle 1), social action theory (Weber)

name: Bourdieu's Capitals
text: Inequality is not just about money.
depends_on: Social stratification (Principle 4), structuration (Principle 2)

name: Network Theory (Granovetter's Weak Ties)
text: It is not your close friends but your acquaintances who are most likely to help you find a job.
depends_on: Symbolic interactionism (Principle 3), graph theory

name: Labeling Theory
text: Who gets called a "criminal" depends less on what they did and more on who is doing the labeling.
depends_on: Symbolic interactionism (Principle 3), social construction of reality (Principle 6)

name: Historical Materialism (Marx)
text: Marx argued that economics is the foundation of everything in society.
depends_on: Political economy, the concept of class, dialectical logic.

name: Rational Choice Theory in Sociology (Coleman)
text: Rational choice theory explains social patterns by asking: what are individuals trying to achieve, and how do their individual choices add up to produce collective outcomes?
depends_on: Microeconomics (utility theory), game theory, methodological individualism.

name: Dramaturgical Analysis (Goffman)
text: Social life is a performance.
depends_on: Symbolic interactionism (Principle 3), social construction of reality (Principle 6)

name: Mechanical and Organic Solidarity (Durkheim)
text: In small traditional societies, everyone is alike and cohesion comes from sameness -- shared beliefs, rituals, and customs.
depends_on: Social facts (Principle 1), anomie (Principle 7)

name: Ideal Types (Weber)
text: An ideal type is a simplified mental model that exaggerates the key features of a social phenomenon to make it easier to study.
depends_on: Epistemology (concept formation), social action theory

name: Intersectionality (Crenshaw)
text: You cannot understand someone's social position by looking at just one thing -- race, or gender, or class -- in isolation.
depends_on: Social stratification (Principle 4), social construction of reality (Principle 6), critical theory

name: World-Systems Theory (Wallerstein)
text: Why do Germany and the United States have such different economic systems — and why do both succeed?
depends_on: - Stratification and Inequality (Principle 6)

name: Justified True Belief (The Classical Analysis of Knowledge)
text: Knowledge is not just believing something true; you need good reasons for your belief.
depends_on: Propositional logic, concept of truth, concept of justification

name: The Problem of Induction (Hume)
text: Science relies on reasoning from what we have observed to what we have not yet observed (induction).
depends_on: Logic (distinction between deductive and inductive validity), empiricism

name: Falsificationism (Popper)
text: A scientific theory must stick its neck out: it must make predictions that could be proved wrong.
depends_on: Problem of induction (Principle 2), deductive logic (modus tollens: if T then O; not O; therefore not T)

name: Paradigm Shifts (Kuhn)
text: Science does not progress steadily by accumulating truths.
depends_on: History of science, sociology of knowledge

name: Underdetermination of Theory by Evidence (Quine-Duhem Thesis)
text: Evidence alone cannot tell you which theory is right.
depends_on: Problem of induction (Principle 2), falsificationism (Principle 3, as the thesis limits falsification)

name: Bayesian Epistemology and Confirmation
text: Bayesian epistemology says that rational belief comes in degrees (not just "believe" or "do not believe") and that you should update your beliefs in light of new evidence using Bayes' theorem.
depends_on: Probability theory, problem of induction (Principle 2)

name: Justified True Belief and Gettier Problems
text: Gettier showed that you can have a well-justified, true belief and still not really "know" something -- because you are right for the wrong reasons.
depends_on: Principle 1 (classical JTB analysis), logic, concept of justification

name: Foundationalism vs Coherentism
text: When you justify a belief, you give a reason.
depends_on: Concept of justification, logic (regress argument), Principle 1

name: Empiricism (Locke, Hume)
text: All real knowledge about the world comes from the senses.
depends_on: Philosophy of perception, concept of experience, logic (analytic/synthetic distinction)

name: Rationalism (Descartes, Leibniz)
text: Some things can be known by thinking, not just by looking.
depends_on: Logic, metaphysics (necessary truths, innate ideas), philosophy of mathematics

name: Skepticism and the Problem of Induction (Hume)
text: The skeptic asks: how do you really know anything?
depends_on: Logic, problem of induction (Principle 2), epistemology (justification)

name: Reliabilism (Goldman)
text: What makes a belief justified?
depends_on: Concept of truth, cognitive psychology (reliability of cognitive processes), Principle 1 (JTB)

name: Contextualism
text: Whether it is true to say someone "knows" something depends on the conversation.
depends_on: Epistemology (knowledge, justification), philosophy of language (context-dependence, pragmatics), Principle 11 (skepticism)

name: Inference to the Best Explanation (IBE / Abduction)
text: When scientists consider competing explanations and choose the one that best fits the evidence, they are using inference to the best explanation.
depends_on: Philosophy of explanation, Bayesian epistemology (Principle 6), logic (non-deductive inference)

name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
text: There is no such thing as pure, unbiased observation.
depends_on: Paradigm shifts (Principle 4), philosophy of perception, cognitive psychology

name: Social Epistemology
text: Knowledge is not just an individual achievement -- it is a social product.
depends_on: Traditional epistemology (Principles 1, 8, 12), sociology of knowledge, philosophy of science (Principles 4, 5)

name: Epistemic Virtues and Virtue Epistemology
text: What makes someone a good knower is not just having true beliefs with good evidence, but being the right kind of thinker.
depends_on: Reliabilism (Principle 12), virtue ethics, Principle 1 (JTB), Principle 7 (Gettier)

name: Naturalized Epistemology (Quine)
text: Quine argued that traditional philosophy's attempts to justify all our knowledge from first principles were doomed to fail.
depends_on: Empiricism (Principle 9), problem of induction (Principle 2), underdetermination (Principle 5)

name: Epistemic Injustice (Fricker)
text: Sometimes people are wronged not by being robbed or physically harmed, but by being denied the status of a knowledgeable person.
depends_on: Social epistemology (Principle 16), reliabilism (Principle 12), philosophy of testimony

name: Knowledge-First Epistemology (Williamson)
text: Feminist epistemology asks: does who you are affect what you can know?
depends_on: - Social Epistemology (Principle P36)

name: Causation
text: What does it mean for one thing to cause another?
depends_on: Modal logic (counterfactuals, possible worlds), empiricism vs. rationalism

name: Laws of Nature
text: Are the laws of physics necessary truths about the universe, or are they just descriptions of patterns that happen to hold?
depends_on: Causation (Principle 1), modal logic (necessity, possibility)

name: Natural Kinds
text: Is "gold" a real category in nature, or just a label we invented?
depends_on: Philosophy of language (reference, rigid designation), causation (Principle 1), laws of nature (Principle 2)

name: Identity and Persistence
text: Is the ship rebuilt plank by plank the same ship (Ship of Theseus)?
depends_on: Logic (Leibniz's Law), philosophy of time, philosophy of physics (spacetime)

name: Mereology (Part-Whole Relations)
text: When do parts make a whole?
depends_on: Logic (formal mereological axioms), identity (Principle 4)

name: Determinism and Indeterminism
text: Is the future completely determined by the present and the laws of physics, or is there genuine randomness in the world?
depends_on: Laws of nature (Principle 2), causation (Principle 1), physics (QM, classical mechanics)

name: Ontological Categories (Substance, Property, Relation)
text: What are the most basic kinds of things that exist?
depends_on: Logic (predication, quantification), philosophy of language (reference)

name: Causation (Hume's Regularity, Counterfactual, Interventionist)
text: What does it mean for one thing to cause another?
depends_on: Principle 1 (causation overview), modal logic, probability theory

name: Personal Identity (Locke, Hume, Bundle Theory)
text: What makes you the same person today as you were ten years ago?
depends_on: Principle 4 (identity and persistence), philosophy of mind, ethics (moral responsibility)

name: Free Will (Compatibilism, Libertarianism, Hard Determinism)
text: Do you have free will?
depends_on: Principle 6 (determinism), causation (Principle 1), philosophy of mind, ethics

name: Possible Worlds (Lewis, Kripke)
text: Could things have been different?
depends_on: Modal logic (necessity, possibility), philosophy of language (reference, rigid designation)

name: Universals vs Particulars (Plato, Aristotle, Nominalism)
text: When we say two different roses are both red, is there one thing -- redness -- that they both share?
depends_on: Logic (predication), philosophy of language, metaphysics (ontological categories, Principle 7)

name: Supervenience
text: Supervenience captures the idea of dependence without identity.
depends_on: Modal logic (possible worlds), metaphysics (properties, identity), Principle 7 (ontological categories)

name: Trope Theory
text: Instead of saying that two roses share the universal "redness," trope theory says each rose has its own particular redness -- a unique instance that resembles but is not identical to the other.
depends_on: Principle 12 (universals vs. particulars), metaphysics (ontological categories, Principle 7), logic

name: Modal Realism (Lewis)
text: David Lewis took possible worlds literally.
depends_on: Principle 11 (possible worlds), modal logic, philosophy of language (indexicals, reference)

name: Grounding
text: Grounding is the "in virtue of" relation.
depends_on: Metaphysics (ontological categories, Principle 7), modal logic, explanation theory

name: Temporal Parts vs. Endurantism
text: When you say "that is the same cat I saw yesterday," what does "the same" mean?
depends_on: Principle 4 (identity and persistence), philosophy of time, philosophy of physics (spacetime)

name: Truthmaker Theory
text: If it is true that "the cat is on the mat," something in the world must make it true.
depends_on: Ontological categories (Principle 7), logic (truth, predication), possible worlds (Principle 11)

name: Dispositions and Categorical Properties
text: Is glass fragile because of some underlying structural property (categorical), or is fragility itself the fundamental nature of the glass?
depends_on: Laws of nature (Principle 2), natural kinds (Principle 3), ontological categories (Principle 7)

name: Structural Realism (Metaphysical)
text: Structural realism says that what science really discovers is the structure of the world -- the mathematical relationships between things -- not the intrinsic nature of the things themselves.
depends_on: Natural kinds (Principle 3), laws of nature (Principle 2), philosophy of physics (quantum mechanics), universals (Principle 12)

name: Abstract Objects (Ontology of Abstracta)
text: Do numbers, sets, and propositions really exist?
depends_on: Ontological categories (Principle 7), laws of nature (Principle 2), philosophy of mathematics

name: Modality and Essence
text: Could things have been different?
depends_on: Possible worlds (Principle 11), natural kinds (Principle 3), laws of nature (Principle 2)

name: Best Systems Account of Laws (Lewis-Ramsey)
text: What is a property, fundamentally?
depends_on: - Laws of Nature (Principle 3)

name: The Species Problem
text: What makes a group of organisms a species?
depends_on: Evolutionary theory, metaphysics (natural kinds, individuals), philosophy of classification

name: Levels of Selection
text: Does evolution select genes, organisms, groups, or all of the above?
depends_on: Evolutionary theory (natural selection, fitness), metaphysics (individuality, causation), Price equation

name: Teleology and Function in Biology
text: Biology is full of purpose-talk: organs have functions, behaviors have goals, adaptations are "for" something.
depends_on: Evolutionary theory (natural selection), metaphysics (causation, explanation), philosophy of science (explanation)

name: Reduction vs. Autonomy of Biology
text: Can we explain everything in biology using just physics and chemistry?
depends_on: Philosophy of science (reduction, explanation, laws), metaphysics (multiple realizability, emergence)

name: The Gene Concept and Its Evolution
text: What is a gene?
depends_on: Molecular biology, genetics, philosophy of science (concepts, natural kinds)

name: Adaptationism and Its Critics
text: Is every feature of an organism there because natural selection put it there?
depends_on: Evolutionary theory (natural selection, drift, constraints), philosophy of science (explanation, confirmation)

name: Species Concepts (Biological, Phylogenetic, Morphological)
text: There are over 26 proposed ways to define what a species is, but the big three are: can they breed together (biological), do they share exclusive common ancestry (phylogenetic), or do they look alike (morphological)?
depends_on: Principle 1 (species problem), evolutionary theory, philosophy of classification

name: Units of Selection Debate (Gene, Individual, Group)
text: When natural selection acts, what is it really selecting -- genes, organisms, or groups?
depends_on: Principle 2 (levels of selection), evolutionary theory, Price equation, philosophy of causation

name: Teleology and Function (Etiological vs Dispositional)
text: When we say the function of the heart is to pump blood, what exactly do we mean?
depends_on: Principle 3 (teleology overview), natural selection, philosophy of explanation

name: Reduction vs Emergence in Biology
text: Can biology be fully explained by physics and chemistry?
depends_on: Principle 4 (reduction vs. autonomy), philosophy of physics, philosophy of mind

name: Philosophy of Ecology (Community, Ecosystem Concepts)
text: Is a forest community a real thing, like an organism, or just a collection of trees that happen to grow in the same place?
depends_on: Metaphysics (natural kinds, individuals, mereology), philosophy of science (models, explanation)

name: Evolutionary Epistemology (Knowledge as Evolved Adaptation)
text: Our ability to know the world evolved.
depends_on: Evolutionary theory, cognitive science, Principle 6 (adaptationism), epistemology

name: Extended Evolutionary Synthesis
text: The standard theory of evolution (the Modern Synthesis) focuses on genes, natural selection, and random mutation.
depends_on: Modern Synthesis (natural selection, genetics), developmental biology, epigenetics, ecology

name: Niche Construction
text: Organisms do not just adapt to their environments -- they change them.
depends_on: Evolutionary theory, ecology, Principle 6 (adaptationism critique), population genetics

name: Units of Selection Debate (Extended)
text: What counts as a biological individual?
depends_on: Principles 2 and 8 (levels/units of selection), evolutionary theory, philosophy of biology (individuality)

name: Biological Individuality
text: Where does one organism end and another begin?
depends_on: Evolutionary theory, microbiology, Principle 1 (species problem), Principle 4 (reduction)

name: Cultural Evolution Theory
text: Culture evolves.
depends_on: Evolutionary theory, Principle 2 (levels of selection), Principle 14 (niche construction), cognitive science

name: Developmental Systems Theory
text: Genes do not contain a blueprint for the organism.
depends_on: Gene concept (Principle 5), niche construction (Principle 14), extended evolutionary synthesis (Principle 13)

name: The Homology Concept
text: Your arm, a whale's flipper, and a bat's wing are all homologous -- they are the same structure inherited from a common ancestor, modified for different purposes.
depends_on: Species concepts (Principle 7), phylogenetics, developmental biology, gene concept (Principle 5)

name: Philosophy of Medicine
text: What counts as a disease?
depends_on: Philosophy of biology (function, natural kinds), epistemology (evidence, justification), ethics

name: The Fitness Concept
text: What does it mean to be "fit" in evolutionary biology?
depends_on: Natural selection (Principle 6), levels of selection (Principle 2), probability

name: Epigenetic Inheritance and the Lamarckism Debate
text: Can the experiences of parents affect the genes of their children -- not by changing the DNA sequence, but by adding chemical tags that turn genes on or off?
depends_on: Gene concept (Principle 5), Extended Evolutionary Synthesis (Principle 13), Developmental Systems Theory (Principle 18)

name: Philosophy of Classification and Taxonomy
text: You are not just you — you are a community.
depends_on: - Biological Individuality (Principle 16)

name: Platonism (Mathematical Realism)
text: Mathematical objects like the number 7 or the set of prime numbers really exist, in a realm separate from the physical world.
depends_on: Metaphysics (existence of abstract objects), epistemology (how can we access abstract entities?)

name: Formalism
text: Mathematics is a game of symbols.
depends_on: Logic (formal systems, proof theory), Godel's incompleteness theorems

name: Intuitionism and Constructivism
text: Mathematics is what we can build in our minds.
depends_on: Philosophy of mind (mental construction), epistemology (knowledge requires construction/proof)

name: Structuralism
text: Mathematics is not about particular objects (like the number 2) but about patterns and structures.
depends_on: Set theory, model theory (multiple models satisfying same axioms), category theory

name: The Indispensability Argument (Quine-Putnam)
text: If math is essential to science, and science describes reality, then mathematical objects must be real.
depends_on: Scientific realism, Quinean holism, philosophy of science

name: Mathematical Truth and the Question of Objectivity
text: What makes "2 + 2 = 4" true?
depends_on: Godel's incompleteness theorems, Tarski's truth definition, all of Principles 1-5

name: Platonism (Mathematical Objects Exist Independently)
text: Platonism says mathematical objects like the number pi, the Mandelbrot set, and infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces are just as real as tables and chairs -- but they exist outside space and time.
depends_on: Principle 1 (Platonism overview), metaphysics (abstract objects), epistemology

name: Formalism (Mathematics as Symbol Manipulation, Hilbert)
text: Hilbert wanted to make mathematics safe by turning it into a perfectly rigorous game: define your axioms, state your rules, and then everything is just symbol manipulation.
depends_on: Principle 2 (formalism overview), logic, Godel's incompleteness theorems, proof theory

name: Intuitionism (Mathematics as Mental Construction, Brouwer)
text: Brouwer insisted that mathematics is a human mental activity, not a formal game or a description of an abstract Platonic realm.
depends_on: Principle 3 (intuitionism overview), philosophy of mind, epistemology

name: Structuralism (Mathematics is about Structures, not Objects)
text: Structuralism says that what matters in mathematics is not the objects themselves but the patterns and relationships between them.
depends_on: Principle 4 (structuralism overview), set theory, model theory, category theory

name: Indispensability Argument (Quine-Putnam)
text: If mathematics is essential to our best scientific theories, and we believe those theories describe reality, then we should believe mathematical objects are real too.
depends_on: Principle 5 (indispensability overview), scientific realism, Quinean holism

name: Philosophy of Mathematical Practice (Lakatos, Proofs and Refutations)
text: Philosophers have traditionally asked "What is mathematical truth?" and "Do numbers exist?" But a newer tradition asks: "How does mathematics actually work?" Lakatos showed that proofs evolve through a back-and-forth of conjectures and counterexamples, much like scientific theories.
depends_on: Principles 1-6 (foundational positions), history of mathematics, sociology of knowledge

name: Logicism (Frege/Russell)
text: Logicism says that mathematics is just logic in disguise.
depends_on: Logic (first-order and higher-order), set theory, Principle 1 (Platonism), Principle 6 (mathematical truth)

name: Fictionalism
text: Fictionalism says mathematical objects do not exist -- there are no numbers, sets, or functions out there in reality.
depends_on: Philosophy of language (fiction, pretense, reference), Principle 5 (indispensability argument, as the target of criticism)

name: Predicativism
text: Predicativism is the view that mathematical definitions must not be circular.
depends_on: Logic (quantification, definition), set theory, Principle 3 (intuitionism/constructivism), proof theory

name: Benacerraf's Dilemma
text: Benacerraf's dilemma is the deepest problem in the philosophy of mathematics.
depends_on: Epistemology (causal theories of knowledge), Principle 1 (Platonism), Principle 4 (structuralism), philosophy of language

name: Godel's Realism (Godel's Platonism)
text: Godel believed that mathematical objects are just as real as physical objects, and that we can perceive them with our mathematical intuition -- a kind of inner sight.
depends_on: Principle 1 (Platonism), Godel's incompleteness theorems, Principle 6 (mathematical truth), epistemology

name: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Mathematics (Wigner)
text: Why does mathematics work so well in physics?
depends_on: Platonism (Principle 1), structuralism (Principle 4), philosophy of physics, epistemology

name: Mathematical Explanation
text: Can mathematics itself explain why something happens, or does it just help us describe what happens?
depends_on: Indispensability argument (Principle 5/11), Platonism (Principle 1), philosophy of explanation

name: Category Theory as Foundational Framework
text: Category theory is a kind of "mathematics of mathematics." Instead of starting with sets and building up, it starts with structures and the mappings between them.
depends_on: Structuralism (Principle 4), logic, set theory, abstract algebra

name: Mathematical Intuition and the Phenomenology of Mathematics
text: Mathematicians often report "seeing" that a theorem is true before they can prove it formally.
depends_on: Platonism (Principle 1), Godel's realism (Principle 17), epistemology

name: The Philosophy of Mathematical Proof
text: What counts as a proof?
depends_on: Formalism (Principle 2), mathematical practice (Principle 12), logic

name: Nominalism and Indispensability Responses
text: Why does mathematics work so well in physics?
depends_on: - Platonism vs. Nominalism (Principles 1, 4)

name: The Mind-Body Problem
text: How is the mind related to the brain?
depends_on: Metaphysics (substance, property, identity, causation), neuroscience, introspection

name: Functionalism
text: What makes something a belief or a desire is not what it is made of but what it does -- how it relates to perception, other mental states, and behavior.
depends_on: Theory of computation (Turing machines), multiple realizability (Principle 5)

name: The Hard Problem of Consciousness (Chalmers)
text: Science can explain how the brain processes information, discriminates stimuli, and controls behavior.
depends_on: Mind-body problem (Principle 1), functionalism (Principle 2), metaphysics of properties

name: Intentionality
text: Thoughts are about things.
depends_on: Philosophy of language (reference, meaning), metaphysics (representation, causation)

name: Multiple Realizability
text: Pain in a human, an octopus, and a hypothetical robot could all be the same type of mental state even though the underlying physical mechanisms are completely different.
depends_on: Functionalism (Principle 2), philosophy of science (reduction, special sciences)

name: The Chinese Room Argument (Searle)
text: A computer might perfectly simulate understanding -- giving the right answers to every question -- without actually understanding anything.
depends_on: Functionalism (Principle 2) and the computational theory of mind, intentionality (Principle 4)

name: Supervenience and Mental Causation
text: Almost all philosophers agree that the mind depends on the brain: change the brain and you change the mind.
depends_on: Metaphysics (causation, identity, supervenience), mind-body problem (Principle 1)

name: Mind-Body Problem (Dualism, Physicalism, Functionalism)
text: The mind-body problem is philosophy's hardest question.
depends_on: Principle 1 (mind-body problem overview), metaphysics (substance, property, identity), neuroscience

name: Consciousness: Hard Problem (Chalmers)
text: Why does anything feel like anything?
depends_on: Principle 3 (hard problem overview), mind-body problem (Principle 1), functionalism (Principle 2)

name: Intentionality (Brentano, Aboutness of Mental States)
text: Your thoughts are about things.
depends_on: Principle 4 (intentionality overview), philosophy of language, metaphysics (causation, representation)

name: Multiple Realizability (Putnam, Against Type Identity)
text: Pain in a human is one kind of brain state, pain in an octopus is a different kind, and a robot might feel pain through entirely different hardware.
depends_on: Principle 5 (MR overview), functionalism (Principle 2), philosophy of science (reduction)

name: Chinese Room Argument (Searle, Against Strong AI)
text: Searle's thought experiment challenges the idea that a computer could ever truly understand anything.
depends_on: Principle 6 (Chinese room overview), functionalism (Principle 2), intentionality (Principle 4), computational theory of mind

name: Computational Theory of Mind (Fodor)
text: Thinking is computing.
depends_on: Theory of computation, functionalism (Principle 2), philosophy of language

name: Qualia and the Knowledge Argument (Jackson)
text: What is the specific feel of seeing the color red?
depends_on: Mind-body problem (Principle 1), hard problem (Principle 3), philosophy of perception

name: The Zombie Argument (Chalmers)
text: Imagine a perfect atom-for-atom copy of you, but with no inner experience whatsoever -- no feelings, no consciousness, just complete darkness inside.
depends_on: Principle 3 (hard problem), Principle 9 (expanded hard problem), modal logic, two-dimensional semantics

name: Higher-Order Theories of Consciousness
text: What makes a mental state conscious?
depends_on: Principle 1 (mind-body problem), Principle 3 (hard problem), cognitive neuroscience

name: Integrated Information Theory (IIT / Phi)
text: IIT says consciousness just is integrated information.
depends_on: Information theory, Principle 3 (hard problem), mathematics (graph theory, information geometry)

name: Predictive Processing and the Free Energy Principle
text: Your brain is a prediction machine.
depends_on: Bayesian inference, information theory, Principle 2 (functionalism), neuroscience

name: Eliminative Materialism
text: Eliminative materialism says our everyday understanding of the mind is fundamentally wrong.
depends_on: Principle 1 (mind-body problem), Principle 8 (expanded mind-body), philosophy of science (theory elimination), neuroscience

name: Enactivism
text: Enactivism says the mind is not a computer processing information from the senses.
depends_on: Mind-body problem (Principle 1), phenomenology, biology (autopoiesis), ecological psychology

name: Panpsychism
text: Panpsychism says that consciousness is not something that magically appears at a certain level of complexity.
depends_on: Hard problem of consciousness (Principle 3), mind-body problem (Principle 1), metaphysics (ontological categories)

name: Extended Mind Thesis
text: Your mind is not just in your head.
depends_on: Functionalism (Principle 2), mind-body problem (Principle 1), embodied cognition

name: Anomalous Monism (Davidson)
text: Davidson tried to thread a needle: the mind is physical (every mental event is a brain event), yet there are no strict laws connecting types of thoughts to types of brain states.
depends_on: Mind-body problem (Principle 1), mental causation (Principle 7), laws of nature

name: Neural Correlates of Consciousness (NCC)
text: Neuroscientists search for the brain activity that corresponds to conscious experience: when you see red, what specific neurons or brain regions are active?
depends_on: Hard problem (Principle 3), mind-body problem (Principle 1), neuroscience

name: Global Workspace Theory (Baars, Dehaene)
text: Where does your mind end and the world begin?
depends_on: - Functionalism (Principle 2)

name: The Measurement Problem and Interpretations of Quantum Mechanics
text: Quantum mechanics is our most successful physical theory, but no one agrees on what it says about reality.
depends_on: Quantum mechanical formalism (Hilbert spaces, Born rule), metaphysics (realism, determinism)

name: The Nature of Spacetime (Substantivalism vs. Relationalism)
text: Is space a thing in itself -- a container that would exist even if there were nothing in it?
depends_on: General relativity (differential geometry, Einstein field equations), metaphysics (identity, individuality of points)

name: Determinism in Physics
text: Classical physics seemed to guarantee that the future is completely determined by the present.
depends_on: QM interpretations (Principle 1), nature of spacetime (Principle 2), laws of nature

name: Symmetry and Physical Reality
text: The deepest laws of physics are connected to symmetries.
depends_on: Lagrangian/Hamiltonian mechanics, group theory, gauge theory

name: The Direction of Time and Statistical Mechanics
text: The laws of physics do not care about the direction of time -- they work the same forwards and backwards.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, thermodynamics, cosmology, time-reversal symmetry

name: Scientific Realism and Quantum Mechanics
text: Does physics describe reality as it really is, or just predict what we will observe?
depends_on: Epistemology (underdetermination, inference to best explanation), metaphysics (realism), QM interpretations (Principle 1)

name: Measurement Problem in QM (Wave Function Collapse, Interpretations)
text: Quantum mechanics' deepest problem is this: the math says that before you look, a particle can be in multiple states at once (superposition).
depends_on: Principle 1 (measurement problem overview), quantum formalism (Hilbert spaces, Born rule), metaphysics

name: Problem of Time in Quantum Gravity
text: In quantum mechanics, time ticks in the background -- it is the stage on which physics plays out.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, general relativity, Principle 2 (spacetime), philosophy of time

name: Conventionality of Simultaneity (Reichenbach, Grunbaum)
text: In special relativity, you synchronize distant clocks by bouncing light between them.
depends_on: Special relativity, Principle 2 (spacetime), philosophy of convention

name: Symmetry and Invariance in Physics (Structural Realism)
text: The deepest lesson of modern physics may be that reality is structure, not stuff.
depends_on: Principle 4 (symmetry), QM (permutation invariance), GR (diffeomorphism invariance), philosophy of science (scientific realism)

name: Arrow of Time (Entropic, Cosmological, Psychological)
text: Time has a direction: eggs break but do not unbreak, we age but do not grow young, we remember yesterday but not tomorrow.
depends_on: Principle 5 (direction of time overview), statistical mechanics, cosmology, philosophy of time

name: Determinism vs Indeterminism
text: Is the future written, or is there genuine randomness in the universe?
depends_on: Principle 3 (determinism overview), QM interpretations (Principle 1), laws of nature, philosophy of probability

name: Bell's Theorem and Non-Locality
text: Bell proved that quantum mechanics cannot be explained by any theory where particles have definite properties before measurement and where nothing travels faster than light.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (entanglement, Born rule), special relativity, Principle 1 (QM interpretations)

name: Black Hole Information Paradox
text: If you throw an encyclopedia into a black hole and it evaporates into featureless thermal radiation, where did the information go?
depends_on: General relativity, quantum field theory, thermodynamics, information theory

name: The Anthropic Principle
text: The universe seems suspiciously well-suited for life.
depends_on: Cosmology, particle physics, probability theory, philosophy of science

name: Problem of Time in Quantum Gravity (Extended)
text: In our most fundamental theories, time vanishes.
depends_on: Principle 8 (problem of time), GR, QM, philosophy of time

name: The Measurement Problem and Decoherence
text: Decoherence explains why the quantum weirdness of superposition and interference disappears for large objects.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, Principle 1 (measurement problem), Principle 7 (measurement problem expanded), statistical mechanics

name: Mach's Principle
text: When you spin a bucket of water and the surface becomes concave, Newton said this shows the water accelerates relative to absolute space.
depends_on: Spacetime (Principle 2), general relativity, philosophy of space and time

name: CPT Symmetry and Its Philosophical Significance
text: The laws of physics are symmetric under a combined operation: swap every particle for its antiparticle, flip the spatial coordinates left-to-right, and reverse the direction of time.
depends_on: Symmetry (Principle 4), quantum field theory, Lorentz invariance, direction of time (Principle 5)

name: Quantum Gravity Approaches
text: General relativity describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime.
depends_on: Problem of time (Principle 8/16), spacetime (Principle 2), Bell's theorem (Principle 13), black hole information paradox (Principle 14)

name: Quantum Logic
text: Does quantum mechanics force us to change the rules of logic itself?
depends_on: QM interpretations (Principle 1), logic (lattice theory), Hilbert space formalism

name: The Equivalence Principle (Philosophical Analysis)
text: Einstein noticed that being in a gravitational field feels exactly like being in an accelerating elevator.
depends_on: Spacetime (Principle 2), symmetry (Principle 4), scientific realism (Principle 6)

name: Underdetermination of Quantum Mechanical Interpretations
text: Modern physics is built on symmetry principles.
depends_on: - Noether's Theorems (Principle 7)

name: Free Energy Landscapes
text: Every biological molecule exists in a landscape of energy states, like a ball rolling over hills and valleys.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics (Boltzmann distribution, partition function), thermodynamics (free energy, entropy)

name: Molecular Motors and Mechanochemical Coupling
text: Biological molecular motors are tiny machines that convert chemical fuel (ATP) into movement and force.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, low-Reynolds-number hydrodynamics, chemical thermodynamics

name: Membrane Biophysics
text: Cell membranes are thin sheets of fat molecules that form spontaneously because their structure minimizes energy in water.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (self-assembly, hydrophobic effect), continuum mechanics (elasticity), fluid dynamics (2D diffusion)

name: Polymer Physics of Biomolecules
text: DNA and proteins are long, flexible chains, and their behavior can be understood using the same physics that describes synthetic polymers.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics (random walks, partition functions), elasticity, thermodynamics

name: Single-Molecule Mechanics and Force Spectroscopy
text: We can now grab individual molecules and pull on them, measuring how much force it takes to unfold a protein, unzip a DNA double helix, or break a molecular bond.
depends_on: Polymer physics (Principle 4), free energy landscapes (Principle 1), statistical mechanics (Kramers' theory)

name: Electrostatics and Screening in Biological Systems
text: Biological molecules are charged and operate in salty water.
depends_on: Electrostatics (Coulomb's law, Gauss's law), statistical mechanics (Boltzmann distribution), thermodynamics

name: Ion Channel Biophysics
text: Ion channels are the molecular gates that control electrical signaling in all cells.
depends_on: Electrostatics, thermodynamics, statistical mechanics.

name: Diffusion and Transport in Biological Systems
text: Inside cells, molecules move by random thermal motion (diffusion).
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, fluid dynamics (Stokes drag).

name: Biomechanics and Elasticity of Biological Materials
text: Biological structures are not rigid — they flex, stretch, and respond to forces.
depends_on: Elasticity theory, polymer physics, thermodynamics.

name: Photosynthesis Biophysics
text: Plants capture sunlight using molecular antennae and transfer the energy with near-perfect efficiency to reaction centers that convert it to chemical fuel.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, thermodynamics, spectroscopy.

name: Protein Folding and the Energy Landscape
text: Proteins fold into precisely the right 3D shape out of an astronomical number of possibilities, and they do it in milliseconds.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, polymer physics, free energy.

name: Stochastic Processes in Biology
text: Inside cells, many molecules exist in tiny numbers — sometimes just a handful.
depends_on: Probability theory, statistical mechanics, chemical kinetics.

name: Kramers' Rate Theory
text: Kramers calculated how fast a molecule crosses an energy barrier due to thermal fluctuations.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, Langevin dynamics, Principle 1 (free energy landscapes)

name: Fluctuation Theorems (Jarzynski, Crooks)
text: If you repeatedly pull apart a molecule, each time measuring the work, the Jarzynski equality lets you extract the exact equilibrium free energy difference from these non-equilibrium measurements.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, thermodynamics, single-molecule techniques (Principle 5)

name: Mechanotransduction
text: Cells feel their mechanical environment.
depends_on: Cell biology, biophysics (Principle 3, Principle 9), biochemistry

name: Liquid-Liquid Phase Separation (LLPS) in Cells
text: Cells organize themselves using liquid droplets -- certain proteins and RNAs spontaneously separate from the surrounding fluid to form dense condensates without needing a membrane.
depends_on: Polymer physics (Principle 4, Flory-Huggins), thermodynamics, statistical mechanics

name: Cooperative Binding and the Hill Equation
text: Hemoglobin picks up oxygen cooperatively: the first oxygen molecule is hard to bind, but once it binds, the protein changes shape, making the next oxygen molecule easier to bind.
depends_on: Free energy landscapes (Principle 1), statistical mechanics, protein structure

name: Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET)
text: FRET is a way to measure distances between molecules at the nanometer scale -- too small for microscopes to see directly.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics (dipole-dipole coupling), photophysics, spectroscopy

name: Patch-Clamp Electrophysiology
text: The patch-clamp technique lets you listen to a single ion channel opening and closing -- one molecule at a time.
depends_on: Ion channel biophysics (Principle 7), electrostatics, membrane biophysics (Principle 3)

name: Biological Pattern Formation (Turing Patterns)
text: How does a leopard get its spots?
depends_on: Diffusion (Principle 8), nonlinear dynamics, partial differential equations

name: Optical Trapping Theory
text: Optical tweezers use a focused laser beam to grab and hold tiny objects -- even single molecules.
depends_on: Electromagnetic theory (radiation pressure, gradient force), polymer physics (Principle 4), single-molecule mechanics (Principle 5)

name: Biological Noise and Stochastic Gene Expression (Detailed)
text: Even genetically identical cells in the same environment can behave very differently because gene expression is inherently noisy.
depends_on: Stochastic processes (Principle 12), Kramers' theory (Principle 13), chemical master equation

name: Allosteric Regulation and Conformational Selection
text: Proteins are not rigid machines -- they breathe and fluctuate between multiple shapes.
depends_on: - Free energy landscapes (Principle 1)

name: Macromolecular Crowding and Excluded Volume Effects
text: Inside a cell, molecules are packed so tightly that there is barely room to move.
depends_on: - Polymer physics (Principle 4)

name: DNA Nanotechnology and Structural DNA Design
text: DNA is not just a carrier of genetic information -- it is the world's most programmable building material.
depends_on: - Polymer physics of biomolecules (Principle 4)

name: Active Matter Physics
text: Ordinary matter sits in equilibrium -- a gas in a box eventually reaches a uniform state.
depends_on: - Diffusion and transport (Principle 8)

name: Protein Design and Inverse Folding (AI-Driven Biophysics)
text: Nature has spent billions of years evolving proteins -- molecular machines that do everything from digesting food to fighting infections.
depends_on: - Free energy landscapes (Principle 1)

name: Mechanobiology (Force as Biological Signal)
text: Cells do not just respond to chemical signals -- they also feel and respond to mechanical forces.
depends_on: - Polymer physics of biomolecules (Principle 4)

name: Cryo-Electron Microscopy Revolution
text: For decades, determining the 3D structure of a protein required growing crystals -- which many important proteins (especially membrane proteins and large complexes) refuse to do.
depends_on: - Protein folding energy landscape (Principle 11)

name: Single-Molecule Force Spectroscopy (Advanced Techniques)
text: How does a cell make a reliable decision when its internal signals are noisy and imprecise?
depends_on: - Stochastic Gene Expression (Principle 37)

name: The Computational Theory of Mind
text: Thinking is computing.
depends_on: Theory of computation (Turing machines), functionalism (philosophy of mind), information theory

name: Embodied Cognition
text: You do not think with your brain alone -- you think with your whole body and the world around it.
depends_on: Phenomenology, ecological psychology, robotics, neuroscience of sensorimotor systems

name: Marr's Three Levels of Analysis
text: To understand how the brain (or a computer) does something, you need to answer three questions at three different levels: (1) What problem is it solving?
depends_on: Functionalism (philosophy of mind), computational theory of mind (Principle 1)

name: Bayesian Brain Hypothesis
text: The brain is a prediction machine.
depends_on: Probability theory (Bayes' theorem), information theory, computational theory of mind (Principle 1)

name: Connectionism vs. Symbolicism
text: Is the mind more like a rule-following computer (processing symbols according to logic) or more like a brain (a network of simple elements that learns from experience)?
depends_on: Theory of computation, AI, neuroscience, philosophy of mind

name: Modularity of Mind
text: Your mind is not a general-purpose computer.
depends_on: Evolutionary biology (natural selection), neuroscience (neural specialization), cognitive psychology

name: Ecological Psychology (Gibson's Affordances)
text: The world offers us possibilities for action — a chair affords sitting, a handle affords grasping.
depends_on: Evolutionary biology, perceptual systems, environment-organism coupling.

name: The Extended Mind and Distributed Cognition
text: Your smartphone, your notebook, and the people you work with are part of your cognitive system, not just tools you use.
depends_on: Functionalism, philosophy of mind, distributed systems.

name: Attention and Cognitive Bottlenecks
text: You can't process everything at once.
depends_on: Information theory, neural coding, working memory.

name: Memory Systems and Consolidation
text: You have different kinds of memory — memory for facts and events (declarative) and memory for skills and habits (procedural).
depends_on: Neuroscience (hippocampus, neocortex), synaptic plasticity, molecular biology.

name: Language and Cognition
text: Humans are uniquely equipped for language.
depends_on: Linguistics, neuroscience, developmental psychology, computation.

name: Consciousness and the Hard Problem
text: Why does seeing red feel like something?
depends_on: Neuroscience, philosophy of mind, information theory.

name: Global Workspace Theory
text: Many brain processes run unconsciously in parallel.
depends_on: Principle 6 (modularity), Principle 12 (consciousness), neuroscience

name: Dual-Process Theory (System 1 / System 2)
text: You have two modes of thinking.
depends_on: Cognitive psychology, Principle 1 (computational theory), behavioral economics

name: Theory of Mind
text: Theory of mind is the ability to understand that other people have their own thoughts and beliefs, which may differ from yours.
depends_on: Developmental psychology, Principle 1 (CTM), Principle 6 (modularity), social neuroscience

name: Neural Darwinism (Edelman)
text: The brain is not programmed; it is selected.
depends_on: Evolutionary biology, developmental neuroscience, Principle 1 (CTM as contrast)

name: Dynamic Systems Approach to Cognition
text: Instead of thinking of the mind as a computer running programs, the dynamic systems approach sees cognition as a continuously evolving physical process -- like a river flowing, not a program executing.
depends_on: Dynamical systems theory, embodied cognition (Principle 2), nonlinear dynamics

name: Mirror Neurons and Simulation Theory
text: Mirror neurons fire both when you do something and when you watch someone else do the same thing.
depends_on: Theory of mind (Principle 15), embodied cognition (Principle 2), neuroscience

name: Cognitive Development as Dynamical Change
text: The brain appears to operate at a special balance point between two extremes: too much order (where neural activity dies out quickly and the brain cannot respond to inputs) and too much chaos (where neural activity explodes uncontrollably, as in a seizure).
depends_on: - Network Neuroscience (Principle 27)

name: Emergence
text: The whole is more than the sum of its parts.
depends_on: Systems with many interacting components, nonlinear interactions

name: Self-Organized Criticality
text: Some systems naturally evolve to a tipping point where small events and large events are connected by the same dynamics.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics (critical phenomena, phase transitions), dynamical systems

name: Power Laws and Heavy-Tailed Distributions
text: In many natural and social systems, the distribution of event sizes follows a pattern where most events are small but a few are enormous -- and these extreme events are far more common than a bell curve would predict.
depends_on: Probability theory, statistical mechanics, network theory

name: Chaos and Sensitivity to Initial Conditions
text: In a chaotic system, tiny differences in starting conditions grow exponentially over time, making long-term prediction impossible even though the system follows completely deterministic rules.
depends_on: Dynamical systems theory, differential equations, topology

name: Network Theory (Small Worlds and Scale-Free Networks)
text: Many real-world systems -- social networks, the Internet, biological networks, airline routes -- share surprising structural properties.
depends_on: Graph theory, probability theory, statistical mechanics (percolation theory)

name: Cellular Automata and Computational Universality
text: Imagine a grid of cells, each either alive or dead.
depends_on: Theory of computation (Turing completeness), dynamical systems, discrete mathematics

name: Agent-Based Modeling
text: Instead of writing equations for the whole system, model each individual agent (person, cell, animal) and its rules, then let them interact.
depends_on: Computation, probability, emergent behavior.

name: Dissipative Structures and Far-From-Equilibrium Systems
text: Order can spontaneously arise from disorder when energy flows through a system.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (non-equilibrium), dynamical systems, bifurcation theory.

name: The Edge of Chaos
text: Too much order means rigidity; too much chaos means instability.
depends_on: Dynamical systems, phase transitions, information theory.

name: Feedback Loops and Nonlinearity
text: When the output of a system feeds back to influence its own input, unexpected behaviors emerge.
depends_on: Control theory, dynamical systems, nonlinear mathematics.

name: Universality and Scaling Laws
text: Very different systems — magnets, fluids, economies, ecosystems — can behave in mathematically identical ways near tipping points.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, renormalization group, dimensional analysis.

name: Adaptation and Evolution in Complex Systems
text: Complex systems that can learn and evolve — organisms, markets, cultures, AI systems — share common principles: generate variation, select what works, and propagate the winners.
depends_on: Evolutionary theory, information theory, optimization.

name: Fitness Landscapes
text: Imagine evolution as a hike through a mountainous landscape where height represents fitness.
depends_on: Evolutionary theory, combinatorial optimization, statistical mechanics (spin glasses)

name: Renormalization Group in Complexity
text: The renormalization group is a powerful tool for understanding how systems look at different scales.
depends_on: Statistical mechanics, scaling and universality (Principle 11), dynamical systems

name: Information Integration and Complexity Measures
text: What makes a system truly complex, as opposed to merely random or merely ordered?
depends_on: Information theory (Shannon entropy, mutual information), computation theory, Principles 1 and 11

name: Percolation Theory
text: Imagine a grid where each cell is randomly filled or empty.
depends_on: Probability theory, statistical mechanics, universality (Principle 11), network theory (Principle 5)

name: Boolean Networks (Kauffman)
text: Kauffman asked: what if you connected genes randomly, with each gene controlled by a few others, and let the network run?
depends_on: Network theory (Principle 5), edge of chaos (Principle 9), cellular automata (Principle 6)

name: Network Robustness and Vulnerability
text: The internet, airline networks, and cellular networks are all organized around a few highly connected "hubs." This makes them remarkably resilient to random failures (most of the time, only minor nodes go down), but catastrophically vulnerable to targeted attacks on hubs.
depends_on: Network theory (Principle 5), percolation theory (Principle 16), power laws (Principle 3)

name: Evolutionary Computation
text: Can we solve hard problems by simulating evolution on a computer?
depends_on: Adaptation and evolution (Principle 12), fitness landscapes (Principle 13), optimization

name: Multilayer and Multiplex Networks
text: Real networks are not simple: people are connected by friendship, work, and family simultaneously; infrastructure systems (power, internet, water) depend on each other.
depends_on: Network theory (Principle 5), percolation theory (Principle 16), network robustness (Principle 18)

name: Reservoir Computing and Echo State Networks
text: What if you could compute with any complex dynamical system?
depends_on: Edge of chaos (Principle 9), network theory (Principle 5), dynamical systems, machine learning

name: Tipping Points and Critical Transitions
text: Some systems do not change gradually -- they can suddenly flip from one state to a completely different one.
depends_on: - Chaos and sensitivity (Principle 4)

name: Stigmergy and Swarm Intelligence
text: How do thousands of ants find the shortest path to food without a leader, a map, or direct communication?
depends_on: - Agent-based modeling (Principle 7)

name: Deep Learning Theory (Double Descent and Grokking)
text: Classical statistics says: make your model too complex and it will overfit (memorize noise instead of learning the pattern).
depends_on: - Emergence (Principle 1)

name: Network Epidemiology
text: Classical epidemiology assumes everyone mixes randomly -- any person is equally likely to encounter any other.
depends_on: - Network theory (Principle 5)

name: Agent-Based Modeling (Computational Foundations)
text: Agent-based modeling is like building a virtual world where you program individual "agents" (people, cells, animals, firms) with simple rules and then watch what happens when they interact.
depends_on: - Emergence (Principle 1)

name: Causal Emergence and Effective Information
text: Does all causation ultimately happen at the level of atoms and molecules?
depends_on: - Emergence (Principle 1)

name: Foundation Models for Scientific Discovery
text: AI is not just analyzing data -- it is making scientific discoveries.
depends_on: - Complex adaptive systems (Principle 12)

name: AI for Scientific Discovery (Automated Science)
text: When two brain regions both carry information about a stimulus, are they saying the same thing (redundancy), different things (unique information), or something that only emerges when you consider both together (synergy)?
depends_on: - Network Theory (Principle 5)

name: Sequence Alignment and Dynamic Programming
text: To compare two biological sequences (e.g., two genes from different species), we line them up to find the best match, allowing for insertions and deletions (gaps).
depends_on: Dynamic programming (Bellman), scoring matrices, gap models, algorithms (complexity analysis)

name: Phylogenetic Inference
text: By comparing DNA or protein sequences, we can reconstruct the evolutionary tree of life -- which species are most closely related and when they diverged.
depends_on: Sequence alignment (Principle 1), evolutionary models (substitution models), statistics (ML, Bayesian inference), graph theory (tree structures)

name: Genome Assembly
text: Sequencing a genome is like shredding a book into small fragments and trying to reconstruct it.
depends_on: Graph theory (Eulerian/Hamiltonian paths, de Bruijn graphs), algorithms, sequence alignment (Principle 1)

name: Network Biology
text: Biology is organized as networks: genes regulate other genes, proteins interact with other proteins, metabolites are connected by chemical reactions.
depends_on: Graph theory, statistics, algorithms, high-throughput experimental data

name: Molecular Dynamics Simulation
text: Molecular dynamics is a computational microscope: it simulates the motion of every atom in a protein, DNA strand, or cell membrane, solving Newton's equations step by step.
depends_on: Classical mechanics (Newton's equations), statistical mechanics, numerical methods, force fields (empirical potential energy functions)

name: Hidden Markov Models in Biological Sequence Analysis
text: Many biological sequences (DNA, proteins) have hidden structure: different regions of a gene serve different functions (exons, introns, regulatory regions), and the boundaries are not directly visible.
depends_on: Probability theory (Markov chains, Bayesian inference), dynamic programming (Viterbi, Forward algorithms)

name: Protein Structure Prediction
text: Given just the sequence of amino acids, can we predict how the protein folds?
depends_on: Machine learning (deep neural networks), evolutionary information (MSAs), biophysics.

name: Gene Regulatory Network Inference
text: Genes don't work in isolation — they turn each other on and off in complex networks.
depends_on: Molecular biology, statistics, dynamical systems, graph theory.

name: Population Genetics Simulation and Coalescent Theory
text: Instead of simulating evolution forward from the past, the coalescent looks backward from the present: when did our genes last share a common ancestor?
depends_on: Probability theory (random processes), population genetics, algorithms.

name: Machine Learning in Biology
text: Modern computational biology increasingly relies on machine learning to find patterns in massive biological datasets — from predicting the effect of mutations to classifying cell types from single-cell data to designing new proteins.
depends_on: Statistics, optimization, neural network theory, large biological datasets.

name: Systems Biology and Multi-Scale Modeling
text: Biology doesn't work at just one level — molecules affect cells, cells affect tissues, tissues affect organisms.
depends_on: Differential equations, network theory, bioinformatics, high-throughput experimental data.

name: Single-Cell Genomics and Spatial Transcriptomics
text: Instead of grinding up tissue and measuring the average gene expression, single-cell genomics measures every individual cell separately.
depends_on: Statistics (high-dimensional), machine learning, molecular biology, microfluidics.

name: BLAST Algorithm and Heuristic Sequence Search
text: BLAST is the most-used tool in bioinformatics.
depends_on: Sequence alignment (Principle 1), extreme value distribution statistics, indexing

name: Metabolic Flux Analysis
text: Metabolism is a vast network of chemical reactions.
depends_on: Linear algebra, linear programming, biochemistry, genomics

name: Molecular Docking and Virtual Screening
text: Molecular docking is like trying every possible key in a lock -- computationally.
depends_on: Molecular dynamics (Principle 5), protein structure prediction (Principle 7), statistical mechanics

name: Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS)
text: GWAS is a brute-force approach to finding which genetic variants are associated with a disease or trait.
depends_on: Statistics (regression, multiple testing), population genetics, genomics

name: RNA-Seq and Transcriptomic Analysis
text: RNA-seq tells you which genes are active (being read) in a cell or tissue, and how much.
depends_on: Genomics, statistics (negative binomial models, multiple testing), machine learning (Principle 10)

name: Gillespie's Stochastic Simulation Algorithm
text: Inside a cell, chemical reactions do not happen continuously -- they happen as discrete, random events.
depends_on: Chemical master equation, probability theory (exponential distribution, Poisson processes), stochastic processes

name: Metagenomics and Microbiome Analysis
text: Most microbes cannot be grown in the lab, so how do we study them?
depends_on: Genome assembly (Principle 3), sequence alignment (Principle 1), machine learning (Principle 10), statistics

name: Variant Calling and Genomic Variant Analysis
text: After sequencing a person's genome, how do you find the places where their DNA differs from the reference?
depends_on: Sequence alignment (Principle 1), Bayesian inference, machine learning (Principle 10), genomics

name: Multi-Omics Data Integration
text: Measuring just one type of molecule (e.g., mRNA) gives an incomplete picture of a cell's state.
depends_on: - Machine learning in biology (Principle 10)

name: CRISPR Screen Analysis
text: CRISPR screens let you knock out every gene in the genome, one at a time, across millions of cells in a single experiment.
depends_on: - Machine learning in biology (Principle 10)

name: Foundation Models in Biology (ESM, AlphaFold)
text: Just as GPT learned language by reading billions of words, biological foundation models learn the "language" of biology by processing millions of protein sequences, gene expression profiles, or molecular structures.
depends_on: - Machine learning in biology (Principle 10)

name: Spatial Transcriptomics Analysis
text: Standard single-cell RNA sequencing tells you what each cell is doing genetically, but it destroys the tissue -- you lose the information about where each cell was located.
depends_on: - Single-cell genomics (Principle 12)

name: Digital Twins in Medicine
text: Imagine a computer model of your own body -- a "digital twin" -- that knows your genome, your medical history, and your current health status, updated in real time from your wearable devices.
depends_on: - Systems biology (Principle 11)

name: Causal Inference in Genomics (Mendelian Randomization)
text: Does high cholesterol actually cause heart disease, or do they just happen to correlate?
depends_on: - GWAS (Principle 16)

name: Foundation Models in Biology (Protein and Genomic Language Models)
text: Just as ChatGPT learned language by reading billions of sentences, protein language models learn the "language" of proteins by reading billions of protein sequences.
depends_on: - Machine learning in biology (Principle 10)

name: Spatial Transcriptomics and Computational Tissue Biology
text: Traditional genomics grinds up a tissue sample and measures the average gene expression, destroying all information about where each cell was.
depends_on: - Single-cell genomics (Principle 19)

name: Protein Design and RFdiffusion
text: For decades, scientists dreamed of designing proteins from scratch -- custom-made molecular machines that do not exist in nature.
depends_on: - Protein structure prediction (Principle 7)

name: Evolutionary Scale Models and Fitness Prediction
text: Just as large language models like GPT learn the rules of human language by reading billions of sentences, protein language models learn the "language" of proteins by reading billions of amino acid sequences shaped by evolution.
depends_on: - Machine Learning in Biology (Principle 10)

name: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
text: When building a predictive model, you face a fundamental tension: a simple model may be systematically wrong (bias), while a complex model may be too sensitive to the specific training data (variance).
depends_on: Probability theory, statistical estimation theory

name: The Curse of Dimensionality
text: In high dimensions, everything is far from everything else, and the amount of data you need to fill the space grows exponentially with the number of features.
depends_on: Geometry (volume of hyperspheres), probability in high dimensions, statistical estimation

name: Cross-Validation
text: How do you know if your model will work on new data?
depends_on: Statistical estimation theory, bias-variance tradeoff (Principle 1)

name: Regularization
text: To prevent a model from memorizing the training data (overfitting), we add a penalty for complexity.
depends_on: Bias-variance tradeoff (Principle 1), optimization theory, Bayesian inference (regularization as prior)

name: Statistical Learning Theory (VC Dimension)
text: How complex can a model class be before it starts memorizing data instead of learning patterns?
depends_on: Probability theory, computational learning theory (PAC learning), combinatorics

name: The Bootstrap and Resampling Methods
text: How do you estimate the uncertainty of a statistical estimate when you have only one dataset?
depends_on: Probability theory (empirical distribution function), computational methods, statistical inference

name: Causal Inference and the Distinction from Correlation
text: Just because two things are correlated does not mean one causes the other.
depends_on: Probability theory, graph theory, experimental design, statistics

name: Dimensionality Reduction and Manifold Learning
text: Data that looks high-dimensional often has a simpler underlying structure.
depends_on: Linear algebra (eigendecomposition, SVD), topology, optimization.

name: Ensemble Methods
text: Instead of relying on one model, train many models and combine their predictions.
depends_on: Bias-variance tradeoff, bootstrap, decision trees.

name: Feature Engineering and Selection
text: How you represent your data to the algorithm is often more important than which algorithm you use.
depends_on: Domain knowledge, statistics, optimization.

name: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
text: Before building any model, look at your data.
depends_on: Descriptive statistics, visualization principles.

name: Deep Learning Fundamentals
text: Deep learning stacks many simple processing layers to learn complex patterns directly from raw data.
depends_on: Linear algebra, calculus (chain rule), optimization, probability.

name: The Manifold Hypothesis
text: Why does machine learning work so well on images, language, and other seemingly high-dimensional data?
depends_on: Differential geometry (manifolds), dimensionality reduction (Principle 8), curse of dimensionality (Principle 2)

name: Transfer Learning and Foundation Models
text: Instead of training a model from scratch for every new task, start with a model that has already learned general patterns from a large dataset and adapt it to your specific problem.
depends_on: Deep learning (Principle 12), representation learning, manifold hypothesis (Principle 13)

name: Fairness, Accountability, and Ethical AI
text: Algorithms can be unfair.
depends_on: Statistics, causal inference (Principle 7), ethics, social science, law

name: No Free Lunch Theorem
text: There is no universally best learning algorithm.
depends_on: Combinatorics, probability theory, computational learning theory

name: Information Criteria (AIC and BIC)
text: When choosing between models, you want the one that best fits the data -- but a more complex model always fits better, even if the extra complexity is just fitting noise.
depends_on: Maximum likelihood estimation, Kullback-Leibler divergence, Bayesian inference, bias-variance tradeoff (Principle 1)

name: Bayesian Optimization
text: Bayesian optimization is a smart way to find the best settings for something when each experiment is expensive.
depends_on: Bayesian inference, Gaussian processes, optimization theory, cross-validation (Principle 3)

name: Simpson's Paradox
text: A treatment can look beneficial in every subgroup but harmful overall -- or vice versa.
depends_on: Causal inference (Principle 7), probability theory, confounding

name: Survival Analysis
text: How do you analyze data where the outcome is "time until something happens" and you do not always get to see the event?
depends_on: Probability theory (hazard and survival functions), maximum likelihood estimation, regression

name: Time Series Decomposition and Forecasting
text: Time series data is everywhere: stock prices, weather, web traffic, sensor readings.
depends_on: Probability theory (stochastic processes), regression, signal processing, deep learning (Principle 12)

name: Conformal Prediction
text: How confident should you be in a prediction?
depends_on: - Cross-validation (Principle 3)

name: Data Drift and Model Monitoring
text: A model trained on last year's data may fail on today's data because the world has changed.
depends_on: - Bias-variance tradeoff (Principle 1)

name: Synthetic Data Generation
text: What if you need data to train a model but cannot use real data because it contains sensitive patient information, or you simply do not have enough?
depends_on: - Deep learning (Principle 12)

name: Causal Machine Learning
text: Standard machine learning predicts what will happen.
depends_on: - Causal inference (Principle 7)

name: Conformal Prediction and Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification
text: Most machine learning models give you a prediction but no honest measure of how uncertain that prediction is.
depends_on: - Bias-variance tradeoff (Principle 1)

name: Foundation Models and Transfer Learning in Data Science
text: Foundation models like GPT-4 and Claude represent a paradigm shift in data science: instead of building a specialized model for each task, you pre-train one massive model on enormous amounts of data, and then adapt it to whatever you need.
depends_on: - Deep learning (Principle 12)

name: LLM-Powered Analytics and Data Agents
text: What if you could analyze data just by asking questions in plain English?
depends_on: - Foundation models (Principle 27)

name: Data Mesh Architecture
text: What if you could create artificial data that looks, acts, and works just like real data — but does not contain any real person's information?
depends_on: - Statistical Learning Theory (Principle 5)

name: The Nature of Information
text: What is information?
depends_on: Probability theory, computation theory, philosophy of language (meaning, truth)

name: Landauer's Principle
text: Erasing information has a physical cost: it must generate heat.
depends_on: Thermodynamics (Second Law, entropy), information theory (Shannon entropy), statistical mechanics

name: It from Bit (Wheeler)
text: Is the universe ultimately made of information rather than matter and energy?
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, information theory, philosophy of physics

name: Information as a Fundamental Physical Quantity
text: Information is not just an abstract concept -- it is a physical quantity with real constraints.
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, general relativity, thermodynamics, information theory

name: Semantic Information and the Challenge of Meaning
text: Shannon's theory tells us how much information a message carries, but not what it means.
depends_on: Information theory (Shannon), philosophy of language (meaning, truth), epistemology

name: Algorithmic Information Theory and Kolmogorov Complexity
text: How complex is a piece of data?
depends_on: Theory of computation (Turing machines, uncomputability), probability theory

name: Levels of Abstraction (LoA)
text: How you observe a system determines what you see.
depends_on: Philosophy of science, information theory, computer science.

name: The Ethics of Information (Information Ethics)
text: Information has moral value.
depends_on: Ethics, information theory, philosophy of information.

name: Digital Physics and Computational Universe Hypothesis
text: What if the universe is literally a computer?
depends_on: Theory of computation, quantum mechanics, information theory.

name: Information Closure and the Chinese Room
text: Even if a computer perfectly simulates understanding Chinese, does it actually understand?
depends_on: Philosophy of mind, computation theory, semantics.

name: Informational Structural Realism
text: At the deepest level, reality is not made of "stuff" (matter) but of patterns and relationships — information structures.
depends_on: Philosophy of science, structural realism, information theory, quantum mechanics.

name: The Philosophy of AI and Machine Ethics
text: As AI becomes more capable, we face profound philosophical questions: Is AI truly intelligent?
depends_on: Philosophy of mind, ethics, computation theory, information theory.

name: Shannon vs. Semantic Information — The Fundamental Divide
text: Shannon's information theory counts bits but ignores meaning.
depends_on: Information theory (Shannon, Principle 1), philosophy of language, epistemology, Principle 5

name: Information Closure
text: Some systems are informationally "self-contained" -- their future behavior is mostly determined by their own internal state, not by what is happening outside them.
depends_on: Information theory (mutual information), dynamical systems, Principle 1, Principle 7

name: Digital Ontology
text: Is the universe fundamentally digital (made of discrete bits) or analog (made of continuous quantities)?
depends_on: Quantum mechanics, theory of computation, Principle 3 (It from Bit), Principle 9 (Digital Physics)

name: Floridi's Method of Levels of Abstraction — Extended
text: Floridi's method of Levels of Abstraction is a powerful tool for thinking clearly about complex systems.
depends_on: Philosophy of science, computer science (data abstraction, interfaces), Principle 7 (basic LoA)

name: The Fourth Revolution (Floridi)
text: Copernicus showed we are not at the center of the universe.
depends_on: Nature of information (Principle 1), information ethics (Principle 8), philosophy of AI (Principle 12), levels of abstraction (Principle 7)

name: Constructor Theory (Deutsch and Marletto)
text: Instead of asking "what happens given these initial conditions and these laws of motion?" constructor theory asks "what transformations are possible and what are impossible, and what must be true for them to be possible?" This is a radical shift.
depends_on: Nature of information (Principle 1), Landauer's principle (Principle 2), information as physical quantity (Principle 4)

name: Quantum Information Philosophy
text: Quantum information is fundamentally different from classical information.
depends_on: Nature of information (Principle 1), it from bit (Principle 3), information as physical quantity (Principle 4), algorithmic information theory (Principle 6)

name: The Logic of Information Flow (Barwise and Seligman)
text: How does information flow from one system to another?
depends_on: Nature of information (Principle 1), semantic information (Principle 5), category theory, logic

name: Info-Computational Naturalism (Dodig-Crnkovic)
text: What if everything in nature -- from atomic interactions to brain processes -- is computation?
depends_on: It from bit (Principle 3), digital physics (Principle 9), nature of information (Principle 1), constructor theory (Principle 18)

name: Philosophy of Computer Simulation
text: When a climate scientist runs a simulation of the Earth's atmosphere, or a biologist simulates protein folding, are the results real knowledge?
depends_on: Epistemology, philosophy of science, digital physics (Principle 9), levels of abstraction (Principle 7)

name: Information Thermodynamics
text: Information and energy are deeply connected.
depends_on: - Landauer's principle (Principle 2)

name: Philosophy of Big Data
text: Big data promised to solve everything: with enough data, patterns reveal themselves without the need for theory.
depends_on: - Nature of information (Principle 1)

name: Right to Be Forgotten and Information Fiduciary
text: Should you have the right to make embarrassing information about yourself disappear from the internet?
depends_on: - Information ethics (Principle 8)

name: AI Existential Risk and the Alignment Problem
text: What happens when we build AI smarter than any human?
depends_on: - Philosophy of AI ethics (Principle 12)

name: Philosophy of Simulation and Digital Ontology
