text: Computer simulations have become indispensable in science: we simulate the climate to predict global warming, simulate galaxies to test cosmological theories, and simulate economies to evaluate policies.
depends_on: - Philosophy of computation (related to Principles 1-5)

name: Epistemic Justice in the Information Age
text: Who gets to know, and whose knowledge counts?
depends_on: - Information ethics (Principle 8)

name: AI Safety Philosophy and Existential Risk
text: How do we make sure that super-intelligent AI does not accidentally (or deliberately) cause a catastrophe?
depends_on: - Philosophy of AI ethics (Principle 12)

name: Digital Personhood and Informational Identity
text: When a weather model predicts a hurricane, when AlphaFold predicts a protein structure, when a neural network diagnoses cancer from a medical image — do these algorithms "know" something?
depends_on: - Philosophy of Computer Simulation (Principle 22)

name: Lift Generation and Airfoil Theory
text: An airplane wing generates lift because air flowing over the curved upper surface speeds up and its pressure drops, while air below moves more slowly at higher pressure.
depends_on: - Fluid mechanics (potential flow, Euler and Navier-Stokes equations)

name: Bernoulli's Principle in Aerodynamics
text: In flowing air, where the velocity increases, the pressure decreases, and vice versa.
depends_on: - Conservation of energy (mechanical energy of fluid elements)

name: Thin Airfoil Theory
text: Thin airfoil theory treats the wing cross-section as a nearly flat plate with some curvature (camber).
depends_on: - Potential flow theory (Laplace equation)

name: Prandtl Lifting-Line Theory
text: A real wing has finite span, so air leaks around the wingtips from high pressure below to low pressure above, creating trailing vortices.
depends_on: - Thin airfoil theory (AE03) for sectional lift

name: Compressible Flow and Mach Number Regimes
text: At low speeds, air acts like an incompressible fluid.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (isentropic relations, stagnation properties)

name: Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation
text: A rocket works by throwing mass (exhaust) backward very fast, which pushes the rocket forward.
depends_on: - Newton's second and third laws

name: Specific Impulse
text: Specific impulse tells you how many seconds one kilogram of propellant can produce one kilogram of thrust.
depends_on: - Rocket equation (AE06)

name: Kepler's Laws and Orbital Mechanics
text: Satellites and planets follow paths shaped by gravity.
depends_on: - Newton's law of universal gravitation

name: Spacecraft Attitude Control
text: A spacecraft must point its antenna at Earth, its solar panels at the Sun, and its instruments at the target --- simultaneously and precisely.
depends_on: - Euler's rotational equations (rigid-body dynamics)

name: Re-entry Aerothermodynamics
text: When a spacecraft comes back from orbit, it is traveling at ~28,000 km/h.
depends_on: - Compressible flow theory (AE05) at hypersonic speeds

name: Structural Weight Optimization
text: Every kilogram of structure is a kilogram less of fuel or payload.
depends_on: - Stress analysis (bending, buckling, fatigue)

name: Aeroelasticity and Flutter
text: An aircraft wing is not rigid --- it bends and twists under air loads.
depends_on: - Structural dynamics (natural modes, frequencies, damping)

name: Radar Cross-Section and Stealth
text: Radar detects aircraft by sending out radio waves and listening for the echo.
depends_on: - Electromagnetic theory (Maxwell's equations, scattering)

name: Satellite Communication Link Budget
text: A satellite communication system must transmit a signal strong enough to be received clearly after traveling tens of thousands of kilometers through space and atmosphere.
depends_on: - Electromagnetic wave propagation (inverse-square law, atmospheric attenuation)

name: Composite Materials in Aerospace
text: Carbon fiber composites are lighter and stronger than aluminum, which is why modern aircraft (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) are more than 50% composite by weight.
depends_on: - Classical lamination theory (ABD matrix)

name: Orbital Perturbations and Station-Keeping
text: Kepler's laws describe a perfect world with a point-mass Earth.
depends_on: - Kepler's laws and orbital mechanics (AE08)

name: Spacecraft Thermal Control
text: In space, a satellite can be broiling in sunlight on one side and freezing in shadow on the other.
depends_on: - Stefan-Boltzmann radiation law (radiative heat transfer in vacuum)

name: Composite Failure Modes in Aerospace Structures
text: Unlike metals, which fail by yielding and cracking in a relatively straightforward way, composites can fail in many different ways: fibers can break, the resin matrix can crack, and the layers can peel apart (delaminate).
depends_on: - Composite mechanics (AE15, lamination theory)

name: Aircraft Flight Dynamics and Stability Derivatives
text: When an aircraft is disturbed from steady flight by a gust or a control input, it responds by pitching, rolling, and yawing.
depends_on: - Aerodynamics (AE01-AE04 for force and moment coefficients)

name: Turbofan Propulsion Cycle Analysis
text: A turbofan engine produces thrust by accelerating a large mass of air by a small amount (the bypass stream) rather than a small mass by a large amount (pure jet).
depends_on: - Brayton cycle thermodynamics (compressor, combustor, turbine analysis)

name: Hypersonic Flow Phenomena
text: At hypersonic speeds --- five times the speed of sound and above --- air stops behaving like a simple gas.
depends_on: - Compressible flow (AE05) extended to very high Mach numbers

name: Space Debris and Orbital Environment Management
text: Earth orbit is increasingly congested with space junk --- dead satellites, rocket parts, and millions of tiny fragments from collisions and explosions.
depends_on: - Orbital mechanics (AE08, AE16) for debris trajectory prediction

name: Mission Design and Delta-V Budgeting
text: Before building a spacecraft, engineers must design the mission trajectory and add up all the velocity changes (delta-v) needed to get from launch to the final orbit or destination.
depends_on: - Orbital mechanics (AE08) and perturbation theory (AE16)

name: Digital Twin for Aerospace Vehicle Lifecycle Management
text: An aerospace digital twin is a virtual copy of a specific airplane or satellite that ages alongside the real vehicle.
depends_on: - Structural weight optimization (AE11)

name: Additive Manufacturing Physics for Aerospace Structures
text: Aerospace 3D printing (additive manufacturing) builds metal parts by melting thin layers of metal powder with a laser.
depends_on: - Structural weight optimization (AE11)

name: Metamaterial and Morphing Aerodynamic Structures
text: Morphing wings change shape smoothly during flight --- twisting, bending, or changing their camber --- instead of using rigid flaps and slats with gaps.
depends_on: - Lift generation and airfoil theory (AE01)

name: Hypersonic Aerothermodynamics and Real-Gas Effects
text: When a vehicle flies faster than five times the speed of sound, the air in front of it gets so hot that it starts to change chemically --- oxygen molecules break apart, nitrogen molecules break apart, and the gas can even become electrically charged plasma.
depends_on: - Compressible flow and Mach regimes (AE05) for shock wave physics

name: Scramjet Propulsion Physics
text: A scramjet is a jet engine with no moving parts that works only at hypersonic speeds.
depends_on: - Compressible flow and Mach regimes (AE05) for inlet shock system design

name: Space Situational Awareness and Conjunction Assessment
text: With over 30,000 tracked objects in orbit (and millions of smaller debris pieces), space is increasingly congested.
depends_on: - Kepler's laws and orbital mechanics (AE08) for orbit propagation

name: Electric Propulsion
text: Chemical rockets are like sprinters: huge thrust for short bursts.
depends_on: - Rocket equation (AE06) --- high I_sp dramatically reduces propellant mass

name: Systems Engineering V-Model
text: Building an aircraft or spacecraft is too complex for any one person or team to grasp entirely.
depends_on: - Requirements engineering (stakeholder analysis, functional decomposition)

name: Boundary Layer Transition and Its Aerodynamic Effects
text: When air flows over an aircraft wing, the thin layer of air nearest the surface (the boundary layer) starts out smooth and orderly (laminar).
depends_on: - Boundary layer theory (Prandtl, 1904)

name: Radiation Shielding for Deep-Space Human Missions
text: Beyond Earth's protective magnetic field, astronauts are exposed to a constant barrage of high-energy particles from the galaxy and occasional intense bursts from the Sun.
depends_on: - Kepler's laws and orbital mechanics (AE08) for trajectory design minimizing transit time

name: Plasma Propulsion: Hall-Effect Thruster Physics
text: A Hall-effect thruster is an electric rocket engine that uses magnetic fields to trap electrons and electric fields to shoot ions out the back at enormous speed — 10 to 20 times faster than a chemical rocket's exhaust.
depends_on: - Electric propulsion fundamentals (AE24) for the I_sp versus thrust tradeoff

name: Aeroelastic Tailoring of Composite Wings
text: Composite materials let aerospace engineers do something impossible with metal wings: make a wing that automatically twists to unload itself when it bends.
depends_on: - Composite materials (AE12) for laminate mechanics and ABD stiffness matrix

name: In-Situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) for Space Missions
text: ISRU means "living off the land" in space — using the materials available at your destination instead of bringing everything from Earth.
depends_on: - Rocket equation (AE06) establishing the mass leverage that ISRU provides

name: Computational Aeroacoustics and Noise Prediction
text: Computational aeroacoustics predicts the noise generated when air flows around and through aerospace vehicles and their components — jet engines, propellers, landing gear, airframe cavities, and helicopter rotors.
depends_on: - Compressible flow (AE05) for the governing equations of gas dynamics

name: Active Flow Control for Aerodynamic Performance
text: Active flow control uses small devices on an aircraft surface to inject tiny amounts of energy into the airflow at precisely the right location and frequency to dramatically change how the air behaves.
depends_on: - Boundary layer theory (AE26) for understanding separation and transition mechanisms

name: Cislunar Trajectory Design and Three-Body Dynamics
text: When navigating between the Earth and Moon, the simple two-body orbit equations used for Earth-centered missions break down because both Earth's and Moon's gravity matter simultaneously.
depends_on: - Orbital mechanics (AE08) for Keplerian orbit fundamentals and Delta-v budgeting

name: Rotating Detonation Engine Thermodynamics
text: A rotating detonation engine replaces the steady, gentle burning in a conventional jet engine combustion chamber with a continuously spinning detonation wave --- an explosion front traveling at supersonic speed around a ring-shaped chamber thousands of times per second.
depends_on: - Turbofan propulsion cycle (AE20) for comparison with conventional Brayton cycle combustion

name: Morphing Aircraft Structures
text: Morphing aircraft can smoothly change their wing shape in flight --- extending or folding the wingspan, curving the wing profile, or twisting the wing --- to be optimally efficient in every flight phase.
depends_on: - Aeroelastic tailoring (AE32) for coupling between structural deformation and aerodynamic loads

name: Biocompatibility
text: When you put something in or on the human body, the body reacts.
depends_on: - Materials science (surface chemistry, degradation products, wear debris)

name: Biomechanics: Tissue Mechanics
text: The body's tissues are remarkable engineering materials, but they behave nothing like steel or aluminum.
depends_on: - Continuum mechanics (stress, strain, constitutive relations)

name: Medical Imaging: X-ray and Computed Tomography
text: X-rays pass through the body, and different tissues absorb different amounts: bone absorbs a lot (appears white), air absorbs very little (appears black), and soft tissues are in between.
depends_on: - X-ray physics (photon interaction with matter: photoelectric effect, Compton scattering)

name: Medical Imaging: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
text: MRI uses a powerful magnet and radio waves to make images of the body's soft tissues with exquisite detail and no radiation.
depends_on: - Nuclear physics (nuclear magnetic moment, precession)

name: Medical Imaging: Ultrasound
text: Ultrasound works like sonar: it sends sound pulses into the body and listens for echoes that bounce back from tissue boundaries.
depends_on: - Acoustics (wave propagation, reflection, refraction, attenuation)

name: Medical Imaging: Positron Emission Tomography (PET)
text: PET imaging traces the biological activity inside the body.
depends_on: - Nuclear physics (positron emission, annihilation, gamma ray detection)

name: Biosensor Design
text: A biosensor is a device that detects a specific biological or chemical substance by combining a biological recognition element (like an enzyme that only reacts with glucose) with a physical detector (like an electrode that measures current).
depends_on: - Biochemistry (enzyme kinetics, antibody-antigen binding, nucleic acid hybridization)

name: Drug Delivery Systems
text: A pill dissolves and floods your body with drug all at once --- the level spikes (possibly causing side effects) and then drops (possibly becoming ineffective).
depends_on: - Polymer science (degradation, diffusion through polymers)

name: Tissue Engineering and Scaffolds
text: Tissue engineering aims to grow replacement body parts in the lab.
depends_on: - Cell biology (stem cells, differentiation, extracellular matrix interactions)

name: Prosthetics and Implant Design
text: When a joint, limb, or organ is damaged beyond repair, an engineered replacement must take over its function.
depends_on: - Biomechanics (BE02) --- loading analysis, stress shielding

name: Physiological Signal Processing: ECG and EEG
text: The heart and brain produce tiny electrical signals that can be picked up by electrodes on the skin.
depends_on: - Electrophysiology (ionic currents in excitable cells)

name: Compartment Models in Pharmacokinetics
text: After you take a drug, it distributes throughout the body and is gradually eliminated.
depends_on: - Ordinary differential equations (first-order linear systems)

name: Rehabilitation Engineering
text: Rehabilitation engineering helps people with disabilities regain independence.
depends_on: - Biomechanics (human movement analysis, ergonomics)

name: Regulatory Principles: FDA Device Classification
text: Before any medical device can be sold, it must be shown to be safe and effective.
depends_on: - Risk assessment (intended use, hazard analysis, failure mode analysis)

name: Neural Interfaces and Brain-Computer Interfaces
text: Neural interfaces are devices that connect directly to the brain or nerves, either to listen to neural signals (for controlling a computer or prosthetic) or to inject electrical stimulation (for treating Parkinson's disease, restoring hearing in cochlear implants, or restoring vision).
depends_on: - Neuroscience (neural coding, brain anatomy, electrode-tissue interface)

name: Bioinstrumentation: Amplification and Noise
text: Biological signals are tiny --- microvolts from the brain, millivolts from the heart --- and they must be extracted from a body that acts as an antenna for 50/60 Hz power-line noise.
depends_on: - Electrical engineering (op-amp theory, noise analysis, filter design)

name: Clinical Trials for Medical Devices
text: Before a new medical device can be marketed for high-risk applications, it must be tested in patients.
depends_on: - Biostatistics (hypothesis testing, power analysis, survival analysis)

name: Microfluidics for Diagnostics
text: Microfluidics shrinks a laboratory onto a chip the size of a credit card.
depends_on: - Fluid mechanics at low Reynolds number (Stokes flow, Poiseuille equation)

name: 3D Bioprinting
text: 3D bioprinting uses modified 3D printers to build living tissue structures layer by layer, using a "bioink" made of cells suspended in a gel-like material.
depends_on: - Tissue engineering (BE09) --- biological requirements for cell survival and tissue formation

name: Wearable Sensor Systems for Health Monitoring
text: Wearable health devices --- smartwatches, fitness bands, patches, and smart clothing --- use tiny sensors to continuously track vital signs and activity.
depends_on: - Optics and photonics (PPG, SpO_2 measurement principles)

name: Radiation Therapy Physics
text: Radiation therapy aims to destroy cancer cells while sparing healthy tissue.
depends_on: - Radiation physics (photon and particle interactions with matter: photoelectric, Compton, pair production)

name: Medical Robotics
text: Surgical robots do not operate autonomously --- they are sophisticated tools that extend the surgeon's capabilities.
depends_on: - Robotics (kinematics, control theory, force feedback)

name: Bioinformatics in Biomedical Engineering
text: Bioinformatics brings computing power to biology and medicine.
depends_on: - Computer science (algorithms, data structures, machine learning, database management)

name: CRISPR-Based Diagnostic Platforms
text: CRISPR, famous for gene editing, has been repurposed as a powerful diagnostic tool.
depends_on: - Biosensor design (BE07)

name: Organ-on-a-Chip Microphysiological Systems
text: Organs-on-chips are thumbnail-sized devices containing living human cells arranged to mimic the structure and function of real organs --- lungs that breathe, hearts that beat, livers that metabolize drugs.
depends_on: - Microfluidics for diagnostics (BE18)

name: Digital Therapeutics: Software as Medicine
text: Digital therapeutics are prescription software programs --- typically smartphone apps --- that treat medical conditions the way drugs do, but through behavioral interventions, cognitive therapy, or physiological feedback instead of molecules.
depends_on: - Physiological signal processing (BE11)

name: Neuromorphic Brain-Computer Interfaces
text: A neuromorphic BCI reads electrical signals from brain neurons using tiny electrode arrays and decodes the person's intended movements or thoughts using a brain-inspired computer chip.
depends_on: - Neural interfaces and BCIs (BE15) for electrode array design and biocompatibility

name: Piezoelectric Biomedical Energy Harvesting
text: Every time your heart beats, your lungs expand, or you take a step, mechanical energy is available that could be converted to electricity using piezoelectric materials.
depends_on: - Biosensor design (BE07) for integration with powered sensing systems

name: Digital Biomarkers and Continuous Phenotyping
text: Digital biomarkers are health measurements gathered passively from the devices people already carry — smartphones and smartwatches.
depends_on: - Wearable sensor systems (BE20) for continuous physiological data acquisition

name: Organ-on-a-Chip Technology
text: An organ-on-a-chip is a tiny device about the size of a USB drive that contains living human cells arranged to mimic how an actual organ works.
depends_on: - Microfluidics (laminar flow at low Reynolds numbers, precise fluid handling)

name: AI in Medical Image Analysis
text: AI can now analyze medical images --- X-rays, CT scans, pathology slides, eye photos --- with accuracy matching or exceeding that of specialist physicians for specific tasks.
depends_on: - Machine learning (convolutional neural networks, transfer learning)

name: Point-of-Care Diagnostics
text: Point-of-care testing brings the laboratory to the patient rather than sending samples to a distant lab.
depends_on: - Biosensor design (BE07) for detection elements

name: Tissue-on-Chip Platforms for Drug Screening
text: Tissue-on-chip platforms are miniature human body models on a microfluidic device — connecting tiny 3D tissue replicas of the liver, heart, kidney, and tumor through channels that mimic blood circulation.
depends_on: - Organ-on-a-chip technology (BE24) for single-organ microfluidic culture

name: Implantable Neural Interface Engineering
text: Implantable neural interfaces are tiny electrode arrays inserted into the brain or nerves to listen to neural signals and send electrical commands back.
depends_on: - Neural interfaces and BCIs (BE15) for signal acquisition fundamentals

name: Bioprinting for Tissue and Organ Fabrication
text: Bioprinting is 3D printing with living cells instead of plastic.
depends_on: - Tissue engineering (BE09) for scaffold design and cell biology

name: Drug-Eluting Implant Engineering
text: Drug-eluting implants are medical devices coated with medication that slowly releases at the implant site.
depends_on: - Drug delivery systems (BE08) for controlled release kinetics and polymer matrix design

name: Photoacoustic Imaging for Deep Tissue Visualization
text: Photoacoustic imaging is a hybrid technique that combines the best features of optical and ultrasound imaging.
depends_on: - Medical imaging fundamentals (BE03-06) for signal detection and image reconstruction

name: Closed-Loop Neuromodulation Systems
text: Closed-loop neuromodulation is a smart brain stimulation system that listens to the brain, detects when something is going wrong, and delivers precisely the right amount of electrical stimulation to correct it — then backs off when the brain returns to normal.
depends_on: - Neural interfaces and BCIs (BE15) for electrode design and neural signal acquisition

name: Lab-on-a-Chip Diagnostic Microfluidics
text: A lab-on-a-chip is a miniature diagnostic laboratory built on a chip the size of a credit card.
depends_on: - Organ-on-a-chip technology (BE24) for microfluidic cell culture and tissue modeling

name: Hydrogel Bioelectronics
text: Hydrogel bioelectronics replaces the hard, rigid materials of conventional electronics — silicon, metal, plastic — with soft, squishy, water-rich materials that feel like biological tissue.
depends_on: - Biocompatibility (BE01) for tissue-material interface biology and foreign body response

name: Acoustic Tweezing and Ultrasonic Particle Manipulation
text: Acoustic tweezers use sound waves — ultrasound frequencies far above human hearing — to push, pull, sort, and trap tiny objects like cells, bacteria, and microparticles without any physical contact.
depends_on: - Bioinstrumentation (BE16) for piezoelectric transducer design and driving electronics

name: Conservation of Mass (Mass Balance)
text: Matter is neither created nor destroyed (in non-nuclear processes).
depends_on: - Conservation of mass (fundamental physical law)

name: Conservation of Energy (Energy Balance)
text: Energy, like mass, is conserved.
depends_on: - First law of thermodynamics

name: Conservation of Momentum (Momentum Balance)
text: Fluids flow because of pressure differences, gravity, and pumping.
depends_on: - Newton's second law

name: Unit Operations Concept
text: Whether you are making gasoline, antibiotics, or beer, you are doing the same basic things: heating, cooling, mixing, separating, and reacting.
depends_on: - Conservation laws (CE01, CE02, CE03)

name: Distillation: McCabe-Thiele Method
text: Distillation separates liquid mixtures by boiling --- the more volatile component concentrates in the vapor.
depends_on: - Mass balance (CE01) and energy balance (CE02)

name: Chemical Reactor Design: CSTR and PFR
text: A CSTR is like a perfectly stirred pot --- the concentration inside is uniform and equals the outlet concentration.
depends_on: - Mass balance (CE01) applied to reacting systems

name: Heat and Mass Transfer Analogies
text: Heat transfer and mass transfer obey the same mathematical structure: something (heat or molecules) diffuses down a gradient, and the rate depends on a driving force and a resistance.
depends_on: - Fourier's law (heat conduction)

name: Fick's Law of Diffusion in Engineering
text: Molecules naturally spread from regions of high concentration to low concentration --- this is diffusion.
depends_on: - Statistical mechanics (random molecular motion)

name: Dimensionless Numbers in Chemical Engineering
text: Dimensionless numbers are ratios that tell you which physical effects dominate.
depends_on: - Dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem)

name: Process Control Fundamentals
text: Chemical processes are inherently dynamic --- temperatures drift, levels change, feed compositions vary.
depends_on: - Feedback control theory (transfer functions, stability)

name: Scale-Up Principles
text: What works in a flask may not work in a factory.
depends_on: - Dimensional analysis (CE09)

name: Catalytic Reactor Design
text: In a catalytic reactor, the reactants must travel from the flowing fluid to the catalyst surface, diffuse into the pores, react, and then the products must diffuse back out.
depends_on: - Fick's law for pore diffusion (CE08)

name: Separation Processes Overview
text: Mixing is spontaneous and easy; un-mixing requires energy and clever engineering.
depends_on: - Phase equilibria (vapor-liquid, liquid-liquid, solid-liquid)

name: Fluidization
text: If you blow air upward through sand fast enough, the sand particles lift off and the bed behaves like a boiling liquid --- particles circulate, mix vigorously, and the bed surface undulates.
depends_on: - Momentum balance (CE03) on fluid and particles

name: Process Safety: HAZOP Methodology
text: Before starting up a chemical plant, engineers systematically ask "what if?" for every parameter at every point in the process.
depends_on: - Process engineering knowledge (normal operating conditions and limits)

name: Batch vs. Continuous Processing
text: Batch processing is like cooking a meal: you put ingredients in a pot, cook for a while, and take the product out.
depends_on: - Mass and energy balances (steady-state vs. transient)

name: Crystallization Engineering
text: Crystallization is how we make solid products with controlled purity and particle size --- from table sugar and pharmaceutical tablets to semiconductor-grade silicon.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (solubility, supersaturation, phase equilibria)

name: Membrane Separation Processes
text: A membrane is like a very selective filter at the molecular level.
depends_on: - Mass transfer (permeation, selectivity, concentration polarization)

name: Gas Absorption and Stripping
text: Gas absorption is like dissolving carbon dioxide in water to make soda, but done on an industrial scale.
depends_on: - Mass transfer (two-film theory, overall mass transfer coefficient)

name: Process Optimization
text: A chemical process has many adjustable parameters: temperatures, pressures, flow rates, reflux ratios, reactor volumes.
depends_on: - Process models (mass and energy balances, thermodynamics, kinetics)

name: Pinch Analysis for Heat Integration
text: In a chemical plant, some streams need to be heated and others need to be cooled.
depends_on: - Energy balance (CE02) for each process stream

name: Polymerization Reactor Design
text: Making a polymer is not like making a small molecule --- the product's properties (strength, flexibility, processability) depend critically on the chain length distribution and branching, which are controlled by reactor conditions.
depends_on: - Chemical kinetics (radical, ionic, coordination polymerization mechanisms)

name: Bioprocess Engineering
text: Bioprocess engineering uses living cells or enzymes as tiny chemical factories to make products: antibiotics, insulin, beer, biofuels, and monoclonal antibodies.
depends_on: - Microbiology and biochemistry (cell growth, metabolism, product formation)

name: Safety Instrumented Systems
text: Process safety depends on layers of protection: good design, operator training, alarms, and --- as a last automated line of defense --- safety instrumented systems.
depends_on: - Process safety (HAZOP, CE15, for identifying safety functions)

name: Environmental Engineering in Chemical Processes
text: Chemical processes inevitably produce waste --- contaminated water, exhaust gases, and solid residues.
depends_on: - Mass balance (CE01) for tracking pollutant flows

name: Process Intensification: Microreactor Engineering
text: Microreactors shrink chemical processes from room-sized vessels to devices with tiny channels (thinner than a human hair).
depends_on: - Conservation of mass (CE01)

name: Hydrogen Economy Thermodynamics and System Engineering
text: Hydrogen can be a carbon-free energy carrier, but thermodynamics sets hard limits at every step.
depends_on: - Conservation of energy (CE02)

name: Carbon Capture Process Engineering
text: Carbon capture takes CO2 out of power plant or factory exhaust.
depends_on: - Gas absorption and stripping (CE19)

name: Process Analytical Technology (PAT)
text: PAT means measuring product quality while it is being made, not after.
depends_on: - Process control (CE10) for feedback control fundamentals

name: Microreactor and Flow Chemistry Engineering
text: Microreactors are tiny chemical factories where reactions happen inside channels thinner than a human hair.
depends_on: - Conservation of mass and energy (CE01, CE02) applied to microscale systems

name: Reactive Distillation Integration
text: Reactive distillation is a clever process that runs a chemical reaction and separates the products simultaneously in the same equipment.
depends_on: - Distillation fundamentals (CE05) for vapor-liquid equilibrium and stage design

name: Henry's Law for Gas-Liquid Equilibrium
text: The amount of gas that dissolves in a liquid is proportional to the gas pressure above it.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (phase equilibrium, fugacity)

name: Process Intensification
text: Process intensification asks: "What if we could make chemical equipment 10 or 100 times smaller while producing the same output?" The answer involves creating conditions where mixing and heat transfer happen so fast that the chemistry, not the transport, is the limiting step.
depends_on: - Transport phenomena (enhanced mixing, heat, and mass transfer at small scales)

name: Electrochemical Reactor Engineering
text: Electrochemical engineering uses electricity to drive chemical reactions at electrodes.
depends_on: - Electrochemistry (Nernst equation, Butler-Volmer kinetics, overpotential)

name: Membrane Separation Processes
text: Membrane separation works like a very precise filter — but at the molecular level.
depends_on: - Conservation of mass (CE01) for material balance across the membrane

name: Plasma-Assisted Combustion and Processing
text: Plasma-assisted combustion uses electrical discharges — like controlled lightning — to create highly reactive atoms and molecules in a gas before or during combustion.
depends_on: - Conservation of energy (CE02) for energy balance of plasma-chemical systems

name: Adsorption-Based Separation Processes
text: Adsorption separation works by passing a gas or liquid mixture through a bed of solid material that preferentially grabs certain molecules and lets others pass through.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (adsorption equilibrium, Gibbs free energy of adsorption)

name: Supercritical Fluid Processing
text: Supercritical fluid processing uses substances heated and pressurized beyond their critical point — a special state where they behave as both liquid and gas simultaneously.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics and phase equilibria (CE01, CE02) for equation of state modeling of supercritical systems

name: Process Digital Twin and Real-Time Optimization
text: A process digital twin is a virtual copy of a chemical plant that runs on a computer in perfect synchronization with the real plant.
depends_on: - Process control (CE10) for feedback and model predictive control fundamentals

name: Green Chemistry Principles in Process Design
text: Green chemistry redesigns chemical processes from the ground up to prevent pollution rather than cleaning it up afterward.
depends_on: - Conservation of mass (CE01) as the basis for atom economy and mass balance calculations

name: Electrochemical Membrane Reactor Engineering
text: Electrochemical membrane reactors combine two processes -- chemical reaction and separation -- into one device.
depends_on: - Electrochemical engineering (CE28) for electrode kinetics and cell design

name: Microreactor and Flow Chemistry Process Intensification
text: Microreactors are tiny continuous-flow chemical reactors where reactions happen in channels thinner than a human hair.
depends_on: - Mass transfer (CE08) for understanding mixing and diffusion at microscale

name: CO2 Capture and Utilization Process Design
text: Carbon capture and utilization tackles the challenge of removing CO2 from power plant exhaust or even from the open atmosphere and turning it into something useful.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (CE02) for minimum separation energy and reaction equilibria

name: Ohm's Law
text: Electrical resistance is like friction for current.
depends_on: - Electromagnetic theory (electric field, current density)

name: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)
text: Electric charge does not pile up at a junction --- what flows in must flow out.
depends_on: - Conservation of electric charge

name: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)
text: If you walk around any closed path in a circuit, adding up voltage rises and drops, you end up right where you started --- at the same potential.
depends_on: - Conservation of energy

name: Thevenin's and Norton's Equivalents
text: No matter how complicated a circuit is inside a black box, from the outside (two terminals) it behaves exactly like a simple source with a single impedance.
depends_on: - Kirchhoff's laws (EE02, EE03)

name: Maximum Power Transfer
text: To squeeze the most power out of a source into a load, you match the load impedance to the conjugate of the source impedance.
depends_on: - Thevenin/Norton equivalents (EE04)

name: Impedance Matching
text: Imagine shouting into a pipe that suddenly changes diameter --- some sound bounces back.
depends_on: - Transmission line theory (distributed circuits)

name: Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem
text: To capture all the information in a signal digitally, you must sample it at least twice as fast as its highest frequency component.
depends_on: - Fourier analysis (frequency-domain representation of signals)

name: Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity
text: Every communication channel has a speed limit determined by how wide the frequency band is (bandwidth) and how strong the signal is relative to the noise.
depends_on: - Information theory (entropy, mutual information)

name: Feedback Control Theory (Negative Feedback)
text: Feedback means measuring the output and using the difference between what you want and what you get (the error) to adjust the input.
depends_on: - Laplace transform (transfer functions, s-domain analysis)

name: Bode Stability Criterion
text: A feedback system becomes unstable when the signal fed back arrives with the wrong timing (phase) and sufficient strength (gain) to reinforce rather than correct the error.
depends_on: - Feedback control theory (EE09)

name: PID Control
text: PID control is the workhorse of industrial automation.
depends_on: - Feedback control theory (EE09)

name: Semiconductor Physics: The p-n Junction
text: When p-type (excess holes) and n-type (excess electrons) semiconductors are joined, carriers near the junction diffuse across and recombine, creating a charge-depleted zone that acts as a one-way valve for current.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (band theory, Fermi-Dirac statistics)

name: MOSFET Operation
text: A MOSFET is a voltage-controlled switch.
depends_on: - p-n junction physics (EE12)

name: Boolean Algebra in Digital Circuits
text: All digital computation reduces to combining true/false (1/0) values with simple logical operations.
depends_on: - Mathematical logic (propositional calculus)

name: Power Electronics Fundamentals
text: Power electronics is the art of reshaping electrical power --- changing voltage levels, converting between AC and DC, and controlling motor speeds --- using fast-switching semiconductor devices.
depends_on: - Semiconductor device physics (EE12, EE13)

name: Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)
text: Every electronic device is both a potential source of electrical noise and a potential victim of it.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (radiation, coupling mechanisms)

name: Signal Integrity in High-Speed Digital Design
text: At low speeds, a wire is just a wire.
depends_on: - Transmission line theory (telegrapher's equations)

name: Transmission Line Theory
text: A transmission line (coaxial cable, PCB trace, waveguide) carries signals as waves traveling in both directions.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (guided wave solutions)

name: Power System Load Flow Analysis
text: A power grid has generators, transmission lines, transformers, and loads spread across a wide area.
depends_on: - Kirchhoff's laws (EE02, EE03) applied to AC power networks

name: Power System Transient Stability
text: All large generators on the power grid spin in synchronism at the same electrical frequency (50 or 60 Hz).
depends_on: - Electromechanical energy conversion (synchronous machine theory)

name: Root Locus Method
text: The root locus is a graphical tool that shows how the behavior of a feedback control system changes as you turn the gain knob up from zero.
depends_on: - Feedback control theory (EE09)

name: State-Space Representation
text: State-space is a way of describing a system using a set of first-order differential equations instead of a single high-order transfer function.
depends_on: - Linear algebra (matrices, eigenvalues, matrix exponential)

name: Discrete Fourier Transform and Digital Signal Processing
text: Digital signal processing converts analog signals to numbers, manipulates those numbers mathematically, and (optionally) converts them back.
depends_on: - Fourier analysis (frequency-domain representation)

name: Pulse-Width Modulation for Power Conversion
text: Power electronics converts electrical power by switching transistors on and off very rapidly.
depends_on: - Power electronics fundamentals (EE15) --- switch-mode operation

name: Antenna Radiation Fundamentals
text: An antenna is the interface between an electrical circuit and the electromagnetic wave world.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (radiation from accelerating charges and time-varying currents)

name: Wide-Bandgap Semiconductor Device Physics
text: GaN and SiC can handle much higher voltages and switch much faster than silicon because their wider bandgap makes them far more resistant to electrical breakdown.
depends_on: - Semiconductor physics: the p-n junction (EE12)

name: Digital Twin Principles for Electrical Power Systems
text: A power grid digital twin is a living computer model of the entire electrical network that continuously ingests data from thousands of sensors across the grid.
depends_on: - Power system load flow analysis (EE19)

name: Hydrogen Electrolysis and Power-to-Gas Thermodynamics
text: Splitting water into hydrogen fuel using electricity requires at least 1.23 volts per cell, but real electrolyzers need about 1.8--2.2 volts because of various energy losses.
depends_on: - Kirchhoff's voltage law (EE03)

name: MEMS/NEMS Electromechanical Physics
text: MEMS and NEMS are tiny machines fabricated on silicon chips using the same techniques as computer chips.
depends_on: - Ohm's law (EE01) and semiconductor physics (EE12) for electrical domain

name: Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting
text: Certain crystals and ceramics generate voltage when squeezed or bent --- this is the piezoelectric effect.
depends_on: - Semiconductor physics (EE12) for power electronics interface

name: Neuromorphic Engineering
text: Neuromorphic chips compute like brains instead of like conventional computers.
depends_on: - MOSFET operation (EE13) for subthreshold analog circuit design

name: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction
text: When a magnetic field through a loop of wire changes --- whether because the magnet moves, the loop moves, or the field strength varies --- a voltage is induced that drives current.
depends_on: - Maxwell's equations (electromagnetic theory)

name: CMOS Logic Design
text: CMOS is the technology behind virtually all modern digital chips --- from microprocessors to memory.
depends_on: - MOSFET operation (EE13) for both NMOS and PMOS devices

name: Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) Fundamentals
text: A PLL is a circuit that locks onto an input frequency and generates a clean, stable output at that frequency (or a multiple of it).
depends_on: - Feedback control theory (EE09) for loop stability analysis

name: Wide-Bandgap Power Electronics (GaN and SiC)
text: Silicon carbide and gallium nitride are semiconductors with wider bandgaps than silicon, which means they can handle higher voltages, switch faster, and operate at higher temperatures while wasting far less energy.
depends_on: - MOSFET operation (EE13) for transistor physics extended to wide-bandgap materials

name: Thermoelectric Energy Conversion
text: Thermoelectric devices generate electricity from heat differences without any moving parts — no turbines, no pistons, no working fluids.
depends_on: - Semiconductor physics (EE12) for carrier transport and band structure engineering

name: Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) Systems
text: Wireless power transfer sends electricity through the air without wires.
depends_on: - Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction (EE26) for the fundamental coupling mechanism

name: Superconducting Power Systems
text: Superconducting power systems use special materials that conduct electricity with absolutely zero resistance when cooled below a critical temperature.
depends_on: - Ohm's law (EE01) as the baseline that superconductors transcend (R = 0)

name: Optical Fiber Communication Fundamentals
text: Optical fiber communication sends data as light pulses through hair-thin glass fibers.
depends_on: - Electromagnetic theory (Snell's law, total internal reflection, Maxwell's equations in dielectrics)

name: Grid-Scale Energy Storage Systems
text: Grid-scale energy storage acts like a giant rechargeable battery for the power grid.
depends_on: - Power electronics (EE15) for bidirectional AC-DC conversion and grid interface

name: Gallium Nitride Power Device Physics
text: Gallium nitride is a semiconductor material that enables power electronic devices far superior to silicon -- switching faster, losing less energy, and operating at higher temperatures.
depends_on: - Semiconductor physics (EE12) for bandgap engineering and carrier transport in heterostructures

name: Piezoelectric Vibration Energy Harvesting
text: Piezoelectric energy harvesting captures the tiny vibrations present everywhere -- in bridges, machinery, buildings, and even human movement -- and converts them into usable electrical energy.
depends_on: - Electromagnetic theory for the piezoelectric constitutive equations

name: Quantum Sensing and Metrology
text: Quantum sensors use the strange behavior of quantum mechanics to make measurements far more precise than any conventional technology allows.
depends_on: - Semiconductor physics (EE12) for understanding solid-state quantum defects

name: Crystal Structure and Symmetry
text: Most engineering metals and ceramics are crystalline --- their atoms are arranged in repeating three-dimensional patterns, like bricks in a wall extended in all directions.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (bonding determines preferred atomic arrangements)

name: Crystal Defects and Their Role
text: A perfect crystal exists only in textbooks.
depends_on: - Crystal structure (MS01) defines the perfect lattice from which defects deviate

name: Phase Diagrams
text: A phase diagram is a map that tells you what phases (solid, liquid, or different crystal structures) are stable at any given temperature and composition.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (Gibbs free energy, chemical potential, equilibrium)

name: Strengthening Mechanisms
text: There are four main ways to make a metal stronger, and they all work by making it harder for dislocations to move: (1) Cold working --- create more dislocations that tangle and block each other.
depends_on: - Dislocation theory (MS02)

name: Diffusion in Solids (Fick's Laws Applied to Materials)
text: Atoms in a solid are not frozen in place --- they vibrate and occasionally jump to neighboring sites, especially at high temperatures.
depends_on: - Crystal defects (MS02) --- vacancies are the diffusion vehicles

name: Corrosion and Oxidation
text: Metals corrode because they naturally want to return to their oxidized state (ores).
depends_on: - Electrochemistry (electrode potentials, Nernst equation)

name: Polymer Structure-Property Relationships
text: Polymers (plastics, rubbers, fibers) are made of extremely long chain molecules.
depends_on: - Organic chemistry (monomer structure, polymerization mechanisms)

name: Ceramic Processing and Properties
text: Ceramics --- from bricks and porcelain to advanced silicon carbide and alumina --- are hard, heat-resistant, and chemically stable, but they break without warning.
depends_on: - Crystal structure (MS01) --- ionic/covalent bonding limits slip systems

name: Composite Mechanics: Rule of Mixtures
text: A composite is a combination of two materials --- strong, stiff fibers embedded in a softer matrix (like rebar in concrete).
depends_on: - Mechanics of materials (stress, strain, elasticity)

name: Semiconductor Materials
text: Semiconductors are materials whose electrical conductivity can be precisely controlled by adding tiny amounts of impurity atoms (doping).
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (band theory, Bloch theorem)

name: Shape Memory Alloys
text: Imagine bending a metal wire and then warming it up, only to watch it spring back to its original shape.
depends_on: - Crystallography (martensitic transformation, habit planes, twinning)

name: Biomaterials: Biocompatibility and Design
text: When you put a material inside the human body (a hip implant, a heart valve, a dental filling), the body reacts to it.
depends_on: - Material properties (mechanical, corrosion, wear)

name: Nanomaterials: Size-Dependent Properties
text: At the nanoscale, materials behave differently from their bulk counterparts.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (confinement effects on electronic states)

name: Additive Manufacturing of Materials
text: 3D printing for metals and other engineering materials builds parts layer by layer using a laser or electron beam to melt powder or wire.
depends_on: - Solidification science (rapid solidification, epitaxial growth)

name: Materials Characterization Techniques
text: To understand and improve materials, you must be able to see and measure their structure at every scale.
depends_on: - Physics (X-ray, electron, and neutron interaction with matter)

name: The Processing-Structure-Property-Performance Paradigm
text: How you make a material (casting, forging, heat treating, 3D printing) controls what it looks like inside (grain size, phases, defects).
depends_on: - All prior principles (MS01-MS15) provide the specific links in the chain

name: Thin Film Deposition and Epitaxy
text: Thin film deposition is how we coat surfaces with extremely thin layers of material --- from anti-reflective coatings on eyeglasses to the transistor layers in microchips.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (film nucleation, growth modes: Frank-van der Merwe, Volmer-Weber, Stranski-Krastanov)

name: Surface Engineering and Coatings
text: The surface of a part is where almost all the action happens: wear, corrosion, friction, and fatigue cracks all start at the surface.
depends_on: - Diffusion (MS05) for case hardening processes

name: Piezoelectric Materials and Electromechanical Coupling
text: Piezoelectric materials are the bridge between mechanical and electrical worlds: squeeze them and they produce a voltage; apply a voltage and they change shape.
depends_on: - Crystallography (non-centrosymmetric crystal structure required for piezoelectricity)

name: High-Entropy Alloys
text: Traditional alloys are based on one dominant element with small additions (steel is mostly iron, brass is mostly copper).
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (entropy of mixing, Gibbs free energy, phase stability)

name: Failure Analysis Methodology
text: When an engineering component fails --- a turbine blade fractures, a pipeline leaks, a bridge member cracks --- failure analysis is the forensic investigation that determines why.
depends_on: - Fracture mechanics (crack propagation modes: Mode I, II, III)

name: Powder Metallurgy and Sintering
text: Powder metallurgy is an alternative to casting or machining: you start with fine metal or ceramic powder, press it into shape (like pressing sand into a sandcastle mold), and then heat it until the particles bond together (sintering).
depends_on: - Diffusion in solids (MS05) --- sintering is fundamentally a diffusion process

name: Material Degradation and Lifetime Prediction
text: No engineering material lasts forever.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics and kinetics (thermally activated processes, Arrhenius law)

name: Metamaterials Design: Architecture Beyond Chemistry
text: Metamaterials get their extraordinary properties from their internal structure --- repeating patterns of struts, voids, or hinges --- rather than from what they are made of.
depends_on: - Crystal structure and symmetry (MS01)

name: Computational Materials Discovery: High-Throughput and Machine Learning
text: Instead of synthesizing thousands of materials one at a time in the lab, scientists now use computers to predict material properties from their atomic structure.
depends_on: - Crystal structure and symmetry (MS01)

name: Self-Healing Materials: Autonomous Damage Repair
text: Self-healing materials can repair themselves after being damaged, like biological tissue.
depends_on: - Polymer structure-property relationships (MS07)

name: High-Entropy Alloy Design Principles
text: Traditional alloys are based on one main element with small additions (steel is iron with a bit of carbon and chromium).
depends_on: - Phase diagrams (MS03) for thermodynamic stability assessment

name: Topology-Optimized Architected Materials
text: Architected materials are materials whose properties come from their internal geometric structure — like how a honeycomb is much stiffer and lighter than a solid block of the same amount of wax.
depends_on: - Crystal structure and symmetry (MS01) for unit cell symmetry analysis

name: Tribological Surface Engineering
text: Tribological surface engineering means modifying just the surface of a component to control how it interacts with other surfaces in contact.
depends_on: - Corrosion and oxidation (MS06) for coating durability in service environments

name: Metamaterials
text: Metamaterials derive their properties not from what they are made of but from how they are structured.
depends_on: - Electromagnetic theory (Maxwell's equations for EM metamaterials)

name: Computational Materials Design (DFT and CALPHAD)
text: Instead of making hundreds of alloys in the lab and testing each one, computational materials design uses quantum mechanics and thermodynamic databases to predict which compositions and processing routes will produce the desired properties.
depends_on: - Quantum mechanics (Schrodinger equation, many-body theory)

name: Self-Healing Materials
text: Imagine a material that can heal its own cracks, like skin healing a cut.
depends_on: - Fracture mechanics (crack propagation triggers healing)

name: Auxetic Materials and Negative Poisson's Ratio Structures
text: Most materials get thinner when you stretch them — pull a rubber band and it narrows in the middle.
depends_on: - Crystal structure and symmetry (MS01) for natural auxetic crystalline phases

name: Functionally Graded Materials (FGMs)
text: Functionally graded materials smoothly transition from one material to another — like a component that is pure metal on one side and pure ceramic on the other, with a seamless blend in between.
depends_on: - Phase diagrams and phase transformations (MS03) for compatibility of graded compositions

name: Shape Memory Polymers and 4D Printing
text: Shape memory polymers are smart plastics that can be molded into a temporary shape and then snap back to their original form when triggered by heat, light, or moisture.
depends_on: - Phase transformations (MS03) for understanding glass transition and melting-based switching

name: Biomimetic Materials Design
text: Biomimetic materials copy the remarkable structural designs that evolution has perfected over millions of years.
depends_on: - Composite mechanics (MS09) for rule of mixtures and platelet composite models

name: Electrospinning and Nanofiber Engineering
text: Electrospinning uses a strong electric field to pull a thin stream of polymer solution from a needle tip, stretching it into fibers a thousand times thinner than a human hair.
depends_on: - Polymer science (MS07) for understanding solution rheology and chain entanglement

name: Thermoelectric Materials and Energy Conversion
text: Thermoelectric materials are special semiconductors that generate electricity directly from a temperature difference — no moving parts, no turbines, no fluids.
depends_on: - Semiconductor physics (MS10) for electronic band structure and carrier transport

name: Gradient Materials by Additive Manufacturing
text: Additive manufacturing can build components that smoothly transition from one material to another within the same part — for example, stainless steel on one end grading into a nickel superalloy on the other, or titanium transitioning to copper.
depends_on: - Functionally graded materials (MS31) for the theoretical framework of continuously varying properties

name: Radiation-Tolerant Nanostructured Materials
text: When materials are bombarded by radiation (neutrons in nuclear reactors, cosmic rays in space), the energetic particles knock atoms out of their positions, creating damage that accumulates over time — the material swells, hardens, becomes brittle, and eventually fails.
depends_on: - Crystal defects and strengthening mechanisms (MS02) for understanding point defect and dislocation behavior

name: Mechanoluminescent Stress-Sensing Materials
text: Mechanoluminescent materials glow when they are squeezed, bent, or stretched — the brighter the glow, the higher the stress.
depends_on: - Semiconductor physics (MS10) for understanding trap levels and luminescent transitions

name: Generalized Hooke's Law (Stress-Strain-Temperature Relations)
text: When you load a material and change its temperature simultaneously, the resulting deformation is the sum of the mechanical strain (from stress) and the thermal strain (from temperature change).
depends_on: - Continuum mechanics (stress and strain tensors)

name: Thermal Expansion
text: Almost all materials expand when heated and contract when cooled.
depends_on: - Atomic bonding (asymmetry of interatomic potential well)

name: Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction
text: Heat flows from hot to cold, and the rate of flow depends on how steep the temperature difference is and how well the material conducts heat.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (temperature, energy conservation)

name: Convective Heat Transfer
text: When a fluid (air, water, oil) flows over a warm surface, it carries heat away.
depends_on: - Fluid mechanics (boundary layers, turbulence)

name: Stefan-Boltzmann Law of Thermal Radiation
text: Every object radiates heat as electromagnetic waves.
depends_on: - Planck's law (spectral distribution of blackbody radiation)

name: Heat Exchanger Analysis (LMTD and NTU Methods)
text: A heat exchanger transfers heat between two fluids without mixing them.
depends_on: - Fourier's law (ME03) and convection (ME04) for determining U

name: Carnot Cycle and Thermodynamic Cycle Efficiency
text: There is an absolute upper limit to how efficiently you can convert heat into work, set by the temperatures you are working between.
depends_on: - First law of thermodynamics (energy conservation)

name: Rankine and Brayton Power Cycles
text: Steam power plants boil water, expand the steam through a turbine, condense it back to water, and pump it back to the boiler.
depends_on: - Carnot efficiency limit (ME07)

name: Refrigeration and Heat Pump Cycles
text: A refrigerator or air conditioner does not "create cold" --- it pumps heat from inside (cold side) to outside (hot side), requiring work input.
depends_on: - Thermodynamic cycle analysis (ME07)

name: Euler Turbomachinery Equation
text: The power a turbine extracts or a compressor adds depends entirely on how much the fluid's swirl (tangential velocity) changes as it passes through the rotor.
depends_on: - Newton's second law (angular momentum form)

name: Tribology: Friction, Wear, and Lubrication
text: Whenever two surfaces rub against each other, there is friction (which resists motion) and wear (which removes material).
depends_on: - Contact mechanics (Hertzian contact theory)

name: Vibration Analysis: Natural Frequency and Damping
text: Every structure and machine has natural frequencies at which it "wants" to vibrate.
depends_on: - Newton's second law

name: Fatigue Analysis in Machine Design
text: Machine parts that are loaded repeatedly --- shafts, gears, springs, bolts --- can break even though no single load cycle is large enough to cause failure.
depends_on: - Stress analysis (bending, torsion, axial, combined loading)

name: Grashof's Law for Four-Bar Mechanisms
text: A four-bar linkage is the simplest mechanism that converts one type of motion into another (like a crank turning rotation into a rocking motion).
depends_on: - Rigid-body kinematics

name: Manufacturing Tolerances and Fits
text: Every part comes out slightly different from the blueprint.
depends_on: - Statistics (process capability, normal distribution of part dimensions)

name: Ashby Material Selection Methodology
text: Choosing the right material is not about picking the strongest or lightest --- it is about finding the best combination of properties for the specific function, objective (minimize weight?
depends_on: - Material properties (mechanical, thermal, electrical, cost, environmental)

name: Reliability Engineering (Bathtub Curve and MTBF)
text: Everything eventually breaks.
depends_on: - Probability and statistics (failure distributions, confidence intervals)

name: Dimensional Analysis and Similitude
text: Before building a full-size bridge, airplane, or ship, engineers test scale models.
depends_on: - Fundamental dimensions (mass, length, time, temperature, etc.)

name: Psychrometrics and HVAC Design
text: Air is not just air --- it contains water vapor, and the amount of moisture profoundly affects human comfort and building energy use.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (ideal gas mixtures, Dalton's law, enthalpy of vaporization)

name: Computational Fluid Dynamics
text: CFD uses computers to simulate fluid flow by dividing the flow domain into millions of tiny cells and solving the equations of fluid motion in each cell.
depends_on: - Navier-Stokes equations (conservation of mass, momentum, energy)

name: Finite Element Analysis for Mechanical Design
text: Finite element analysis (FEA) is the computer-based method mechanical engineers use to predict stresses, deformations, temperatures, and vibration modes in complex parts.
depends_on: - Continuum mechanics (stress, strain, constitutive models)

name: Additive Manufacturing for Mechanical Design
text: 3D printing has moved from prototyping to producing functional mechanical parts.
depends_on: - Materials science (rapid solidification, microstructure-property relationships)

name: Mechatronics Integration
text: Most modern machines are mechatronic: they combine mechanical parts with electronics, sensors, and software.
depends_on: - Mechanical engineering (dynamics, mechanisms, thermal management)

name: Robotics: Forward and Inverse Kinematics
text: A robot arm has several rotating joints connected by links.
depends_on: - Rigid-body mechanics (homogeneous transformations, rotation matrices)

name: Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH) Engineering
text: Every car, appliance, and machine produces noise and vibration.
depends_on: - Vibration analysis (ME12) --- structural dynamics and modal analysis

name: 4D Printing: Shape-Memory Additive Manufacturing
text: 4D printing adds time as the fourth dimension to 3D printing.
depends_on: - Additive manufacturing for mechanical design (ME22)

name: Digital Twin Framework for Mechanical Systems
text: A digital twin for a machine --- an engine, turbine, or robot --- is a computer replica that stays synchronized with the real machine through continuous sensor data.
depends_on: - Finite element analysis for mechanical design (ME21)

name: Process Intensification in Mechanical Design
text: Process intensification makes chemical and thermal equipment dramatically smaller and more efficient by redesigning it from first principles.
depends_on: - Convective heat transfer (ME04)

name: Tribology Fundamentals: Friction, Wear, and Lubrication
text: Tribology is the science of surfaces rubbing together.
depends_on: - Hooke's law and contact mechanics (Hertz theory)

name: Piezoelectric Actuators and Precision Motion
text: Piezoelectric actuators are solid-state devices that expand or contract when voltage is applied, with no moving parts, no friction, and no backlash.
depends_on: - Feedback control theory for closed-loop nanopositioning

name: Topology-Optimized Conformal Cooling Channels
text: In injection molding and die casting, parts must be cooled inside the mold before removal.
depends_on: - Fourier's law of heat conduction (ME03) for mold thermal analysis

name: Topology Optimization in Mechanical Design
text: Traditional design starts with a shape and checks if it works.
depends_on: - Finite element analysis (ME21) for structural response calculation

name: Digital Twin Concept
text: Imagine having a perfect computer model of a jet engine, a wind turbine, or a factory floor that updates itself in real time with sensor readings from the actual machine.
depends_on: - Physics-based simulation (FEA, CFD, multibody dynamics)

name: Shape Memory Alloy Actuators in Mechanical Systems
text: SMA actuators are solid-state devices that produce motion by changing temperature.
depends_on: - Martensitic transformation thermodynamics (MS11 in materials science)

name: Shape Memory Polymer Actuators and Multifunctional Structures
text: Shape memory polymers are plastics that remember their original shape.
depends_on: - Thermodynamics (entropy-driven shape recovery above T_trans)

name: Microfluidic Heat Exchangers
text: Microfluidic heat exchangers cool things incredibly effectively by forcing liquid through channels thinner than a human hair.
depends_on: - Fourier's law of heat conduction (ME03) for wall thermal resistance

name: Compliant Mechanisms for Precision Engineering
text: Compliant mechanisms are structures that move by bending rather than by using hinges and bearings.
depends_on: - Elasticity theory (beam bending, large deflection analysis)

name: Soft Robotics Actuators and Continuum Mechanisms
text: Soft robotics replaces the rigid metal arms and motors of traditional robots with squishy, inflatable structures made of silicone rubber.
depends_on: - Continuum mechanics (hyperelastic constitutive models for large-strain elastomers)

name: Origami-Inspired Engineering and Deployable Mechanisms
text: Origami engineering takes the art of paper folding and applies it to create structures that can transform between a flat, compact state and a large, functional 3D shape.
depends_on: - Kinematic mechanism theory (degree-of-freedom analysis, rigid-body motion)

name: Digital Thread and Model-Based Systems Engineering
text: The digital thread is the idea that every piece of information about a product — from the first customer requirement through 3D design, stress analysis, manufacturing instructions, test results, and field service records — is connected in a single, continuous digital chain.
depends_on: - Digital twin concept (ME27) as the operational endpoint of the digital thread

name: Biomechanics of Terrestrial Locomotion
text: The biomechanics of locomotion explains how humans and animals walk and run efficiently.
depends_on: - Newton's laws (ME01) for force-acceleration relationships in gait analysis

name: Vibration-Based Energy Harvesting Systems
text: Every vibrating surface is a potential energy source.
depends_on: - Vibration analysis (ME06) for natural frequency matching and resonance exploitation

name: Rotating Detonation Engine Thermodynamics
text: A rotating detonation engine uses controlled explosions -- not the gentle burning of conventional jet engines -- to combust fuel far more efficiently.
depends_on: - Thermodynamic cycles (ME02) for efficiency comparison with Brayton and Humphrey cycles

name: Hooke's Law for Structural Materials
text: When you pull or push on a material gently enough, it deforms in direct proportion to the applied force, and it springs back when you let go.
depends_on: - Continuum mechanics (stress and strain definitions)

name: Equilibrium (Static)
text: A structure that is not accelerating must have all its forces and twisting effects perfectly balanced.
depends_on: - Newton's first and third laws

name: Euler Buckling (Column Stability)
text: A long, thin column under compression will suddenly bow sideways at a specific load rather than simply shortening.
depends_on: - Hooke's law (SE01)

name: Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory
text: When a beam bends, the top might compress while the bottom stretches (or vice versa).
depends_on: - Hooke's law (SE01)

name: Mohr's Circle for Stress Transformation
text: Stress at a point changes depending on the orientation of the plane you examine.
depends_on: - Continuum mechanics (Cauchy stress tensor)

name: Saint-Venant's Principle
text: If you apply the same total force and moment to a structure but distribute them differently over a small patch, the stress fields will differ near the patch but will look essentially identical far away.
depends_on: - Elasticity theory

name: Factor of Safety
text: Engineers deliberately design structures to be stronger than strictly necessary because materials vary, loads are uncertain, analysis is approximate, and construction is imperfect.
depends_on: - Probability and statistics (load and resistance distributions)

name: Load Path Continuity
text: Forces do not vanish --- they must travel through the structure.
depends_on: - Static equilibrium (SE02)

name: Structural Redundancy and Indeterminacy
text: A structure is "redundant" when it has more supports or members than the bare minimum needed to stand.
depends_on: - Static equilibrium (SE02)

name: Plastic Hinge Theory (Limit Analysis)
text: Steel and other ductile materials do not break the instant they start yielding --- they deform plastically.
depends_on: - Equilibrium (SE02)

name: Prestressing Principle
text: Concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension.
depends_on: - Hooke's law (SE01)

name: Finite Element Method in Structures
text: Instead of solving the full equations of elasticity for a complex shape, you break the structure into many small, simple pieces (triangles, rectangles, tetrahedra).
depends_on: - Variational principles (minimum potential energy or virtual work)

name: Fatigue Life and S-N Curves (Wohler's Law)
text: Bending a paper clip back and forth will break it even though the force is too small to break it in one pull.
depends_on: - Stress analysis (bending, axial, torsion)

name: Griffith Fracture Criterion
text: Materials contain tiny flaws.
depends_on: - Elasticity theory (stress fields near crack tips)

name: Wind Loading on Structures
text: Wind exerts pressure on the windward face and suction on the leeward face and roof.
depends_on: - Fluid mechanics (Bernoulli's equation, boundary layer theory)

name: Seismic Design Philosophy
text: Buildings in earthquake zones are not designed to remain undamaged in every earthquake --- that would be prohibitively expensive.
depends_on: - Structural dynamics (modal analysis, response spectra)

name: Soil-Structure Interaction
text: A building does not sit on infinitely rigid ground --- the soil beneath it compresses, shifts, and absorbs energy.
depends_on: - Geotechnical engineering (soil modulus, damping)

name: Composite Material Behavior in Structures
text: Composites like carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer are strong in the fiber direction but weaker across it.
depends_on: - Hooke's law generalized to anisotropic materials (SE01)

name: Connection Design Philosophy
text: The chain is only as strong as its weakest link, and in structures, that weak link is often a connection.
depends_on: - Equilibrium (SE02)

name: Response Spectrum Method for Seismic Analysis
text: Rather than simulating a building's full time-history response to an earthquake (computationally expensive), engineers use a response spectrum: a chart that shows the worst-case shaking experienced by structures of different natural periods.
depends_on: - Structural dynamics (modal analysis, natural frequencies, mode shapes)

name: Vortex-Induced Vibration and Lock-In
text: Wind flowing past a bridge cable, chimney, or tower creates alternating vortices that push the structure side to side.
depends_on: - Fluid mechanics (vortex shedding, Reynolds number dependence)

name: Progressive Collapse Resistance
text: If one column in a building is destroyed --- by a bomb, a vehicle impact, or a gas explosion --- the structure should not collapse like a house of cards.
depends_on: - Structural redundancy (SE09)

name: Performance-Based Structural Design
text: Traditional structural design simply asks: "Will the building collapse in a big earthquake?" Performance-based design asks much more: "In a small earthquake, will it remain fully operational?
depends_on: - Seismic design philosophy (SE16) and response spectrum method (SE20)

name: Durability and Corrosion of Reinforced Concrete
text: Reinforced concrete is not permanent --- it degrades.
depends_on: - Diffusion theory (Fick's laws applied to chloride and CO_2 transport)

name: Structural Health Monitoring
text: Rather than waiting for cracks to become visible or a bridge to fail, engineers can instrument structures with sensors that continuously listen for signs of trouble.
depends_on: - Structural dynamics (modal properties as damage indicators)

name: Digital Twin Methodology for Structural Systems
text: A digital twin is a living computer model of a real structure --- a bridge, building, or dam --- that updates itself using sensor measurements from the actual structure.
depends_on: - Structural health monitoring (SE25)

name: Topology Optimization Convergence for Additive Manufacturing
text: Topology optimization uses computers to figure out the most efficient shape for a structural part by removing material wherever it is not needed.
depends_on: - Finite element method in structures (SE12)

name: Metamaterial-Based Seismic Cloaking
text: Seismic metamaterials are engineered arrays of buried structures --- concrete columns, steel-mass resonators, or even boreholes --- arranged around a building to deflect or absorb earthquake waves before they arrive.
depends_on: - Seismic design philosophy (SE16)

name: Tensegrity Structural Systems
text: Tensegrity structures are built from bars that float within a web of cables, never touching each other.
depends_on: - Equilibrium (SE02) and load path continuity (SE08)

name: Topology-Optimized Lattice Structures
text: Instead of using solid material everywhere, lattice structures fill a part with tiny repeating patterns --- like a honeycomb or a miniature truss network --- where the pattern can change from region to region.
depends_on: - Topology optimization (SE26) for macro-scale material distribution

name: Robustness and Fail-Safe Structural Design
text: A robust structure is one that does not collapse catastrophically when one part is damaged or removed --- like a net that still holds even when one strand is cut.
depends_on: - Structural redundancy and indeterminacy (SE09)

name: Topology Optimization for Structural Design
text: Topology optimization asks the computer: "Given this space, these loads, and this much material, where should material be placed for maximum structural efficiency?" The result often looks like bones or tree branches --- organic shapes where every piece of material is working hard.
depends_on: - Finite element method (SE12) for structural analysis at each iteration

name: Creep and Relaxation in Concrete Structures
text: Concrete under sustained load keeps deforming slowly over months and years, even though no additional load is applied.
depends_on: - Hooke's law (SE01) for initial elastic response

name: Capacity Design Hierarchy
text: Capacity design is like installing a fuse in an electrical circuit: you deliberately make certain elements the weakest link so that when overloaded, failure occurs there --- where you have designed it to be safe and ductile --- rather than in a brittle or critical location.
depends_on: - Plastic hinge theory (SE10) for identifying ductile mechanisms

name: Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) Systems
text: Structural health monitoring is like giving a building, bridge, or aircraft a nervous system.
depends_on: - Finite element method (SE12) for baseline model development and damage simulation

name: Functionally Graded Materials in Structural Applications
text: Instead of bonding two different materials together with a sharp boundary — which creates stress concentrations that can cause cracking and delamination — functionally graded materials smoothly transition from one material to another across a distance.
depends_on: - Hooke's law (SE01) for elastic behavior at each point along the gradient

name: Compliant Mechanism Design
text: A compliant mechanism is a structure that bends to create motion instead of using hinges and joints.
depends_on: - Euler-Bernoulli beam theory (SE02) for large-deflection analysis of flexible members

name: Structural Glass Engineering
text: Structural glass engineering goes beyond using glass as a window — it makes glass carry structural loads like beams, columns, and floors.
depends_on: - Griffith fracture criterion (SE14) for understanding glass failure initiation

name: Cable-Stayed and Suspension Structure Mechanics
text: Cable-stayed and suspension structures use strong steel cables to hold up bridge decks and roofs over enormous spans.
depends_on: - Equilibrium (SE02) for force balance in cable-tower-deck systems

name: Performance-Based Wind Engineering
text: Performance-based wind engineering goes beyond checking whether a building survives the design wind storm.
depends_on: - Wind loading (SE15) for aerodynamic pressure distributions and dynamic response

name: Fiber-Reinforced Polymer Composite Structural Systems
text: FRP composites combine strong fibers with plastic resin to create structural materials that are 4-5 times stronger than steel per unit weight and completely immune to corrosion.
depends_on: - Hooke's law (SE01) for elastic stress-strain relationships in composite laminates

name: Prestressed Concrete Advanced Loss Estimation and Time-Dependent Analysis
text: Prestressed concrete uses tensioned steel cables to compress the concrete before loading, preventing tensile cracks.
depends_on: - Euler-Bernoulli beam theory (SE02) for flexural analysis of prestressed members

name: Origami-Based Metamaterial Structures
text: Origami metamaterials are engineered structures based on folding patterns that give them extraordinary mechanical behaviors impossible in conventional materials.
depends_on: - Compliant mechanism design (SE34) for fold-line mechanics as torsional hinges

name: Precision Medicine and Pharmacogenomics
text: Precision medicine recognizes that patients are not all the same and that their genetic makeup affects how they respond to drugs.
depends_on: - Evidence-based medicine (P06)

name: Liquid Biopsy in Clinical Diagnosis
text: A liquid biopsy is a simple blood draw that can detect cancer, track how well treatment is working, and identify drug-resistance mutations --- all without an invasive tissue biopsy.
depends_on: - Diagnostic sensitivity and specificity (P09)

name: Digital Therapeutics and Prescription Software
text: Digital therapeutics are prescription smartphone apps that treat medical conditions through structured behavioral programs, just as drugs treat conditions through chemistry.
depends_on: - Evidence-based medicine (P06)

name: Gut-Brain Axis in Clinical Medicine
text: Your gut and brain are in constant conversation through nerves, hormones, immune signals, and chemicals made by gut bacteria.
depends_on: - Homeostasis and disease (P01) for systems-level physiological integration

name: Tumor Mutational Burden as a Biomarker
text: Tumor mutational burden counts how many DNA mutations a cancer has.
depends_on: - Evidence-based medicine (P06) for biomarker validation methodology

name: Implementation Science in Clinical Practice
text: Implementation science is about closing the gap between what we know works in medicine and what actually happens in clinics and hospitals.
depends_on: - Evidence-based medicine (P06) for identifying interventions worthy of implementation

name: Precision Medicine
text: Precision medicine recognizes that every patient is different and aims to match the right treatment to the right patient at the right time.
depends_on: - Genomics and molecular biology (next-generation sequencing)

name: Telemedicine Principles
text: Telemedicine lets doctors care for patients without being in the same room.
depends_on: - Telecommunications infrastructure (broadband, mobile networks)

name: Antimicrobial Resistance and Clinical Stewardship
text: Bacteria, viruses, and fungi evolve resistance to the drugs used to kill them.
depends_on: - Microbiology (resistance mechanisms, susceptibility testing)

name: Polypharmacy and Deprescribing Principles
text: As people age and accumulate chronic conditions, they often end up taking many medications simultaneously — sometimes 10, 15, or more.
depends_on: - Evidence-based medicine (P06) for assessing benefit-harm balance of each medication

name: Chronotherapy and Circadian Medicine
text: Your body runs on a 24-hour internal clock that affects almost everything — when your blood pressure peaks, when your liver is most active at processing drugs, when your immune system is strongest, and when tumor cells divide fastest.
depends_on: - Physiological homeostasis and feedback (P01) for understanding rhythmic regulation

name: Circulating Tumor DNA as a Liquid Biopsy
text: When cancer cells die, they release tiny fragments of their DNA into the bloodstream.
depends_on: - Precision medicine (P26) for genomic-guided therapy selection

name: Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) in Clinical Assessment
text: Point-of-care ultrasound puts a portable ultrasound machine in the hands of the treating clinician — at the bedside, in the emergency department, or in the field — to answer specific clinical questions within minutes.
depends_on: - Diagnostic sensitivity and specificity (P09) for understanding POCUS test characteristics

name: Shared Decision-Making and Patient-Centered Care
