text: Shared decision-making recognizes that for many medical decisions, there is no single "right" answer — the best choice depends on what matters most to the patient.
depends_on: - Evidence-based medicine (P06) for providing the outcome probabilities that inform shared decisions

name: Sepsis Pathophysiology and Protocolized Management
text: Sepsis occurs when the body's response to an infection spirals out of control, damaging its own organs.
depends_on: - Inflammation cascade (P13) for understanding the dysregulated immune response

name: Tumor Dormancy and Metastatic Latency
text: After cancer surgery removes the primary tumor, individual cancer cells may already have traveled to distant organs --- bone marrow, liver, lungs, brain --- where they can sit dormant for years or even decades before waking up and growing into metastases.
depends_on: - Precision medicine (P26) for genomic profiling of dormancy-associated signatures

name: Digital Twin Modeling in Clinical Medicine
text: A clinical digital twin is a virtual replica of an individual patient --- built from their medical images, lab results, genetic data, and continuous monitoring --- that doctors can use to simulate different treatments before applying them to the real patient.
depends_on: - Precision medicine (P26) for the patient-specific data integration paradigm

name: Psychedelic-Assisted Psychotherapy
text: Psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy is a carefully structured treatment combining psychedelic drugs with professional psychological support for conditions like treatment-resistant depression, PTSD, and addiction.
depends_on: - Shared decision-making (P36) for informed consent in psychedelic therapy

name: Wastewater-Based Epidemiology
text: By testing a city's sewage, epidemiologists can estimate how many people are infected with a pathogen --- even those without symptoms who never get tested --- days before clinical cases appear.
depends_on: - Incidence and prevalence (P01)

name: Digital Epidemiology and Syndromic Surveillance 2.0
text: Digital epidemiology uses data from the internet, smartphones, and wearable devices to track disease outbreaks in near-real time.
depends_on: - Syndromic surveillance (P24)

name: Target Trial Emulation for Observational Causal Inference
text: Target trial emulation is a disciplined way to use observational data (hospital records, insurance claims) to answer cause-and-effect questions that would ideally be answered by a randomized trial.
depends_on: - Causal inference: counterfactual framework (P25)

name: Syndromic Surveillance Systems
text: Syndromic surveillance is an early warning system that detects disease outbreaks before doctors even diagnose them.
depends_on: - Incidence and prevalence (P01) for baseline disease frequency estimation

name: Social Network Analysis in Disease Transmission
text: People do not interact randomly — they have specific networks of contacts at home, work, school, and social settings, and some people have far more contacts than others.
depends_on: - Basic reproduction number R0 (P08) for threshold calculation on networks

name: Negative Outcome Control Methods
text: Geospatial epidemiology maps where diseases occur and uses statistical methods to find patterns.
depends_on: - Incidence and prevalence (P01) — the rates being mapped

name: Syndemic Theory in Epidemiology
text: Syndemic theory recognizes that diseases do not occur in isolation — they cluster in vulnerable populations and make each other worse.
depends_on: - Social determinants of health (from public health) for the structural drivers

name: Precision Public Health and Geospatially Targeted Interventions
text: Traditional public health applies the same interventions broadly to entire populations — everyone gets the same screening recommendation, the same vaccination campaign, the same health message.
depends_on: - Geospatial epidemiology (P28) for small-area disease mapping and cluster detection

name: Mendelian Randomization in Causal Epidemiology
text: Mendelian randomization is a clever way to test whether something actually causes a disease, using genetics as a natural experiment.
depends_on: - Causal inference frameworks (P25) for the instrumental variable theoretical foundation

name: Genomic Epidemiology and Phylodynamics
text: Genomic epidemiology uses the DNA sequences of pathogens to track how diseases spread — like using fingerprints to trace a crime.
depends_on: - SIR and SEIR models (P10) for the epidemic dynamics that phylodynamics estimates

name: Difference-in-Differences Causal Inference
text: Difference-in-differences is a method for estimating whether a policy or intervention actually caused a change in health outcomes when a randomized trial is impossible.
depends_on: - Confounding (P04) as the bias that DiD addresses through the parallel trends assumption

name: Agent-Based Modeling in Epidemic Simulation
text: Agent-based models simulate an epidemic by creating a virtual city (or country) populated by millions of virtual people, each with their own age, household, school or workplace, and daily routine.
depends_on: - SIR/SEIR models (P10) as the compartmental disease dynamics embedded within each agent

name: Interrupted Time Series Analysis for Policy Evaluation
text: Interrupted time series analysis is a method for evaluating whether a policy --- a new law, a public health campaign, a drug safety warning --- actually changed health outcomes.
depends_on: - Difference-in-differences (P36) as a related quasi-experimental method

name: Multiplex Spatial Epidemiology and Syndemic Mapping
text: Multiplex spatial epidemiology maps multiple diseases simultaneously across geography to find where they cluster together --- the neighborhoods and communities where diabetes AND depression AND substance use AND poverty all concentrate, reinforcing each other as a syndemic.
depends_on: - Geospatial epidemiology (P28) for spatial modeling methodology and disease mapping

name: Emulated Target Trials Using Electronic Health Records
text: Target trial emulation asks: if we could run the perfect randomized trial to answer this clinical question, what would it look like?
depends_on: - Causal inference (P25) for the potential outcomes framework and confounding adjustment

name: CAR-T Cell Engineering: Design Principles
text: CAR-T therapy takes a patient's own immune T cells, genetically reprograms them in the laboratory to recognize a specific marker on cancer cells, grows billions of these engineered cells, and infuses them back into the patient.
depends_on: - Clonal selection theory (P02)

name: mRNA Vaccine Immunology
text: mRNA vaccines deliver genetic instructions (not the virus itself) wrapped in a tiny fat bubble.
depends_on: - Vaccine principles (P11)

name: Systems Immunology: Multi-Omics Network Analysis
text: Systems immunology studies the immune system as a whole, integrated network rather than examining individual components in isolation.
depends_on: - Cytokine networks (P08)

name: Trained Immunity (Innate Immune Memory)
text: The traditional view was that only the adaptive immune system (T and B cells) has memory — that innate immune cells (like macrophages) respond identically every time.
depends_on: - Innate vs. adaptive immunity (P01) for the paradigm that trained immunity challenges

name: Immune Exhaustion and T Cell Dysfunction
text: When T cells fight a chronic infection (like HIV or hepatitis) or a cancer that they cannot clear, they become progressively exhausted — like a soldier who has been fighting for too long without rest.
depends_on: - T cell activation: two-signal model (P04) for the activation context that precedes exhaustion

name: Mucosal-Associated Invariant T (MAIT) Cells
text: Traditional immunology studies one cell type, one receptor, or one cytokine at a time.
depends_on: - Clonal selection (P02) — the diversity being measured at single-cell level

name: Mucosal Immunology and Secretory IgA
text: The mucosal immune system guards the body's vulnerable interior surfaces — the gut, lungs, and reproductive tract — where we are most exposed to the outside world.
depends_on: - Innate vs. adaptive immunity (P01) for the innate immune components of mucosal defense

name: Trained Immunity: Innate Immune Memory
text: The textbook says only the adaptive immune system (T cells and B cells) has memory.
depends_on: - Innate vs. adaptive immunity (P01) for the paradigm being expanded

name: Immune Checkpoint Therapy: Beyond PD-1 and CTLA-4
text: The first generation of immunotherapy drugs (anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4) release the brakes on T cells, enabling them to attack cancer.
depends_on: - Checkpoint inhibition (P15) for the foundational PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade mechanism

name: Chimeric Antigen Receptor NK Cell Immunotherapy
text: CAR-NK cells are a next-generation cancer immunotherapy that takes natural killer cells — the immune system's innate assassins — and supercharges them with a synthetic targeting receptor.
depends_on: - Innate vs. adaptive immunity (P01) for understanding NK cell biology and activation

name: Immune Metabolism Reprogramming in Disease
text: Immune cells dramatically rewire their internal metabolism when they are activated.
depends_on: - T cell activation (P04) for the signaling pathways that trigger metabolic reprogramming

name: Bispecific Antibody Engineering for Immune Redirection
text: Bispecific antibodies are Y-shaped molecules where each arm grabs a different target.
depends_on: - Antibody structure and function (P05) for understanding immunoglobulin engineering

name: Complement System Therapeutic Targeting
text: The complement system is a rapid-fire chemical cascade in the blood that punches holes in bacteria, coats them for immune cell consumption, and recruits inflammatory cells to infection sites.
depends_on: - Innate vs. adaptive immunity (P01) for complement as a bridge between innate and adaptive systems

name: Mucosal-Associated Invariant T Cell Biology
text: MAIT cells are a recently appreciated type of immune cell that represents a major component of human immunity --- making up 1-10% of all blood T cells and up to half of all T cells in the liver.
depends_on: - Innate vs. adaptive immunity (P01) for MAIT cells as innate-like lymphocytes bridging both systems

name: Tissue-Resident Memory T Cell Immunosurveillance
text: Tissue-resident memory T cells are immune sentinels permanently stationed in the body's barrier tissues --- skin, lungs, gut, reproductive tract --- where they stand guard long after an infection has resolved.
depends_on: - Immunological memory (P13) for the memory T cell differentiation framework

name: Spatial Transcriptomics in Tissue Pathology
text: Spatial transcriptomics lets pathologists see which genes are active in every tiny region of a tissue sample, creating a detailed "molecular map" overlaid on the familiar microscope image.
depends_on: - Cellular adaptation (P01)

name: Liquid Biopsy Pathology: Circulating Tumor DNA and Beyond
text: Liquid biopsy examines tiny traces of cancer in a patient's blood --- fragments of tumor DNA, whole tumor cells, and small vesicles shed by tumors.
depends_on: - Neoplasia: benign vs. malignant (P08)

name: Artificial Intelligence in Diagnostic Pathology
text: AI pathology uses deep learning algorithms to analyze digital microscope images of tissue, performing tasks that traditionally require years of pathologist training.
depends_on: - Digital pathology and computational pathology (P19)

name: Tumor Mutational Burden and Neoantigen Pathology
text: Every cancer has DNA mutations, but some cancers have far more mutations than others.
depends_on: - Hallmarks of cancer (P10) for understanding mutational processes

name: Ferroptosis and Regulated Non-Apoptotic Cell Death
text: Ferroptosis is a recently discovered way that cells die, driven by iron and oxidative damage to the fatty membranes of cells.
depends_on: - Cell injury and death (P02) for the classification of cell death modalities

name: Spatial Transcriptomics in Tissue Pathology
text: Traditional pathology examines tissue as a mixture of many cell types — the result is an average that can hide what individual cells are doing.
depends_on: - Molecular pathology (P17) — molecular characterization as the foundation

name: Digital Pathology and AI-Assisted Histopathological Diagnosis
text: Digital pathology replaces the traditional microscope with a computer screen: glass slides are scanned into extremely high-resolution digital images that pathologists can view, annotate, and share anywhere in the world.
depends_on: - Molecular pathology (P17) for ground truth molecular labels used in AI training

name: Liquid Biopsy Pathology: Circulating Tumor Cells and Cell-Free DNA
text: A liquid biopsy is a blood test that detects cancer by finding tumor cells or tumor DNA fragments floating in the bloodstream.
depends_on: - Neoplasia (P08) for understanding tumor cell biology and shedding mechanisms

name: Ferroptosis as a Pathological Cell Death Mechanism
text: Ferroptosis is a way cells die when iron-driven chemical reactions cause their membrane lipids to go rancid — essentially, the cell membrane oxidizes from the inside out, like metal rusting.
depends_on: - Cell injury and death (P02) for the cell death classification framework

name: Clonal Hematopoiesis and Premalignant Evolution
text: As people age, some of their blood-forming stem cells acquire random DNA mutations that give them a growth advantage over neighboring cells.
depends_on: - Neoplasia (P08) for understanding clonal expansion and premalignant states

name: Extracellular Vesicle Pathobiology
text: Extracellular vesicles are tiny bubbles released by cells that carry messages to other cells throughout the body.
depends_on: - Cell injury and death (P02) for understanding apoptotic body formation and release

name: The Tumor Immune Microenvironment as Pathological Ecosystem
text: The tumor immune microenvironment is the neighborhood surrounding a tumor — a complex mix of immune cells, blood vessels, connective tissue, and signaling molecules that collectively determine whether the immune system will attack or tolerate the cancer.
depends_on: - Tumor immunology (P21 in immunology) for T cell-mediated anti-tumor mechanisms

name: Multiplex Spatial Proteomics in Tissue Pathology
text: Multiplex spatial proteomics allows pathologists to simultaneously visualize 40-60 different proteins on a single tissue slide, painting a detailed molecular portrait of every cell in a tissue sample while preserving its spatial architecture.
depends_on: - Tumor microenvironment pathology (P26) for the biological context of spatial analysis

name: Tumor Dormancy Pathology and Disseminated Tumor Cell Niches
text: When cancer is removed by surgery, microscopic cancer cells may already have seeded distant organs --- especially bone marrow.
depends_on: - Neoplasia (P08) for understanding tumor cell biology and metastatic cascade

name: Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype in Disease Pathology
text: Senescent cells are cells that have permanently stopped dividing but refuse to die.
depends_on: - Cell injury and death (P02) for the cell death and cell fate decision framework

name: PROTAC Targeted Protein Degradation
text: PROTACs are molecular "hitmen" that hijack the cell's own protein disposal system.
depends_on: - Receptor theory: agonists, antagonists, and efficacy (P03)

name: Antibody-Drug Conjugate Pharmacology
text: An antibody-drug conjugate is a guided missile: an antibody steers a potent toxin specifically to cancer cells.
depends_on: - The law of mass action in receptor binding (P02)

name: Pharmacomicrobiomics: Gut Microbiome-Drug Interactions
text: The trillions of bacteria in your gut are a previously overlooked "organ" that processes drugs before and after your body absorbs them.
depends_on: - Bioavailability and first-pass metabolism (P06)

name: Pharmacovigilance Signal Detection
text: After a drug is approved and millions of people start taking it, rare side effects that were invisible in clinical trials (which test only thousands of patients) begin to emerge.
depends_on: - Therapeutic index and safety (P09) for adverse event characterization

name: Pharmacomicrobiomics
text: The trillions of bacteria in your gut can activate, inactivate, or transform the medications you take, dramatically affecting whether a drug works or causes side effects.
depends_on: - Pharmacokinetics: ADME (P05) for drug disposition framework

name: RNA Therapeutics Pharmacology
text: Gene therapy treats disease by delivering a working copy of a gene directly into a patient's cells.
depends_on: - Biologics (P25) — gene therapies as a class of biologic medicines

name: Chronopharmacology and Circadian Drug Dosing
text: Your body's internal clock affects how it handles every medication.
depends_on: - Pharmacokinetics: ADME (P05) for time-dependent drug disposition

name: Microbiome Therapeutics and Fecal Microbiota Transplantation
text: Fecal microbiota transplantation — essentially transplanting the gut bacteria from a healthy person into a sick person — is remarkably effective for treating recurrent C.
depends_on: - Pharmacomicrobiomics (P30) for understanding drug-microbiome interactions

name: Allosteric Modulation and Bitopic Ligand Pharmacology
text: Most drugs work by fitting into the same binding site as the body's natural chemical messenger, directly blocking or activating the receptor.
depends_on: - Receptor theory (P03) for the occupancy-response framework

name: Pharmacogenomic Dosing Algorithms
text: Pharmacogenomic dosing algorithms use a patient's DNA to calculate the right drug dose for that specific individual.
depends_on: - Pharmacogenomics (P13) for the genetic basis of drug response variability

name: Nanomedicine Drug Delivery Systems
text: Nanomedicine wraps drugs inside tiny particles (1/1000th the width of a hair) to deliver them more effectively to diseased tissue while protecting healthy tissue.
depends_on: - Pharmacokinetics: ADME (P05) for understanding how nanoparticle formulation alters drug distribution

name: Multi-Target Drug Pharmacology (Polypharmacology)
text: For decades, drug design aimed for a "magic bullet" — one drug, one target, one disease.
depends_on: - Receptor theory (P03) for understanding binding affinity and efficacy at multiple targets

name: Computational Drug Repurposing Methods
text: Drug repurposing uses computers to find new uses for drugs that are already approved and proven safe.
depends_on: - Receptor theory (P03) for understanding drug-target binding interactions

name: Covalent Drug Design and Targeted Electrophilic Pharmacology
text: Most drugs work by temporarily sitting in a protein's binding pocket, like a key in a lock, and eventually falling out.
depends_on: - Receptor theory (P03) for the binding equilibrium framework extended to irreversible kinetics

name: Radiopharmaceutical Theranostics
text: Radiopharmaceutical theranostics is the concept of "see it, treat it" --- using the same molecular targeting vehicle to both image and treat cancer.
depends_on: - Pharmacokinetics: ADME (P05) for radiopharmaceutical biodistribution and clearance

name: Planetary Health
text: Planetary health recognizes that human health ultimately depends on the health of Earth's natural systems.
depends_on: - Social determinants of health (P02)

name: Wastewater Surveillance for Population Health Monitoring
text: Analyzing sewage provides a real-time, anonymous health check-up for an entire community.
depends_on: - Surveillance systems (P11)

name: Climate-Sensitive Disease Surveillance and Adaptation
text: Climate change is making heat waves deadlier, expanding the range of mosquito-borne diseases, and intensifying floods and droughts that threaten health.
depends_on: - Levels of prevention (P01)

name: One Health Framework
text: One Health recognizes that human health cannot be understood in isolation from animal health and the environment.
depends_on: - Social determinants of health (P02) for the environmental and economic drivers of zoonotic risk

name: Social Determinants of Health Measurement
text: Social determinants of health are the conditions where people are born, grow, live, work, and age that powerfully shape their health outcomes — often more so than medical care itself.
depends_on: - Health equity (P03) for the ethical framework demanding measurement

name: Infodemic Management and Health Misinformation
text: Planetary health is the idea that human health and the health of the planet are inseparable.
depends_on: - One Health (P12) — zoonotic disease and human-animal-environment nexus

name: Health Equity Measurement Frameworks
text: Health equity measurement puts numbers on unfairness.
depends_on: - Social determinants of health (P30) for the structural drivers being measured

name: Syndemic Theory in Public Health Practice
text: A syndemic occurs when multiple health problems come together in a population and make each other worse, driven by shared social conditions.
depends_on: - Social determinants of health (P30) for the structural conditions driving syndemics

name: Implementation Science for Public Health Scale-Up
text: Implementation science is about closing the enormous gap between what we know works in public health and what actually happens in communities.
depends_on: - Health systems: WHO building blocks (P08) for the organizational context of implementation

name: Health Technology Assessment (HTA) Methodology
text: Health technology assessment answers a critical question: does this new treatment provide enough health benefit to justify its cost?
depends_on: - Cost-effectiveness analysis (P07) for the economic evaluation framework

name: Equity-Focused Health Impact Assessment
text: Equity-focused health impact assessment asks: who benefits and who is harmed when a new policy is implemented, and are the benefits and harms distributed fairly across different groups in society?
depends_on: - Social determinants of health (P02) for the conceptual framework of health equity

name: Climate Change Adaptation in Public Health Systems
text: Climate change is not just an environmental issue — it is a public health emergency.
depends_on: - Environmental health (P14) for exposure-response relationships of environmental hazards

name: Precision Public Health Genomics
text: Precision public health genomics uses genetic testing not just for individual patients, but for entire populations, to identify people at high risk of diseases like cancer, heart disease, or drug reactions before they ever get sick.
depends_on: - Precision public health (P33 in epidemiology) for the targeting framework

name: Participatory Action Research in Community Health
text: Participatory action research flips the traditional research model: instead of academic experts studying a community from the outside, PAR makes community members full partners in the research --- from deciding what questions to ask, to collecting data, to interpreting results, to taking action on findings.
depends_on: - Social determinants of health (P30) for the structural conditions that PAR addresses

name: Wastewater-Based Epidemiology for Population Health Surveillance
text: Wastewater-based epidemiology turns a city's sewer system into a giant diagnostic tool.
depends_on: - Surveillance systems (P11) for the public health surveillance framework

name: Regenerative Architecture
text: Regenerative architecture goes beyond sustainability (doing less harm) to actively improving the environment.
depends_on: - Vitruvian Triad (A01)

name: Biophilic Design: Nature-Human Connection in the Built Environment
text: Biophilic design brings nature into buildings based on the scientific finding that humans are healthier, happier, and more productive when connected to natural elements.
depends_on: - Vitruvian Triad (A01)

name: Circular Economy Principles in Architecture
text: Circular architecture treats buildings as temporary assemblies of valuable components rather than permanent structures destined for demolition and landfill.
depends_on: - Vitruvian Triad (A01)

name: Passive Survivability
text: Passive survivability asks: if the power goes out for days or weeks — during a hurricane, an ice storm, or a heat wave — can this building keep its occupants alive without any electricity, gas, or running water?
depends_on: - Building envelope performance (P06) for the thermal barrier that maintains conditions

name: Evidence-Based Design in Healthcare Architecture
text: Evidence-based design applies the same rigorous evidence standards used in medicine to the design of healthcare buildings.
depends_on: - Universal design and accessibility (P12) for inclusive healthcare environments

name: Cradle-to-Cradle Material Cycles in Architecture
text: Instead of building everything on a muddy construction site exposed to weather and labor shortages, modular construction builds rooms, apartments, or entire floors in a factory — with precision, quality control, and no rain delays.
depends_on: - Building Information Modeling (A22) — digital coordination essential for off-site precision

name: Computational Morphogenesis in Architecture
text: Computational morphogenesis lets architecture grow itself.
depends_on: - Structural principles (P04, P05) for structural performance as a morphogenetic driver

name: Responsive Architecture and Kinetic Building Systems
text: Responsive architecture means buildings that move and adapt, like living organisms responding to their environment.
depends_on: - Building envelope performance (P06) for the thermal and optical properties being modulated

name: Post-Occupancy Evaluation (POE) Methodology
text: Post-occupancy evaluation is the practice of going back to a building after it has been occupied and measuring whether it actually performs as the architects and engineers intended.
depends_on: - Passive solar design (P08) and building envelope performance (P06) for the energy design intent being evaluated

name: Hygrothermal Performance of Building Envelopes
text: Hygrothermal performance is about how heat and moisture move together through the walls and roof of a building.
depends_on: - Building envelope performance (P06) for the functional requirements of wall assemblies

name: Thermal Comfort Theory (Fanger and Adaptive Models)
text: Thermal comfort theory predicts whether people will feel comfortable in a building based on measurable environmental conditions and what they are wearing and doing.
depends_on: - Building envelope performance (P06) for managing heat gains and losses

name: Life Cycle Assessment in Building Design
text: Life cycle assessment for buildings adds up all the environmental damage caused by a building from the moment raw materials are dug out of the ground to the moment the building is demolished and its materials are landfilled or recycled.
depends_on: - Embodied carbon principles (P28) for understanding material-stage carbon impacts

name: Daylighting Design Science
text: Daylighting design science brings natural light deep into buildings in a way that is comfortable (no glare), uniform (no dark corners), and sufficient (enough light to work without turning on electric lights for most of the day).
depends_on: - Passive solar design (P08) for the thermal implications of glazing design

name: Architectural Acoustics and Room Tuning
text: Architectural acoustics is the science of how sound behaves in rooms --- what makes a concert hall sound magnificent, a classroom intelligible, and an open office tolerable.
depends_on: - Acoustic design fundamentals (P14) for sound transmission and noise control principles

name: Mass Timber Structural Systems
text: Mass timber construction uses large, engineered wood panels and beams to build tall buildings that rival the height and performance of concrete and steel structures.
depends_on: - Structural principles (P04 in architecture) for load path and structural system design

name: 4D Printing in Product Design
text: 4D printing adds the dimension of time to 3D-printed products.
depends_on: - Materials and manufacturing constraints (P08)

name: Metamaterial Design for Functional Products
text: Metamaterial design creates products whose physical behavior comes from their internal geometric structure rather than the material itself.
depends_on: - Form follows function in products (A01)

name: Digital Product Passport and Lifecycle Transparency
text: A digital product passport is like a birth certificate and medical record for every product.
depends_on: - Product lifecycle (P09)

name: Affordance Theory in Product Design
text: Affordance theory explains why some products are immediately intuitive and others require an instruction manual.
depends_on: - Usability heuristics (P06) for the broader framework of evaluating product usability

name: Human-Centered Design Thinking Process
text: Design thinking is a structured process for innovation that starts with deeply understanding the people you are designing for, rather than starting with a solution or technology.
depends_on: - User-centered design (P17) for the principle of grounding decisions in user understanding

name: Design for Longevity and Emotional Durability
text: Most product design focuses on how things look, but people experience products with all their senses.
depends_on: - Human factors and ergonomics (P03) — perceptual capabilities and limitations

name: Speculative Design and Design Fiction
text: Speculative design creates products and experiences from imaginary futures to help us think about where we are headed and whether we want to go there.
depends_on: - Design thinking process (P07) for the human-centered inquiry that speculative design extends into the future

name: Transition Design for Systemic Societal Change
text: Transition design tackles the biggest challenges facing society — climate change, inequality, food insecurity, public health crises — by using design to help communities and institutions shift from current unsustainable systems to sustainable ones.
depends_on: - Systems thinking in design (P19) for understanding systemic interconnections

name: Design for Behavioral Change (Design for Sustainable Behavior)
text: Design for behavioral change uses the design of products and systems to nudge, guide, or help people act more sustainably without relying on willpower or education alone.
depends_on: - Human factors and ergonomics (P03) for understanding cognitive and behavioral capabilities

name: Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)
text: DFMA asks: can we redesign this product so it has fewer parts, is easier to put together, and costs less to make — without sacrificing functionality?
depends_on: - Materials and manufacturing constraints (P08) for understanding process capabilities

name: Kansei Engineering (Affective Design)
text: Kansei engineering answers the question: "How do we make a product feel elegant, powerful, playful, or luxurious?" It bridges the gap between the subjective, emotional reactions people have to products and the concrete design decisions (shape, color, material, texture) that create those feelings.
depends_on: - Emotional design (P12) for the theoretical foundation of emotion in product experience

name: Parametric and Generative Design for Products
text: Parametric and generative design lets the computer design products.
depends_on: - Form follows function (A01) for the principle that structural performance drives form

name: Co-Design and Participatory Design Methods
text: Co-design puts the people who will actually use a product, service, or system at the center of the design process --- not just as testers at the end, but as creative partners from the very beginning.
depends_on: - User-centered design (P17) as the precursor methodology that co-design extends

name: Speculative and Critical Design
text: Speculative and critical design creates provocative objects and scenarios that ask "what if?" rather than solving immediate practical problems.
depends_on: - Design thinking process (P13) for the ideation and prototyping methods adapted for speculation

name: Systems-Oriented Design for Complex Problems
text: Systems-oriented design recognizes that the most important design challenges --- climate change, healthcare inequality, food system transformation, urban sustainability --- are not product problems but system problems.
depends_on: - Design thinking process (P13) for the human-centered methods adapted for systemic contexts

name: Parametric Urbanism
text: Parametric urbanism uses computer algorithms to design cities and neighborhoods.
depends_on: - Urban growth models (P05)

name: Urban Digital Twin and Smart City Infrastructure
text: An urban digital twin is a living computer model of a city that mirrors what is happening in real time --- traffic flowing, energy being consumed, air quality changing, water systems operating.
depends_on: - Data-driven urban planning (P27)

name: Regenerative Urbanism and Nature-Based Solutions
text: Regenerative urbanism designs cities that heal the environment rather than just damaging it less.
depends_on: - Green infrastructure (P11)

name: Tactical Urbanism Theory and Practice
text: Tactical urbanism is the idea of trying out urban changes cheaply and temporarily before committing to expensive permanent construction.
depends_on: - Placemaking (P13) for the community engagement and public space activation principles

name: Urban Food Systems Planning
text: Urban food systems planning recognizes that food is a spatial and infrastructure issue, not just an agricultural one.
depends_on: - Zoning theory (P03) for land use designations enabling urban agriculture

name: Age-Friendly City Design
text: Cities face increasing heat waves, flooding, droughts, and storms because of climate change.
depends_on: - Urban heat island effect (P10) — the phenomenon being mitigated

name: Complete Streets Design
text: Complete streets are designed for everyone, not just cars.
depends_on: - Walkability (P08) for pedestrian-oriented design fundamentals

name: Food-Sensitive Urban Design
text: Food-sensitive urban design recognizes that where people live determines what they eat, which determines their health.
depends_on: - Zoning theory (P03) for land use designations enabling food system integration

name: Tactical Urbanism and Incremental Urban Transformation
text: Tactical urbanism is the practice of making quick, cheap, temporary changes to streets and public spaces to test whether a design idea actually works before spending millions on permanent construction.
depends_on: - Walkability (P08) for the pedestrian-oriented outcomes being tested

name: Urban Commons and Shared Space Governance
text: Urban commons are shared resources in cities — parks, community gardens, coworking spaces, shared streets, local waterways — that belong to everyone and require collective management to avoid degradation.
depends_on: - Participatory planning (P21) for the democratic governance processes managing urban commons

name: Stormwater Management and Low-Impact Development
text: Low-impact development manages rainwater where it falls rather than piping it away through storm drains.
depends_on: - Green infrastructure (P11) for the broader framework of nature-based urban solutions

name: Urban Freight and Last-Mile Logistics Planning
text: Urban freight planning addresses the trucks, vans, and delivery vehicles that keep cities supplied with goods — from restaurant ingredients to e-commerce parcels to construction materials.
depends_on: - Transit-oriented development (P07) for integrating freight hubs into mixed-use transit nodes

name: Smart City Digital Infrastructure and Data Governance
text: Smart city infrastructure uses sensors, data, and algorithms to make cities work better --- managing traffic flow in real time, predicting where flooding will occur, optimizing garbage collection routes, and monitoring air quality block by block.
depends_on: - Participatory planning (P21) for democratic governance of smart city technology

name: Urban Heat Island Mitigation Strategies
text: Cities are hotter than the countryside because dark roofs and roads absorb sunlight, concrete and asphalt store heat during the day and release it at night, there are fewer trees and less soil to provide cooling evaporation, and buildings and vehicles pump out waste heat.
depends_on: - Green infrastructure (P11) for the vegetation-based cooling strategies

name: Participatory Digital Planning and Civic Technology
text: Participatory digital planning brings public engagement in planning decisions online, reaching thousands of residents instead of the dozens who attend public meetings.
depends_on: - Participatory planning (P21) for the theoretical framework of public participation in planning

name: CRISPR-Based Crop Engineering
text: CRISPR is a molecular scissors tool that lets plant breeders make precise changes to a crop's DNA --- knocking out genes that make plants susceptible to disease, activating genes that improve nutritional quality, or fine-tuning genes that control flowering time.
depends_on: - Mendelian inheritance in plant breeding (AG09)

name: Vertical Farming: Controlled Environment Agriculture Physics
text: Vertical farms grow crops indoors in stacked layers under LED lights, without soil and with precisely controlled temperature, humidity, and nutrients.
depends_on: - Photosynthetic efficiency limits (AG02)

name: Regenerative Grazing and Soil Carbon Sequestration
text: Regenerative grazing uses cattle and other livestock as land management tools, mimicking the movement patterns of wild herds.
depends_on: - Soil health principles (AG22)

name: Allelopathy in Crop Systems
text: Allelopathy is chemical warfare among plants.
depends_on: - Integrated Pest Management (AG08) for allelopathy as a weed management component

name: Biofortification
text: Biofortification means breeding staple crops — rice, wheat, beans, sweet potato, pearl millet — to contain more vitamins and minerals so that people eating their normal diets get better nutrition without changing their food habits.
depends_on: - Mendelian inheritance in plant breeding (AG09) for conventional biofortification breeding

name: Silvopasture: Integrated Tree-Livestock Systems
text: Controlled environment agriculture grows crops indoors under precisely controlled conditions — think of a factory for lettuce, herbs, or strawberries.
depends_on: - Photosynthetic efficiency limits (AG02) — light use efficiency determines energy cost per unit of biomass

name: Conservation Agriculture and No-Till Systems
text: Conservation agriculture means farming without plowing.
depends_on: - Soil health principles (AG22) for the biological mechanisms of no-till soil improvement

name: Deficit Irrigation and Regulated Water Stress
text: Deficit irrigation is the counterintuitive strategy of deliberately giving crops less water than they need at specific times.
depends_on: - Water use efficiency (AG06) for the soil-plant-atmosphere continuum

name: Cover Crop Ecology and Multi-Functional Services
text: Cover crops are planted not to harvest but to protect and improve the soil between cash crop seasons.
depends_on: - Biological nitrogen fixation (AG04) for the legume nitrogen contribution

name: Crop-Livestock Integration Systems
text: Crop-livestock integration puts animals and crops on the same farm — not as separate enterprises but as partners in a cycle.
depends_on: - Soil fertility management (AG17) for nutrient cycling between crop and livestock phases

name: Biological Pest Control and Conservation Biocontrol
text: Biological pest control uses nature's own pest killers — predators, parasites, and pathogens — to keep crop pests in check without synthetic pesticides.
depends_on: - Integrated pest management (AG08) for the broader pest management framework within which biocontrol operates

name: Plant Phenotyping and High-Throughput Field Screening
text: Plant phenotyping measures the physical traits of crops — how tall they grow, how much leaf area they produce, how quickly they mature, how efficiently they photosynthesize, how well they tolerate drought.
depends_on: - Mendelian inheritance and plant breeding (AG09) for the genetic basis of traits being phenotyped

name: Intercropping Systems Ecology and Overyielding
text: Intercropping grows two or more crops together on the same field --- such as maize with beans, wheat with clover, or cassava with cowpea --- and typically produces more total food per hectare than growing either crop alone.
depends_on: - Liebig's law and nutrient management (AG01) for nutrient complementarity between species

name: Plant Growth-Promoting Rhizobacteria (PGPR)
text: Plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria are beneficial bacteria that live on and around plant roots, acting as natural fertilizer factories, disease protectors, and growth stimulators.
depends_on: - Biological nitrogen fixation (AG04) for the nitrogen fixation mechanisms shared with symbiotic diazotrophs

name: Pollinator-Dependent Crop Management
text: Many of the world's most important food crops --- almonds, apples, blueberries, coffee, cacao, watermelons, and dozens more --- depend on bees, butterflies, and other pollinators to transfer pollen and produce fruit.
depends_on: - Biological pest control (AG36) for the shared requirement of non-crop habitat supporting beneficial insects

name: Precision Fermentation for Food Ingredients
text: Precision fermentation is like brewing beer, but instead of making alcohol, the microorganisms are programmed to make specific food ingredients --- milk proteins without cows, egg proteins without chickens, or collagen without animals.
depends_on: - Fermentation biochemistry (FS18)

name: CRISPR Applications in Food Crop Improvement
text: CRISPR gene editing lets food scientists precisely modify the genes of food crops to improve nutritional value, extend shelf life, reduce allergens, and enhance processing quality --- all without adding foreign DNA.
depends_on: - Food preservation principles (FS04)

name: Food Systems Lifecycle Assessment and Planetary Boundaries
text: Food LCA tracks the total environmental footprint of what we eat, from farm to fork to landfill.
depends_on: - Food packaging science (FS16)

name: Food Matrix Effects on Nutrient Bioavailability
text: The same nutrient can behave completely differently depending on the food it comes in.
depends_on: - Nutrition: macronutrients and bioavailability (FS14) for the nutrient absorption framework

name: Maillard Reaction Kinetics and Control
text: The Maillard reaction is what makes bread crusts golden, coffee aromatic, grilled steak flavorful, and chocolate brown.
depends_on: - Thermal preservation (FS03) for time-temperature relationships in heat processing

name: High-Pressure Processing (HPP) Thermodynamics
text: The world needs more protein for a growing population, but producing that protein primarily from animals — especially beef and dairy — uses too much land, water, and energy and produces too many greenhouse gas emissions.
depends_on: - Protein denaturation (FS09) — structure-function relationships determining texture and functionality

name: Fermentation Biotechnology and Starter Culture Engineering
text: Fermentation is humanity's oldest biotechnology — turning milk into cheese, grapes into wine, cabbage into kimchi — and it is now a precise science.
depends_on: - Fermentation biochemistry (FS18) for the metabolic pathways producing flavor, acid, and gas

name: Food Nanotechnology and Nanoencapsulation
text: Food nanotechnology works at the scale of billionths of a meter to solve practical food problems.
depends_on: - Emulsion science (FS07) for the colloidal chemistry underlying nanoemulsions and nanoparticles

name: Food Authenticity and Fraud Detection
text: Food fraud is a multi-billion-dollar global problem: olive oil diluted with cheaper seed oils, honey adulterated with sugar syrup, fish mislabeled as a more expensive species, organic products that are actually conventionally grown.
depends_on: - Food safety regulation (HACCP, FS12) for the regulatory framework requiring authenticity

name: Non-Thermal Plasma Processing for Food Safety
text: Cold plasma processing uses electrically charged gas — a soup of reactive molecules, UV light, and charged particles — to kill bacteria, molds, and viruses on food surfaces without heating the food.
depends_on: - Food microbiology (FS11) for understanding microbial inactivation mechanisms and kinetics

name: Encapsulation Technology for Bioactive Ingredients
text: Encapsulation wraps sensitive food ingredients inside tiny protective shells — like putting a vitamin pill inside an even tinier capsule.
depends_on: - Emulsion science (FS07) for the emulsification step underlying many encapsulation methods

name: Predictive Microbiology and Quantitative Risk Assessment
text: Predictive microbiology uses mathematical models to forecast what harmful bacteria will do in food under different conditions — how fast they will grow at a given temperature, how long they survive in an acidic marinade, how quickly they are killed during cooking.
depends_on: - Food microbiology (FS11) for the growth kinetics and environmental limits of foodborne pathogens

name: Ohmic Heating for Continuous Food Processing
text: Ohmic heating cooks food by passing electricity directly through it — the food itself becomes the heating element.
depends_on: - Thermal preservation principles (FS03) for the D-value and z-value kinetics governing microbial inactivation during ohmic sterilization

name: Supercritical CO2 Extraction Technology
text: Supercritical CO2 extraction uses carbon dioxide in a special state — heated and pressurized beyond its critical point — as a solvent to pull desired compounds out of food.
depends_on: - Lipid oxidation and stability (FS06) for the oxidation-sensitive compounds (omega-3 fatty acids, carotenoids) that benefit from the oxygen-free scCO2 extraction environment

name: Cellular Agriculture and Cultured Meat Science
text: Cultured meat grows real animal meat from cells in a bioreactor instead of raising and slaughtering whole animals.
depends_on: - Protein chemistry and functionality (FS09) for the molecular basis of meat texture, flavor, and nutritional properties that cultured meat must replicate

name: Agroforestry Carbon Accounting and Climate Mitigation
text: Agroforestry --- growing trees alongside crops or livestock --- is one of the most effective land-based strategies for capturing carbon from the atmosphere.
depends_on: - Forest growth models and yield tables (FO04)

name: Forest Microbiome and Tree Health
text: Every forest tree lives in intimate partnership with billions of microorganisms.
depends_on: - Forest pathology (FO17)

name: Forest-Based Bioeconomy and Mass Timber Engineering
text: The forest bioeconomy uses wood from sustainably managed forests to replace carbon-intensive materials like steel, concrete, and plastic.
depends_on: - Forest growth models and yield tables (FO04)

name: Seed Bank and Seed Ecology in Forest Regeneration
text: Forest regeneration after fire, logging, or windstorm depends on where seeds come from and whether they find suitable places to grow.
depends_on: - Forest succession (FO01) for the temporal sequence of species replacement

name: Mass Timber Engineering and Cross-Laminated Timber
text: Mass timber is a revolution in construction: building tall buildings — 10, 15, even 25 stories — primarily from engineered wood instead of concrete and steel.
depends_on: - Wood science: anatomy and properties (FO11) for material behavior of timber

name: Mycorrhizal Networks in Forest Ecosystem Function
text: Drones are transforming how forests are monitored and managed.
depends_on: - Remote sensing (FO08) — drones as a complement to satellite and airborne sensing

name: Wood Modification Technology and Thermally Modified Timber
text: Wood is an excellent building material but it rots, swells, shrinks, and gets eaten by fungi and insects when it gets wet.
depends_on: - Wood science: anatomy and properties (FO11) for the cell wall chemistry and structure being modified

name: Urban Forestry and Canopy Ecosystem Services
text: Urban trees are not just decorative — they are critical infrastructure.
depends_on: - Forest health and pathology (FO17) for pest and disease management of urban trees

name: Forest Bioeconomy and Non-Timber Forest Products
text: Forests produce far more than lumber.
depends_on: - Sustainable yield principle (FO05) for ensuring NTFP harvest does not exceed regeneration capacity

name: Forest Carbon Flux Measurement and Eddy Covariance
text: Eddy covariance measures how much carbon dioxide a forest breathes in and out, minute by minute, for years at a time.
depends_on: - Carbon accounting in forests (FO13) for the carbon balance framework that eddy covariance quantifies

name: Assisted Natural Regeneration and Close-to-Nature Silviculture
text: Assisted natural regeneration restores forests by working with nature rather than against it.
depends_on: - Forest succession (FO01) for the ecological processes driving natural regeneration

name: Forest Landscape Restoration and the Bonn Challenge
text: Forest landscape restoration rebuilds degraded forests and the benefits they provide — clean water, carbon storage, biodiversity, timber, and livelihoods — across entire landscapes.
depends_on: - Forest succession (FO01) for the ecological trajectory of restored landscapes

name: Dendroecology and Tree-Ring Science
text: Dendroecology reads the history of forests by studying tree rings.
depends_on: - Forest growth models (FO04) for the biological growth processes that produce annual rings

name: Community Forestry Governance and Tenure
text: Community forestry governance is about local communities managing their own forests — making and enforcing the rules for who can use the forest, what can be harvested, and how the forest is protected.
depends_on: - Forest economics (FO06) for the economic incentive structures that shape community forest management decisions

name: Forest Microbiome and Soil-Tree Feedback Systems
text: The forest microbiome is the invisible ecosystem of microorganisms — fungi, bacteria, and other microbes — that lives in and on every part of a forest, from the soil to the treetops.
depends_on: - Forest succession (FO01) for the changing microbial community composition during successional development

name: Soil Microbiome Engineering
text: Soil microbiome engineering is the deliberate manipulation of the trillions of microorganisms living in soil to help crops grow better, resist disease, and capture more nutrients.
depends_on: - Soil microbiome metagenomics (SS21)

name: Soil Carbon Measurement and Verification
text: Measuring how much carbon is stored in soil sounds simple but is actually one of agriculture's hardest measurement challenges.
depends_on: - Soil organic matter dynamics (SS05)

name: Soil-Climate Feedback Mechanisms
text: Soils are both victims and amplifiers of climate change.
depends_on: - Soil organic matter dynamics (SS05)

name: Mycorrhizal Network Ecology
text: Beneath the soil surface, the roots of different plants are often connected by a vast network of fungal threads — mycorrhizal fungi that form partnerships with plant roots.
depends_on: - Mycorrhizal networks (SS10) for the basic symbiosis biology

name: Soil Aggregate Hierarchy Theory
text: Soil is not a random pile of particles — it is organized into a hierarchy of clumps within clumps, like Russian nesting dolls.
depends_on: - Soil structure and aggregation (SS08) for the physical basis of aggregate formation

name: Seed Bank Ecology and Persistence
text: Plants change the soil they grow in, and those changes affect what can grow there next.
depends_on: - Crop rotation (AG03) — plant-soil feedback as the mechanistic basis for rotation benefits

name: Phytoremediation and Plant-Soil Contaminant Interactions
text: Phytoremediation uses plants as living cleanup machines for contaminated soil.
depends_on: - Soil contamination and remediation (SS21) for the contaminant sources and baseline remediation strategies

name: Pedotransfer Functions and Digital Soil Mapping
text: Soil scientists cannot measure every property of every soil everywhere — it would take centuries and cost billions.
depends_on: - Soil classification and taxonomy (SS02) for the pedological data serving as PTF inputs

name: Soil Salinization Processes and Reclamation
text: When salts build up in soil — from irrigation water, rising salty groundwater, or natural processes in dry climates — the soil becomes saline, and most crops cannot grow.
depends_on: - Soil water retention and movement (SS06) for the capillary rise and leaching dynamics governing salt transport

name: Soil Microbial Ecology and Functional Guilds
text: A single teaspoon of healthy soil contains more microorganisms than there are people on Earth — billions of bacteria, fungi, archaea, and other tiny life forms.
depends_on: - Soil organic matter dynamics (SS05) for the substrate supply driving microbial activity

name: Soil Organic Carbon Fractionation and Turnover
text: Not all soil carbon is equal.
depends_on: - Soil organic matter dynamics (SS05) for the inputs and decomposition processes governing SOC

name: Soil-Groundwater Interactions and Contaminant Transport
text: Soil-groundwater interactions are about how water and pollutants move downward through soil to reach the groundwater we drink.
depends_on: - Darcy's law (SS07) for the fundamental water flow equation in porous media

name: Biochar Soil Amendment Science
text: Biochar is essentially charcoal made from plant waste that is mixed into soil as an amendment.
depends_on: - Soil organic matter dynamics (SS05) for understanding how biochar interacts with native soil organic carbon pools

name: Soil Crust Biology and Dryland Soil Surface Ecology
text: Biological soil crusts are the living skin of desert soils — a thin, fragile layer of tiny organisms (cyanobacteria, mosses, lichens) that form a community on the soil surface.
depends_on: - Soil structure and aggregation (SS08) for the physical mechanisms by which biocrust organisms bind soil particles

name: Electrochemical Soil Sensing and Real-Time Nutrient Monitoring
text: Electrochemical soil sensors work like tiny chemical laboratories buried in the soil, continuously measuring nutrient levels, pH, and salinity without needing to collect soil samples and send them to a lab.
depends_on: - Soil chemistry (SS02) for the chemical speciation and equilibria governing nutrient availability in soil solution
